E1.6. ARE THERE ACTIVITIES THAT PREDISPOSE US TO HAPPINESS?

E1.6.A. LOVE AND FRIENDSHIP
Joy in love comes from sharing experiences 
with chosen and loved ones.

E1.6.A.1 DOES LOVE MAKE US HAPPY?

E1.6.A.1.1. TRUE LOVE RESONATES IN THE WORLD
We marvel at the unending renewal of true love. This sense of wonder applies both to the love for a person and the love for the world - from one, we transition to the other. The love for a man or a woman nurtures and is nurtured by the love for the world. In "infinite resonance", the fruit of our interaction with others and the world offers an "infinite flavor", even though "each of us is finite". This is because "Fundamental happiness relies more than anything on what may be referred to as a friendly interest in beings and things". This love can be enriched by a love relationship, but it transcends it. Baruch Spinoza refers to this expansive love as beatitude, or "Intellectual Love of God" or Nature. Prior to the 20th century, in the West, Spinoza was perhaps the only one who could articulate this love, which feels like an "awakening", and which Asian philosophies have spoken of eloquently for centuries.

E1.6.A.1.1.a. ECHOES OF HUMANITY: CELEBRATING LOVE, CONNECTION, AND SHARED EXISTENCE

E1.5.C.4.1.a_REF2_PO.wE1.6.A.1.1.a.

E1.5.C.4.1.a_REF1_PO.wE1.6.A.1.1.a.

E1.6.A.1.1.a_REF2_CUR RENOIR'S "PICKING FLOWERS" REFLECTS THE JOY OF BEING TOGETHER AND OPENNESS TO THE WORLD AROUND US. "In Renoir's Picking Flowers, the two girls are really together because they are friends. How does the painter make us feel this?
By the way he arranges his figures in the space of the picture. We can clearly see that they are not two young girls indifferent to each other, sitting casually next to each other. The relationship between their bodies is harmonious, and the bouquet of white flowers in the centre of the picture is a sign of this union. The two girls are musically attuned to him. But it's not just the composition that brings out this unity. The way Renoir unleashes the full effect of colour plays an important role. Colour dissolves lines and contours to become emotion. The airy feeling of true ternity.
This radiant deployment shows us the joy of being together as one of the most important ways of being open to trees and flowers, grasses and wind - to the inviting landscape of an advanced spring. Friendship, by keeping us together, one beside the other, opens us to the world".
(Fabrice Midal, 3 minutes of philosophy to become human again)

E1.6.A.1.1.a_REF3_PO "SWIFTS TURN IN THE HEIGHTS OF THE AIR; 
HIGHER STILL TURN THE INVISIBLE STARS.
When day withdraws to the ends of the earth 
their fires shine on a dark expanse of sand...

We live in a world of motion and distance.
The heart flies from tree to bird, 
from bird to distant star, 
from star to love; and love grows 
in the quiet house, turning and working, 
servant of thought, holding a lamp in one hand."
(Philippe Jaccottet)

E1.6.A.1.1.a_REF4_LIT IN A MARRIAGE, "THIRD THINGS" SUCH AS OBJECTS, PRACTICES, ARTS, OR INSTITUTIONS ARE ESSENTIAL AS THEY PROVIDE A SHARED FOCUS AND JOINT CONTENTMENT, BRINGING THE COUPLE TOGETHER IN SHARED ATTENTION.
"We did not spend our days gazing into each other’s eyes. We did that gazing when we made love or when one of us was in trouble, but most of the time our gazes met and entwined as they looked at a third thing. Third things are essential to marriages, objects or practices or habits or arts or institutions or games or human beings that provide a site of joint rapture or contentment. Each member of a couple is separate; the two come together in double attention. Lovemaking is not a third thing but two-in-one. John Keats can be a third thing, or the Boston Symphony Orchestra, or Dutch interiors, or Monopoly. [...]

We had our summer afternoons at the pond, which for ten years made a third thing. After naps we loaded up books and blankets and walked across Route 4 and the old railroad to the steep slippery bank that led down to our private beach on Eagle Pond. Soft moss underfoot sent little red flowers up. Ghost birches leaned over water with wild strawberry plants growing under them. Over our heads white pines reared high, and oaks that warned us of summer’s end late in August by dropping green metallic acorns. Sometimes a mink scooted among ferns… Jane dozed in the sun as I sat in the shade reading and occasionally taking a note in a blank book. From time to time we swam and dried in the heat."
(Donald Hall, The Third Thing, 2004)

E1.6.A.1.1.a_REF5_PH TRUE INTIMACY IN RELATIONSHIPS ENABLES PURE PERCEPTION AND SPONTANEOUS RESPONSE, FOSTERING A DEEP SENSE OF ELATION AND AWARENESS. Trust between partners allows them to share their inner worlds without fear, enhancing their mutual experience.
"Most human beings never really see another person after they are five years old. In an intimate relationship, each party returns to the original naïve Child ego state, where he is free of such Parental prohibitions and Adult requirements, and can see, hear, and taste in its purest form what the world has to offer. This freedom of the Child is the essential part of intimacy, and it turns the whole universe, including the sun, moon, and stars, into a golden apple for both parties to enjoy. […]

Once the Child is free of Adult caution and Parental criticism, he has a sense of elation and awareness. He begins to see and hear and feel the way he really wants to, the way he originally did before he was corrupted by his living parents. In this autonomous state, he no longer has to name things, as is usually required by his Adult, nor account for his behavior, as demanded by his Parent. He is free to respond directly and spontaneously to what he sees and hears and feels. Because the two parties trust one another, they freely open up their secret worlds of perception, experience, and behavior to each other, asking nothing in return except the delight of opening the gates without fear."
(Eric Berne, Sex in Human Loving)

E1.6.A.1.1.a_REF6_PO EXTREME LOVE IS A FEELING OF WONDER AND INCREDIBILITY IN FRONT OF THE LOVED ONE'S EXISTENCE "Extreme love is the feeling of the impossibility of the loved one's existence, so touching, so ravishing, and such that it arouses a strange joy or desire, a kind of nervous infinity, in the finite form of an object, an inexhaustible sensitive treasure. 
At the same time, this existing impossibility becomes divinity. Sensual satisfaction is no more than a more or less happy circumstance in the relations between the idol and the idolater, but not an essential event as in ordinary love.
Is it possible that you exist? YOU ARE, that is an inconceivable miracle. YOU ARE, and this amazes all that I am, transforms all values, turns the stones around you into gold, colours things that are dead and worthless, cancels the steps that go towards you and the fatigues, measures the times that are far from you with quite different measures [...]. 
'YOU EXIST, YOU ARE,' says love, and with its eyes it expresses its amazement, its impossibility of believing that what is absolutely desired, wanted, necessary, is real, is at the same time a being and an idea, its own creation and the offering of destiny. And we observe the strangeness that it takes faith to believe in what is, to accept the existence and presence of the inestimable object, as well as to give substance and power to what is merely fantasy and the production of hope or despair".
(Paul Valéry, Mélanges, 1939)

E1.6.A.1.1.a_REF6.1_PO "A TENDERNESS THAT BECAME DEMENTIA,
A weakness with I know not what vastness 
That you yourself, all of you, and even your last kiss 
cannot quite quench;
A thirst that does not extinguish the supreme chalice,
And even on the summit, where moans delight,
A silent wish, a mad need to have more than you 
Than you, than your life and mine, and our whys;
A thirst, a hunger, fatal, absolute,
Tantalus' living presence and intimate grape, 
Tending to weep at the fall of day 
If he lost this day without biting into some love...
This thirst, this hunger, this pain of an extreme soul. 
It's me, my dear hell, your work and my poem.
And it's you, your looks, your mouth with its voice 
All that I hold by you fingertips, 
All that I possess as well as escapes me,
You, my source, my fruit, my torment and my grape,
It is we of whom since dawn, alas, I speak
Before my enigma and your mysteries".
(Paul Valéry, poem to Mrs Jean Voilier, unpublished)


E1.6.A.1.1.a_REF6.2_PH "LOVE AND DUALITY.—What else is love but understanding and rejoicing that another lives, works, and feels in a different and opposite way to ourselves? That love may be able to bridge over the contrasts by joys, we must not remove or deny those contrasts. Even self-love presupposes an irreconcileable duality (or plurality) in one person.”
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human II, Aphorism 75, 1878)

E1.6.A.1.1.a_REF7_CUR WE MARVEL AT THE INFINITE RENEWAL OF TRUE LOVE. This capacity for wonder is that of the love of a person, but also of the love of the world - from one we go to the other - the love of a man or a woman nourishes and is nourished by the love of the world. In “infinite resonance”, the fruit of our encounter with the other and with the world offers an “infinite flavour”, according to François Cheng. Walter Sickert's painting of his wife (Lainey) in their garden seems to us to illustrate this point perfectly.

E1.6.A.1.1.a_REF8_PH “THE NEED FOR INTIMACY IN A HUMAN LIFE AND IN A HUMAN SOCIAL LIFE IS AS FOUNDATIONAL AS OUR DAILY HUNGER AND OUR NEVER ENDING THIRST, and needs to be met in just the same practical way, every day, just as necessarily and just as frequently: in touch, in conversation, in listening and in seeing, in the back and forth of ideas; intimate exchanges that say I am here and you are here and that by touching our bodies, our minds or our shared work in the world, we make a world together… Intimacy is our evolutionary inheritance, the internal force that has us returning to another and to the world from our insulated aloneness again and again, no matter our difficulties and no matter our wounds.”
Intimacy finds its ultimate expression in all the forms of surrender human beings find difficult to embrace. [...]

Intimacy always carries the sense of something hidden about to be felt and known in surprising ways; something brought out and made visible, that previously could not be seen or understood. In intimacy what is hidden will become a gift, discovered and rediscovered again and again in the eyes of both giver and receiver. […]

To become human is to become visible, while carrying what is hidden as a gift to others.”
(David Whyte, Consolations II, 2024)

E1.6.A.1.1.b. FUNDAMENTAL HAPPINESS DEPENDS MORE THAN ANYTHING ELSE UPON WHAT MAY BE CALLED A FRIENDLY INTEREST IN PERSONS AND THINGS.
A love that can be nourished by a love relationship, but that goes beyond it. This broader love goes against the doxa that to love is to choose, to eliminate and therefore to destroy the "rest".

E1.6.A.1.1.b_REF1_PH "FUNDAMENTAL HAPPINESS DEPENDS MORE THAN ANYTHING ELSE UPON WHAT MAY BE CALLED A FRIENDLY INTEREST IN PERSONS AND THINGS.
A friendly interest in persons is a form of affectionateness, but not the form which is grasping and possessive and seeking always an emphatic response. This latter form is very frequently a source of unhappiness. The kind that makes for happiness is the kind that likes to observe people and finds pleasure in their individual traits, that wishes to afford scope for the interests and pleasures of those with whom it is brought into contact without desiring to acquire power over them or to secure their enthusiastic admiration. The person whose attitude towards others is genuinely of this kind will be a source of happiness and a recipient of reciprocal kindness. His relations with others, whether slight or serious, will satisfy both his interests and his affections; he will not be soured by ingratitude, since he will seldom suffer it and will not notice when he does. The same idiosyncrasies which would get on another man’s nerves to the point of exasperation will be to him a source of gentle amusement. He will achieve without effort results which another man, after long struggles, will find to be unattainable. Being happy in himself, he will be a pleasant companion, and this in turn will increase his happiness. But all this must be genuine; it must not spring from an idea of self-sacrifice inspired by a sense of duty. A sense of duty is useful in work, but offensive in personal relations. People wish to be liked, not to be endured with patient resignation. To like many people spontaneously and without effort is perhaps the greatest of all sources of personal happiness. [...]"
(Bertrand Russell, The conquest of happiness, 1962)

E1.6.A.1.1.b_REF2_PH "TO PAINT IS TO LOVE AGAIN. It’s only when we look with eyes of love that we see as the painter sees. His is a love, moreover, which is free of possessiveness. What the painter sees he is duty-bound to share. Usually he makes us see and feel what ordinarily we ignore or are immune to. His manner of approaching the world tells us, in effect, that nothing is vile or hideous, nothing is stale, flat and unpalatable unless it be our own power of vision. To see is not merely to look. One must look-see. See into and around. [...]
The practice of any art demands more than mere savoir faire. One must not only be in love with what one does, one must also know how to make love. In love self is obliterated. Only the beloved counts. Whether the beloved be a bowl of fruit, a pastoral scene, or the interior of a bawdy house makes no difference. One must be in it and of it wholly. Before a subject can be transmuted aesthetically it must be devoured and absorbed. If it is a painting it must perspire with ecstasy."
(Henry Miller, To paint is to love again, 1963)

E1.6.A.1.1.b_REF3_TH SIGMUND FREUD EXPLORES THE PURSUIT OF HAPPINESS THROUGH LOVE, POINTING OUT THAT SEXUAL LOVE PROVIDES AN INTENSE SENSE OF PLEASURE AND SERVES AS A MODEL FOR OUR PURSUIT OF HAPPINESS. However, he notes the vulnerability to suffering that this quest engenders, especially when the loved object or one's own love is lost.
"I am, of course, speaking of the way of life which makes love the centre of everything, which looks for all satisfaction in loving and being loved. A psychical attitude of this sort comes naturally enough to all of us; one of the forms in which love manifests itself—sexual love—has given us our most intense experience of an overwhelming sensation of pleasure and has thus furnished us with a pattern for our search for happiness. What is more natural than that we should persist in looking for happiness along the path on which we first encountered it? The weak side of this technique of living is easy to see; otherwise no human being would have thought of abandoning this path to happiness for any other. It is that we are never so defenceless against suffering as when we love, never so helplessly unhappy as when we have lost our loved object or its love. But this does not dispose of the technique of living based on the value of love as a means to happiness. There is much more to be said about it."
(Sigmund Freud, Civilization and its Discontents, 1930)

E1.6.A.1.1.b_REF3.1_TH FREUD DISCUSSES THE ABILITY OF SOME PEOPLE TO FIND HAPPINESS BY LOVING UNIVERSALLY RATHER THAN SEEKING TO BE LOVED, BY REDIRECTING LOVE FROM ITS SEXUAL AIMS TO AN EQUANIMOUS TENDERNESS. He cites St Francis of Assisi as an extreme example of this approach. Freud raises two objections: non-selective love loses its value, and not all people deserve to be loved.
“A small minority are enabled by their constitution to find happiness, in spite of everything, along the path of love. But far-reaching mental changes in the function of love are necessary before this can happen. These people make themselves independent of their object's acquiescence by displacing what they mainly value from being loved on to loving; they protect themselves against the loss of the object by directing their love, not to single objects but to all men alike; and they avoid the uncertainties and disappointments of genital love by turning away from its sexual aims and transforming the instinct into an impulse with an inhibited aim. What they bring about in themselves in this way is a state of evenly suspended, steadfast, affectionate feeling, which has little external resemblance any more to the stormy agitations of genital love, from which it is nevertheless derived. Perhaps St. Francis of Assisi went furthest in thus exploiting love for the benefit of an inner feeling of happiness. Moreover, what we have recognized as one of the techniques for fulfilling the pleasure principle has often been brought into connection with religion; this connection may lie in the remote regions where the distinction between the ego and objects or between objects themselves is neglected. According to one ethical view, whose deeper motivation will become clear to us presently, this readiness for a universal love of mankind and the world represents the highest standpoint which man can reach. Even at this early stage of the discussion I should like to bring forward my two main objections to this view. A love that does not discriminate seems to me to forfeit a part of its own value, by doing an injustice to its object; and secondly, not all men are worthy of love.”
(Sigmund Freud, Civilization and its Discontents, 1930)

E1.6.A.1.1.b_REF3.2_TH FOR ALL FREUD'S GENIUS, isn't his inability to extend his subjectivity in an "oceanic" way, as his friend Romain Rolland wrote, at the root of his resistance to the possibility of universal love? It could also be argued that an "oceanic love" doesn't have to go to the extreme of loving all men - it's enough for intense happiness to extend one's love to a wider circle - it seems to us that when Freud uses the example of the sublime, he denies himself the possibility of thinking about extended love - a middle ground between possessive sexual love and unattainable universal love; a middle ground, but not inferior in the strength of the joy that such an approach can bring us.

E1.6.A.1.1.b_REF4_PH ""IMAGES OF THE NEUTRAL

1) DEPRECIATIVE IMAGES

With the exception of a few philosophers and Blanchot, i.e. everywhere in the doxa, Neutral has bad press: images of Neutral are depreciative. Every bad image is encapsulated in a bad adjective (again, the pernicious role of the adjective). Here's [one] of those bad adjectives:

Flaccid 
Fichte (Lesson VII): contemptuous description of the skeptic who doesn't want true knowledge: 'In this flabby, distended, multiple false being, a multitude of antitheses, contradictions, live peacefully side by side. In it, nothing is distinguished or separated, but everything is confused, everything is intertwined. These people hold nothing to be true and nothing to be false; they love nothing and hate nothing. They neither love nor hate, because for cognition, for love, for hate, for every affection, they need that energetic concentration of which they are incapable, because they must distinguish and separate in the bosom of the manifold, and choose the single object of their cognition and affection.' The very endoxal idea that to love is to choose, to eliminate and thus to destroy the 'rest' + assimilation of the multiplicity of desires to indecision and thus to softness, to the flaccid vitalist idea: only that which destroys what surrounds it lives, is alive. (To which one could reply that to embrace the Neutral would, on the contrary, represent an extreme concentration of energy, if only that which is necessary to endorse precisely the (false, but unavoidable) image of the flaccid!)".
(Roland Barthes, The Neutral, course at the Collège de France, 1977-1978)

(Valérie Rouzeau, 2019)

E1.6.A.1.1.b_REF6_PH I DO NOT THINK THAT LOVE IS “JUST AN EMOTION,” BUT THAT IT IS CONSTITUTIVE IN OUR WHOLE MENTAL STRUCTURE (AND, THEREFORE, IN THE DEVELOPMENT OF OUR BRAINS).
“I do not (at least consciously) have a steady sense of life’s meaning. I keep losing it, and having to re-achieve it, again and again. I can only re-achieve (or “remember”) it when I am “inspired” by things or events or people, when I get a sense of the immense intricacy and mystery, but also the deep ordering positivity, of Nature and History.
I do not believe in, never have believed in, any “transcendental” spirit above Nature; but there is a spirit in Nature, a cosmogenic spirit, which commands my respect and love; and it is this, perhaps most deeply, which serves to “explain” life, give it “meaning.” […]
I think “the meaning of life” is something we have to formulate for ourselves, we have to determine what has meaning for us… It clearly has to do with love — what and whom and how one can love. […]
I do not think that love is “just an emotion,” but that it is constitutive in our whole mental structure (and, therefore, in the development of our brains).”
(Oliver Sacks, Letter to Hugh S. Moorhead, 1990 - Published in Letters from Oliver Sacks)

E1.6.A.1.1.b_REF7_PO ”BY THE TULIPS
By the tulips people stop to take
Pictures. One wonders which are more
Beautiful: the people or the tulips?
Lush, almost flourescent, like cups,
Like vases, like wet crimson towels
Hanging loose about the naked style.
Or an Annamese girl in striped mini
Just below her drawers, on her forehead
A pale red moon. Or two Indian women
In brilliant prints and gold nose pellets,
Nipples pressing through silk. Or an old
Man with his mother, identical blue chips
Glinting through corrugated skin. Families
Like flower beds, varieties of love
And anguish, phenotype and genotype,
And Babel, magnificent garden!
Or the glory of laughter, that needs
No language, the glee of children racing
Away, the silence of tulips calling
Wildly, pouring out love in perfume.”
(Nicholas Gordon, Philosophical poems, 2002)

E1.6.A.1.1.b_REF8_PO “KISS
When Lynne saw the lizard floating
in her mother-in-law’s swimming pool,
she jumped in. And when it wasn’t
breathing, its body limp as a baby
drunk on milk, she laid it on her palm
and pressed one fingertip to its silky breast
with just about the force you need
to test the ripeness of a peach, only quicker,
a brisk little push with a bit of spring in it.
Then she knelt, dripping wet in her Doc Martens
and camo T-shirt with the neck ripped out,
and bent her face to the lizard’s face,
her big plush lips to the small stiff jaw
that she’d pried apart with her opposable thumb,
and she blew a tiny puff into the lizard’s lungs.
The sun glared against the turquoise water.
What did it matter if she saved one lizard?
One lizard more or less in the world?
But she bestowed the kiss of life,
again and again, until
the lizard’s wrinkled lids peeled back,
its muscles roused its own first breath
and she set it on the hot cement
where it rested a moment
before darting off.”
(Ellen Bass)

Listen to the poem read by Maria Popova:
https://youtu.be/pU95Lv6Fzmk

E1.6.A.1.1.b_REF9_CUR BRÍGIDA BALTAR (1959–2022): TO MAKE THE WORLD A SHELTER
“Arguably one the finest Brazilian artists of her generation, Baltar kept herself devoted to uncompromised poetics, embracing her life and body with her aesthetic ambition to grasp the world as an extension of her intimacy. Dealing with the notion of shelter, and the nostalgia of both home and origin, Baltar’s work was as eloquent as subtle, grounded in the idea of a ceaseless metamorphosis, and truthful to a sense of closeness the artist stood out against the backdrop of a futile culture of hyper-exposure.”
(Nara Roesler New York)

Watch the video on YouTube: https://youtu.be/wVoNYVmLgoI?si=wK7a0fFBLfBe34iM

E1.6.A.1.1.c. BARUCH SPINOZA REFERS TO THIS EXPANDED LOVE AS BEATITUDE, OR THE "INTELLECTUAL LOVE OF GOD" OR OF NATURE.
In the West, prior to the 20th century, Spinoza was one of the few who spoke about this type of love, often compared to an "awakening," a concept frequently discussed in Asian philosophies for centuries. Thankfully, in the 20th century, connections were made with the East, and schools of thought such as phenomenology and the Palo Alto school started to articulate concepts in line with Spinoza's ideas.

E1.6.A.1.1.c.i. THE SOUL'S SUPREME VIRTUE IS TO KNOW THINGS THROUGH THE THIRD KIND OF KNOWLEDGE, WHICH LEADS TO BLISS
Spinoza's Ethics posits that beatitude, or bliss, stems from an intuitive understanding of God's presence within us. Proposition 25 (Part V) indicates that the soul's highest virtue lies in knowing things through this third kind of knowledge, leading to beatitude. This bliss is not an emotion driven by external factors but the result of fulfilling our inherent nature. Unlike spiritual deification, which involves renouncing the self and the world, Spinoza's concept of beatitude is rooted in self-affirmation and the joy of being alive in the human world.

E1.6.A.1.1.c.i_REF1_PH UNLIKE SPIRITUAL DEIFICATION, WHICH IMPLIES A RENUNCIATION OF THE EGO, THE SPINOZIST BEATITUDE AFFIRMS AND CULTIVATES OUR EMBODIED INDIVIDUALITY, ROOTING US IN OUR EARTHLY EXISTENCE. This beatitude stems from an intuitive knowledge of the divine life within us, distinct from traditional mystical ecstasy. 
"In the Ethics, from the note of v. 23 (GF p. 325), 'We feel and we know by experience that we are eternal,' the whole sequence to the end (prop. 42) is a long, almost hymn-like development on the beatitude we derive from intuitive knowledge of God's life within us. Prop. 25: 'The highest effort (conatus) of the soul and its highest virtue (virtus) is to know things by the third kind of knowledge' (GF 325). We understand that it's no longer a question of knowledge, but of beatitude, of the closest proximity of God's life to the very heart of our life, like a focus of eternity that radiates throughout our being, at least as long as we keep ourselves alive, as long as we persevere in existing. And so Spinoza, the atheist, finds himself in the tradition of mystical experience, the third kind of knowledge being strangely similar to the unitive fruition of the soul, lovingly united with the God who creates it and brings it into being, with the difference that it is not the soul alone that enjoys this union (as in the case of the mystics): Ecstasy is rapture, the body is abandoned like an inert, unnecessarily heavy disguise), but indifferently - for the one and the other are two expressions of one and the same substance - of the spirit (Mens is the creator of concepts, producer of its own rationality, while Anima only makes itself available for the gift of grace, receptive rather than productive) as well as of the body, which is also raised to blissful life, in glory and great health. It is an earthly beatitude that does not elevate us to the third heaven, as Paul of Tarsus was happy to envision (2 Cor 12), but rather keeps our feet firmly on the ground, affirming and cultivating our incarnate individuality, our own will, the nature that is expressed in us in a singular and unique way. This is why the Spinozan beatitude must be distinguished from the spiritual divinization that takes place in holy souls who have been able to empty themselves, to make themselves capable of receiving divinity at the cost of their own destruction, according to the teaching of Eckhart, so alive in the northern countries, the beguinage countries, and this, even if Spinoza's words sometimes seem to recall this wisdom, because the beatitude of the fifth book of the Ethics is that of self-affirmation, not of renunciation, an exultation of experiencing oneself alive on the earth of men, not that of detachment, which renounces oneself as well as the world and surrenders to rapture in the hereafter. "
(Jacques Darriulat, Spinoza and the Fullness of this World, 6-"We feel and experience that we are eternal", 2017)

Read the full essay: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Spinoza/Introduction/Introd6.html

E1.6.A.1.1.c.i_REF1.1_PH FOR SPINOZA, BEATITUDE COMES FROM THE INTUITIVE AND IMMEDIATE UNDERSTANDING OF THINGS THROUGH THE THIRD KIND OF KNOWLEDGE, WHICH LEADS TO THE INTELLECTUAL LOVE OF GOD [OR NATURE]. This beatitude is not an outward passion, but an inward fulfillment of one's nature. Spinoza distinguishes this beatitude from joy, which depends on external causes, and emphasizes that God [or Nature] is an immanent, not a transcendent cause.
"It is the supreme effort of the mind and its sovereign virtue to understand things by the third kind of knowledge that produces 'the intellectual love of God' (Prop. 33 to 37; Prop. 33): 'The intellectual love of God that comes from the third kind of knowledge is eternal,' which leads to beatitude (prop. 38 at the end, i.e., prop. 42; prop. 38): 'The more the mind understands through the second and third kinds of knowledge, the less it suffers from feelings that are evil, and the less it apprehends death'. The enigmatic theme of immediate, intuitive knowledge that has been emerging since the earliest writings thus finds its crowning, speculative fulfillment in the last book of the Ethics: the whole of this meditation tends only toward this ultimate goal. By beatitude Spinoza - who borrows the word from the vocabulary of theologians, who use it to designate the perfect enjoyment of the blessed, who are received into paradise and immersed in the face vision of the divinity - does not mean a passion due to an external cause, but, on the contrary, the fulfillment (gaudium) of our own nature, apprehended from the source that gives us life. In contrast to the joy (laetitia) we derive from the encounter with an external cause, which increases our power to act, beatitude (beatitudo) has its source in ourselves, and it is from ourselves, insofar as we experience ourselves as existing, that we draw the power to elevate ourselves to beatitude. And if Spinoza can write that this supreme pleasure derives from our union with God [or Nature], then we must make it clear that this God [or Nature] is an immanent cause, not a transitive or transcendent one, internal and not external (I, 18: 'God is the immanent but non-transitive cause of all things')".
(Jacques Darriulat, Spinoza and the Fullness of this World, 6-"We feel and experience that we are eternal", 2017)

Read the full essay: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Spinoza/Introduction/Introd6.html

E1.6.A.1.1.c.i_REF1.2_PH FOR SPINOZA, LIFE IS WORTH LIVING NOT BECAUSE OF CHOICES BASED ON IMAGINARY FREEDOM, BUT BECAUSE OF THE EXPERIENCE OF PARTICIPATING IN DIVINE LIFE [OR NATURE], WHICH MAKES US FEEL AND EXPERIENCE OUR ETERNITY INDEPENDENT OF TIME.
"Life is not worth living because of the choices we might make, based on an imaginary freedom that we attribute to ourselves only because we ignore the true causes that determine us and the necessity that leads us as if by the hand. Life is worthwhile in that it offers us the experience - limited, it is true, in time and space - of participating in the life that is in God [or in Nature], of feeling ourselves to be eternal, by the very fact of the essence that gives us existence: 'We feel and experience that we are eternal [...] We feel that our spirit, insofar as it surrounds the essence of the body under the aspect of eternity - sub aeternitatis specie - is eternal, and that its existence cannot be defined by time, that is, explained by duration' (V, scolie de 23)."
(Jacques Darriulat, Spinoza and the Fullness of this World, 6-"We feel and experience that we are eternal", 2017)

Read the full essay: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Spinoza/Introduction/Introd6.html

E1.6.A.1.1.c.i_REF1.3_PH “WHAT IT IS — IS WHAT ELSE IT IS. NOT JUST THAT THIS AFTERNOON’S THICK, BOULDER-CLOUDS RESEMBLE THE MOUNTAINS I LOVED AS A CHILD, BUT THAT THE ONE SCENE COLLAPSES IN ON THE OTHER, TIME REWORKS AND FOLDS TOGETHER. AND I LIVE IN BOTH PLACES.
What it is — is what else it is. For this reason I am often startled by the simplest gestures of things: a leaf scratching along sideways moves as a crab does, so much so that the animal’s likeness comes powerfully in, and the shock of seeing a crab on the sidewalk trumps reason. And though I tell myself “it’s fall; leaves *dry*, scratch and blow, not *crabs*,” I’m jittery walking down the street — not frightened exactly, I can’t say afraid — but always the scene I’m in breaks open and floods. The stuff of an *elsewhere* comes in, as when, among the dried, speckled shells of crabs this summer, a snowball rolled oceanward before returning itself to a clump of sea-foam. The flap of an awning blows in wind — and it’s a low-flying bird’s wing. The dark underside of a mushroom’s gills, grown tiered and up-curved after rain, makes a tiny Sydney Opera House. Right there, hillside of the reservoir. Australia, just a few blocks from home. […]
These moments of recurrence/concurrence are not messages fluttering toward, bearing secrets, but stories in which we are part of the telling. We are, for a spell, of the path where shape forms, where flux assembles, briefly, a center. And there are so many centers.”

(Lia Purpura, On Looking)

“The sun
The moon
The stars

And
The rain
The wind
The rainbow
The echoes in the mountains

Why are
Only the oldest
Always the newest?”

(Michio Mado)

E1.6.A.1.1.c.ii. INTUITIVE KNOWLEDGE OF THE ESSENCE OF LIFE LEADS TO THE HIGHEST ENJOYMENT
Every individual possesses the gift of existence and the strength to persevere in life. This allows them to find contentment within themselves and enjoy their existence. Happiness is present in the here and now, and divine activity can be experienced through meditation and awareness. Understanding the essence of life intuitively leads to supreme enjoyment. Life is its own objective and conclusion, and happiness is virtue itself. Life is an invaluable gift, and there is no need to seek salvation to experience the greatest joy.

E1.6.A.1.1.c.ii_REF1_PH ACCORDING TO SPINOZA, LIFE HAS NO END OTHER THAN ITSELF AND THE INTUITIVE KNOWLEDGE OF THE INFINITE POWER THAT SUSTAINS IT. Beatitude lies in the awareness of this infinite existence, with no need for reward beyond. Virtue and beatitude are inseparable, and eternity is found in the present life, perceived by the very essence of existence.
"All men are born and die. But it is up to them, as long as they are granted the gift of Being and continue in its glory, to reconcile with themselves and enjoy the flame that burns within them, to quench their thirst at the Source of Life that radiates within them and grants them the infinite potency of Existence. And to enjoy this eternity, we don't have to prolong our existence indefinitely. On the contrary, it's in the here and now that this beatitude is given to us, provided that we become aware of this life-giving potency within us, and according to the depth that the practice of meditation will be able to give to the intuitive knowledge of this infinite life of which we are a singular expression. For infinity is an intensive magnitude, not an extensive one, and there is no limit to the intensity with which we can participate, through the exercise of attention, through the penetration of consciousness, in the divine activity that brings us into existence and continually perseveres in that impulse. Indeed, as we have seen, it is the hallmark of intuitive knowledge to be able, through practice, to expand the field of its investigation, the radius of its horizon of evidence. This knowledge, or awareness, is accompanied by the highest pleasure, for it makes us inwardly experience the act of divine causation, which does not affect us passionately, through the effect of an external cause, but fills us inwardly, through the intuition of our own essence. Only the creature enjoys the infinite potrncy of existence, since it receives it from the immanent God who gives it life, and not God Himself, who gives existence by the necessity of His infinite power, and therefore cannot experience this power as a gift that fills it, since it is its own nature: 'We conclude that a created thing can be said to enjoy existence, because indeed existence is not of its essence, but God cannot be said to enjoy existence, for God's existence is God himself; so also is his essence; from which it follows that created things enjoy duration, but God in no way enjoys it. '(Metaphysical Thoughts, II, 1; GF I, 359). This suggests that the status of the creature is perhaps more enviable than that of God, who eternally and out of his own necessity produces, without being able to enjoy or even be aware of it, the generation of bodies in matter and the continuation of ideas in minds. [...] Spinoza's God generates and produces; he neither loves nor knows (or rather, he loves only himself, insofar as he is the cause of himself and thus affects himself by giving himself existence). But it's very important for our beatitude that we know Him [God or Nature] - intuitively, not deductively, subjectively, not objectively, with an all-intellectual love that has its source in the depths of ourselves, and not in the imprint or image that clouds our reason with the shock of an alienating encounter. The 'intellectual love of God [or Nature]' from which we derive the beatitude of feeling and experiencing ourselves as eternal is not the passion of love of the imagination; it is an 'intellectual' love born of the intuitive knowledge of the mind of its essence as of the essence of the body of which it is the idea, knowledge of the conatus that sustains all existence in the effort it exerts to persevere in existing. [...] 
Thus, according to Spinoza, life has no end other than itself and the intuitive knowledge of the infinite potency that brings it into existence. It has neither meaning nor purpose, since it is its own meaning and purpose. So there is no need for any reward in the hereafter, for there is no gift or reward more wonderful than existence itself and the beatitude it inspires in the mind that knows how to be attentive to its continuous activity. It's enough to live and be aware of the infinity of this gift. And it is futile to hope for salvation as the price of this gift, which is already supreme and inestimable in itself: 'Beatitude is not the reward of virtue, but virtue itself' (Prop. 42, last proposition of the Ethics). Paradoxically, this extraordinary work teaches us that we live in paradise and enjoy eternity. In fact, we need only this world to find the eternity and perfection that man has only been able to imagine in another world. For, as it says on the very first page of Book I of the Ethics: 'By eternity I mean existence itself, insofar as it is conceived as necessarily following from the sole definition of an eternal thing' (I, def. 8), i.e., insofar as it is known by its very essence, the effect of the infinite potency of divine causation; and in another way, on the very first page of Book II: 'By reality and by perfection I mean the same thing.' This world is perfect. All is accomplished."
(Jacques Darriulat, Spinoza and the Fullness of this World, 6-"We feel and experience that we are eternal", 2017)

Read the full essay: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Spinoza/Introduction/Introd6.html

E1.6.A.1.1.c.ii_REF1.1_PH "PROPOSITION 36
The intellectual love of the mind towards God is that very love of God whereby God loves himself, not in so far as he is infinite, but in so far as he can be explained through the essence of the human mind regarded under the form of eternity ; in other words, the intellectual love of the mind towards God is part of the infinite love wherewith God loves himself.

Proof.
This love of the mind must be referred to the activities of the mind [...] ; it is itself, indeed, an activity whereby the mind regards itself accompanied by the idea of God as cause[...] ; that is [...], an activity whereby God, in so far as he can be explained through the human mind, regards himself accompanied by the idea of himself ; therefore (by the last Prop.), this love of the mind is part of the infinite love wherewith God loves himself. Q.E.D.

COROLLARY
Hence it follows that God, in so far as he loves himself, loves man, and, consequently, that the love of God towards men, and the intellectual love of the mind towards God are identical.

SCHOLIUM
From what has been said we clearly understand, wherein our salvation, or blessedness, or freedom, consists: namely, in the constant and eternal love towards God, or in God's love towards men. "
(Baruch Spinoza, Ethics part 5)

Read the Ethics online: http://app.ethica-spinoza.net/en/text

E1.6.A.1.1.c.ii_REF1.2_PH FOR SPINOZA, BEATITUDE IS IN REALITY A METAPHOR OF PERFECT LOVE “We know that, for Spinoza, beatitude is, as joy, an ‘intellectual love of God’, the latter being Nature itself as a unified totality. And man's love for 'God' is part of the love by which 'God' (i.e. Nature and mankind) loves himself (Spinoza, Eth., V, 36). It is a cosmic, reflexive joy. It is, in fact, a metaphor for perfect love.”
(Robert Misrahi, The Acts of Joy, 1987)

E1.6.A.1.1.d. LOVE THOROUGHLY SOLVED EVERYTHING
Two or three times in my life I discovered love. 
Each time it seemed to solve everything. 
Each time it solved a great many things 
    but not everything.
Yet left me as grateful as if it had indeed, and 
thoroughly, solved everything."
(Mary Oliver)

melancholy leaves me breathless.

    drowse of creation,

when it began to break.

E1.6.A.1.1.d_REF2_PO WHAT YOU REALLY WANT IS LOVE'S CONFUSING JOY

can give, you're an employee.

E1.6.A.1.2. IF EQUAL AFFECTION CANNOT BE, LET THE MORE LOVING ONE BE ME.
This is how W.H. Auden encapsulates the generosity of a lover of life. Similarly, Bertrand Russell suggests, "Love at its fullest is an indissoluble combination of the two elements, delight and well-wishing."

E1.6.A.1.2_REF1_PO "THE MORE LOVING ONE

Looking up at the stars, I know quite well
That, for all they care, I can go to hell,
But on earth indifference is the least
We have to dread from man or beast.

How should we like it were stars to burn
With a passion for us we could not return?
If equal affection cannot be,
Let the more loving one be me.

Admirer as I think I am
Of stars that do not give a damn,
I cannot, now I see them, say
I missed one terribly all day.

Were all stars to disappear or die,
I should learn to look at an empty sky
And feel its total dark sublime,
Though this might take me a little time."
(W.H. Auden)

E1.6.A.1.2_REF2_PH "THE GOOD LIFE IS ONE INSPIRED BY LOVE AND GUIDED BY KNOWLEDGE. Neither love without knowledge, nor knowledge without love can produce a good life. [...]
Love is a word which covers a variety of feelings; I have used it purposely, as I wish to include them all. Love as an emotion — which is what I am speaking about, for love “on principle” does not seem to me genuine — moves between two poles: on one side, pure delight in contemplation; on the other, pure benevolence. Where inanimate objects are concerned, delight alone enters in; we cannot feel benevolence towards a landscape or a sonata. This type of enjoyment is presumably the source of art. It is stronger, as a rule, in very young children than in adults, who are apt to view objects in a utilitarian spirit. It plays a large part in our feelings towards human beings, some of whom have charm and some the reverse, when considered simply as objects of aesthetic contemplation. [...]
Love at its fullest is an indissoluble combination of the two elements, delight and well-wishing. The pleasure of a parent in a beautiful and successful child combines both elements; so does sex-love at its best. But in sex-love benevolence will only exist where there is secure possession, since otherwise jealousy will destroy it, while perhaps actually increasing the delight in contemplation. Delight without well-wishing may be cruel; well-wishing without delight easily tends to become cold and a little superior. A person who wishes to be loved wishes to be the object of a love containing both elements."
(Bertrand Russell, What I believe, 1925)

E1.6.A.1.2_REF3_PO "THERE IS ONLY ONE QUESTION;
HOW TO LOVE THIS WORLD."
(Mary Oliver, Spring, House of light, 1990)

E1.6.A.1.2_REF4_PO "AFTER THE MOVIE

My friend Michael and I are walking home arguing about the movie.
He says that he believes a person can love someone
and still be able to murder that person.

I say, No, that’s not love. That’s attachment.
Michael says, No, that’s love. You can love someone, then come to a day

when you’re forced to think “it’s him or me”
think “me” and kill him.

I say, Then it’s not love anymore.
Michael says, It was love up to then though.

I say, Maybe we mean different things by the same word.
Michael says, Humans are complicated: love can exist even in the murderous
heart.

I say that what he might mean by love is desire.
Love is not a feeling, I say. And Michael says, Then what is it?

We’re walking along West 16th Street—a clear unclouded night—and I hear
my voice
repeating what I used to say to my husband: Love is action, I used to say to
him.

Simone Weil says that when you really love you are able to look at someone
you want to eat and not eat them.

Janis Joplin says, take another little piece of my heart now baby.

Meister Eckhart says that as long as we love any image we are doomed to live
in purgatory.

Michael and I stand on the corner of 6th Avenue saying goodnight.
I can’t drink enough of the tangerine spritzer I’ve just bought—

again and again I bring the cold can to my mouth and suck the stuff from
the hole the flip top made.

What are you doing tomorrow? Michael says.
But what I think he’s saying is “You are too strict. You are a nun.”

Then I think, Do I love Michael enough to allow him to think these things of
me even if he’s not thinking them?

Above Manhattan, the moon wanes, and the sky turns clearer and colder.
Although the days, after the solstice, have started to lengthen,

we both know the winter has only begun."
(Marie Howe)

E1.6.A.1.2_REF5_PH THAT GOTAMA REGARDS THE HIGHEST GOOD TO BE CARE IS CONSISTENT WITH HIS FAMOUS LAST WORDS: “THINGS FALL APART; TREAD THE PATH WITH CARE.”
“Gotama […] says that all skillful states “are rooted and converge in care, and care is considered the chief among them.” [”Just as the footprints of all living beings that walk fit into the
footprint of an elephant”]. Another way of phrasing the question would be, “What, in the
dharma, constitutes the highest good?” That Gotama regards the highest good to be care is consistent with his famous last words: “Things fall apart; tread the path with care.” […]

[This attempt at translating appamada as care fails] to communicate that appamada is a negative term like the English “impeccability,” which is also a negative (im/in = without + peccare = sin), although we tend not to notice the negative and consider impeccability a wholly positive attribute. The first a- of appamada is also a privative (“not”); pamada means something like “negligence” or “indolence”. […] It suggests that we spend a great deal of time stumbling about distracted, veering
from one thought to the next, forgetting what we had intended to do as soon as a more diverting possibility presents itself. 
When rendered as “care,” we can understand appamada to include both a vigilant attention (being care-full) and a heartfelt concern for the well-being of oneself and others (being caring)  […].
(Stephen Batchelor, After Buddhism, rethinking the dharma for a secular age, 2016)

E1.6.A.1.2_REF5.1_PH “AS THE PERSPECTIVE OF ONE PURSUING A PATH, CARE IS AN EXISTENTIAL SENSIBILITY RATHER THAN A DISCRETE MENTAL ACT. Buddhist analyses of care describe it as a configuration of different interwoven elements. Such care is grounded in an energetic state of contentment, kindness, and clarity. When this careful caring is activated, greed, hatred, and confusion are (for the time being) suspended. The twelfth-century Tibetan lama Thogmé Zangpo defined care as “a keen concern for engagement and letting go.” Care is that which cherishes and cultivates virtues while also relinquishing compulsive behavior. As an awareness of one’s values and a diligent commitment to realize them, it deprives Mara of a foothold, thereby guarding one against his attacks.
Such a path of vigilant care is not an exclusively Buddhist concern. Whatever sheds light on impermanence, suffering, contingency, and emptiness can contribute to a path that inclines toward nirvana, even if it originates in a secular tradition that is skeptical of religion, or in a religion that denies the validity of Buddhism. In recognizing the existence of “solitary buddhas” (paccekabuddha), who gain insight into the nature of contingency independently of a teacher or Buddhist community, Buddhism affirms that the attainment of nirvana can occur outside of a Buddhist context. Job and Jesus, Pascal and Montaigne, evolutionary biology and neuroscience, Roland Barthes and William Blake offer glimpses of self and world that illuminate the path opened up by Buddha.
Although a devout Buddhist, Shantideva aspires “always to be a student of everyone, respectfully accepting unsolicited words which are of help, openly rejoicing in whatever is well said.” To enclose oneself in the confines of a tradition and community where one feels at home and unthreatened cuts one off from the myriad sources of awakening that are everywhere present, were one only prepared to reach out and embrace them. “There is nothing whatsoever,” affirms Shantideva, “that is not to be learned by one who aspires to awaken.”
In such encounters between traditions are the seeds of a contemporary culture of awakening likely to germinate. This culture might already be fermenting in the dialogues and exchanges between declared Buddhists and “solitary buddhas” who, unbeknown to themselves, are gouging pathways to nirvana. It can embrace both secular and religious elements while in itself being neither secular nor religious.”
(Stephen Batchelor, Living with the Devil, 2004)

E1.6.A.1.3. LOVE REALLY IS A UNIQUE TRUST PLACED IN CHANCE
Love really is a unique trust placed in chance. It takes us into key areas of the experience of what is difference and, essentially, leads to the idea that you can experience the world from the perspective of difference and transformation. Security in a relationship lies neither in looking back to what it was in nostalgia, nor forward to what it might be in dread or anticipation, but living in the present relationship and accepting it as it is now

E1.6.A.1.3_REF1_PH "LOVE REALLY IS A UNIQUE TRUST PLACED IN CHANCE. It takes us into key areas of the experience of what is difference and, essentially, leads to the idea that you can experience the world from the perspective of difference. [...]
Love… is a quest for truth… truth in relation to something quite precise: what kind of world does one see when one experiences it from the point of view of two and not one? What is the world like when it is experienced, developed and lived from the point of view of difference and not identity? That is what I believe love to be. [...]
To make a declaration of love is to move on from the event-encounter to embark on a construction of truth. The chance nature of the encounter morphs into the assumption of a beginning. And often what starts there lasts so long, is so charged with novelty and experience of the world that in retrospect it doesn’t seem at all random and contingent, as it appeared initially, but almost a necessity. That is how chance is curbed: the absolute contingency of the encounter with someone I didn’t know finally takes on the appearance of destiny. The declaration of love marks the transition from chance to destiny, and that’s why it is so perilous and so burdened with a kind of horrifying stage fright. […]
The locking in of chance is an anticipation of eternity… The problem then resides in inscribing this eternity within time. Because, basically, that is what love is: a declaration of eternity to be fulfilled or unfurled as best it can be within time: eternity descending into time. […]
Happiness in love is the proof that time can accommodate eternity. And you can also find proof … in the pleasure given by works of art and the almost supernatural joy you experience when you at last grasp in depth the meaning of a scientific theory."
(Alain Badiou, In Praise of Love, 2009)

E1.6.A.1.3_REF2_PO "THERE IS A FAITH IN LOVING FIERCELY
THE ONE WHO IS RIGHTFULLY YOURS,
especially if you have
waited years and especially
if part of you never believed
you could deserve this
loved and beckoning hand
held out to you this way. [...]
so that when
we finally step out of the boat
toward them, we find
everything holds
us, and everything confirms
our courage"
(David Whyte)

E1.6.A.1.3_REF2.1_PO "THE TRUELOVE

There is a faith in loving fiercely
the one who is rightfully yours,
especially if you have
waited years and especially
if part of you never believed
you could deserve this
loved and beckoning hand
held out to you this way.

I am thinking of faith now
and the testaments of loneliness
and what we feel we are
worthy of in this world.

Years ago in the Hebrides,
I remember an old man
who walked every morning
on the grey stones
to the shore of baying seals,
who would press his hat
to his chest in the blustering
salt wind and say his prayer
to the turbulent Jesus
hidden in the water,

and I think of the story
of the storm and everyone
waking and seeing
the distant
yet familiar figure
far across the water
calling to them

and how we are all
preparing for that
abrupt waking,
and that calling,
and that moment
we have to say yes,
except it will
not come so grandly
so Biblically
but more subtly
and intimately in the face
of the one you know
you have to love

so that when
we finally step out of the boat
toward them, we find
everything holds
us, and everything confirms
our courage, and if you wanted
to drown you could,
but you don’t
because finally
after all this struggle
and all these years
you simply don’t want to
any more
you’ve simply had enough
of drowning
and you want to live and you
want to love and you will
walk across any territory
and any darkness
however fluid and however
dangerous to take the
one hand you know
belongs in yours."
(David Whyte)

Listen to the poem read by David Whyte:
https://soundcloud.app.goo.gl/KJnug

E1.6.A.1.3_REF2.2_CUR THE EXPECTATION OF AN ENCOUNTER
“This work by André Breton is an autobiographical narrative, focused on the "events of objective chance" which led him to his decisive encounter in 1934 with his future wife Jacqueline Lamba, whose "scandalous beauty" was immortalised by Dora Maar. The work illustrated with photographs by Dora Maar, Brassaï, Henri Cartier-Bresson, Rogi André, and Man Ray. The photograph of The Invisible Object, a sculpture by Alberto Giacometti, was taken in the artist's studio by Dora Maar in 1934. Between its empty hands, the figure seems to be holding "an invisible, yet present object," the expectation of an encounter”. For Breton, it represents "the emanation itself of the desire to love and be loved seeking its genuine human purpose and in its painful ignorance”
(Curatorship: Centre Pompidou, Paris)

E1.6.A.1.3_REF3_LIT GOOD RELATIONSHIP ARE AKIN TO A DANCE, WHERE PARTNERS MOVE TOGETHER WITH A LIGHT TOUCH, LIVING IN THE MOMENT AND ALLOWING FOR GROWTH AND CHANGE. True security in relationships comes from accepting their fluidity and present state, rather than clinging to past forms or fearing future changes.
"The pure relationship, how beautiful it is! How easily it is damaged, or weighed down with irrelevancies — not even irrelevancies, just life itself, the accumulations of life and of time. For the first part of every relationship is pure, whether it be with friend or lover, husband or child. It is pure, simple and unencumbered. It is like the artist’s vision before he has to discipline it into form, or like the flower of love before it has ripened to the firm but heavy fruit of responsibility. Every relationship seems simple at its start. The simplicity of first love, or friendliness, the mutuality of first sympathy seems, at its initial appearance — even if merely in exciting conversation across a dinner table — to be a self-enclosed world. Two people listening to each other, two shells meeting each other, making one world between them… It is free of ties or claims, unburdened by responsibilities, by worry about the future or debts to the past. And then how swiftly, how inevitably the perfect unity is invaded; the relationship changes; it becomes complicated, encumbered by its contact with the world. [...]

We mistakenly feel that failure to maintain its exact original pattern is tragedy. It is true, of course, the original relationship is very beautiful. Its self-enclosed perfection wears the freshness of a spring morning. Forgetting about the summer to come, one often feels one would like to prolong the spring of early love, when two people stand as individuals, without past or future, facing each other. One resents any change, even though one knows that transformation is natural and part of the process of life and its evolution. Like its parallel in physical passion, the early ecstatic stage of a relationship cannot continue always at the same pitch of intensity. It moves to another phase of growth which one should not dread, but welcome as one welcomes summer after spring. [...]

One learns to accept the fact that no permanent return is possible to an old form of relationship; and, more deeply still, that there is no holding of a relationship to a single form. This is not tragedy but part of the ever-recurrent miracle of life and growth. All living relationships are in process of change, of expansion, and must perpetually be building themselves new forms. But there is no single fixed form to express such a changing relationship. [...]

One comes in the end to realize that there is no permanent pure-relationship and there should not be. It is not even something to be desired. The pure relationship is limited, in space and in time. In its essence it implies exclusion. It excludes the rest of life, other relationships, other sides of personality, other responsibilities, other possibilities in the future. It excludes growth. [...]

A good relationship has a pattern like a dance and is built on some of the same rules. The partners do not need to hold on tightly, because they move confidently in the same pattern… To touch heavily would be to arrest the pattern and freeze the movement, to check the endlessly changing beauty of its unfolding. There is no place here for the possessive clutch, the clinging arm, the heavy hand; only the barest touch in passing. Now arm in arm, now face to face, now back to back… Because they know they are partners moving to the same rhythm, creating a pattern together, and being invisibly nourished by it.

The joy of such a pattern is not only the joy of creation or the joy of participation, it is also the joy of living in the moment. Lightness of touch and living in the moment are intertwined. One cannot dance well unless one is completely in time with the music, not leaning back to the last step or pressing forward to the next one, but poised directly on the present step as it comes. [...]

When you love someone you do not love them all the time, in exactly the same way, from moment to moment. It is an impossibility. It is even a lie to pretend to. And yet this is exactly what most of us demand. We have so little faith in the ebb and flow of life, of love, of relationships. We leap at the flow of the tide and resist in terror its ebb. We are afraid it will never return. We insist on permanency, on duration, on continuity; when the only continuity possible, in life as in love, is in growth, in fluidity — in freedom, in the sense that the dancers are free, barely touching as they pass, but partners in the same pattern. The only real security is not in owning or possessing, not in demanding or expecting, not in hoping, even. Security in a relationship lies neither in looking back to what it was in nostalgia, nor forward to what it might be in dread or anticipation, but living in the present relationship and accepting it as it is now."
(Anne Morrow Lindbergh, Gift from the Sea, 1955)

E1.6.A.1.3_REF4_PO "
« WHY DO I LOVE » YOU, SIR ? 
Because -
The Wind does not require the Grass 
To answer - Wherefore when He pass 
She cannot keep Her place. 

Because He knows - and 
Do not You -
And We know not -
Enough for Us 
The Wisdom it be so -

The Lightning - never asked an Eye 
Wherefore it shut - when He was by -
Because He knows it cannot speak -
And reasons not contained - Of Talk -
There be - preferred by Daintier Folk -
The Sunrise - Sir - compelleth Me -
Because He’s Sunrise - and I see -
Therefore - Then -
I love Thee -"
(Emily Dickinson, 1862)

E1.6.A.1.3_REF5_F “In Christopher Nolan's film Interstellar, there appears to be a contradiction in the plot: Cooper transmits the message "STAY" via gravity from inside the black hole—attempting to tell his daughter Murph to convince his past self not to leave her. Yet he also transmits the coordinates of NASA's secret base, which ultimately leads him to embark on the interstellar mission. Once inside the black hole, Cooper understands that his journey's purpose was to collect quantum data and transmit it to Murph to save humanity trapped on Earth. If he knew he had to go, why did he also send the contradictory message "STAY"?

That's an excellent question that gets to the absolute heart of *Interstellar*'s emotional and philosophical core. No, it is not a contradiction or a plot hole, but rather a deliberate and crucial part of the story that highlights the conflict between Cooper the father and Cooper the savior of humanity.

Here’s a breakdown of why he sends both messages:

---

### ## The Two Coopers: Emotional vs. Rational

Inside the Tesseract, Cooper is a being who exists across time, and we see him act in two distinct phases: first as a father driven by emotion, and second as a man driven by rational purpose.

1. **The "STAY" Message (The Father's Love):** This is Cooper's immediate, raw, and intensely human reaction. He is forced to re-watch the single most painful moment of his life: abandoning his beloved daughter. Overwhelmed by love, regret, and the pain in her eyes, his first instinct is to try and stop it. He's not yet thinking about the mission or saving humanity; he is simply a father desperately wanting to undo his greatest mistake. This is his **heart** speaking.
2. **The NASA Coordinates (The Savior's Duty):** After this emotional outburst, and with the help of TARS, Cooper begins to understand the mechanics of the Tesseract and his true purpose there. The "Bulk Beings" (future humans) didn't bring him there to change the past, but to *fulfill* it. He realizes that for Murph—and humanity—to survive, he *must* leave. Accepting his destiny, he rationally and methodically sends the NASA coordinates, ensuring the loop of events happens as it must. This is his **head** speaking.

He doesn't send both messages at the same time. He sends "STAY" in a moment of pure anguish, and then, upon understanding the bigger picture, he sends the coordinates to complete his mission.

---

### ## It's a Bootstrap Paradox, Not a Contradiction

The film operates on a **Bootstrap Paradox**, also known as a causal loop. This means an event has no clear origin because it is its own cause.

- Future Cooper (the "ghost") is the reason past Cooper leaves on the mission.
- Past Cooper leaving is the reason he can become the "ghost" in the future.

In this model, **you cannot change the past; you can only be the cause of it.** The "STAY" message was *always* part of the history Murph experienced. Cooper doesn't have a choice to *not* send it; he is simply fulfilling his role as the "ghost" she remembered from her childhood. He has to play out the entire sequence of events exactly as they happened, which includes both sending the "STAY" message and the coordinates.

---

### ## The Power of Love as a Physical Force

Ultimately, the film's central theme, as stated by Dr. Brand, is that **love is the one force that can transcend time and space.** The Tesseract itself is the ultimate proof of this. The Bulk Beings constructed this complex, five-dimensional space centered on one specific point: Murph's childhood bedroom. They did this because they knew the only thing powerful enough to guide Cooper to the right moments in time was his profound love and connection to his daughter.

His "STAY" message is the purest demonstration of that love. It is the very force that the entire solution to saving humanity is built upon.”
(Gemini, September 24, 2025)

Watch the scene: https://youtu.be/GtTkcM9BfXM?si=xikpGR3_HCMzor2t

E1.6.A.1.4. WHO WE ARE AND WHO WE BECOME DEPENDS, IN PART, ON WHOM WE LOVE

E1.6.A.1.4_REF1_PH EMOTIONS REVIVE PAST MEMORIES, CREATING MULTI LAYERED EXPERIENCES INFLUENCED BY PREVIOUS FEELINGS. HUMAN MINDS, SHAPED BY NEURAL MEMORIES, TEND TO REPEAT FAMILIAR THOUGHTS AND PERCEPTIONS. Limbic resonance connects individuals, allowing emotional states to influence each other. Relationships involve mutual emotional influence, where each partner's mind and heart can reshape the other's emotional pathways, demonstrating the concept of limbic revision.
"When an emotional chord is struck, it stirs to life past memories of the same feeling. […]
A particular emotion revives all memories of its prior instantiations. Every feeling (after the first) is a multilayered experience, only partly reflecting the present, sensory world. [...]
Because human beings remember with neurons, we are disposed to see more of what we have already seen, hear anew what we have heard most often, think just what we have always thought. Our minds are burdened by an informational inertia whose headlong course is not easy to slow… No individual can think his way around his own Attractors (*), since they are embedded in the structure of thought… Because limbic resonance and regulation join human minds together in a continuous exchange of influential signals, every brain is part of a local network that shares information — including Attractors. […]
Through the limbic transmission of an Attractor’s influence, one person can lure others into his emotional virtuality. All of us, when we engage in relatedness, fall under the gravitational influence of another’s emotional world, at the same time that we are bending his emotional mind with ours. Each relationship is a binary star, a burning flux of exchanged force fields, the deep and ancient influences emanating and felt, felt and emanating. [...]
In a relationship, one mind revises another; one heart changes its partner. This astounding legacy of our combined status as mammals and neural beings is limbic revision: the power to remodel the emotional parts of the people we love, as our Attractors activate certain limbic pathways, and the brain’s inexorable memory mechanism reinforces them. Who we are and who we become depends, in part, on whom we love."
(*) An attractor network is a network of neurons often recurrently connected, whose time dynamics settle to a stable pattern.
(Thomas Lewis, Fari Amini, and Richard Lannon, A General Theory of Love, 2001)

E1.6.A.1.4_REF2_LIT THERE IS A COMPLEXITY AND INHERENT DISSATISFACTION IN HUMAN RELATIONSHIPS, WHERE INDIVIDUALS ARE CONSTANTLY TORN BETWEEN SEEKING CONNECTION AND DESIRING ESCAPE. These hidden emotional currents, more than external events, shape our happiness and unhappiness.
"One realizes that even in harmonious families there is this double life: the group life, which is the one we can observe in our neighbor’s household, and, underneath, another — secret and passionate and intense — which is the real life that stamps the faces and gives character to the voices of our friends. Always in his mind each member of these social units is escaping, running away, trying to break the net which circumstances and his own affections have woven about him. One realizes that human relationships are the tragic necessity of human life; that they can never be wholly satisfactory, that every ego is half the time greedily seeking them, and half the time pulling away from them. [...]
In those simple relationships of loving husband and wife, affectionate sisters, children and grandmother, there are innumerable shades of sweetness and anguish which make up the pattern of our lives day by day, though they are not down in the list of subjects from which the conventional novelist works…
These secret accords and antipathies which lie hidden under our everyday behavior … more than any outward events make our lives happy or unhappy."
(Willa Cather, Not Under Forty, 1936)

E1.6.A.1.4_REF2.1_LIT "“TO LOVE WITHOUT KNOWING HOW TO LOVE WOUNDS THE PERSON WE LOVE,” the great Zen teacher Thich Nhat Hahn wrote in his short, potent meditation on how to love. Developmentally, we humans learn — or mislearn — how to love through our formative attachment patterns, modeled by and cultivated within the family — patterns that imprint our emotional identity and shape the defaults of how we connect, be they wounding or harmonizing. Family dynamics thus become inseparable from our sense of identity, and although we might eventually rewire our attachment patterns through new relationships and ample self-work, we can never fully unmoor ourselves from those formative affections, for they are woven into the mysterious thread that makes us and our childhood selves one person."
(Maria Popova, The Marginalian)

E1.6.A.1.4_REF3_CUR "SLEEPING FIGURE belongs to the Personages, a group of more than eighty totemic wood sculptures Bourgeois made between 1945 and 1950. The artist characterized these works as surrogates for family and friends, many of whom she left behind in France when she moved to New York in 1938. In this case, she reduced the human figure to three long oval shapes, flanked by thin poles. She once said of her sculptures, "The look of my figures is abstract, and to the spectator they may not appear to be figures at all. They are the expression, in abstract terms, of emotions and states of awareness.""
(Curatorship, MomA, NYC)

E1.6.A.1.4_REF4_CUR THE SISTERS' EXPRESSIONS SHOW US HOW SAFE THEY FEEL TOGETHER "The painting is unfinished and the outlines of a cat are just visible in the younger girl's lap; her sister Mary is tweaking its tail. Mary is shown here aged about nine or ten; Margaret is eight or nine.
This tender portrait of two young girls by their father quietly celebrates their enduring sibling bond. A young recent visitor to the National Gallery said that the sisters' expressions show us how safe they feel together. Physically and emotionally close, their gentleness with each other invites us to reflect on relationships in which we feel most supported, comfortable and loved"
(Curatorship: National Gallery, London)

E1.6.A.1.5. AN INNATE PREDISPOSITION TO A CERTAIN EMOTIONAL "TEMPERATURE"
Why do numerous couples separate and miss out on the happiness they initially aspired to? It appears that an inherent predisposition to a certain emotional "temperature" can make some couples' relationships more challenging.

E1.6.A.1.5_REF1_SCI “AN HONORABLE HUMAN RELATIONSHIP — that is, one in which two people have the right to use the word ‘love’ — is a process, delicate, violent, often terrifying to both persons involved, a process of refining the truths they can tell each other,” Adrienne Rich wrote in her stunning meditation on relationships. A happy human relationship, it turns out, is contingent not upon the nature and delivery process of these truths, particularly the difficult truths, but upon the nature of the hearer — upon our emotional orientation and sensitivity, which appears to be encoded in our DNA via a particular gene that regulates serotonin in the brain. So indicates the fascinating research of U.C. Berkeley psychophysiologist and behavioral neuroscientist Robert Levenson.
Known as 5-HTTLPR (serotonin-transporter-linked polymorphic region) and located on chromosome 17 of your DNA, this gene comes in two varieties — one with a short allele and the other with a long allele. Decades of research have revealed a strong positive correlation between the short-allele type and a high precedence of depression, anxiety, and attention disorders, suggesting that people with the short allele respond more negatively to emotional friction within a relationship and seeding the assumption that having this gene is plainly problematic for one’s psychoemotional health. But Levenson’s lab uncovered a much more nuanced and surprisingly optimistic reality — rather than predisposing to more negative emotional responses, the short allele appears to predispose simply to more emotional responses, serving as a kind of psychoemotional magnifying glass that renders all emotions, the lows as well as the highs, more deeply and intensely felt."
(Maria Popova, Brain Pickings on Robert Levenson's research at U.C. Berkley)

Read more on Brain Pickings:
https://www.brainpickings.org/?s=robert+levenson%27s

Watch Robert Levenson explain on Youtube:
https://youtu.be/xn38OsTeXlk

E1.6.A.1.5_REF2_LIT BUT BEFORE
“She left the house slamming the door. But before that, he had told her to fuck off. But before that, she'd asked him to at least clean up the mess he'd made. But before that, he had spilled wine on the carpet. But before, she had doubted that he would spill all the wine on the carpet. But before, he had said he would spill all the wine on the carpet. But before, she had said that it wasn't her fault that he didn't have a job. But before that, he had said that she didn't have to tell him that he couldn't even afford a rug. But before that, she had said that her mother deserved respect, after all, she was the one who had furnished the apartment, from the fan to the carpet. But before that, he had said that her mother was a cow. But before that, her mother had left the apartment slamming the door. But before that, he had asked her mother to leave, preferably without slamming the door. But before that, her mother had said that he was fatter. But before that, he had said that her mother was older. But before that, her mother had asked if he'd gotten the job. But before that, he had said that her mother arriving by surprise was just what was needed. But before that, her mother had arrived by surprise. But before that, they had kissed and apologized and promised not to fight. But before that, he had asked why nothing he does is ever right. But before that, she had complained that he didn't even know how to open a wine. But before that, he had tried to open a wine. But before that, she had suggested that he open the wine. But before, they had kissed. But before, they had left their children at his sister's house. But before, they had said it would be a beautiful evening. E1.6.A.1.5_REF2.1_LIT BUT AFTER
“They woke up and realized that that night they would be celebrating seven years together. They agreed to celebrate both: the anniversary and the new job. He said it was just an interview and that nothing was certain yet. She said she trusted him blindly. But he was turned down at the interview. He cried. She said she was very proud of the husband he was. They went to the supermarket and bought the best wine. They said it would be a beautiful evening. They left their children at his sister's house. They kissed. She suggested he open the wine. He tried and she complained that he doesn't even know how to open a wine. He asked why nothing he does is ever good. They kissed and apologized and promised not to fight. Her mother arrived by surprise. He said that was all he needed. Her mother asked if he had passed the job interview. He said she was an old woman. She said he'd put on weight. He asked his mother-in-law to leave, preferably without slamming the door. He said her mother was a cow. She said that her mother deserved respect, after all, she was the one who furnished the house: from the fan to the carpet. He said she didn't have to tell him that he couldn't afford a carpet. She said it wasn't her fault that he didn't have a job. He said he would spill all the wine on the carpet. She doubted it. He did. She asked him to clean up his mess. He told her to fuck off.
