E1.6.B.2.1.d. THE MAN WHO EASILY ACQUIRES THINGS FOR WHICH HE FEELS ONLY A VERY MODERATE DESIRE, CONCLUDES THAT THE FULFILLMENT OF DESIRE DOES NOT BRING HAPPINESS

E1.6.B.2.1.d_REF1_PH "IT IS COMMON IN OUR DAY, as it has been in many other periods of the world’s history, to suppose that those among us who are wise have seen
through all the enthusiasms of earlier times and have become aware that there is nothing left to live for. The men who hold this view are genuinely unhappy, but they are proud of their unhappiness, which they attribute to the nature of the universe and consider to be the only rational attitude for an enlightened man. [...]"
(Bertrand Russell, The Conquest of Happiness, 1962)

E1.6.B.2.1.d_REF1.1_PH THE EASY SATISFACTION OF NATURAL NEEDS AND THE LACK OF EFFORT IN LIFE DUE TO GREAT WEALTH DEPRIVE MAN OF AN ESSENTIAL ELEMENT OF HAPPINESS. The renunciation of certain desired things is essential to happiness.
"The feeling is one born of a too easy satisfaction of natural needs. The human animal, like others, is adapted to a certain amount of struggle for life, and when by means of great wealth homo sapiens can gratify all his whims without effort, the mere absence of effort from his life removes an essential ingredient of happiness. The man who acquires easily things for which he feels only a very moderate desire concludes that the attainment of desire does not bring happiness. If he is of a philosophic disposition, he concludes that human life is essentially wretched, since the man who has all he wants is still unhappy."
(Bertrand Russell, The Conquest of Happiness, 1962)

E1.6.B.2.1.d_REF2_PH "SIGNS OF FREEDOM AND DEPENDENCE. - Satisfying one's most urgent needs as much as possible, even if imperfectly, is the way to achieve freedom of mind and person. Accepting the satisfaction of one's many and even superfluous needs, as perfectly as possible, ultimately leads to a state of dependence. The sophist Hippias, who had acquired and created everything he wore, both inside and out, is thus the representative of the current that leads to the highest freedom of mind and person. It does not matter that everything is equally well made, equally perfect: pride takes over where defects are found. [...]"
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human III, Aphorism 318, 1878)

E1.6.B.2.1.e. IS A PROPER APPRECIATION OF ONE'S OWN ABILITIES NECESSARY FOR HAPPINESS?

E1.6.B.2.1.e._REF1_PH "THE PLEASURE OF CARRYING OUT A TASK REQUIRES SUCH DIFFICULTIES WHICH MAKES SUCCESS DOUBTFUL AT FIRST GLANCE, although it is ultimately usually attained. This is perhaps the main reason why a fair appreciation of one's own merits is necessary for happiness. The man who underestimates himself is always surprised by success, while the one who has excessive self-confidence is just as often surprised by failure. The first surprise is pleasant, the second is not. That is why, in order to be successful, wisdom demands that one be neither overly conceited nor too modest."
(Bertrand Russell, The conquest of happiness, 1962)

E1.6.B.2.1.e._REF2_PH ALAIN BADIOU MAINTAINS THAT IN ORDER TO PRESERVE THE HUMANITY IN YOU, IT IS CRUCIAL TO WANT TO CHANGE THE WORLD DESPITE THE CHALLENGES. Let us choose the risk of disaster, which includes the possibility of true happiness, over the "non-being" of an existence of survival and limited social satisfaction.
"The condition is that you never stop wanting what you want. So there comes a time when you have to want to change the world, to save the image of humanity that is in you, instead of giving in to the injunction of the impossible. [...]
Dissaster is better than "dis-being"... It is better to take the risk of disaster, and thus of true happiness, than to forbid it from the outset. I call "dis-being" that conservative disposition of the human subject that brings him back to his animal survival, to his sole satisfaction and to his social place. "Dis-being is what prevents a subject from experiencing what it is truly capable of.
(Alain Badiou)

E1.6.B.2.1.e._REF2.1_PH WLADEMIR DIAS PINO TELLS US THAT "FREEDOM IS ALWAYS EXPERIMENTAL.
Echoing this, for Alain Badiou, happiness is when we discover that we are capable of something we did not know we were capable of: it is enjoying the powerful and creative existence of something that seemed impossible.

E1.6.C.1.6_REF2.2_CUR.wE1.6.B.2.1.e. HUMANS HAVE A COMPLEX RELATIONSHIP WITH BOUNDARIES, SIMULTANEOUSLY SEEKING AND AVOIDING THEM. WE'RE DRAWN TO EDGES LIKE COASTLINES AND CLIFFS, COMPELLED BY THEIR POTENTIAL FOR BOTH SAFETY AND RISK. BOUNDARIES DEFINE OUR COMFORT ZONES, YET ALSO CHALLENGE US TO GROW BEYOND THEM. Edges represent life's core dilemma: balancing safety with growth. Limits test our courage and expand our abilities, with each conquered boundary revealing new horizons. In essence, edges are where we discover ourselves.

E1.6.B.2.1.f. WHO STILL AGREES WITH JOHNSON?
We don't agree with his position

E1.6.B.2.1.f_REF1_LIT JOHNSON COULD NOT RECONCILE WORK AND LEISURE AS COMPLEMENTARY PARTS OF THE SAME LIVING PROCESS
"Talking of trade, he observed, ‘ It is a mistaken notion that a vast deal of money is brought into a nation by trade. It is not so. Commodities come from commodities ; but trade produces no capital accession of wealth. However, though there should be little profit in money, there is a considerable profit in pleasure, as it gives to one nation the productions of another ; as we have wines and fruits, and many other foreign articles, brought to us.’ BOSWELL. ‘ Yes, Sir, and there is a profit in pleasure, by its furnishing occupation to such numbers of mankind.’ JOHNSON. ‘ Why, Sir, you cannot call that pleasure to which all are averse, and which none begin but with the hope of leaving off ; a thing which men dislike before they have tried it, and when they have tried it.’ BOSWELL. ‘ But, Sir, the mind must be employed, and we grow weary when idle.’ JOHNSON. ‘ That is, Sir, because others being busy, we want company ; but if we were all idle, there would be no growing weary ; we should all entertain one another. There is, indeed, this in trade :— it gives men an opportunity of improving their situation. If there were no trade, many who are poor would always remain poor. But no man loves labour for itself.’ BOSWELL. ‘ Yes, Sir, I know a person who does. He is a very laborious Judge, and he loves the labour.’ JOHNSON. ‘ Sir, that is because he loves respect and distinction. Could he have them without labour, he would like it less.’ BOSWELL. ‘ He tells me he likes it for itself.’ JOHNSON —’ Why, Sir, he fancies so, because he is not accustomed to abstract.’ "
(Boswell’s Life of Johnson, revised third edition 1799, first published in 1791)

E1.6.B.2.2. CELEBRATING LIFE BEYOND RESULTS
For those who no longer focus on results, only action matters. This mindset makes us more methodical, rigorous, and demanding than any result-oriented goal could. Unconstrained by the need for results, we act to celebrate and elevate life. Paradoxically, this freedom requires us to be more efficient than if we were solely focused on outcomes. We neither rejoice in success nor grieve in failure. The outcomes of our actions are intended for others, while their style and selflessness reflect our freedom.

E1.6.B.2.2_REF1_PH THE INDIFFERENCE TO THE RESULT FREES THE ACTION FROM THE FEAR OF FAILURE AND THE SEARCH FOR SUCCESS, ALLOWING A CELEBRATION OF LIFE AND A TRUE FREEDOM OF ACTION. Our action becomes freer and more disinterested, which paradoxically leads to greater efficiency and rigor.
"Spinoza records a very simple observation, so obvious that it is hardly noticed, and yet it is enough to denounce the most ordinary alienation of the contemporary world and its generalized hallucination. "The image that Peter has of Paul," he observes, "concerns Peter and not Paul. My image is not me: it is him. A second consequence is that it is enough for us to have become indifferent to our image for our actions to cease immediately to be mortgaged by concern for their outcome. As long as we believed that our character or reputation was involved in each of our undertakings, our image depended on their success or failure. To act was to risk our image: to expose ourselves, to take a chance, to give ourselves up. Although we led our action, we suffered it even more, because, the effect reacting to the cause, we became the result of our result. But from the moment we are no longer dominated or overpowered by our image, our action is freed from its result. At the same time, we are free to do all that duty commands us to do, and we are at the service of the world.

Far from exempting us from action, such indifference to the result encourages us to act, for we have nothing to fear from it. Far from making us inattentive, negligent, or casual, on the contrary, it compels us to a zeal, a fervor, a high standard, a tenacity, a scrupulousness that we would never have had if we had acted only for a result. Just as those who work for money measure their efforts by what they are paid, those whose actions are motivated by results are more concerned with the end than the means, with what they achieve than what they do. It is not the action that matters to them, but what it should bring them: not the dynamics of mediation, but the immediacy that follows. As Plato might have said, they are industrious only out of laziness, and devoted only out of self-interest. They also guard against the vice of giving more than they expect to receive, or ever doing more than the result requires. This, by the way, is how almost all slaves behave.

Conversely, for those for whom the result no longer matters, only the action matters. Our freedom therefore makes us more methodical, rigorous, and demanding through willpower than any desire for results ever could. Without worrying about the result, we then act to celebrate and glorify life. Paradoxically, our freedom also forces us to be more efficient than we could ever be if we were obsessed with results. Quite simply, we will be neither more joyful in the face of success nor more saddened by failure. For the result of our actions is only for others to see, while the style and selflessness of our actions are a commitment of our freedom.
(Nicolas Grimaldi, Ambiguities of freedom, PUF, 1999)

E1.6.B.2.2_REF2_PH "GOAL [vs. END]
What we strive for, what we pursue, what we want to achieve or obtain. 
The Stoics distinguished between the end (telos) and the goal (skopos). The goal is external to the action: it is, for example, the target at which the archer aims. The end is internal: it is not a question of hitting the target (it does not depend on us: a sudden gust of wind can cause the arrow to deviate), but of aiming at it correctly. The goal is always to come; the end is always present. The goal is the object of hope; the end, of will. 
The wise act without purpose (what the Orientals call "detachment from the fruit of the act"), but not without end. And they achieve the goal all the better because they do not care about achieving it. 
Action is enough for them.
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.B.2.2_REF3_PH A HEALTHY OBJECTIVE WOULD NOT BE TO ACHIEVE A SPECIFIC GOAL, BUT TO FOCUS ATTENTION ON BUILDING A PIECE OF WORK.
"I will be more vigilant in this experiment that I am about to undertake, because I am counting on the capacities that the experiences that I have already lived through and accumulated have secretly developed in me. In other words, I will know better what difficulties I will encounter and, in turn, what resources I can mobilize in a more appropriate way because I will be more familiar with the situation I am about to face. Instead of accepting the stage I have reached, I pull myself together and raise my standards in view of what I have done. This verb, it is true, is unpretentious. But it is without remorse or resignation, and it is even imbued with hope, not an easy hope, but an accredited hope, supported by what I have already done: because I am no longer in the projected intention, but I have already gained a foothold in this labor that is experience. [...]"
(François Jullien, A Second Life, 2017)

E1.6.B.2.2_REF4_PO "LOVE QUIETS FEAR.
And a sweet and powerful
Positive obsession
Blunts pain,
Diverts rage,
And engages each of us
In the greatest,
The most intense
Of our chosen struggles. [...]

Prodigy is, at its essence, adaptability and persistent, positive obsession. Without persistence, what remains is an enthusiasm of the moment. Without adaptability, what remains may be channeled into destructive fanaticism. Without positive obsession, there is nothing at all."
(Octavia Butler, Parable of the Talents, 1998)

E1.6.B.2.2_REF5_PH SISYPHUS SMILES AS HE DREAMS IN BLUE THE SKY: "After having laboriously climbed to the top of the mountain, the rock, by the grace of its equilibrium, stands still for a few moments and then rolls back down the slope. Sisyphus refrains from following it. Finally happy and breathing the fresh air of the mountain tops, he lies down on the soft grass, his back to the ground and his eyes to the sky. Sisyphus smiles as he dreams in the blue sky".
(Jacques Darriulat, Aphorisms, 2022)

E1.6.B.2.2_REF6_PH “The path to differentiated results looks like madness to the masses. As the adage goes, if you do what everyone else does, you'll get the same results everyone else gets. Extraordinary success requires misunderstood choices.”
(Farnam Street Brain Food)

E1.6.B.2.3. THE VALUE OF EXERCISE IS ITS EXPERIENCE
According to Peter Sloterdijk, humans create their lives through systems of exercise. These are operations that enhance a person's skills for the next performance of the same task, acting as immune devices that help manage the lack of meaning. While the value of work lies in its product, the value of exercise lies in its experience.

E1.6.B.2.3.a. WHEREAS THE VALUE OF WORK IS ITS PRODUCT, THE VALUE OF EXERCISE IS ITS EXPERIENCE. 

E1.6.B.2.3.a_REF1_PH THE MODERN WORLD IS CHARACTERIZED BY THE HIGHEST MOBILIZATION OF HUMAN FORCES FOR WORK AND PRODUCTION, WHEREAS THE ANCIENT WORLD FOCUSED ON EXERCISE AND PERFECTION OF BEING.
"If one were to summarize in one sentence the essential fundamental difference between the modern world and the ancient world, and define with the same sentence the two states of the world, it would be the following: is modern the epoch that has achieved the highest mobilization of human forces under the sign of work and production, while all the forms of life in which the most extreme mobilization took place in the name of exercise and perfection [of being] bear the qualifier of ancient. [...] Since the type of activity constituted by exercise has emerged from the shadow of work - together with the aesthetic game - a new type of ecosystem is developing on the modern front, composed of activities in which the absolute primacy of the value of the product is revised in favor of the values of exercise, the values of performance and the values of living.
No one, therefore, can be a credible contemporary today if he or she does not feel the extent to which the performance dimension is taking precedence over the work dimension."
(Peter Sloterdijk, You must change your life, 2009)

E1.6.B.2.3.a_REF1.1_PH "TO WORK IN ORDER TO PRODUCE OR TO EXERCISE ONESELF AIMING AT THE PERFECTION OF BEING?
In this painting, Tuymans is inspired by Rubens (we can see the similarity in the version of Rubens' painting in which we have modified the color spectrum to be closer to Tuymans'). Do both painters focus in their paintings on the action or on the actor?
"If one were to summarize in one sentence the essential fundamental difference between the modern world and the ancient world, and define with the same sentence the two states of the world, it would be the following: is modern the epoch that has achieved the highest mobilization of human forces under the sign of work and production, while all the forms of life in which the most extreme mobilization took place in the name of exercise and perfection [of being] bear the qualifier of ancient. [...] Since the type of activity constituted by exercise has emerged from the shadow of work - together with the aesthetic game - a new type of ecosystem is developing on the modern front, composed of activities in which the absolute primacy of the value of the product is revised in favor of the values of exercise, the values of performance and the values of living.
No one, therefore, can be a credible contemporary today if he or she does not feel the extent to which the performance dimension is taking precedence over the work dimension."
(Peter Sloterdijk, You must change your life, 2009)

E1.6.B.2.3.a_REF2_CUR "IS ZADRAZILS INTEREST IN THE SUBJECT OF THIS PAINTING DERIVED BY HIS FOCUS ON PRODUCTION AS A RESULT OF WORK OR ON EXERCISING AS A TECHNIQUE FOR SELF-PERFECTIONISM?
"On his walks through Brooklyn, Franz Zadrazil discovers an advertisement for the paint store chain "Pintchik" on the façade of a house. This quotes the farmer couple from the famous painting American Gothic (1930) by Grant Wood, one of the images most often staged by U.S. pop culture. Wood's farmer is holding a pitchfork, a sign of the hard work he must do. In the Pintchik ad, the pitchfork has become a paintbrush. To further emphasize what is being advertised, numerous oversized dummy paintbrushes are mounted on the roof of the building."
(Curatorship, Pinakothek der Moderne, Munich)

E1.6.B.2.3.a_REF3_PH  “RENOIR, THE PAINTER, IS BELIEVED TO HAVE SAID THIS MAGNIFICENT PHRASE: 'THIS DRAWING TOOK ME FIVE MINUTES BUT IT TOOK ME SIXTY YEARS TO GET THERE.'
Life grows at its own pace. Too often, I am thirsty for progress and change. I look for profit everywhere and cut myself off from what is abundantly lavished on me every moment. And what fruit has it given me today?
(Alexandre Jollien, La Pharmacopée n°35)

E1.6.B.2.3.a_REF4_PH "I DO NOT LIKE THE IDEA OF HAPPINESS — IT IS TOO MOMENTARY — I would say that I was always busy and interested in something — interest has more meaning to me than the idea of happyness."
(Georgia O’Keeffe)

E1.6.B.2.3.a_REF5_CUR HOWEVER, SOME WORKS ARE DIFFICULT TO TRANSFORM INTO A POETIC SPIRAL!
What is Beuys telling us with this zinc fleece of Jason's that looks like a manger? In the myth, the golden fleece is a solar symbol. Its conquest allows one to obtain the status of a hero and, for some of them, sovereignty, which is indeed Jason's destiny in the legend. The quest of modern man in the consumer society that Beuys denounces is less glorious: at best, can he hope to feed at the trough of the pigs that lead the horde?
In this society, the feminine element would intervene to calm the madness of men... this is how I interpret Bathtub for a Heroine: the effervescent masculine ardor plunging into the bathtub of the feminine in order to regain its spirits. That's how I see these enigmatic works. What do you think?

E1.6.B.2.3.a_REF6_LIT ON THE PLATEAU OF TALENT
“They can’t take it any further. And why not? Because they won’t put in the effort. Because they haven’t had the discipline pounded into them. They’ve been spoiled. They have just enough talent so they’ve been able to play things well without any effort and they’ve had people telling them how great they are from the time they’re little, so hard work looks stupid to them. They’ll take some piece another kid has to work on for three weeks and polish it off in half the time, so the teacher figures they’ve put enough into it and lets them go to the next thing. […] They never find out what it means to be hammered by the teacher; they lose out on a certain element required for character building. It’s a tragedy.”
(Haruki Murakami)

together

E1.6.B.2.3.b. SEEKING EASY RESULT IS THE ANTITHESIS OF THE POETIC SPIRAL

E1.6.B.2.3.b_REF1_PH "EVERYONE LOOKS FOR THE MIRACLE MOMENT – THE MOMENT WHEN SUCCESS HAPPENS.

We are drawn to these moments because we want to know the secret. We want to know the ingredient that we are missing. The ingredient that makes the recipe.

The problem is … there is no miracle moment. If you want to understand success, you can’t focus on what’s visible.

Results are simply one more step in a long chain of steps that led to that moment.

Nature offers a great example with bamboo, which takes ups up to 5 years to develop its roots. For years, to the outside observer, no visible progress has been made. Meanwhile, the bamboo grows below the surface, developing its roots and storing energy. Then, all at once, it starts to grow. Years of stored energy result in exponential growth, sometimes reaching over 50 feet in a matter of weeks.

That’s how results happen. Slowly and then all at once.

Everyone wants the results. No one wants the process that leads to them. That's boring.

There are two main lessons to take away:

Not all progress is visible. Don’t beat yourself up when things aren’t visible. One workout won't make you fit, but it is better than no workout. A small deposit in your bank account today won’t get you to your goal, but it moves you closer. The daily grind is part of the process.

Consistently doing boring things well leads to extreme outperformance. Most of the time, we know what we need to do. The problem is because we don't immediately see the results, we stop. It's as if we tell ourselves, "I ate healthily and went to the gym all week, and I'm still not as fit as I want, so what's the point?"

You have to be smart enough to know you're making progress without any obvious signs of progress.

Rome wasn’t built in a day, but it was built one brick at a time."
(Farnam Street - Brain food, november 2022)

E1.6.B.2.3.b_REF2_PH “PRACTICE UNTIL YOU GET IT RIGHT, AND YOU'LL NEED PERFECT CONDITIONS.
Practice until you can't get it wrong, and conditions no longer matter.”
(Shane Parrish, FS Brain Food, June 1, 2025)

E1.6.B.2.3.c. EXERCISE ARE IMMUNIZING DEVICES.
They enable groups to become organized, self-perpetuating "systems". They assist groups in becoming organized, self-sustaining "systems". Not only has humanity developed biological defense systems, but it has also established socio-immunological systems like justice and mutual aid, as well as psycho-immunological systems such as beliefs and rituals. These systems are designed to protect against both internal and external threats. Currently, these immune systems face challenges due to the anthropotechnical shift, which questions humanity's place in the world and its role as a creator of meaning.

E1.6.B.2.3.c_REF1_PH PETER SLOTERDIJK EXPLORES HOW IMMUNE SYSTEMS ENABLE ORGANISMS, CULTURES, AND SYSTEMS TO SUSTAIN AND REPRODUCE THEMSELVES IN CONSTANT INTERACTION WITH A POTENTIALLY INVASIVE ENVIRONMENT. He identifies three human immune systems: biological, socio-immunological (legal, solidaristic, military), and psycho-immunological (symbolic), and emphasizes the importance of cultural studies in understanding and managing these complex systems.
"Only immune systems enable systems to become systems, living beings to become living beings, and cultures to become cultures. Only by virtue of their immune qualities do they attain the status of self-organizing units that maintain and reproduce themselves in constant connection with a potentially and actually invasive and irritating environment. [...]
"Rilke says at the beginning of the Eighth Elegy: "With all its gaze, the living perceives the 'open'-life itself is an exodus that establishes a relationship between the interior and the environment. Although almost all organisms or integrities transcend into the spaces of surprise and conflict of the first level, which are always attributed to them as environments (even plants do it, and even more so animals), very few reach the movement of transcendence of the second level - as far as we know, only humans do so. It is this movement that opens the boundaries of the environment (Umwelt) to make it a world (Welt), as an integral composed of the manifest and the latent. The second stage is the work of language. Language not only builds the "house of being" - Heidegger borrowed this expression from Zarathustra's animals, who tell the convalescent: "The same house of being is being built forever" - it is also the vehicle for the tendencies to flee the house, with which man, by the force of his inner excesses, moves out into the open. There's no need to explain why it is only in the second transcendence that the oldest parasite of the world, the upper world, appears. [...]
In the human sphere, there are no less than three immune systems that operate in a strong cooperative interweaving and a strong functional complementarity: on top of the biological substrate, largely automated and independent of consciousness, two complementary systems have been formed in human beings in the course of their mental and socio-cultural evolution, aimed at treating injuries by anticipation: On the one hand, the socio-immunological practices, especially legal and solidaristic, but also military, with which man settles his confrontations with distant and alien aggressors and beings who, although they are his neighbors, are no less a cause of offense or nuisance in "society"; on the other hand, the symbolic or psycho-immunological practices with which man has always managed to control, more or less well, the vulnerability that fate has given him, including mortality, in the form of imaginary anticipations and mental armaments. One of the ironies of these systems is that they are capable of undergoing an explication of their hidden side, even though they exist from the outset in dependence on consciousness and consider themselves as phenomena transparent to themselves. They do not operate behind the backs of their subjects, but are fully integrated into their intentional behavior - yet it is possible to understand this behavior better than its naive agents. Because the non-naïve handling of symbolic immune systems has today become a condition for the survival of "cultures" themselves, "cultural studies" are necessary.  [...]"
(Peter Sloterdijk, You must change your life, 2009)

E1.6.B.2.3.c_REF2_PH PETER SLOTERDIJK'S "YOU MUST CHANGE YOUR LIFE" (2009) summarized in this 5'51" video

Watch it on YouTube:
https://youtu.be/K_M7GYpvo5E

E1.6.B.2.3.c_REF3_PH "DO I SPEND TOO MUCH TIME AT THIS EPHEMERAL TASK [GARDENING]? In spring, summer, and autumn I work harder at it than at writing, and I expect that looks crazy, but what it does is balance all the anxieties and tensions and keep me sane. Sanity (plus flowers) does make sense."
(May Sarton, The House by the Sea, 1977)

E1.6.B.2.3.d. FOR PETER SLOTERDIJK, THERE IS NO SUCH THING AS RELIGION, ONLY "MISUNDERSTOOD SYSTEMS OF SPIRITUAL EXERCISES".
These can be practiced collectively or individually. The distinction between believers and non-believers vanishes and is replaced by that between practitioners and non-practitioners. In his view, man produces man not through work or interaction, but through his life in "exercises", which are any operations that enhance a person's skills until their next performance of the same task.

E1.6.B.2.3.d_REF1_PH THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN TRUE RELIGION AND SUPERSTITION IS OBSOLETE. THE REAL FOCUS SHOULD BE ON THE ANTHROPOLOGICAL ASPECT OF HUMAN EXISTENCE, WHICH INVOLVES SYMBOLIC IMMUNE SYSTEMS AND RITUAL FRAMEWORKS. Human beings create themselves not through labor or self-improvement but through life exercises that stabilize or improve their qualifications.
"A movement back to religion is just as unlikely as a return to religion for the simple reason that there are no religions or « religions », but only systems of misunderstood spiritual exercises, whether these are practised in collectives - traditionally: church, order, ummah, sangha - or in personalised forms - in interaction with the personal god with whom the citizens of modernity take out private insurance. The unfortunate distinction between « true religion » and superstition thus loses its purpose. There are now only systems of practice more or less capable of spreading, more or less worthy of expansion. The false opposition between believers and nonbelievers also disappears, replaced by the distinction between practitioners and non-practitioners, or practitioners otherwise. [...] What really comes back and deserves all the intellectual attention has a more anthropological than « religious » twist - it is, in a word, to take into consideration the immune constitution of the human creature. After several centuries of experimentation with new forms of life, it has come to be understood that human beings, whatever their ethnic, economic and political conditions of existence, do not live only in « material situations », but also and rather in symbolic immune systems and ritual frameworks. [...]
Marx and the Young Hegelians expressed the thesis that man himself generates man. The meaning conveyed by this phrase was instantly disfigured by a chatter that spoke of labour as man's only essential action. But if man does indeed produce man, it is not precisely through work and its objective results, nor through that « work on oneself » which is in vogue today [...]: he does it through his life in exercises.
By « exercise » I mean any operation by which the qualification of the practitioner is stabilised or improved until the next performance of the same operation, whether or not it is declared as an exercise."
(Peter Sloterdijk, You must change your life, 2009)

E1.6.B.2.3.e. IN REPETITION, WE PROVIDE A NEW INFLECTION TO THE THE EXERCISE EACH TIME

E1.6.B.2.3.e_REF1_PH WALKING THE SAME ROUTES DAILY HELPS CLEAR THE MIND, FOSTERING A DEEPER AWARENESS AND CURIOSITY ABOUT THE WORLD, AKIN TO THE CONTINUOUS DISCOVERY AND RENEWAL FOUND IN LOVE. This process, much like the repetition of a declaration of love, maintains its freshness and intensity through constant reinterpretation.
"When I walk — which I do every day, as basic sanity-maintenance, whether in the forest or the cemetery or the city street — I walk the same routes, walk along loops, loops I often retrace multiple times in a single walk. This puzzles people. Some simply don’t get the appeal of such recursiveness. Others judge it as dull. But I walk to think more clearly, which means to traverse the world with ever-broadening scope of attention to reality, ever-widening circles of curiosity, ever-deepening interest in the ceaselessly flickering constellation of details within and without. In this respect, walking is a lot like love — for one human being to love another is to continually discover new layers of oneself while continuously discovering new layers of the other, and in them new footholds of love.
"I decide that [the declaration of love], though I repeat and rehearse it day by day through the course of time, will somehow recover, each time I utter it, a new state. Like the Argonaut renewing his ship during his voyage without changing its name, the subject in love will perform a long task through the course of one and the same exclamation, gradually dialecticizing the original demand though without ever dimming the incandescence of its initial address, considering that the very task of love and of language is to give to one and the same phrase inflections which will be forever new."
(Maria Popova, Brain Pickings' review of Roland Barthes' Roland Barthes by Roland Barthes, 1977)

E1.6.B.2.3.e_REF2_PH THE IDEA OF PROGRESS AND THE INTUITION OF DISCONTINUOUS TIME [...]

To fully grasp the meaning of habit, we must combine two seemingly contradictory concepts: repetition and beginning. However, this objection disappears when we recognize that every particular habit depends on the general, clear, and conscious habit that is the will. Therefore, we can define habit in its full sense by this formula, which reconciles the two opposites that are too quickly set against each other by criticism: Habit is the will to begin repeating oneself. [...]

Undoubtedly, the idea of eternal return occurred to Mr. Roupnel, but he immediately understood that this fruitful and true idea could not be absolute. By being reborn, we accentuate life. "For we do not rise again in vain! The beginning again is not an eternal, unchanging entity! Our mental acts and thoughts are repeated according to the ritual of ever-acquired habits and are invested with ever-increasing physical fidelity. If our faults worsen and clarify their disastrous contours, our useful and beneficial actions also leave firmer imprints on the trail of eternal footsteps. With each new beginning, a new firmness rests on the act, gradually bringing unknown abundance to the results. Let us not say that the act is permanent; rather, it is constantly enhanced by the precision of its origins and effects. We live each new life as a passing work, yet life bequeaths all its fresh impressions to life. The act revisits its intentions and consequences, ever more enamored of its rigor, and completes what never completes it. [...]
" Rather than a doctrine of eternal return, Roupen's thesis is a doctrine of eternal returns. [...]

"The idea of progress," he rightly says, "is logically associated with the idea of beginning again and repetition."

The habit itself implies progress. The repeated act, due to acquired habit, is performed with greater speed and precision. The gestures that execute it lose their excessive amplitude and unnecessary complexity. They become simpler and shorter. Superfluous movements disappear. The act reduces expenditure to the bare minimum: the energy and time required. As dynamism improves and becomes more precise, work and results are perfected. (5) [...]

This is "perfection, undoubtedly very slight, but logically undeniable and sufficient to introduce differentiation of moments and, consequently, the element of duration." Thus, we see that this duration is the expression of dynamic progress. Having reduced everything to dynamism, we can simply say that continuous duration, if it exists, is the expression of progress." (6).
We can understand that a scale of perfection can be applied to a group of moments brought together by active chronotropisms, or changes in rhythm. Through a strange reciprocity, progress in the aesthetic, moral, or religious sense allows us to be certain of the passage of time. Moments are distinct because they are fruitful. They are not fruitful because of the memories they can evoke, but because they are accompanied by a temporal novelty that is adapted to the rhythm of progress. [...]

Sensation is variety; memory is the only thing that makes things uniform. Therefore, there is always the same difference in method between Mr. Bergson and us. He considers time to be full of events at the level of consciousness of those events. Then, he gradually erases the events or the consciousness of those events. He believes that he will then reach time without events or the consciousness of pure duration. Conversely, we can only experience time by multiplying conscious moments. If we relax our meditation due to laziness, there may still be enough moments enriched by the life of the senses and flesh for us to have a vague feeling that we are lasting. However, if we want to clarify this feeling, we find this clarification only in a multiplication of thoughts. For us, the awareness of time is always an awareness of the use of moments; it is always active, never passive. In short, our awareness of duration is an awareness of the progress of our inner being, whether that progress is real, simulated, or imagined. This progression makes the complex clearer and simpler, and the well-renewed rhythm is more coherent than pure repetition. Moreover, if we achieve uniformity in our meditation through skillful construction, it seems like yet another conquest because we find this uniformity in an ordering of creative moments or in one of those general, fruitful thoughts that encompass a thousand dependent thoughts. Duration is a treasure that cannot be found through abstraction. We construct its framework by placing concrete moments rich in conscious, measured novelty one after the other without allowing them to touch. The coherence of duration is the coordination of a method of enrichment. [...]

Notes: (5) Siloë, p. 157. (6) Siloë, p. 158."
(Gaston Bachelard, Intuition of the Instant, Chapter III, 1931)


E1.6.B.2.3.f. THE ARTIST'S CREATIVE MISSION
Nietzsche believed that art is the will to turn the future into an endless realm of uplifting possibilities. He claimed that artists don't create from nothing, but rather, they build upon and transform what has come before. Creation, for him, is the elevation of the improbable. The artist has the metaphysical duty to continually increase this "mountain of the improbable".
According to Nietzsche, only a person free from resentment, who can accept the necessity of raising this cultural mountain higher with each generation, can truly embrace this concept. Such an individual would not impose his own imperfection as a duty upon his descendants, and would rather disappear than remain unchanged.

E1.6.B.2.3.f._REF1_PH "THE ARTIST´S METAPHYSICS
[...] "... there are always fewer who climb with me on ever higher mountains: I am raising a chain of ever holier mountains". [...] Only the building of new steeper walls can compensate for the flattening of the mountain due to the habit of living on it.
It is important to understand how much Nietzsche addresses himself here as an artist to other artists: the desire to raise improbability to the level of a mountain made of mountains expresses the most extreme point that an artistic confession can reach. Only the artistic will to transform the future into a space of infinite possibilities for the elevation of art makes the final sentence of the rule of reproduction we quoted understandable: "You must create a creator [...] a first movement, a wheel that rolls of its own accord". This rule contains nothing less than Nietzsche's theology after the death of God: there will continue to be God and gods, but they will only be immanent to man, and only insofar as there are creators who relate to what has been achieved and  go higher, faster and further. [...]
Only a man of this type would no longer take himself as the yardstick for the future of the next generation and a fortiori of his ancestors. Only he could [...] approve of the idea that the cultural mountain of the improbable will have to rise by one more level in each generation. He would not make his own imperfection a duty for his descendants. He would rather die for good than return unchanged. That, according to the law of the normalisation of the improbable, the previous peaks appear in the perception of his descendants as landscapes of hills or plains is for him as understandable as it is welcome. [...] What has existed  before is the base camp for the next departure. From this point on, only the acrobatic path remains.
(Peter Sloterdijk, You must change your life, 2009)

E1.6.B.2.3.f._REF2_PH TO BE HUMAN MEANS TO EXIST IN AN OPERATIONALLY CURVED SPACE WHERE ACTIONS ACT BACK ON THE ACTOR
"To be human means to exist in an operationally curved space where actions act back on the actor, the product of work on the worker, communications on the communicator, thoughts on the thinker, feelings on the feeler. All these species of feedback loops have, I assert, an ascetic character, that is, the character of exercise - though, as we have already said, they must be classified for the most part in the undeclared and unperceived ascetics, the hidden routines of training. Only openly practising human beings explicitly raise the ascetic circle of existence to the level of visibility. They create the self-referential relations that commit the individual to participate in his subjectivation. [...] « I want to renaturalize asceticism as well; instead of the intention of negation, the intention of strengthening » [...Friedrich Nietzsche, Posthumous Fragments, Autumn 1887].
The existence of the human being of tomorrow must be based entirely on exercise and mobility, including gymnastics of the will and tests of courage imposed on one's own strength. Nietzsche even envisages a training for moral virtues in which one proves one's « strength of keeping-his-word » [which] is a matter of training, discipline, education and of one's personal project - the « superman » [übermensch] does not imply a biological programme, but an artistic programme, not to say: an acrobatic one."
(Peter Sloterdijk, You must change your life, 2009)

E1.6.B.2.3.g. BY CONTEMPLATING A WORK OF ART, WE CAN "CLIMB ABOARD" THE ARTIST'S POETIC SPIRAL, OPENING UP NEW HORIZONS FOR US

E1.6.B.2.3.g_REF1_PO  "ARCHAIC TORSO OF APOLLO

We cannot know his legendary head
with eyes like ripening fruit. And yet his torso
is still suffused with brilliance from inside,
like a lamp, in which his gaze, now turned to low,

gleams in all its power. Otherwise
the curved breast could not dazzle you so, nor could
a smile run through the placid hips and thighs
to that dark center where procreation flared.

Otherwise this stone would seem defaced
beneath the translucent cascade of the shoulders
and would not glisten like a wild beast's fur:

would not, from all the borders of itself,
burst like a star: for here there is no place
that does not see you. You must change your life!"
(Rainer Maria Rilke)

E1.6.B.2.3.g_REF1.1_CUR “In his reliefs, Arp had eventually adopted the frame, using it to interrogate and undermine the conventions of painting. He likewise created sculptures, such as the multipart works and Human Concretions, that rejected both the pedestal and the verticality of the sculptural monolith, but he soon turned to these elements with similarly subversive results. In this early Torso, nearly half the sculpture is the large vertical block on which is anchored the titular twisting form. The block, which provides ballast to the soaring torso, may mimic a pedestal, but it does not quite perform like one, as it is too low to bring the torso to an appropriate viewing height.”
(Curatorship: Peggy Guggenheim Collection, Venice)

E1.6.B.2.3.g_REF2_LIT ENGAGING IN ACTIVITIES LIKE READING, DRAWING, OR MUSIC CONNECTS US TO SOMETHING GREATER, PROVIDING A SENSE OF MEANING AND HOMECOMING. When creators work with spirit and generosity, their art allows others to find their own sense of beauty and meaning.
"When you love something like reading — or drawing or music or nature — it surrounds you with a sense of connection to something great. If you are lucky enough to know this, then your search for meaning involves whatever that Something is. It’s an alchemical blend of affinity and focus that takes us to a place within that feels as close as we ever get to “home.” It’s like pulling into our own train station after a long trip — joy, relief, a pleasant exhaustion.
If a writer or artist creates from a place of truth and spirit and generosity, then I may be able to enter and ride this person’s train back to my own station. It’s the same with beautiful music and art.
Beauty is meaning."
(Anne Lamott, Stitches: A Handbook on Meaning, Hope and Repair)

E1.6.B.2.3.g_REF3_CUR "LUIZA CROSMAN’S VISUAL ESSAY “UNRAVELING INSTITUTIONAL DYNAMICS” LOOKS AT NEW WAYS TO THINK AND ACT within the frame of institutional critique through diagrams. It experiments with the relationship between art, economical, and social systems, and the idea of « production of subjectivity ». Authors quoted are: AA Bronson, Andrea Frasier, Luis Camnitzer, Hito Steyerl, Anton Vidokle, Suely Rolnik, and Kim Gordon."
Watching this presentation by Luiza Crosman, I realize that she is also in Peter Sloterdijk's line of thought : Life as an occupation that is justified in itself, a discipline of training, an artistic spiral that produces something in the end but which goal is not production in itself.

Watch the animation on Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/p/BwOqHMgB9Iq/?utm_source=ig_web_copy_link

E1.6.B.2.3.h. IS VIKTOR FRANKL'S LOGOTHERAPY AN EXERCISE SYSTEM?
Viktor Frankl's logotherapy emphasizes finding meaning in one's life, even amid severe difficulties such as those endured in Nazi concentration camps. Although Frankl used the term "meaning," could he be referring to the "poetic spiral" exercise, as defined by Peter Sloterdijk? Frankl stated, "[In the camps,] one could either transform one's experiences into triumphs, making one's life a victory over oneself, or simply ignore these challenges and vegetate".

E1.6.B.2.3.h_REF1_PH VIKTOR FRANKEL EXPLORES HOW EXPERIENCES IN EXTREME CIRCUMSTANCES, SUCH AS CONCENTRATION CAMPS, CAN PROVIDE AN OPPORTUNITY TO FIND DEEPER MEANING IN LIFE. He emphasizes that even without pleasure or creativity, suffering and death are an integral part of human life. How a person accepts and responds to his or her fate, with dignity and courage or not, determines the depth of his or her spirituality and humanity. 
"Experiences in a Concentration Camp

[...] An active life serves the purpose of giving man the opportunity to realize values in creative work, while a passive life of enjoyment affords him the opportunity to obtain fulfilment in experiencing beauty, art, or nature. But there is also purpose in that life which is almost barren of both creation and enjoyment and which admits of but one possibility of high moral behaviour: namely, in man's attitude to his existence, an existence restricted by external forces. A creative life and a life of enjoyment are banned to him. But not only creativeness and enjoyment are meaningful. If there is a meaning in life at all, then there must be a meaning in suffering. Suffering is an ineradicable part of life, even as fate and death. Without suffering and death human life cannot be complete.

The way in which a man accepts his fate and all the suffering it entails, the way in which he takes up his cross, gives him ample opportunity—even under the most difficult circumstances—to add a deeper meaning to his life. It may remain brave, dignified and unselfish. Or in the bitter fight for selfpreservation he may forget his human dignity and become no more than an animal. Here lies the chance for a man either to make use of or to forgo the opportunities of attaining the moral values that a difficult [...]

As we said before, any attempt to restore a man's inner strength in the camp had first to succeed in showing him some future goal. Nietzsche's words, "He who has a why to live for can bear with almost any how," could be the guiding motto for all psychotherapeutic and psychohygienic efforts regarding prisoners. Whenever there was an opportunity for it, one had to give them a why—an aim—for their lives, in order to strengthen them to bear the terrible how of their existence. Woe to him who saw no more sense in his life, no aim, no purpose, and therefore no point in carrying on. He was soon lost. The typical reply with which such a man rejected all encouraging arguments was, "I have nothing to expect from life any more." What sort of answer can one give to that?

What was really needed was a fundamental change in our attitude toward life. We had to learn ourselves and, furthermore, we had to teach the despairing men, that it did not really matter what we expected from life, but rather what life expected from us. We needed to stop asking about the meaning of life, and instead to think of ourselves as those who were being questioned by life—daily and hourly. Our answer must consist, not in talk and meditation, but in right action and in right conduct. Life ultimately means taking the responsibility to find the right answer to its problems and to fulfil the tasks which it constantly sets for each individual.

These tasks, and therefore the meaning of life, differ from man to man, and from moment to moment. Thus it is impossible to define the meaning of life in a general way. Questions about the meaning of life can never be answered by sweeping statements. "Life" does not mean something vague, but something very real and concrete, just as life's tasks are also very real and concrete. They form man's destiny, which is different and unique for each individual. No man and no destiny can be compared with any other man or any other destiny. No situation repeats itself, and each situation calls for a different response. Sometimes the situation in which a man finds himself may require him
to shape his own fate by action. At other times it is more advantageous for him to make use of an opportunity for contemplation and to realize assets in this way. Sometimes man may be required simply to accept fate, to bear his cross. Every situation is distinguished by its uniqueness, and there is always only one right answer to the problem posed by the situation at hand.

When a man finds that it is his destiny to suffer, he will have to accept his suffering as his task; his single and unique task. He will have to acknowledge the fact that even in suffering he is unique and alone in the universe. No one can relieve him of his suffering or suffer in his place. His unique opportunity lies in the way in which he bears his burden.

For us, as prisoners, these thoughts were not speculations far removed from reality. They were the only thoughts that could be of help to us. They kept us from despair, even when there seemed to be no chance of coming out of it alive. Long ago we had passed the stage of asking what was the meaning of life, a naive query which understands life as the attaining of some aim through the active creation of something of value. For us, the meaning of life embraced the wider cycles of life and death, of suffering and of dying.

Once the meaning of suffering had been revealed to us, we refused to minimize or alleviate the camp's tortures by ignoring them or harbouring false illusions and entertaining artificial optimism. Suffering had become a task on which we did not want to turn out backs. We had realized its hidden opportunities for achievement, the opportunities which caused the poet Rilke to write, "Wie viel ist aufzuleiden!"(How much suffering there is to get through!) Rilke spoke of "getting through suffering"
as others would talk of "getting through work." There was plenty of suffering for us to get through. Therefore, it was necessary to face up to the full amount of suffering, trying to keep moments of weakness and furtive tears to a minimum. But there was no need to be ashamed of tears, for tears bore witness that a man had the greatest of courage, the courage to suffer. Only very few realized that. Shamefacedly some confessed occasionally that they had wept, like the comrade who answered my question of how he had gotten over his oedema, by confessing, "I have wept it out of my system."[...]"
(Viktor Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning, 1946)

E1.6.B.2.3.h_REF2_PH VIKTOR FRANKL RECOUNTS HOW HE RISED FROM HIS SITUATION IN A CONCENTRATION CAMP by imagining himself giving a lecture on the psychology of prisoners, thus transforming his suffering into a subject for scientific study, in accordance with Spinoza's idea that suffering ceases when one has a clear and distinct understanding of it.
"I remember a personal experience. Almost in tears from pain (I had terrible sores on my feet from wearing torn shoes), I limped a few kilometres with our long column of men from the camp to our work site. Very cold, bitter winds struck us. I kept thinking of the endless little problems of our miserable life. What would there be to eat tonight? If a piece of sausage came as extra ration, should I exchange it for a piece of bread? Should I trade my last cigarette, which was left from a bonus I received a fortnight ago, for a bowl of soup? How could I get a piece of wire to replace the fragment which served as one of my shoelaces? Would I get to our work site in time to join my usual working party or would I have to join another, which might have a brutal foreman? What could I do to get on good terms with the Capo, who could help me to obtain work in camp instead of undertaking this horribly long daily march?

I became disgusted with the state of affairs which compelled me, daily and hourly, to think of only such trivial things. I forced my thoughts to turn to another subject. Suddenly I saw myself standing on the platform of a well-lit, warm and pleasant lecture room. In front of me sat an attentive audience on comfortable upholstered seats. I was giving a lecture on the psychology of the concentration camp! All that oppressed me at that moment became objective, seen and described from the remote viewpoint of science. By this method I succeeded somehow in rising above the situation, above the sufferings of the moment, and I observed them as if they were already of the past. Both I and my troubles became the object of an interesting psychoscientific study undertaken by myself. What does Spinoza say in his Ethics?—"Affectus, qui passio est, desinit esse passio simulatque eius claram et distinctam formamus ideam." Emotion, which is suffering, ceases to be suffering as soon as we form a clear and precise picture of it.[...]"
(Viktor Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning, 1946)

E1.6.B.2.3.h_REF3_PH VIKTOR FRANKL EMPHASIZES THE NEED TO MEET LIFE'S CHALLENGES WITH ACTION AND APPROPRIATE BEHAVIOR, AS EACH SITUATION IS UNIQUE AND REQUIRES A SPECIFIC RESPONSE. He emphasizes the importance of changing our attitude toward life, focusing on what life expects of us rather than the other way around.
"I remember two cases of would-be suicide, which bore a striking similarity to each other. Both men had talked of their intentions to commit suicide. Both used the typical argument—they had nothing more to expect from life. In both cases it was a question of getting them to realize that life was still expecting something from them; something in the future was expected of them. We found, in fact, that for the one it was his child whom he adored and who was waiting for him in a foreign country. For the other it was a thing, not a person. This man was a scientist and had written a series of books which still needed to be finished. His work could not be done by anyone else, any more than another person could ever take the place of the father in his child's affections.

This uniqueness and singleness which distinguishes each individual and gives a meaning to his existence has a bearing on creative work as much as it does on human love. When the impossibility of replacing a person is realized, it allows the responsibility which a man has for his existence and its continuance to appear in all its magnitude. A man who becomes conscious of the responsibility he bears toward a human being who affectionately waits for him, or to an unfinished work, will never be able to throw away his life. He knows the "why" for his existence, and will be able to bear almost any "how." [...]"
(Viktor Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning, 1946)

E1.6.B.2.3.h_REF4_PH VIKTOR FRANKL ARGUES THAT MENTAL HEALTH IS BASED ON A TENSION BETWEEN OUR ACCOMPLISHMENTS AND OUR FUTURE GOALS. THIS TENSION IS ESSENTIAL AND CANNOT BE AVOIDED BECAUSE IT MOTIVATES THE INDIVIDUAL TO ACHIEVE MEANINGFUL GOALS. Existential emptiness, on the other hand, can lead to the search for power or pleasure to compensate for the lack of meaning in life.
"Logotherapy in a Nutshell

[...] Thus it can be seen that mental health is based on a certain degree of tension, the tension between what one has already achieved and what one still ought to accomplish, or the gap between what one is and what one should become. Such a tension is inherent in the human being and therefore is indispensable to mental well-being. We should not, then, be hesitant about challenging man with a potential meaning for him to fulfil. It is only thus that we evoke his will to meaning from its state of latency. I consider it a dangerous misconception of mental hygiene to assume that what man needs in the first place is equilibrium or, as it is called in biology, "homeostasis," i.e., a tensionless state. What man actually needs is not a tensionless state but rather the striving and struggling for a worthwhile goal, a freely chosen task. What he needs is not the discharge of tension at any cost but the call of a potential meaning waiting to be fulfilled by him. What man needs is not homeostasis but what I call "nod-dynamics," i.e., the existential dynamics in a polar field of tension where one pole is represented by a meaning that is to be fulfilled and the other pole by the man who has to fulfil it. And one should not think that this holds true only for normal conditions; in neurotic individuals, it is even more valid. If architects want to strengthen a decrepit arch, they increase the load which is laid upon it, for thereby the parts are joined more firmly together. So if therapists wish to foster their
patients' mental health, they should not be afraid to create a sound amount of tension through a reorientation toward the meaning of one's life. [...]

THE EXISTENTIAL VACUUM

The existential vacuum is a widespread phenomenon of the twentieth century. This is understandable; it may be due to a twofold loss which man has had to undergo since he became a truly human being. At the beginning of human history, man lost some of the basic animal instincts in which an animal's behaviour is imbedded and by which it is secured. Such security, like Paradise, is closed to man forever; man has to make choices. In addition to this, however, man has suffered another loss in his more recent development inasmuch as the traditions which buttressed his behaviour are now rapidly diminishing. No instinct tells him. what he has to do, and no tradition tells him what he ought to do; sometimes he does not even know what he wishes to do. Instead, he either wishes to do what other people do (conformism) or he does what other people wish him to do (totalitarianism). [...]

The existential vacuum manifests itself mainly in a state of boredom. Now we can understand Schopenhauer when he said that mankind was apparently doomed to vacillate eternally between the two extremes of distress and boredom. In actual fact, boredom is now causing, and certainly bringing to psychiatrists, more problems to solve than distress. And these problems are growing increasingly crucial, for progressive automation will probably lead to an enormous increase in the leisure hours available to the average worker. The pity of it is that many of these will not know what to do with all their newly acquired free time. [...]

Moreover, there are various masks and guises under which the existential vacuum appears. Sometimes the frustrated will to meaning is vicariously compensated for by a will to power, including the most primitive form of the will to power, the will to money. In other cases, the place of frustrated will to meaning is taken by the will to pleasure. That is why existential frustration often eventuates in sexual compensation. We can observe in such cases that the sexual libido becomes rampant in the existential vacuum."
(Viktor Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning, 1946)

E1.6.B.2.3.h_REF5_PH VIKTOR FRANKL BELIEVES THAT EACH PERSON HAS A UNIQUE AND IRREPLACEABLE MISSION, AND THAT RESPONSIBILITY LIES AT THE HEART OF HUMAN EXISTENCE. The meaning of life is subjective, and varies according to individuals and their circumstances.
"THE MEANING OF LIFE

I doubt whether a doctor can answer this question in general terms. For the meaning of life differs from man to man, from day to day and from hour to hour. What matters, therefore, is not the meaning of life in general but rather the specific meaning of a person's life at a given moment. To put the question in general terms would be comparable to the question posed to a chess champion: "Tell me, Master, what is the best move in the world?" There simply is no such thing as the best or even a good move apart from a particular situation in a game and the particular personality of one's opponent. The same holds for human existence. One should not search for an abstract meaning of life. Everyone has his own specific vocation or mission in life to carry out a concrete assignment which demands fulfilment. Therein he cannot be replaced, nor can his life be repeated. Thus, everyone's task is as unique as is his specific opportunity to implement it.

As each situation in life represents a challenge to man and presents a problem for him to solve, the question of the meaning of life may actually be reversed. Ultimately, man should not ask what the meaning of his life is, but rather he must recognize that it is he who is asked. In a word, each man is questioned by life; and he can only answer to life by answering for his own life; to life he can only respond by being responsible. Thus, logotherapy sees in responsibleness the very essence of human existence.

THE ESSENCE OF EXISTENCE

This emphasis on responsibleness is reflected in the categorical imperative of logotherapy, which is: "Live as if you were living already for the second time and as if you had acted the first time as wrongly as you are about to act now!" It seems to me that there is nothing which would stimulate a man's sense of responsibleness more than this maxim, which invites him to imagine first that the present is past and, second, that the past may yet be changed and amended. Such a precept confronts him with life's finiteness as well as the finality of what he makes out of both his life and himself. [...] 

By declaring that man is responsible and must actualize the potential meaning of his life, I wish to stress that the true meaning of life is to be discovered in the world rather than within man or his own psyche, as though it were a closed system. I have termed this constitutive characteristic "the self-transcendence of human existence." It denotes the fact that being human always points, and is directed, to something, or someone, other than oneself—be it a meaning to fulfil or another human being to encounter. The more one forgets himself—by giving himself to a cause to serve or another person to love—the more human he is and the more he actualizes himself. What is called selfactualization is not an attainable aim at all, for the simple reason that the more one would strive for it, the more he would miss it. In other words, self-actualization is possible only as a side-effect of self-transcendence. [...]"
(Viktor Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning, 1946)

E1.6.B.2.3.h_REF6_PH VIKTOR FRANKL ASSERTS THAT EVEN IN DESPERATE SITUATIONS, IT IS POSSIBLE TO FIND MEANING IN EXISTENCE BY TRANSFORMING SUFFERING INTO PERSONAL FULFILLMENT.
"THE MEANING OF SUFFERING

We must never forget that we may also find meaning in life even when confronted with a hopeless situation, when facing a fate that cannot be changed. For what then matters is to bear witness to the uniquely human potential at its best, which is to transform a personal tragedy into a triumph, to turn one's predicament into a human achievement. When we are no longer able to change a situation — just think of an incurable disease such as inoperable cancer — we are challenged to change ourselves. [...]

Edith Weisskopf-Joelson, before her death professor of psychology at the University of Georgia, contended, in her article on logotherapy, that "our current mental-hygiene philosophy stresses the idea that people ought to be happy, that unhappiness is a symptom of maladjustment. Such a value system might be responsible for the fact that the burden of unavoidable unhappiness is increased by unhappiness about being unhappy."4 And in another paper she expressed the hope that logotherapy "may help counteract certain unhealthy trends in the present-day culture of the United States, where the incurable sufferer is given very little opportunity to be proud of his suffering and to
consider it ennobling rather than degrading" so that "he is not only unhappy, but also ashamed of being unhappy."

In accepting this challenge to suffer bravely, life has a meaning up to the last moment, and it retains this meaning literally to the end. In other words, life's meaning is an unconditional one, for it even includes the potential meaning of unavoidable suffering. [...]"
(Viktor Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning, 1946)


Solzhenitsyn, who lived through the Russian Gulag, also tells us that in extreme situations of slavery, believing that happiness is the goal of life reduces our ability to resist.

E1.6.B.2.3.h_REF6.1_PH THE ABILITY TO SUFFER WELL IS MORE THAN HALF OF LIFE
"Action and suffering, which together make up our lives, are a whole; they are one. A child suffers its begetting, it suffers its birth, its weaning; it suffers here and suffers there until in the end it suffers death. But all the good in a man, for which he is praised or loved, is merely good suffering, the right kind, the living kind of suffering, a suffering to the full. The ability to suffer well is more than half of life — indeed, it is all life. Birth is suffering, growth is suffering, the seed suffers the earth, the root suffers the rain, the bud suffers its flowering."
(Herman Hesse, Letter to a Young German, 1919 - later included in his 1946 anthology If the War Goes On…)

E1.6.B.2.3.h_REF7_PH "THE CASE FOR A TRAGIC OPTIMISM
Postscript 1984

LET US FIRST ASK OURSELVES WHAT SHOULD BE understood by "a tragic optimism." In brief it means that one is, and remains, optimistic in spite of the "tragic triad," as it is called in logotherapy, a triad which consists of those aspects of human existence which may be circumscribed by: (1) pain; (2) guilt; and (3) death. This chapter, in fact, raises the question, How is it possible to say yes to life in spite of all that? How, to pose the question differently, can life retain its potential meaning in spite
of its tragic aspects? After all, "saying yes to life in spite of everything," to use the phrase in which the title of a German book of mine is couched, presupposes that life is potentially meaningful under any conditions, even those which are most miserable. And this in turn presupposes the human capacity to creatively turn life's negative aspects into something positive or constructive. In other words, what matters is to make the best of any given situation. "The best," however, is that which in Latin is called optimum—hence the reason I speak of a tragic optimism, that is, an optimism in the face of tragedy and in view of the human potential which at its best always allows for: (1) turning suffering into a human achievement and accomplishment; (2) deriving from guilt the opportunity to change oneself for the better; and (3) deriving from life's transitoriness an incentive to take responsible action.

It must be kept in mind, however, that optimism is not anything to be commanded or ordered. One cannot even force oneself to be optimistic indiscriminately, against all odds, against all hope. And what is true for hope is also true for the other two components of the triad inasmuch as faith and love cannot be commanded or ordered either."
(Viktor Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning, 1946)

E1.6.B.2.4. WE CAN LOSE OUR TASTE FOR EXERCISE AND BECOME BORED.
This is acedia.

E1.6.B.2.4_REF1_PH "AKÈDIA / AKEDIA

Acedy

The feeling, the state of a monk who disinvests in asceticism, who can no longer bring himself to invest in it (≠ who loses his faith). It's not a loss of belief; it's a loss of investment. Depressive state: mel-ancholy, lassitude, sadness, boredom, loss of heart. Life (spiritual life) seems monotonous, aimless, impossible to bear, futile: the as-cetic ideal has been obscured, it has no appeal. Cassien (Institu-tions, 10):29 "<...> what the Greeks called Akedia 30 and what we might call boredom or anxiety of heart (taedium sive anxietas cor-dis)." Phenomenon that comes up fairly often in stories of oriental ermetism. [...]

It's a state (of decline as the result of a blockage) that's closer to aphanisis (notion introduced by Jones:36 state of non-desire, the fear of non-desire) than to castration (to the fear of castration). [...]

Robinson Crusoe, or rather Selkirk the sailor, gives a very good description of that process: "When those Appetites <needs> were satisfied, the Desire of Society was as strong a Call upon him, and he appeared to himself least necessitious when he wanted every-thing; for the Supports of his Body were easily attained, but the eager Longings for seeing again the Face of Man during the Interval of craving bodily Appetites, were hardly supportable. He grew de-jected, languid, and melancholy, scarce able to refrain from doing himself Violence <...>" [...]. »

I'm providing these references to Hans Castorp and Robinson Crusoe because I want suggest that acedy is not exclusively linked to the monastic state. We're not monks and yet we're interested in acedy. Acedy has a specific relation to a form of "asceticism," that is to say to the practice (in the etymological sense)42 of a particular lifestyle.43 What's at stake in acedy isn't belief, an idea, the option of faith (acedy isn't a "doubt"), it's no longer having any invest-ment in a way of life. Acedy: the repeated, extenuated, insistent moment when you find you've had enough of your way of life, of your relationship to the world (to the "worldly"). I can wake up in the morning, see the schedule for the week unfolding before my eyes, and feel hopeless. It's repetitive, it goes round and round: the same old tasks, the same old meetings, and yet there's no invest-ment whatsoever-irrespective of the fact that, when viewed sepa-rately, the different bits of that schedule are endurable, sometimes even pleasant. [...]

The relation between acedy and Living-Together? Historically, a notion with a particular link to eremitic asceticism: painful disin-vestment in the asceticism of solitude the eremite reenters the world. Coenobitism: in that it entails the monk's integration into a strong communal structure, was probably conceived at least in part-as a means of combating acedy. Acedy (modern acedy): no longer being capable of investing in other people, in Living-with-several-other-people and yet at the same time being incapable of investing in solitude. → Throwing it all away, but without even somewhere to throw it: waste without a waste bin."
(Roland Barthes, How to live together, Course at the Collège de France, 1976-1977)

E1.6.B.2.4_REF2_PSI DR. JULIE GURNER ON THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN DISCIPLINE AND MOTIVATION:
“Oftentimes people get the advice that you have to rely on discipline. ... one of the things that I find is that discipline requires a lot of push, right? “I’m very disciplined about my workout. I’m really going to push it.” Motivation is a pull. It is something that is internal that fuels you, that drives you. A number of the people that I tend to work with are very obsessed. You don’t have to push them to go to a meeting. [...]

I think that oftentimes discipline is very useful and I think it’s useful in a few different cases. If you want to get back on a virtuous cycle, maybe you’ve fallen out of your exercise routine and so you need to get back into it and you really do have to push yourself that day to get back to the gym. ... But I feel as though discipline is often really… I think it’s talked about because it feels tough to do. We’re doing the hard thing. We’re slogging through. But when we are at our best, we’re not slogging through. Great people are obsessed and they’re not slogging through either. They are driven. They are motivated. They are deeply, deeply engaged."
(Farnam Street, Brain food)

Listen to the entire episode on Apple podcasts:
https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/169-dr-julie-gurner-part-1-caring-deeply-challenging/id990149481?i=1000618441141

Spotify:
https://open.spotify.com/episode/4x5gbxlt2GQwsCxgV24fYi?si=ee6b47cf397243d2&nd=1&dlsi=600f8a4896f44111

or dive right into the start of the excerpt above on YouTube:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C5D6HAskUaE&t=25s

E1.6.B.2.5. ARE ALL EXERCISES THE SAME?
Susan Wolf suggests that a meaningful life doesn't necessarily equate to a happy or altruistic one. Instead, it is distinct from fulfillment or morality. It involves engaging in activities that have objective value. Such activities can relate to personal relationships or crafts, like cooking for the family or creating a Halloween costume. To identify what is objectively valuable, one can employ communal intuition, asking if the activity pursues knowledge or excellence, exhibits creativity or aesthetics, or serves others. Pursuing excellence in any domain, such as athletics, can be meaningful as it exemplifies virtues like discipline and teamwork to others.

E1.6.B.2.5._REF1_PH "WHAT MAKES A LIFE MEANINGFUL? IS THE CHOICE OF WHERE WE FIND MEANING COMPLETELY ARBITRARY? These questions are different from the question 'What is the meaning of life?'
The meaning of life is a metaphysical question - whether my life is meaningful is a different question. Meaning is one dimension of a good life, together with happiness/fulfillment and morality. Susan Wolf advocates that a meaningful life is not necessarily a happy one or one dedicated to others. Meaning is separate from fulfillment or morality: it requires one to have engagements that are objectively valuable. Even if Sisyphus was happy, Wolf argues stone pushing does not constitute an objectively valuable good even if it is subjectively valuable to Sisyphus. So what is objectively valuable? Activities that we engage in out of our personal relationship with others - such as cooking meals for the family or making a halloween costume - or out of relationship with a craft - such as putting in long hours of piano practice to attain excellence.
For Wolf, Sartre's existentialism collapses in the end if what you choose to spend your freedom on has no objective value. Finding your passion and going after it is not enough. 
What makes something objectively valuable then? We can use our communal intuition about what is valuable and what isn't. We can also ask if it is in the pursuit of knowledge or excellence? Is it creative or aesthetic? Does it provide a service to others?
The pursuit of excellence in any field (ex: athletic) is meaningful in that it poses the exercise of virtues as an example for others (discipline, team work, etc.) A recluse could do something that then goes to other people and becomes meaningful. Even if a beautiful work is destroyed and nobody ever has seen it except its creator, why is it we feel the life of this artist was still meaningful?
Like happiness that you attain better by not trying too hard to reach it, meaning is better achieved by not becoming obsessive about it."
(Susan Wolf on Meaning In Life, Philosophy Bites)

Listen to the episode: http://philosophybites.com/2010/07/susan-wolf-on-meaning-in-life.html

E1.6.B.2.6. WILL AUTOMATION TURN US ALL INTO ARTISTS?
Following the logic of the poetic spiral, it doesn't matter if machines can produce better than us. That's because it's the act of creation that brings us the most joy, not the output. If a universal income guarantees our comfort, we could all become artists... provided we have the required discipline.

E1.6.B.2.6_REF1_PH "THINKING AGAINST YUVAL HARARI’S THE RISE OF THE USELESS CLASS
Yuval Harari's perspective is intriguing in many ways, yet it has a significant limitation: he only considers outcomes, not processes. The pleasure of an artist lies not in the final product but in the process of creation. The significance of improving a task is not in the end product, but in the joy of achieving greater perfection in a specific process, whether it be a sport, cooking, gardening, or playing an instrument. The fact that machines may outperform humans in producing end results has no bearing on human happiness.

Indeed, if machines take over all the mundane jobs necessary for human survival and comfort, we could all potentially live an "artistic life", dedicating our time to improving practices we enjoy.

You could argue that many people do not know how or want to focus more on processes rather than outcomes. They continue to define themselves by their achievements (in terms of outcomes) rather than their activities (in terms of playful and leisurely practices). The fact that our culture hasn't fostered this shift in mentality is the real issue, not the threat posed by machines."
(Pierre Schindler)

Extracts of Yuval Harari’s book that exemplify our difference of perspective:

“So what will people do? Art is often said to provide us with our ultimate (and uniquely human) sanctuary. In a world where computers have replaced doctors, drivers, teachers and even landlords, would everyone become an artist? Yet it is hard to see why artistic creation would be safe from the algorithms. According to the life sciences, art is not the product of some enchanted spirit or metaphysical soul, but rather of organic algorithms recognizing mathematical patterns. If so, there is no reason why non-organic algorithms couldn’t master it. […]

The coming technological bonanza will probably make it feasible to feed and support people even without any effort from their side. But what will keep them occupied and content? One answer might be drugs and computer games. Unnecessary people might spend increasing amounts of time within 3D virtual-reality worlds that would provide them with far more excitement and emotional engagement than the drab reality outside. Yet such a development would deal a mortal blow to the liberal belief in the sacredness of human life and of human experiences. What’s so sacred about useless bums who pass their days devouring artificial experiences? […]”

E1.6.B.2.6_REF2_PH "THE HIGHEST AND HARDEST TASK OF LIFE MAY BE TO BECOME ENTIRELY OURSELVES — to continually purify and clarify who and what we are, shedding the shoulds of culture, convention, and expectation to discover the innermost musts: those deepest and truest callings of the authentic self, or what we might call soul. And yet the great paradox is that the self is not a fixity but a perpetual fluidity, reshaped by every experience we have: every love and every loss, every person we meet, every place we visit, and every book we read. And so it must be: “A self that goes on changing is a self that goes on living,” Virginia Woolf wrote, for she understood that the finest souls “are always the supplest.”

The exquisite challenge of becoming oneself but remaining supple is at the center of every life, but it is amplified in the creative life — there is no greater tragedy for an artist than to stagnate and stiffen into a fixity, a template of oneself that ceases to create and instead caters to a self-myth. “Are we going to ossify,” the young Emily Dickinson wrote rhetorically to her great love and muse at the outset of an uncommonly creative life, throughout which she refused to ossify, as a person and as an artist."

In Sweat — his magnificent history of exercise as a lens on the body and the soul — Bill Hayes offers an antidote to ossifying rooted in the parallels between creative practice and athletic training.

Drawing an analogy between the science of exercise and his own art — writing — he considers the six principles that sustain long-term personal fitness."
(Maria Popova's review on Bill Hayes' Sweat, 2022 - in The Marginalian)

E1.6.B.2.6_REF2.1_PH BILL HAYES' "SWEAT" OUTLINES SIX KEY TRAINING PRINCIPLES APPLICABLE TO BOTH PHYSICAL AND CREATIVE ENDEAVORS:
"1.
