Hadot doubts the feasibility of this ideal of silent wisdom and suggests that philosophy is a never-ending quest motivated by this ideal, combining critical discourse and personal transformation.
"It is also interesting to note that, in the Conference on Ethics, in addition to the feeling of astonishment at the existence of the world, which he says is his experience par excellence, he alludes to another experience that is also familiar to him, the feeling of absolute security, which can be expressed as follows (although he notes that this expression is logically nonsense): "I have a clear conscience, nothing can happen to me, no matter what", a phrase with a stoic tone, because the person who admits that there is no evil other than moral evil also knows that, if he wants, nothing bad can happen to him. These two feelings, these two experiences of astonishment and security, which cannot be adequately expressed because they are affective experiences, are deeply related in that they both imply a disposition of disinterest and indifference toward the determined objects of the world. If Wittgenstein insists so much on the limits of language, it is because he ultimately wants to offer a glimpse of a state of silent wisdom that would be attained by those who have gone beyond the propositions of the Tractatus. All the discourse of the book will have been rejected like a ladder that has become useless, destroying itself after having fulfilled its therapeutic function (like the philosophical discourse of the skeptics of antiquity, which they considered to be a simple purgative to be eliminated with bad moods), to give way to the silence of a life of wisdom, in which the problem of life will be solved by its own disappearance. "He must overcome these suggestions," says Wittgenstein (6.54), "then he sees the world correctly" (wouldn't this be a recollection of the contemplation of the world, freed from the principle of sufficient reason, that Schopenhauer spoke of?)
Personally, I doubt that this ideal of a life of quiet wisdom is attainable. Wittgenstein's own life shows that he was unable to adhere to it. I would be more inclined to think, as I have said elsewhere, that one should not oppose a philosophy conceived as pure discourse to a wisdom that would be a silent way of life. For wisdom is not a state that would put an end to philosophy, but an unattainable ideal that motivates the philosopher's endless search. Philosophy, therefore, since it is an aspiration to wisdom, must be at once and indissolubly a critical discourse and an exercise in self-transformation".
(Pierre Hadot, Wittgenstein and the Limits of Language, 2004, extract from the preface of 2014)

E1.6.C.1.7.d. QUESTIONS THAT CAN MAKE OR UNMAKE A LIFE
Once we become the "ravine of the world", we come upon
"questions
that can make
or unmake
a life,
questions
that have patiently
waited for you,
questions
that have no right
to go away"

E1.6.C.1.7.d_REF1_PO "SOMETIMES
Sometimes
if you move carefully
through the forest,
breathing
like the ones
in the old stories,
who could cross
a shimmering bed of leaves
without a sound,
you come to a place
whose only task
is to trouble you
with tiny
but frightening requests,
conceived out of nowhere
but in this place
beginning to lead everywhere.
Requests to stop what
you are doing right now,
and
to stop what you
are becoming
while you do it,
questions
that can make
or unmake
a life,
questions
that have patiently
waited for you,
questions
that have no right
to go away."
(David Whyte, Everything Is Waiting for You)

E1.6.C.2. THE RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN ART AND HAPPINESS IS PROFOUND AND MULTIFACETED, offering numerous pathways to joy and fulfillment. Art serves as a powerful medium that enriches our lives by helping us be more present in the world, deepening our understanding of reality, and providing tools to navigate life's challenges.
Through its transformative power, art elevates the human spirit and opens new dimensions of perception and understanding. It acts as a bridge between our inner world and the external reality, offering not just aesthetic pleasure but also spiritual and emotional enrichment. Whether through music's ability to distill and intensify our emotions, or visual art's power to crystallize moments in time, art provides us with unique tools to experience life more fully and find meaning in our existence.
This exploration delves into thirteen distinct ways that art intersects with happiness. Each pathway offers unique insights into how artistic expression and appreciation can contribute to a more fulfilling and joyful life.

1. BEING PRESENT
2. DEVELOPING TACIT UNDERSTANDING WITH REALITY
3. OVERCOMING SUFFERING
4. GIVING US THE COURAGE TO FIGHT
5. REMINDING US OF BALANCE AND GOODNESS
6. FOSTERING SELF-UNDERSTANDING
7. EXPANDING THE BOUNDARIES OF WHO WE ARE BY INVITING THE UNKNOWN
8. MAKING LIFE LIGHTER
9. PRESERVING WHAT TRULY MATTERS
10. RECONCILING US WITH THE WORLD
11. ORGANIZING THE CHAOS
12. EXPLORING MORALITY
13. PUTING US ON A SPIRITUAL PATH
14. VARIATION ON ALL THAT IS REGULAR AND SYMMETRICAL IN LINES, POINTS, AND RHYTHMS – AND THEIR VIOLATION
15. THE PRIMARY EVOLUTIONARY FUNCTION OF ART IS TO SERVE AS A FITNESS DISPLAY

"TO THE GREAT IN ART.—That enthusiasm for some object which you, O great man, introduce into this world causes the intelligence of the many to be stunted. The knowledge of this fact spells humiliation. But the enthusiast wears his hump with pride and pleasure, and you have the consolation of feeling that you have increased the world's happiness."
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human II, Aphorism 132, 1878)


E1.6.C.2.1. BEING PRESENT
Art leads us to be more present to the world, more intensely alive. Art and music possess profound transformative powers in human experience. Music has the unique ability to distill and intensify our emotions, while art elevates the human spirit to new heights of perception and understanding. As François Cheng and Jacques Darriulat explain, authentic art crystallizes moments in time, challenges our routine existence, and transforms the ordinary into the extraordinary through the creative mind, ultimately serving as a powerful medium for human expression and transcendence.

E1.6.C.2.1.a. MUSIC GENERATES PENETRATING, INTENSE AND TRANSFORMATIVE EMOTIONS
It is the very nature of music to distill our feelings and to offer us the essence of a penetrating and intense experience, expressed in such a way that we can contemplate it at the very moment it carries us away.

E1.6.C.2.1.a_REF1_MU AARON COPLAND DISCUSSES THE UNIQUE AND EPHEMERAL NATURE OF MUSICAL EXPERIENCE, EMPHASIZING THAT MUSIC IS IN A CONSTANT STATE OF BECOMING AND REQUIRES IMAGINATIVE ENGAGEMENT. Listening, an innate talent to some degree, can be developed and refined.
"At no point can you seize the musical experience and hold it. Unlike that moment in a film when a still shot suddenly immobilizes a complete scene, a single musical moment immobilized makes audible only one chord, which in itself is comparatively meaningless. This never-ending flow of music forces us to use our imaginations, for music is in a continual state of becoming. [...]
Listening is a talent, and like any other talent or gift, we possess it in varying degrees… There are two principal requirements for talented listening: first, the ability to open oneself up to musical experience; and secondly, the ability to evaluate critically that experience. Neither of these is possible without a certain native gift. Listening implies an inborn talent of some degree, which, again, like any other talent, can be trained and developed. This talent has a certain « purity » about it. We exercise it, so to speak, for ourselves alone; there is nothing to be gained from it in a material sense. Listening is its own reward; there are no prizes to be won, no contests of creative listening. But I hold that person fortunate who has the gift, for there are few pleasures in art greater than the secure sense that one can recognize beauty when one comes upon it… Recognizing the beautiful in an abstract art like music partakes somewhat of a minor miracle. [...]
There is something about music that keeps its distance even at the moment that it engulfs us. It is at the same time outside and away from us and inside and part of us. In one sense it dwarfs us, and in another we master it. We are led on and on, and yet in some strange way we never lose control. It is the very nature of music to give us the distillation of sentiments, the essence of experience transfused and heightened and expressed in such fashion that we may contemplate it at the same instant that we are swayed by it."
(Aaron Copland, Music and Imagination, 1952)

E1.6.C.2.1.b. ART ELEVATES, TRANSFORMS AND CHALLENGES US
"Authentic art is in itself a conquest of the spirit. It elevates humanity to the level of the Creator, carving out a spark of emotion and memorable enjoyment from destiny's darkness. It also provides a glimmer of passion and compassion that can be shared. Through its ever-evolving forms, it promotes the idea of an "open life" by breaking down the barriers of routine and introducing a new way of perceiving and living.
Art is always a crystallization of a "here and now" moment, marking the emergence of a presence within time. It revives the grand rhythm [of the cosmos] through its refined forms, making it a supreme tool for humans to defy destiny and death."

E1.6.C.2.1.b_REF1_PH "AUTHENTIC ART IS IN ITSELF A CONQUEST OF THE SPIRIT. It elevates humanity to the level of the Creator, carving out a spark of emotion and memorable enjoyment from destiny's darkness. It also provides a glimmer of passion and compassion that can be shared. Through its ever-evolving forms, it promotes the idea of an "open life" by breaking down the barriers of routine and introducing a new way of perceiving and living.
[...] Art is always a crystallization of a "here and now" moment, marking the emergence of a presence within time. It revives the grand rhythm [of the cosmos] through its refined forms, making it a supreme tool for humans to defy destiny and death. This does not diminish the value of other types of activities and commitments. Rather, art justifies its existence through its own being, or "the thing in itself". It provides humans with a reason for their earthly existence."
(François Cheng, Five Meditations on Beauty, 2006)

E1.6.C.2.1.b_REF2_PO IT’S A REPEATED CRY BY A THOUSAND SENTINELS 
"These curses, these blashphemies, these complaints,
These ecstasies, these cries, these tears, these Te Deum,
Are an echo reiterated by a thousand labyrinthes;
It’s for the mortal hearts a divine opium!

It’s a repeated cry by a thousand sentinels,
An order returned by a thousand megaphones,
It’s a lighthouse illuminating a thousand citadels,
A call to hunters lost in the large wood!

Because it’s truly, Lord, the best testimony
That we could give our dignity
That this ardent sob that rolls from age to age
And comes to die at the edge of your eternity!"
(Charles Baudelaire, The Lighthouses, The flowers of evil, 1857)

E1.6.C.2.1.b_REF3_PO JUST AS BAUDELAIRE SUCCESSFULLY CONVEYED THE WILD AND SENSUAL FORCE OF HIS EXPERIENCE WITH HIS COMPANION'S HAIR, art provides us with examples of presence in the world. These examples can inspire us to enhance our ability to capture the moment.

E1.6.C.2.1.b_REF3.1_PO "LA CHEVELURE


   O fleece, that foams down unto the shoulders bare!
   O curls, O scents which lovely languidness exhale!
   Delight! to fill this alcove's sombre atmosphere
   With memories, sleeping deep within this tress of hair,
   I'll wave it in the evening breezes like a veil!

   The shores of Africa, and Asia's burning skies,
   A world forgotten, distant, nearly dead and spent,
   Within thy depths, O aromatic forest! lies.
   And like to spirits floating unto melodies,
   Mine own, Belovèd! glides within thy sacred scent.

   There I will hasten, where the trees and humankind
   With languor lull beside the hot and silent sea;
   Strong tresses bear me, be to me the waves and wind!
   Within thy fragrance lies a dazzling dream confined
   Of sails and masts and flames--O lake of ebony!

   A loudly echoing harbour, where my soul may hold
   To quaff, the silver cup of colours, scents and sounds,
   Wherein the vessels glide upon a sea of gold,
   And stretch their mighty arms, the glory to enfold
   Of virgin skies, where never-ending heat abounds.

   I'll plunge my brow, enamoured with voluptuousness
   Within this darkling ocean of infinitude,
   Until my subtle spirit, which thy waves caress,
   Shall find you once again, O fertile weariness;
   Unending lullabye of perfumed lassitude!

   Ye tresses blue--recess of strange and sombre shades,
   Ye make the azure of the starry Realm immense;
   Upon the downy beeches, by your curls' cascades,
   Among your mingling fragrances, my spirit wades
   To cull the musk and cocoa-nut and lotus scents.

   Long--foraye--my hand, within thy heavy mane,
   Shall scatter rubies, pearls, sapphires eternally,
   And thus my soul's desire for thee shall never wane;
   For art not thou the oasis where I dream and drain
   With draughts profound, the golden wine of memory?"
(Charles Baudelaire, The flowers of evil, 1857)

E1.6.C.2.1.c. THE ARTIST GIVES SHAPE TO THE WORLD AND REVEALS THE FULLNESS OF THE MOMENT
The artist gives shape to the world, bringing the phenomenon to a kind of incandescence that reveals the fullness of the moment: "In its energies the objects of the world are made new, alchemized by their passage through the imaginal, musical, world-foraging and word-forging mind."

E1.6.C.2.1.c_REF1_PH "ART IS THE REVEALER OF ETERNITY, AND THE ARTIST MAKES US INTERESTED IN LIFE (the phenomenon recreated by art functions as a lure for desire, and the "veil of Maya" is the adornment of a seductress) through the invention of new values, which transfigure life in our eyes. 
This thesis, of the transfiguration of the world by art, of its re-creation through the invention of new "values", turns the classical maxim on its head: it is not art that imitates nature, it is conversely nature that imitates art. [...] Without art, we would miss the beauty of this world, and life would perish with boredom. In this way, the artist is the true meta-physician, since it is his task to raise the stage of this world to the glory of eternity, to reunite heaven and earth, to rediscover the earthly paradise.
This is the foundation of a theory of style, and even of what Nietzsche calls "the great style", which is that higher activity of the will to power, which brings form out of chaos, which brings value out of the undifferentiated. Art shapes and recreates the world. Style understood in this way is not the expression of subjectivity, the projection of psychology: on the contrary, it is objective rather than subjective, sculpting the shape of the world, making phenomena appear in the scintillation of their presence, in the eternity of the present. The " great style " affirms the value of the fleeting and the contingent transfigured into the eternal (Baudelaire), the rare brilliance of form, and "against the value of that which remains eternally identical (the naiveté of Spinoza, Descartes too), the value of that which is most brief, most perishable, the seductive glitter of gold on the belly of the serpent vita" (posthumous fragment, autumn 1887, OC: XIII, p. 26). In another posthumous fragment, from spring 1888: 14 [61] (OC, XIV, p. 48), entitled "Music and the Great Style", Nietzsche wrote: "The greatness of an artist is not measured by the beautiful feelings he arouses; that is what good women believe [alluding to Wagnerian women, always hysterical according to Nietzsche]. But to the extent that it approaches great style, to the extent that it is capable of great style [...] To master the chaos that one is: to force one's chaos to become form; to become necessity in form: to become logical, simple, unequivocal, mathematical; to become law - that is the great ambition." Great style gives form to the world, and the artist brings the phenomenon to a kind of incandescence that reveals the fullness of the present. Through this formalism, this cult of form that turned him away from Wagner and towards Bizet's Carmen, Nietzsche considerably apollinated the Dionysus of The Birth of Tragedy, and drew closer to certain aesthetic currents of his time, which he was nevertheless unaware of. [...] Art thus plays an active part in the progress of visibility: it discovers new possibilities for us, enriching our vision by bringing to light nuances, contrasts and iridescence that we had previously failed to see. It makes us see. Without the aesthetic stimuli with which painting has enriched our visual field, we might still see the world in black and white: "The rest of us are stuck in the perceptions that our eyes offer us, and our power of visual representation soon reaches its limit, an impenetrable darkness paralyzes us. The artist, on the other hand, finds within himself the means to express and grasp with increasing precision those vague, indeterminate processes by which we perceive a visible world as a whole" (Fiedler, On the Origin of Artistic Activity, ed. Kohn, p. 87). There's nothing unconscious about this work on the visible. On the contrary, according to Fiedler, the artist is in a sense hyperconscious: he elevates sensation to the awareness of his own activity, he perceives actively and no longer passively: "If, for others, the artist seems to carry out his activity in a kind of dream, for him, this activity is a real awakening" (ibid. p. 90). We perceive the visible in a kind of twilight state; the artist calls us to a higher lucidity. [...]"
(Jacques Darriulat, "Become what you are", 2007-08

E1.6.C.2.1.c_REF2_LIT "THE DAILY ROUTINE OF MOST ADULTS IS SO HEAVY AND ARTIFICIAL THAT WE ARE CLOSED OFF TO MUCH OF THE WORLD. We have to do this in order to get our work done. I think one purpose of art is to get us out of those routines. When we hear music or poetry or stories, the world opens up again. We’re drawn in — or out — and the windows of our perception are cleansed, as William Blake said. The same thing can happen when we’re around young children or adults who have unlearned those habits of shutting the world out."
(Jonathan Whyte, Interview with Ursula K. Le Guin, Talking on the Water: Conversations about Nature and Creativity, 2016)

E1.6.C.2.1.c_REF3_PO "GOOD ART IS A TRUING OF VISION, IN THE WAY A SAW IS TRUED IN THE SAW SHOP, TO CUT MORE CLEANLY. It is also a changing of vision. Entering a good poem, a person feels, tastes, hears, thinks, and sees in altered ways. Why ask art into a life at all, if not to be transformed and enlarged by its presence and mysterious means? … And by changing selves, one by one, art changes also the outer world that selves create and share…. Inside the intricate clockworks of language and music, event and life, what allows and invites us to feel and know as we do, and then increase our feeling and knowing? Such a question cannot be answered. “We” are different, from one another and, moment by moment, from even ourselves. “Art,” too, is a word deceptively single of surface. Still, following this question for thirty years has given me pleasure, and some sense of approaching more nearly a destination whose center cannot ever be mapped or reached. [...]

A mysterious quickening inhabits the depths of any good poem—protean, elusive, alive in its own right…. We feel something stir, shiver, swim its way into the world when a good poem opens its eyes. Poetry’s work is not simply the recording of inner or outer perception; it makes by words and music new possibilities of perceiving…. The eyes and ears must learn to abandon the habits of useful serving and take up instead a participatory delight in their own ends. A work of art is not a piece of fruit lifted from a tree branch: it is a ripening collaboration of artist, receiver, and world. […]

All writers recognize this surge of striking; in its energies the objects of the world are made new, alchemized by their passage through the imaginal, musical, world-foraging and word-forging mind.

This altered vision is the secret happiness of poems, of poets. It is as if the poem encounters the world and finds in it a hidden language, a Braille unreadable except when raised by the awakened imaginative mind. [...]


Poetry itself, when allowed to, becomes within us a playable organ of perception, sounding out its own forms of knowledge and forms of discovery. Poems do not simply express. They make, they find, they sound (in both meanings of that word) things undiscoverable by other means. […]

A poem is not the outer event or phenomenon it ostensibly describes, nor is it the feeling or insight it may seem to reveal or evoke. A poem may involve both, but is, more complexly, a living fabrication of new comprehension — “fabrication” meaning, not accidentally, both “lie,” “falsehood,” and, more simply and fundamentally, anything created and made: the bringing of something freshly into being. Fabric, whether of material or mind, is an interwoven invention: some substance — silk or cotton, wool or image — made stronger, larger than itself, by the dual-natured meeting of warp thread and weft thread. A work of art holds our lives as they are known when fully engaged with the multiple, crossing experience-strands of self, language, culture, emotion, senses, and mind. [...]

The desire of monks and mystics is not unlike that of artists: to perceive the extraordinary within the ordinary by changing not the world but the eyes that look… To form the intention of new awareness is already to transform and be transformed. [...]

A poem plucks the interconnection of the experiencing self and all being. In poetry’s words, life calls to life with the same inevitability and gladness that bird calls to bird, whale to whale, frog to frog. Listening across the night or ocean or pond, they recognize one another and are warmed by that knowledge."
(Jane Hirshfield,  Ten Windows: How Great Poems Transform the World, 2015)

E1.6.C.2.1.c_REF4_PO "GARDEN

If the gardener abandoned his task in the middle of it 
and sat down tired in a chair
and spent the whole afternoon
under fat roses that are only roses
that blind us with joy
while the garden
in himself
contorts itself 
sourcing out of himself
the intricate sex of the camellias 
and the death and madness of the lilies 
and the suburban boredom of guavas 
subject to archaical commotions 
he might feel like a poet 
looking at the poem 
that doesn't know how to end."
(Ana Martins Marques, Paper boats, Life under water, 2009

E1.6.C.2.1.c_REF5_CUR TO BREATHE THE SHADE IS TO REVEAL
“Men and trees do not breathe to the same rhythm. On entering Giuseppe Penone's installation, the scent given off by walls covered in bay leaves reminds us of that unconscious bodily necessity. The vegetal enters our lungs, the work literally invading us as we advance towards an illuminated form in which one recognizes, precisely, the human respiratory system. Outside his drawings, the figurative is very rare in Penone's work. Yet the imitation here is in keeping with its context, for it was produced by arranging bay leaves and casting them in bronze. To breathe the shade is to reveal.”
(Curatorship: Centre Pompidou, Paris)

E1.6.C.2.2. DEVELOPING TACIT UNDERSTANDING WITH REALITY
Art at its peak implies a profound connection between humanity and the world, often referred to in Chinese as a "tacit understanding." It goes beyond mere rational knowledge to establish an intimate perception that connects us spiritually with the world. Through the metaphor of landscape, a place can transcend its physical existence to create a resonant connection with the observer. This concept is particularly significant as it offers a counterpoint to the Enlightenment's rational approach, which, while advancing scientific understanding, led to a disconnect from nature and diminished appreciation of emotional and aesthetic experiences.

E1.6.C.2.2.a. TACIT UNDERSTANDING WITH REALITY
François Cheng introduces us to three core concepts of Chinese aesthetics that are used to evaluate works of art. The highest quality a piece must possess is divine resonance. This is a presence that aligns with the divine spirit and enlightenment. The pinnacle of art suggests a deep, soul-to-soul connection between humans and the divine, or the world for those who are non-believers. This concept is referred to as "tacit understanding" in the Chinese language.

E1.6.C.2.2.a_REF1_PH FRANÇOIS CHENG EXPLORES THREE FUNDAMENTAL CONCEPTS OF CHINESE AESTHETICS: YIN-YUN (UNIFYING INTERACTION), QI-YUN (RHYTHMIC BREATH), AND SHEN-YUN (DIVINE RESONANCE). These concepts serve as criteria for evaluating works of art, ranging from the unification of elements to the divine presence felt in the work. He also emphasizes the notion of yi-jing (soul dimension), which evokes a connivance between the human and the divine, and the importance of emptiness in Chinese art as a symbol of expectation and potential.
"For my part, I will retain three notions of Chinese aesthetics that seem fundamental to me. All three relate primarily to visual art, but are also valid for other art forms. They form a three-tiered "system" that can be used as a criterion for evaluating and judging works of art. I will present them in the following organic order: yin-yun, qi-yun and shen-yun.

1. As a basic level, yin-yun, "unifying interaction. [...] Thus, in the brush stroke that realizes yin-yun, the carnal relationship is forged between the feeling body of the artist and the feeling body of the landscape. All in all, yin-yun is indeed this intrinsic quality of a work: a unifying order that emerges from a multi-level interaction between the various elements that make up a material, between matter and spirit, and ultimately between the human subject and the living universe, which is itself a subject.

2. As an intermediate level, qi-yun, "rhythmic breathing. [A rhythm does not unfold in time and space, it is the generator of its space-time. The emergence of a rhythmic space is one with the constitutive transformation of all the elements of a work of art into a moment of form, into a moment of rhythm. This rhythm is not before us; it is not something that can be possessed. We are in the rhythm. Present to it, we discover ourselves present to ourselves. We exist in this opening of being. Rhythm is a form of surprised existence... Rare are the works in which we have reason to be present.

3. Finally, at the highest level, shen-yun, "divine resonance. This term refers to the supreme quality a work must possess: to be of the highest order. It is difficult to grasp because it seems to suggest something abstract and vague. The Chinese are careful not to freeze it in an overly rigid definition, considering the quality it evokes as a state that can be experienced without being able to explain it. [...] The appearance of a presence - not in the figurative or anthropological sense of the word, which can be felt or sensed, but the very presence of the divine spirit. In all its invisible aspects, this presence corresponds to what Chinese theorists call xiang-wai-zhi-xiang, "the image beyond images. It is also close to what Chan spirituality experiences as enlightenment. When we stand in front of a natural scene, a tree in bloom, a bird flying away with a cry, a ray of sunlight or moonlight illuminating a moment of silence, we suddenly go to the other side of the scene, we find ourselves beyond the screen of phenomena, and we experience the impression of a presence that is self-evident, It comes to us, whole, undivided, inexplicable and yet undeniable, like a generous gift that makes everything wonderfully present, spreading a light of original color, whispering a song that is native from heart to heart, from soul to soul.

I have just said the word "soul"; it brings to mind the notion of yi-jing, "soul dimension," which we encountered in the second meditation on the rose, and which in Chinese aesthetic thought is somewhat equivalent to shen-yun, "divine resonance. Like shen, yi, "disposition of the heart, of the soul," is what both human beings and the living universe are endowed with. The yi-jing thus suggests a soul to soul understanding between man and the divine, which the Chinese language refers to as mo-qi, "tacit understanding.
An understanding that is never complete: there will always be a hiatus, a suspension, a gap to be filled. The sought-after infinity is in fact an un-infinity. The void that stretches across a scroll of Chinese painting is there to signify this. This breathless emptiness conceals an expectation, a listening, ready to welcome a new arrival, to herald a new understanding. For his part, the artist is always ready to endure pain and suffering, hardship and ruin, to the point of being consumed by the fire of his act, of being drawn into the space of the work. He knows that beauty, more than a given, is the supreme gift of what has been offered. And that for man, more than an achievement, it will always be a challenge, a gamble".
(François Cheng, Five meditations on beauty – Fifth meditation, 2006)

E1.6.C.2.2.b. TACIT UNDERSTANDING BRINGS THE WORLD OUT OF ITS INDIFFERENCE AND CONNECTS IT TO MY VITALITY
"Knowledge is rooted in objectification and abstraction, while connivance stems from  a tacit understanding of the world. Connivance allows us to perceive the world in a more intimate way, uncovering a spiritual dimension and establishing a connection between us and the world. It's a shift from reason, not denying conscious reason, but harmonizing with the resonance and fluctuations of nature.
“In essence, this transformation occurs when a country becomes a landscape. What I grasp in it is no longer alien to me, but communicates, 'speaks to me', 'touches me'. It is not a self-projecting subject that casts itself into the landscape, making it the confidant of its emotion (the common stance of cheap romanticism), but rather the landscape, by standing out, pulls me into its field of tension, resonating with its variation. As Stendhal remarked, the landscape is like a bow "that played on my soul". From beneath the mind asserting itself autonomously (the island subject knowing and wanting), it revives my more original affiliation to the world, a world that has risen from its indifference and into which my vitality plugs in.”

E1.6.C.2.2.b_REF1_PH FRANÇOIS JULLIEN EXPLORES THE TRANSFORMATION OF "LAND" INTO "LANDSCAPE" AS A SHIFT FROM OBJECTIVE KNOWLEDGE TO AFFECTIVE AND PERCEPTIVE CONNIVANCE. He emphasizes that this relationship is not a subjective projection, but a resonance with the landscape, which becomes a partner and a vital link, without falling into irrationalism or mysticism. The landscape, by distinguishing itself, reveals our original belonging to the world.
"There is a 'landscape' when my cognitive capacity switches and is reversed into collusion; when the relationship of objectification, and above all of observation, that I maintain with the world is transformed into tacit understanding and communication: from knowing that I was vis-à-vis the 'country' (the knowledge of geography), I become collusive again in a landscape. Not, strictly speaking, that I "personify" elements of the landscape, or that I "project" myself into them, or that I lend my subjectivity to things, or that I animate the inanimate, as has been said and rehashed, as if to compensate for the rationalism of knowledge, the ordinary romanticism, the chatty romanticism, in Europe - all operations in which the subject still wants to be the master and which are only devices. But this transformation does take place: when the land becomes landscape, what I perceive in it is no longer foreign to me, but beckons to me, "speaks to me," "touches me," as we say colloquially (but can we ever escape this colloquialism?). That is to say, as the Chinese literary painters have expressed it so beautifully, that the perceptive becomes affective at the same time, or that I then perceive from the inside as from the outside of myself; by which a dimension of infinity or of spirit then emanates from the physicality of things and is released from it, with which my mind "understands" (softly, as the Chinese strategically put it). The country becomes the landscape - do we know, or rather "do we really know", but the Chinese language of thought has proved particularly gifted in expressing this implicitness, when this "corner of the country" thus establishes itself as a partner, the tension that promotes it integrates me and is shared: there is a "landscape", in other words, when a relationship is established (re-established) with this "country" that makes me go back upstream - finds its source on this side of that which is established by knowing reason; when the place suddenly becomes a "link"; and when this "corner of the country" then "becomes the world": when I connect with it as with that which makes the world. [...] 
At the same time, there is no fusional shift, no compensatory slide into the ecstatic or the mystical, as so much bad lyricism in Europe has advocated, I do not then lapse into any irrationalism (this is an upstream of reason and not its polemical refusal), on which the famous "communion" or symbiosis with nature, coming as reparation for knowing reason, at least leaves ambiguity hanging in the air. [...]
Let it not, then, be a subject-self that projects itself into the landscape, making it the confidant of its emotion (which I have just stigmatized as the banal attitude of bad romanticism), but let it be the landscape that, by setting itself apart, draws me into its field of tension, resonates with its variation (the landscape is like a bow "that has played on my soul," said Stendhal), brings back - from beneath the mind that asserts itself as autonomous (the insular subject that knows and wants) - my more original belonging to the world: a world that has emerged from its indifference and to which my vitality is bound."
(François Jullien, Chinese thought vis-à-vis philosophy, 2015)

E1.6.C.2.2.b_REF2_PH FRANÇOIS JULLIEN EXPLORES THE ALTERNATION BETWEEN TWO REGIMES OF THINKING "To tell the truth, we live in the alternation between these two registers, or rather these two regimes [...] I press more on one key or the other of my inner keyboard; I constantly move the cursor between one pole and the other. For we live by moving discreetly from one to the other, and in this agility, by advancing (asserting) the knowing, or by retreating, conniving, regressing from our autarkic self. Now, the ideal for the knowing subject is modeling. But how can we go one step further and name the other side of this, the side that has remained in the shadows, which means that the capacity is acquired and grows, but silently, without even paying attention to this continuous process, until we finally see the result?
(François Jullien, Chinese thought vis-à-vis philosophy, 2015)

E1.6.C.2.2.b_REF3_TH IN [GREAT WORKS OF ART] A FRAGMENT OF THE UNITARY REALITY IS APPREHENDED — A DEEPER, MORE PRIMORDIAL, AND AT THE SAME TIME MORE COMPLETE REALITY that we are fundamentally unable to grasp with our differentiated conscious functions, because their development is oriented toward a sharper perception of sections of polarized reality.

“Although the creative process, in all its gripping possession, feels so profoundly personal, in its highest form it is inseparable from the universal, from the immensity of the one reality we share, the one experience we share — the fundamental unity which sparked quantum pioneer Erwin Schrödinger’s koan-like pronouncement that “the over-all number of minds is just one.” We habitually lose sight of that oneness as the mind splits into consciousness and the unconscious. Creativity is what we call the process of integrating the two so that we may feel more fully and see more deeply into the nature of reality.”

(Maria Popova review´s of Erich Neumann’s Art and the Creative Unconscious, 1960 - in The Marginalian)

“When we consider the totality of the human psyche, in which consciousness and the unconscious are interdependent both in their development and in their functions, we see that consciousness can develop only where it preserves a living bond with the creative powers of the unconscious… It must not be forgotten that the outside world that we apprehend with our differentiated consciousness is only a segment of reality, and that our consciousness has developed and differentiated itself as a specialized organ for apprehending this particular segment of reality… We pay a heavy price for the sharpness of our conscious knowledge, which is based on the separation of the psychic systems and which breaks down the one world into the polarity of psyche and world. This price is a drastic curtailment of the reality that we experience. […]

In [great works of art] a fragment of the unitary reality is apprehended — a deeper, more primordial, and at the same time more complete reality that we are fundamentally unable to grasp with our differentiated conscious functions, because their development is oriented toward a sharper perception of sections of polarized reality. In the differentiation of consciousness we seem to be doing the same thing as when we close our eyes in order to enhance our hearing, in order that we may be “all ears.” Unquestionably this exclusion sharpens and intensifies our hearing. But in thus excluding the other senses we perceive only a segment of the total sensory reality, which we experience more adequately and fully if we not only hear it but also see, smell, taste, and touch it. […]

In the rapture and beauty of the creative moment… consciousness and the unconscious momentarily become a creative unity and a third term, a part of the one reality. […]

Always and everywhere [the creative person] is driven to rediscover, to reawaken, to give form to this world. But he does not find this world as though seeking something outside him; rather, he knows that this encounter with full reality, the one world, in which everything is still “whole,” is bound up with his own transformation toward wholeness. For this reason he must, in every situation, in every constellation, refresh the openness into which alone the open world can enter.”
(Erich Neumann,  Art and the Creative Unconscious, 1960)

Read it in The Marginalian: 
https://www.themarginalian.org/2025/05/02/erich-neumann-art-unconscious/?mc_cid=d302573a35&mc_eid=0b6483decb

E1.6.C.2.2.b_REF4_CUR ILLUSTRATION OF THE EVER-CHANGING NATURE OF REALITY
“Early in his career, Arp criticized classical art, writing, "The illusionist sculptures of the Greeks ... led man to overestimate his nature, to separation and discord." Nonetheless, he was also fascinated by other aspects of antiquity, such as the writings of the pre-Socratic philosopher Heraclitus, who insisted on the ever-changing nature of reality. In the 1950s, Arp's trips to Greece and the eastern Mediterranean further stimulated his interest. One of his most elegant and popular-late works, Classical Sculpture appears misleadingly conventional. Assuming the form of a standing, probably female, figure, it is irresolvably ambiguous in execution: moving slowly around the sculpture reveals shifting contours that make it difficult to ascertain the front and profile aspects of the figure.”
(Curatorship: Peggy Guggenheim Collection, Venice)

E1.6.C.2.2.b_REF5_CUR NEW PERCEPTUAL APPROACHES TO REALITY.
“The New Realists have become aware of their collective uniqueness. New realism = new perceptual approaches to reality.”
“Les Nouveaus réalistes ont rpis cnscience de leur singularité collective. Nouveau réalisme = nouvelles approches perceptives du réel.”

E1.6.C.2.2.c. EITHER JOY IS REASONABLE - BUT IT'S NOT JOY - OR IT ISN'T, AND IS THEREFORE PARADOXICAL, BUT TRUE. 
"If I have reasons to be happy and I don't want to see the tragedy of existence, my joy is illusory, based as it is on an incomplete representation of a world reduced to its good moments. It's a fleeting joy that lacks substance and depth."
(Clément Rosset in the program À Voie Nue broadcast on France Culture on June, 1st 2018)

E1.6.C.2.2.c_REF1_PH CLÉMENT ROSSET EXPLORES THE JOYFUL NATURE OF MUSIC, DEFINING IT AS A REVELATION OF REALITY WITHOUT WORDS AND UNCONDITIONAL JOY. Unlike illusory joy, which is based on an incomplete vision of reality, true joy is paradoxical and profound. Music replaces traditional philosophical foundations as the primary revelation of the meaning and cause of existence.
"Clément Rosset - Music and Joy Music, expressing only itself, reveals the wondrous fact of existence - and is therefore jubilant. It is reality without words, joy without cause, reality revealed. 
If I have reason to be happy and do not want to see the tragedy of existence, my joy is illusory because it is based on the incomplete representation of a world reduced to its good moments. It is a fleeting joy that lacks substance and depth. Either joy is reasonable - but it is not joy - or it is not reasonable and therefore paradoxical, but true. True joy is a mad joy...in spite of everything. There is a tradition of Christian mysticism that is consistent with this vision.
Joy is amazement (not acceptance, not affirmation, if by that we mean a justified or intellectualistic affirmation) at the fullness of reality - the rejection of the double. The fabrication of the double has two main motivations: the refusal of reality (anything but this!) or the expression of a lack - the inability to feel the fullness of reality.
For Schopenhauer, aesthetic experience and music are comforting. For Rosset, joy is no consolation. Music does not console, it allows the causeless blossoming of joy. If, for Nietzsche, "life without music would be a mistake," it is because he believes that the conditions for the absolute affirmation of the Spinozist type are fulfilled by the immediate and absolute experience that music provides.
In the drunken song in the penultimate chapter of Zarathustra, Nietzsche also tells us: "I have discovered the truth of truths: joy is deeper than sorrow. It is the virtuous circle of joy that can change from passive to active in Spinoza.
In La force majeure, Clément Rosset writes: "Music is what occupies all the nerve centers of the 'philosophy of Nietzsche,' for whom it replaces exactly everything that, in this or that system, is called upon to act as a principle or a foundation: it is what answers all questions and thus takes the place of theology, metaphysics, and physics. It is the primary revelation that provides once and for all sufficient information about the meaning, cause and purpose of all existence."
(Clément Rosset on the program À Voie Nue broadcast on France Culture on June 1, 2018)

Listen to the podcast in French: https://www.radiofrance.fr/franceculture/podcasts/a-voix-nue/clement-rosset-autant-etre-heureux-et-ne-pas-se-tourmenter-puisque-le-pire-est-certain-9752496

E1.6.C.2.2.d. THE CONCEPT OF OURSELVES AS A BRIDGE TO NATURE HAS BEEN FORGOTTEN IN RECENT TIMES
The Enlightenment era introduced a fresh, rational perspective in comprehending the world, but it also led to a growing disconnect from nature and diminished the importance of emotions and beauty. Schelling's philosophy of unity brought together what the scientific revolution had separated: nature and humankind. His philosophy became the driving force of Romanticism. Art was perceived as the manifestation of the bond between the individual and nature and was deemed essential for understanding the world. Nevertheless, the concept of ourselves as a bridge to nature has been forgotten in recent times.

E1.6.C.2.2.d_REF1_PH THE ENLIGHTENMENT DISTANCED HUMANITY FROM NATURE BY FOCUSING ON RATIONALITY AND OBJECTIVITY, BUT SCHELLING'S PHILOSOPHY REUNITED NATURE AND HUMANKIND, emphasizing the emotional and visceral connection to nature. His ideas became central to Romanticism, asserting that art, as a union of the self and nature, is essential for understanding the world.
"The Enlightenment had truly enlightened. But this new rational approach had also created a certain distancing from nature and excluded the roles of feeling and beauty. Nature had become something that was investigated from a so-called objective perspective. Light, for example, was no longer appreciated for its kaleidoscopic play of iridescent colours, Novalis said, but for its refraction and “mathematical obedience”: hence its elevation to the term “Enlightenment” itself. This was why Schelling’s students fell for their young professor. He reunited what the scientific revolution had separated: nature and humankind. No matter how much scientists observed, calculated and experimented, there was something emotional, something visceral and perhaps inexplicable about humanity’s connection to nature. However we feel it, nature can soothe, heal or simply fill us with joy. Schelling gave us the philosophical explanation.
And by doing so, his philosophy of oneness became the heartbeat of Romanticism.  [...]

An artwork — a painting, a sculpture, a poem — was therefore the expression of the union between the self and nature. Whatever an artist produced was created by nature through him or her. Nature — the unconscious product of the self — and the conscious self came together in the artistic creation. Art was therefore essential in order to make sense of the world, Schelling declared. Neither rational thought nor the most accurate scientific instruments held the key to understanding the world. Art was the finite or concrete representation of the infinite. Art opened “the holiest of holies,” Schelling wrote. It was the revelation of the universe through the creative production of an artist."
(Andrea Wulf, Magnificent Rebels: The First Romantics and the Invention of the Self, 2022)

E1.6.C.2.2.d_REF1.1_PH MARIA POPOVA DISCUSSES HOW THE ORIGINAL PHILOSOPHY OF ROMANTICISM, INTENDED TO CONNECT INDIVIDUALS TO EACH OTHER AND NATURE, HAS MUTATED INTO A SELF-CENTERED IDEOLOGY. She argues that reclaiming the original intent can transform the self into a source of creativity and connection.
"But all great ideas, if followed not critically but cultishly, run the risk of metastasizing into dogmas. Today, we are living with one such metastasis of Romanticism in our staggering epidemic of selfing — rather than connecting us to each other and the living world as kindred elements in a system, the inflamed I has folded us unto ourselves: living proteins of ego. It is by returning to the original philosophy, before its mutation, that we stand a chance of reclaiming the self as a crucible of creativity and a portal of connection to nature."
(Maria Popova, The Marginalian, Review on Andrea Wulf's Magnificent Rebels: The First Romantics and the Invention of the Self, 2022)

E1.6.C.2.2.d_REF1.2_PO “ONE MORE CONTRADICTION
Did I fulfill what I had to, here, on earth?
I was a guest in a house under white clouds
Where rivers flow and grasses renew themselves.
So what if I were called, if I was hardly aware.
The next time early I would search for wisdom.
I would not pretend I could be just like the others:
Only evil and suffering come from that.
Renouncing, I would choose the fate of obedience.
I would suppress my wolf's eye and greedy throat.
A resident of some cloister floating in the air
With a view on the cities glowing below,
Or onto a stream, a bridge and old cedars,
I would give myself to one task only.
Which then, however, could not be accomplished”
(Czeslaw Milosz)

E1.7.A.3.1.wE1.6.C.2.2. POETRY: AN ART THAT CONVEYS A SENSE OF THE UNIVERSE.
Paul Valéry characterizes poetry and poetic emotion as an emerging perception of a world or comprehensive system of relationships. In this system, beings, things, events, and actions are indistinctly but accurately linked to the modes and laws of our general sensibility. To preserve this emotion, humans have devised methods to capture and replicate it through various arts, including music, painting, and architecture.

E1.6.C.2.3. OVERCOMING SUFFERING
Art helps the artist sublimate his pain; by hi sexample, he helps us to remember the rightful place of suffering in a good life. It is not that artists suffer more than others, or that one must suffer to be an artist, but the artist has a way of sublimating pain that others do not.

E1.6.C.2.3.a. ONE OF THE UNEXPECTEDLY IMPORTANT THINGS THAT ART CAN DO FOR US IS TEACH US HOW TO SUFFER MORE SUCCESSFULLY.
We can see a great deal of artistic achievement as “sublimated" sorrow on the part of the artist, and in turn, in its reception, on the part of the audience. 

E1.6.C.2.3.a_REF1_PH ART HELPS US BECOME MORE WHOLE BY EXPANDING OUR CAPACITY FOR POSITIVE EMOTIONS AND HELPING US PROCESS NEGATIVE ONES WITH DIGNITY. "Since we’re creatures of infinite inner contradiction, art can help us be more whole not only by expanding our capacity for positive emotions but also by helping us to fully inhabit and metabolize the negative — and by doing so with dignity and by reminding us « of the legitimate place of sorrow in a good life » :
« One of the unexpectedly important things that art can do for us is teach us how to suffer more successfully. … We can see a great deal of artistic achievement as “sublimated" sorrow on the part of the artist, and in turn, in its reception, on the part of the audience. The term sublimation derives from chemistry. It names the process by which a solid substance is directly transformed into a gas, without first becoming liquid. In art, sublimation refers to the psychological processes of transformation, in which base and unimpressive experiences are converted into something noble and fine — exactly what may happen when sorrow meets art [...]
Art can offer a grand and serious vantage point from which to survey the travails of our condition. »"
(Maria Popova´s review of Alain de Botton & John Armstrong's Art as Therapy)

E1.6.C.2.3.a_REF2_LIT “THE STUBBORN ART OF TURNING SUFFERING INTO STRENGTH: VÁCLAV HAVEL’S EXTRAORDINARY LETTERS FROM PRISON
I have got to make everything that has happened to me good for me,” Oscar Wilde wrote from prison. “There is not a single degradation of the body which I must not try and make into a spiritualising of the soul.”  

The cruel kindness of life is that our sturdiest fulcrum of transformation is the devastation of our hopes and wishes — the losses, the heartbreaks, the diagnoses that shatter the template of the self, leaving us to reconstitute a new way of being from the rubble. In those moments, brutal and inevitable, we come to realize that no prayer or protest will bend reality to our will, that we are being bent to it instead and we have two options only: bow or break. Suffering, surrender, transformation — this may be the simplest formulation of the life process. It is the evolutionary mechanism of adaptation by which every creature on Earth became what it is. It is existential mechanism by which we become who we are. In a universe where free will may well be an illusion, what we make of our suffering may be the measure and meaning of our freedom. “Everything can be taken from a man,” Viktor Frankl wrote in his epochal memoir of surviving the unsurvivable, “but one thing: the last of the human freedoms — to choose one’s attitude in any given set of circumstances, to choose one’s own way.”  

Just before dawn on May 29, 1979, the Czechoslovakian State Security Police barged into the home of the playwright, essayist, and poet Václav Havel (October 5, 1936–December 18, 2011), dragged him out of bed, and threw him in a municipal jail along with ten other members of the Committee to Defend the Unjustly Prosecuted — a human rights movement formed to bring to light cases of people harassed and imprisoned for speaking up against the dictatorship.  

Havel was not surprised. A decade earlier, he had discovered a listening device in the ceiling of his Prague apartment. He had been trailed by the secret police ever since. He had watched his books removed from schools and public libraries, his plays banned from the stage.  

“It’s Tuesday evening and I’ve just returned from court a sentenced man,” he wrote to his wife Olga when he was found guilty on charges of “subversion” a month after his forty-third birthday. “I’m taking my sentence, as they say, philosophically.” The philosophy he drew from the experience would lead him to write the finest thing I have ever read about the meaning of hope.

Havel was sent to a prison ruled by a sadistic admirer of Hitler who in his heyday had presided over a Stalinist prison camp. Now, all the more embittered by the knowledge that he was nearing the end of his career, the warden spent his days tormenting his captives in body and in mind. The prisoners, whose days were filled with hard labor, were allowed to write to just one person, a single four-page letter a week. Havel chose Olga — “a working-class girl, very much her own person, sober, unsentimental,” who had always been the first reader of all his work and his “main authority when it comes to judging it.” Looking back on his life, he would recall: “I needed an energetic woman beside me to turn to for advice and yet still be someone I could be in awe of.” 

The letters had to be legible, with nothing corrected or crossed out. Quotation marks, foreign expressions, underlining, and humor were forbidden. Those deemed to contain too many “thoughts” were confiscated. Once, Havel was thrown into solitary confinement after it was discovered that he had been writing on behalf of an illiterate Roma man, just as Whitman had done for illiterate Civil War soldiers an epoch ago and a world away.  

Still, as if to remind us that constraint is a catalyst of creativity, Havel managed to contraband a wealth of “thoughts” in these spare dispatches to his wife. They survive as Letters to Olga — the extraordinary record of the philosophy he drew from his plight, out of which arises a lucid and luminous field guide to suffering as an instrument of self-refinement, an ode to the refusal of having one’s spirit broken by any depredation of the body or the mind, and a stubborn insistence on kindness as the only lifeline amid cruelty.

Days after his sentence, with an eye to the five years ahead — an unimaginable time horizon of freedom — Havel outlines his spiritual strategy for survival:  

I find myself in a radically new existential situation, and the first thing I have to do is learn to live with it, which means finding a completely new structure of values and a new perspective on everything — other hopes, other aims, other interests, other joys. I have to create a new concept of time for myself and ultimately a new concept of life.  

But, in consonance with the visionary Elizabeth Peabody’s admonition that the greatest danger to the gifted is middle age, “when a false wisdom tempts them to doubt the divine origin of the dreams of their youth,” Havel realizes that this new concept is in fact a return to a prior purity occluded by the self we ossify into when we begin believing our own myth, which may be the greatest danger to the artist. (The recovery of that deeper purity is what Hermann Hesse meant when he contemplated discovering the soul beneath the self.) Suddenly horrified by the way we have of caving in on ourselves by becoming our own favorite subject, Havel tells Olga:  

Learning to live with this new situation and one of the tasks I’ve set myself during this long stay in prison will be a kind of “self-consolidation.” When I began to write plays, I wasn’t as inwardly burdened as I have been in recent years; I had… far more equanimity; I saw most things in proportion; I had a balanced outlook and a sense of humor, without a trace of uptightness, hysteria, bitterness. The positions I took were not absolute; I wasn’t constantly brooding over myself, absorbed in my own feelings, etc. — and at the same time I possessed a kind of harmonious inner certitude. Obsessive critical introspection is the other side of “pigheadedness.”  

With an eye to the fault lines that often become frontiers of growth, he adds:  

Jail, of all places, may seem to you a strange instrument of this self-reconstitution, but I truly feel that when I’m cut off from all my former commitments for so long, I might somehow achieve inner freedom and a new mastery over myself. I don’t intend to revise my view of the world, of course, but rather to find a better way of fulfilling the demands that the world — as I see it — places on me. I don’t want to change myself, but to be myself in a better way… It also seems to me that the only way for someone like me to survive here is to breathe his own meaning into the experience.  

Prison calibrated his metric for what constitutes a good or bad day. A hot bath, a healthy meal, and “a marvelous session of yoga” left him gladdened to the bone. Of the bad days he could say little — no record survives of the abuses he endured — other than reporting on the “sheer agony” of his hemorrhoids. (“It’s worse here than it would be outside… You’re alone with your pain and you have to go through with it.”) He decided that, “theoretically,” nothing could stop him from writing a new play while in prison. (He did.) He decided that, practically, he could use the time to improve his English and learn German. In one of his provision lists to Olga, in between a hard case for his glasses, a pocket calendar, warm socks, and “a lot of vitamins,” he requested the German-Czech dictionary from their home and a language textbook. And then he itemized his resolutions for serving his sentence:  

to remain at least as healthy as I am now (and perhaps cure my hemorrhoids); 
generally reconstitute myself psychologically; 
write at least two play; 
improve my English; 
learn German at least as well as I know English; 
study the entire Bible thoroughly. 

Three years into his imprisonment, the state police visited Havel and told him he could be home within the week if only he would write a single sentence renouncing his views and asking for pardon. Unlike Galileo, he refused. Four months later, Havel fell ill with a fever so high that he feared he would not live. So did the wardens, who threw him in the back of a police van and drove him fifty miles to a prison hospital in Prague as he shivered with the delirium of death handcuffed in his pajamas.  

When he slowly returned to the land of the living, Havel gambled that the hospital censors might be less severe than the prison’s and composed the first detailed letter to Olga describing his struggle. It made it. An epoch before social media, she immediately reached out to his friends aboard. Petitions on his behalf began pouring in from all over the world through this borderless network of solidarity.  

One evening after he was sent back to prison, as he was about to go to sleep, several guards suddenly barged into his cell, along with a doctor and “a woman official of some kind.” They informed Havel that his sentence was terminated. He was so astonished that, in a literal embodiment of Dorris Lessing’s metaphor of the prisons we choose to live inside, he asked to spend one more night in his cell. They refused — he was now a civilian. He was taken out in his pajamas.

When Havel reentered the real world, he devoted himself to eradicating the tyrannical impulse that makes dictatorships and their systemic attack on the dignity of human beings possible. Six years after his release from prison, he was unanimously elected president of Czechoslovakia by the Federal Assembly. The following year, when the country held its first free election in nearly half a century, the was re-elected by the people. As tensions between Czechs and Slovaks rose in the 1990s, he governed a divided nation by the personal credo he had articulated in one of his prison letters to Olga — a sentiment as true of physical imprisonment as of the prisons of the mind we enter whenever we succumb to divisive ideologies or take a victim stance toward our suffering:  

I’ve discovered that in lengthy prison terms, sensitive people are in danger of becoming embittered, developing grudges against the world, growing dull, indifferent and selfish. One of my main aims is not to yield an inch to such threats, regardless of how long I’m here. I want to remain open to the world, not to shut myself up against it; I want to retain my interest in other people and my love for them. I have different opinions of different people, but I cannot say that I hate anyone in the world. I have no intention of changing in that regard. If I did, it would mean I had lost.”
(Maria Popova, The Marginalian)

Read it in the Marginalian: https://www.themarginalian.org/2025/02/06/vaclav-havel-letters-to-olga/?mc_cid=2afb17e3f9&mc_eid=0b6483decb

E1.6.C.2.3.b. SCHOPENHAUER BELIEVED THAT ART ALLOWS US TO TRANSCEND THE SUFFERING OF LIFE AND DEATH BY CONTEMPLATING IT AS A SPECTACLE.
Without the intensity of love and the preoccupation with death, would we truly comprehend what it means to be alive? Without these emotions, wouldn't we be stuck in the monotony of daily routine? As discussed in the reflections on beauty, isn't our capacity to find joy in beauty, or even our ability to recognize it, partly influenced by our level of comfort with death?

E1.6.C.2.3.b_REF1_PH JACQUES DARRIULAT DISCUSSES HOW PLATO AND SCHOPENHAUER DEAL WITH THE FUNDAMENTAL EXPERIENCES OF LOVE AND DEATH. The idea of death, Darriulat argues, is essential to understanding life as an incomprehensible miracle and to living fully as a human being.
"Timothée Coyras]: For Plato, there are two fundamental experiences, love and death, which are perhaps experiences of an aesthetic nature...
[Jacques Darriulat]: The violence of love and the anxiety of death are the two demons that pull us by the hair and tear us out of the hypnosis of the cave. Without this double call, without this double sign that reminds them of the mystery, men would live without thinking about what it means to live and what it means to be a man.
[Jacques Darriulat]: Exactly, for Schopenhauer, the will as representation is relieved of the weight of death. The will begins to move toward its extinction, it calms down, because in contemplation it becomes disinterested. Death, or more precisely the suffering of the living, the agony of torment, becomes a spectacle in the work. The violin cries in my place, the symphony dies in my place, and like a spectator-god I contemplate this extinction. It is not I who die, it is the musical phrase that dissolves into silence; it is not I who love, it is the fortissimo that expresses the fury of passion. I linger in contemplation, I transport myself beyond love and life. [...]

To return to Plato, I think only when violence is done to me and I am confronted with the unthinkable. The unthinkable is the infinity of desire and the infinity of silence, the mystery of death. The thought of death must remain in this questioning. There is no proof of immortality in the Phaedo; to assume the immortality of the soul is a "beautiful risk," a gamble, and the prize is never certain. Death is the riddle that calls us to think; without the thought of death, we would go round in circles in the cage of routine, and life would never appear to us for what it is: an incomprehensible miracle. There is this horizon of silence, like the empty sea in front of the beach, and we must be careful not to forget it if we want to live like men and not like animals. The prisoner, overwhelmed by the flood of sensations, falls into the hypnosis of presence; the thinker contemplates absence, he remembers those who are absent".
(Jacques Darriulat, Interview with Nicolas Rousseau and Timothée Coyras, part two, 2010)

E1.6.C.2.3.b_REF1.1_CUR "CANOVA CREATED A MASTERPIECE OF NEOCLASIC SCULPTURE IN CARRARA MARBLE FOR HIS MAUSOLEUM, which is unique in this church. The monument is almost five meters high, but it's embedded in the southern wall of the nave, so you don't notice it when you enter. The perfect, pure lines of the pyramid are as striking as the bottomless central opening that leads to the world of the dead. The life-size funeral procession, which unfolds on an extraordinary marble carpet, offers the spectator a scene that is both tragic and serene. Each of the five figures, except the old man, supported by an allegory of charity, has his head bowed. A dynamic present in the movement of their draped garments, the positions of their bodies and the garlands of flowers makes them a homogeneous and fluid group. Virtue, accompanied by two girls, carries an urn. This is symbolic because this monument is a cenotaph. Marie-Christine was buried, like the entire imperial family, in the Capuchin crypt, which I recommend you visit. The only element that recalls the archduchess is the bas-relief medallion with her portrait at the top of the pyramid, supported by a magnificent duo in high relief. Opposite the procession are a genius and a lion, both mourning the death of Marie-Christine. The movement of this group, marked in the marble by the shield and the inclination of the genius's limbs, contrasts with that of the procession. Together they reinforce the lines of the pyramid and draw our attention to the inscription in golden letters on the door: Uxori optimae Albertus (To the Best Wife, Albert). It was indeed Albert who commissioned Canova to create this sublime monument. A simple and affectionate message.
The sculptor went to Vienna in 1805 to assemble the various sculptural elements produced in his workshop. Interestingly, he used a very similar design for his own tomb, which can be admired in the church of Santa Maria Gloria dei Frari in Venice."

E1.6.C.1.6.wE1.6.C.2.3.b. THE TRANSIENCE OF BEAUTY: A REFLECTION ON MORTALITY
Could it be the fleeting nature of many beautiful things that lends them a hint of impending disappearance as soon as they appear? Our comfort with the concept of death might then reflect our ability to find joy in beauty, or even our ability to recognize it. Immersion in nature can evoke a sense of freedom and a heightened awareness of being alive, while simultaneously reminding us of our finitude. Ultimately, it's a complex yet genuine joy.

E1.6.C.2.3.c. IS PAIN A NECESSARY ELEMENT FOR CREATIVITY?
Many great artists have a conflicted sense of self and strive for uniqueness, even against societal norms. They use art as a medium to connect with others and validate their distinctiveness. So, is pain a prerequisite for creativity? What's certain is that the creative process can be tormenting. As a songwriter, Cave's perceive his role as helping people overcome their grief through music. He posits that songwriting involves remaining conscious of one's own wounds without indulging them, pursuing joy, venturing past convention, and accepting that our most cherished beliefs lie beyond our existing knowledge.

E1.6.C.2.3.c_REF1_PH "DO YOU NEED TO BE HURT OR MENTALLY ILL TO BE A GREAT ARTIST?
LATABAN, ENSENADA, MEXICO

What makes a great song?
MILA, AMSTERDAM, HOLLAND

Dear Lataban and Mila,

It seems to me that many of the great artists, especially in music, appear to possess a wildly conflicted sense of themselves, yet persistently pursue their own uniqueness, even when it runs at odds with the orthodoxies of the day. They tend to be outliers, abyss-gazers and misfits, whose sense of isolation is further amplified by their notoriety, and who use their art as a way of connecting to people and legitimising their own strangeness. They understand, on some level, that it is the nature of their vocation to steal across the borders of convention, collect up ideas and bring them back to the world. This requires a certain reckless spirit and it may be that some spend too long on the other side and that it becomes difficult for them to return. However, whether any of this is actual mental illness, I don’t know. This term feels too elastic, too nebulous to comment.

Whether being hurt is a necessary requirement to creativity is only true in so much as to live is partly to suffer. You cannot create without suffering, because you cannot live without suffering. One of the jobs of a songwriter, as I see it, is to develop strategies to help people transcend their sorrows. This is the unique duality of a great song – that it can reflect back at the listener the extent of their own hurt, yet lift them beyond it at the same time. How often have we wept ourselves to transcendence through music?

Part of the process for the songwriter is to stay alert to the nature of their own wounds without indulging them, to gravitate towards happiness when possible, to venture beyond convention and acceptability without losing oneself, to understand that our most treasured beliefs exist on the far side of what we think we know, and to go there cautiously, yet fearlessly and, perhaps most importantly of all, to remember to return.

Love, Nick"
(Nick Cave, The Red Hand Files, ISSUE #81 / JANUARY 2020)

E1.6.C.2.3.c_REF2_PH NICK CAVE EMPHASIZES THAT AUTHENTIC CREATIVE STRUGGLE AND THE REDEMPTIVE ARTISTIC ACT, WHICH RESONATE DEEPLY WITH HUMAN VULNERABILITY AND SUFFERING, are unique to human experience and cannot be genuinely replicated by AI.
"[An] authentic creative struggle that precedes the invention of a unique lyric of actual value; it is the breathless confrontation with one’s vulnerability, one’s perilousness, one’s smallness, pitted against a sense of sudden shocking discovery; it is the redemptive artistic act that stirs the heart of the listener, where the listener recognizes in the inner workings of the song their own blood, their own struggle, their own suffering. This is what we humble humans can offer, that AI can only mimic, the transcendent journey of the artist that forever grapples with his or her own shortcomings. This is where human genius resides, deeply embedded within, yet reaching beyond, those limitations."
(Nick Cave, The Red Hand Files, ISSUE #218 / JANUARY 2023)

E1.6.C.2.3.c_REF3_LIT "YOU MUST NOT FEAR, HOLD BACK, COUNT OR BE A MISER WITH YOUR THOUGHTS AND FEELINGS. It is also true that creation comes from an overflow, so you have to learn to intake, to imbibe, to nourish yourself and not be afraid of fullness. The fullness is like a tidal wave which then carries you, sweeps you into experience and into writing. Permit yourself to flow and overflow, allow for the rise in temperature, all the expansions and intensifications. Something is always born of excess: great art was born of great terrors, great loneliness, great inhibitions, instabilities, and it always balances them."
(Anais Nin, The Diary of Anais Nin, Vol. 4, 1944-47)

E1.6.C.2.3.c_REF4_PH "IN CONTRAST WITH THE LINE OF THE ROMANTICS WHO SAW THE ARTIST CONDEMNED TO ANXIETY AND SENTIMENTALITY BECAUSE OF THE FALL, an the paradise lost, an alternative view of artistic creation is based on a need for internal balance and music in the soul: Sophocles, Aristotle, Da Vinci, Bach, Jane Austin."
(In our Times, BBC4)

Listen to the program: https://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/p005489g

E1.6.B.1.7.wE1.6.C.2.3.c. WHAT ABOUT THE MYTH OF THE UNHAPPY CREATOR?
While artists like Marina Abramovic claim their best work came from unhappiness, research suggests that creative success comes despite mental struggles, not because of them. The connection between creativity and mental health is more nuanced, with evidence of genetic and familial links. Artists like Nick Cave reject the notion that suffering or addiction is necessary for creativity, instead emphasizing that sobriety and mental wellness can lead to profound artistic expression. While artists may have heightened sensitivity to both joy and pain, suffering itself is not a prerequisite for artistic creation, and the idea of suffering as essential to art is particularly tied to modern Western and Christian sensibilities.

E1.6.C.2.3.d. FOR FREUD, SUBLIMATION INVOLVES ENHANCING THE PLEASURE OBTAINED FROM PSYCHIC AND INTELLECTUAL ACTIVITIES. This is done to protect oneself from suffering by shifting the focus of inner pulsions, making one unaffected by the external world's rejection.
However, this method can't entirely shield one from suffering and isn't universally applicable. It presupposes specific gifts and dispositions, which are not common.
Art, conversely, involves gaining satisfaction from illusions acknowledged as such. These illusions exist in areas of imaginary life, serving as a source of pleasure and comfort.

E1.6.C.2.3.d_REF1_TH SIGMUND FREUD EXPLAINS THAT ART AND ILLUSION OFFER A TEMPORARY ESCAPE, BUT ARE NOT SUFFICIENT TO FORGET REAL SUFFERING. The sublimation of drives and the search for psychic and intellectual satisfactions can alleviate suffering, but these methods are not universally accessible and do not completely protect against pain.
"Another technique for fending off suffering is the employment of the displacements of libido which our mental apparatus permits of and through which its function gains so much in flexibility. The task here is that of shifting the instinctual aims in such a way that they cannot come up against frustration from the external world. In this, sublimation of the instincts lends its assistance. One gains the most if one can sufficiently heighten the yield of pleasure from the sources of psychical and intellectual work. When that is so, fate can do little against one. A satisfaction of this kind, such as an artist's joy in creating, in giving his phantasies body, or a scientist's in solving problems or discovering truths, has a special quality which we shall certainly one day be able to characterize in metapsychological terms. At present we can only say figuratively that such satisfactions seem ‘finer and higher’. But their intensity is mild as compared with that derived from the sating of crude and primary instinctual impulses; it does not convulse our physical being. And the weak point of this method is that it is not applicable generally: it is accessible to only a few people. It presupposes the possession of special dispositions and gifts which are far from being common to any practical degree. And even to the few who do possess them, this method cannot give complete protection from suffering. It creates no impenetrable armour against the arrows of fortune, and it habitually fails when the source of suffering is a person's own body.

While this procedure already clearly shows an intention of making oneself independent of the external world by seeking satisfaction in internal, psychical processes, the next procedure brings out those features yet more strongly. In it, the connection with reality is still further loosened; satisfaction is obtained from illusions, which are recognized as such without the discrepancy between them and reality being allowed to interfere with enjoyment. The region from which these illusions arise is the life of the imagination; at the time when the development of the sense of reality took place, this region was expressly exempted from the demands of reality-testing and was set apart for the purpose of fulfilling wishes which were difficult to carry out. At the head of these satisfactions through phantasy stands the enjoyment of works of art—an
enjoyment which, by the agency of the artist, is made accessible even to those who are not themselves creative. People who are receptive to the influence of art cannot set too high a value on it as a source of pleasure and consolation in life. Nevertheless the mild narcosis induced in us by art can do no more than bring about a transient
withdrawal from the pressure of vital needs, and it is not strong enough to make us forget real misery."
(Sigmund Freud, Civilization and its Discontents, 1930)

E1.6.C.2.3.d_REF2_PH "ALL THOSE WHO CREATE A MATERIAL OR ARTISTIC WORK, PUNCTUAL OR SYNTHETIC, KNOW IT: whatever the difficulties, the anxieties or the struggles, the movement of the subject and the awareness that she has of herself in her deepest interiority are a a kind of contentment, a form of joy."
(Robert Misrahi, Acts of joy, 1987)

E1.6.C.2.3.d_REF2.1_PH MARY OLIVER REFLECTS ON THE PERSISTENT CHALLENGES OF SHAME AND GRIEF, LIKENING THEM TO A BURDEN ONE CARRIES. Despite these difficulties, she emphasizes the importance of engaging with the world's admirable energies and finding solace in meaningful work. Through meticulous effort, one can transform chaotic thoughts into something beautiful and enduring, creating a fulfilling life through work and love.
"I don’t mean it’s easy or assured; there are the stubborn stumps of shame, grief that remains unsolvable after all the years, a bag of stones that goes with one wherever one goes and however the hour may call for dancing and for light feet. But there is, also, the summoning world, the admirable energies of the world, better than anger, better than bitterness and, because more interesting, more alleviating. And there is the thing that one does, the needle one plies, the work, and within that work a chance to take thoughts that are hot and formless and to place them slowly and with meticulous effort into some shapely heat-retaining form, even as the gods, or nature, or the soundless wheels of time have made forms all across the soft, curved universe — that is to say, having chosen to claim my life, I have made for myself, out of work and love, a handsome life."
(Mary Oliver, "Staying alive" in Upstream)

E1.6.C.2.3.d_REF2.1.a_PH MARY OLIVER REFLECTS ON THE PASSAGE OF TIME, THE LOSS OF LOVED ONES, AND THE TRANSIENT NATURE OF POSSESSIONS. Despite these changes, she emphasizes the importance of steadfastness in her passions and the autonomy over her own life, ultimately accepting the impermanence of existence with grace.
"And now my old dog is dead, and another I had after him, and my parents are dead, and that first world, that old house, is sold and lost, and the books I gathered there lost, or sold — but more books bought, and in another place, board by board and stone by stone, like a house, a true life built, and all because I was steadfast about one or two things: loving foxes, and poems, the blank piece of paper, and my own energy — and mostly the shimmering shoulders of the world that shrug carelessly over the fate of any individual that they may, the better, keep the Niles and the Amazons flowing. And that I did not give to anyone the responsibility for my life. It is mine. I made it. And can do what I want to with it. Live it. Give it back, someday, without bitterness, to the wild and weedy dunes."
(Mary Oliver, "Staying alive" in Upstream)

E1.6.C.2.3.d_REF3_PH "IT'S A TOUGH ASSIGNMENT!", says Woody Allen.
Woody Allen on The Meaninglessness of Life & The Role of The Artist

Watch the interview on Yotube: https://youtu.be/2MsuqvLIttk

E1.6.C.2.4. GIVING US THE COURAGE TO FIGHT
Artistic beauty instills in us the courage to face reality. The hope it nourishes is not a method to ignore real-life challenges, but a source of strength to surmount them.

E1.6.C.2.4.a. ART CAN ENCOURAGE US TO CHERISH AND CELEBRATE HOPE
Beauty in art could be dismissed as feeding sentimentality and ignoring significant issues, numbing us and leaving us insufficiently critical. However, these concerns are misguided, for optimism and hope are critical skills that are essential for a good life. Art encourages us to cherish and celebrate hope, and to appreciate an ideal image without considering it a false depiction of reality. A beautiful but partial vision can be all the more precious to us because we understand how rarely life meets our desires.
