Here the enjoyment lies in the excitement itself, in the victory over tedium.—The memory, too, of unpleasant things, so far as they have been overcome or make us appear interesting to the listener as subjects for art (as when the singer describes the mishaps of a daring seaman), can inspire great joy, the credit for which is given to art.—A more subtle variety is the joy that arises at the sight of all that is regular and symmetrical in lines, points, and rhythms. For by a certain analogy is awakened the feeling for all that is orderly and regular in life, which one has to thank alone for all well-being. So in the cult of symmetry we unconsciously do homage to rule and proportion as the source of our previous happiness, and the joy in this case is a kind of hymn of thanksgiving. Only when a certain satiety of the last-mentioned joy arises does a more subtle feeling step in, that enjoyment might even lie in a violation of the symmetrical and regular. This feeling, for example, impels us to seek reason in apparent unreason, and the sort of æsthetic riddle-guessing that results is in a way the higher species of the first-named artistic joy.—He who pursues this speculation still further will know what kind of hypotheses for the explanation of æsthetic phenomena are hereby fundamentally rejected."
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human II, Aphorism 119, 1878)

E1.6.C.2.15 KEVIN SIMLER AND ROBIN HANSON ARGUE THAT ART'S PRIMARY EVOLUTIONARY FUNCTION IS TO SERVE AS A FITNESS DISPLAY FOR SEXUAL SELECTION, WHERE ITS VALUE LIES NOT IN ITS INTRINSIC BEAUTY BUT IN THE EXTRINSIC SKILL AND IMPRACTICALITY THAT SIGNAL THE ARTIST'S WORTH, while the act of discernment itself becomes a parallel display for the public.

“ART […]

Art poses a challenge for evolutionary thinkers. It’s a costly behavior, both in time and energy, but at the same time it’s impractical […] In his book The Mating Mind, the evolutionary psychologist Geoffrey Miller gives a promising answer. Miller argues that while ecological selection (the pressure to survive) abhors waste, sexual selection often favors it. The logic […] is that we prefer mates who can afford to waste time, energy, and other resources. What’s valuable isn’t the waste itself, but what the waste says about the survival surplus—health, wealth, energy levels, and so forth—of a potential mate.
To appreciate the power of this idea, let’s turn once again to the nonhuman world. […]

Importantly, human artists don’t need to be conscious of this motive. Humans, as we’ve seen many times throughout the book, are adept at acting on unconscious motives, especially when the motive in question (e.g., showing off) is antisocial and norm-violating. What’s important isn’t whether we’re aware that we’re using art as a fitness display, but rather the fact that art works as a fitness display. It serves a useful and important purpose, both to artists and consumers, so we shouldn’t be surprised to find ourselves endowed with instincts both to make and enjoy art. […]

There’s no doubt that observing art often induces strong feelings that we deeply crave, such as awe and an appreciation of beauty, and that creating art often gives us a strong sense of accomplishment and connection.
The argument we’re making in this chapter is simply that “showing off” is one of the important motives we have for making art, and that many details of our artistic instincts have been shaped substantially by this motive. Not only do artists want to show off, but consumers simultaneously use art as a means to judge the artist. That’s one of the big reasons people appreciate art, and we can’t understand the full range of phenomena unless we’re willing to look at art as a fitness display. […]

Now we’re ready to understand the most important difference between the conventional view of art (in any of its forms: beauty, communication, etc.) and the fitness-display theory. The difference is that the conventional view locates the vast majority of art’s value in its intrinsic properties, along with the experiences that result from perceiving and contemplating those properties. Beauty, for example, is typically understood as an experience that arises from the artwork itself. According to the conventional view, artists use their technical skills and expressive power to create the final physical product, which is then perceived and enjoyed by the consumer. The extrinsic properties, meanwhile, are mostly an aside or an afterthought; in the conventional view, they aren’t crucial to the transaction.
In contrast, in the fitness-display theory, extrinsic properties are crucial to our experience of art. As a fitness display, art is largely a statement about the artist, a proof of his or her virtuosity. And here it’s often the extrinsic properties that make the difference between art that’s impressive, and which therefore succeeds for both artist and consumer, and art that falls flat. If a work of art is physically (intrinsically) beautiful, but was made too easily (like if a painting was copied from a photograph), we’re likely to judge it as much less valuable than a similar work that required greater skill to produce. One study, for example, found that consumers appreciate the same artwork less when they’re told it was made by multiple artists instead of a single artist—because they’re assessing the work by how much effort went into it, rather than simply by the final result. […]

Consider our emphasis on originality in works of art. We prize originality and spurn works that are too derivative, however pleasing they might otherwise be to our senses or intellect. Here again, we betray our concern for using art to evaluate the artist. Insofar as art is a perceptual experience, it shouldn’t matter whether the artist copied another artist in producing the work, but it makes a world of difference in gauging the artist’s skill, effort, and creativity.
“We find attractive,” says Miller, “those things that could have been produced only by people with attractive, high-fitness qualities such as health, energy, endurance, hand–eye coordination, fine motor control, intelligence, creativity, access to rare materials, the ability to learn difficult skills, and lots of free time.”
Artists, in turn, often respond to this incentive by using techniques that are more difficult or demanding, but which don’t improve the intrinsic properties of the final product. “From an evolutionary point of view,” writes Miller, “the fundamental challenge facing artists is to demonstrate their fitness by making something that lower-fitness competitors could not make, thus proving themselves more socially and sexually attractive.” Artists routinely sacrifice expressive power and manufacturing precision in order to make something more “impressive” as a fitness display. […]
As both artists and consumers, we’re often eager to jump in and explore the expressive and aesthetic possibilities of each new medium and manufacturing technique. But just as often, we hold out. Whenever we prefer things made “the old-fashioned way”—handwritten instead of printed, homemade instead of store-bought, live instead of prerecorded—we’re choosing to celebrate the skill and effort of an artist over the intrinsically superior results of a more mechanical process. […]

WHY ART IS IMPRACTICAL […]

The waste is important. It’s only by doing something that serves no concrete survival function that artists are able to advertise their survival surplus. […]
Thus impracticality is a feature of all art forms. But we can see it with special clarity in those art forms that need to distinguish themselves from closely related practical endeavors. Consider the difference between clothing, which is a necessity, and fashion, which is a luxury. Fashion often distinguishes itself from mere clothing by being conspicuously impractical, non-functional, and sometimes even uncomfortable. “The history of European costume,” writes Alison Lurie, “is rich in styles in which it was literally impossible to perform any useful function: sleeves that trailed on the floor, . . . powdered wigs the size, color and texture of a large white poodle, . . . and corsets so tight that it was impossible to bend at the waist or take a normal breath.” Even today we encumber ourselves in the name of style. High heels, for example, are awkward for walking and brutal on the feet—which is precisely how they’re able to convey the message, “I care about fashion.” 

DISCERNMENT
The fitness-display theory also helps us understand why artistic discernment—the skill of the savvy consumer or critic—is an important adaptive skill.
Discernment helps us answer a question we’re often asking ourselves as we navigate the world: “Which way is high status?” […] Without the ability to distinguish “good” art from “bad” art, we run the risk of admiring less fit, lower-status artists. […] It’s only by shopping around and sampling a wide variety of art that we learn to appreciate which skills are common (banging two rocks together) and which are rare (elaborate rhythms). An unrefined palate won’t appreciate a Michelin-starred restaurant. An untrained ear can’t appreciate the genius of Bach. […]
We spend an incredible amount of our leisure time refining our critical faculties in this way. Rarely are we satisfied simply to sit back and passively enjoy art (or any other type of human achievement for that matter). Instead we lean forward and take an active role in our experiences. We’re eager to evaluate art, reflect on it, criticize it, calibrate our criticisms with others, and push ourselves to new frontiers of discernment. And we do this even in art forms we have no intention of practicing ourselves.
Thus discernment, artistic or otherwise, is a critical skill, and yet it can be something we take for granted, in part because we do it so effortlessly. Think about how rarely we’re impressed by truly unimpressive people. When it happens, we feel as though we’ve been taken in by a charlatan. It can even be embarrassing to demonstrate poor aesthetic judgment. We don’t want others to know that we’re inept at telling good art from bad, skilled artists from amateurs. This suggests that we evaluate each other not only for our first-order skills, but for our skills at evaluating the skills of others.”
(Kevin Simler and Robin Hanson, The Elephant in the Brain, 2017)

E1.6.C.2.CONCLUSION
Art emerges as a powerful force that enriches human experience in multiple dimensions. It serves as a bridge to deeper understanding, helping us be more present in the world and intensifying our lived experience⁠⁠. Through art, we develop a tacit understanding with reality that goes beyond rational knowledge, establishing an intimate spiritual connection with the world⁠⁠.

Art not only helps us transcend suffering and gives us courage to face life's challenges, but it also acts as a moral compass, helping restore balance to our emotions and reminding us of what truly matters⁠⁠. It expands our boundaries by inviting us to embrace the unknown⁠⁠, while simultaneously helping us organize chaos and make life lighter⁠⁠.

Perhaps most importantly, art serves as a point of salvation, with the potential to put beauty back into the world and reconcile us with both ourselves and others⁠⁠. Through artistic expression, we can explore morality⁠, preserve meaningful experiences⁠⁠, and ultimately find a path to spiritual understanding⁠⁠. In this way, art becomes not just a form of expression, but a fundamental tool for human growth, understanding, and connection.

E1.6.D. KNOWLEDGE
The pursuit of knowledge through intellectual activity and, over a longer period of time, the emergence of lucidity. Without lucidity, education does not necessarily make us happier.

E1.6.D.1. ACTION VS. CONTEMPLATION
According to Aristotle, the contemplative sage finds happiness in intellectual activity:
This sage seeks intellectual activity as an alternative to collective action. The wise individual in this state will find satisfaction in contemplation, a use of one’s time reserved for a select few. However, they do not live out this state as a human, but as the divine within them.

E1.6.D.1.1. TRUE WISDOM IS DISILLUSIONED AND LUCID
Contemplation refers to the conscious approach of considering things without any desire to possess, use, or judge them. It is a state of pure joy derived from knowledge. Wisdom is a lifestyle, a blend of theoretic and practical understanding. It is identifiable by a certain serenity, joy, freedom, and love. Wisdom is an approximate, unstable state that varies according to each individual's circumstances. True wisdom, disillusioned with itself, achieves maximum happiness through lucidity.

E1.6.D.1.1_REF1_PH "CONTEMPLATION 

The attentive and disinterested gaze. Metaphorically, it refers to the attitude of consciousness when it is content to contemplate what is, without wanting to possess, use, or judge it. It is the summit of the spiritual life: the pure joy of knowing (Aristotle's theoretical life, Spinoza's amor intellectualis) or the true love of the true. The self seems to dissolve in the contemplation of its object: there is no one left to save, and that is salvation itself. [...]

WISDOM [...] 
Wisdom, true wisdom, is not an insurance policy against all risks, nor a panacea, nor a work of art. It is a rest, but a joyful and free rest, in truth. Knowledge? This is indeed the meaning of the word, both for the Greeks (sophia) and for the Latins (sapientia). But it is a very special knowledge. "Wisdom can be neither a science nor a technique," said Aristotle; it is less about what is true or effective than about what is good, for oneself and for others. Knowledge? Perhaps. But it is a way of life. [...]

The Greeks distinguished between theoretical or contemplative wisdom (sophia) and practical wisdom (phronèsis). But one is hardly possible without the other, or rather, true wisdom would be the combination of the two. It can be recognized by a certain serenity, but even more by a certain joy, a certain freedom, a certain eternity (the wise man lives in the present: he feels and experiences, as Spinoza said, that he is eternal), a certain love... "Of all the goods that wisdom offers us for the happiness of life as a whole, friendship is by far the greatest", insisted Epicurus (Maxims, XXVII). Self-love, fear, lack or lies are no longer obstacles. There is only the joy of knowledge: there is only love and truth. Therefore, we all have our moments of wisdom, when love and truth are enough for us. And of madness, when they tear us apart or fail us. True wisdom must be disillusioned with itself, as we see in Ecclesiastes. It is no longer an ideal; it is a state, always approximate, always unstable (it is eternal, like love, only as long as it lasts), it is an experience, it is an act. It is not an absolute, despite the Stoics (we are more or less wise), but a maximum (as such relative): it is the maximum of happiness, in the maximum of lucidity. It depends on the situation of this or that person, on the capacities of this or that person (wisdom is not the same in Auschwitz or in Paris, for Etty Hillesum or for Cavaillès) - in short, on the state of the world and of oneself. It is not an absolute; it is the way, always relative, of inhabiting reality, which is the only absolute in truth. This wisdom is better than all the books ever written about it, which run the risk of separating us from it. It is up to each of us to invent our own. "Even if we could be learned in the knowledge of others," said Montaigne, "we can only be wise in our own wisdom" (Essays, 1.25)."
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionnary, 2013)

E1.6.D.1.2. IMMANENCE THROUGH INTELLECTUAL EXCELLENCE
Engaging in intellectual activities brings us closer to divinity, even if our comprehension of the divine is imperfect. Aristotle proposed an ethic rooted in immanence, achieved not through the pursuit of spiritual realms, but by transcending oneself using the means at our disposal. He championed the joy derived from understanding the truth of a shared world, a stark contrast to the vision of a personal world embodied by Bernardo Soares in Fernando Pessoa's Book of Intranquility.

E1.6.D.1.2_REF1_PH "FOR ARISTOTLE, IT IS THE JOY OF KNOWING THE TRUTH OF A COMMON WORLD and not that of dreaming a world that is unique to us, like that of Bernardo Soares, a character in Fernando Pessoa's The Book of Intranquility. Aristotle is firmly anchored in reality while Bernardo Soares calls the reality of the world of men of action a fiction.
At first glance contradictory, the two positions perhaps deliver us two facets of reality that reconcile in Eastern philosophy: contemplation allows us to escape from our singularity to commune with the world; in doing so, it appears to us that the vision we had from the point of view of our singularity was an illusion of what reality really is."
(Pierre Schindler)

E1.6.D.1.2_REF2_LIT "THERE WAS A TIME WHEN I WAS ANNOYED BY THINGS THAT NOW MAKE ME SMILE. And one of them, which I'm reminded of almost every day, is the insistence with which everyday people who are active in life smile at poets and artists. They don't always do it, as newspaper thinkers believe, with an air of superiority. They often do it with affection. But it's always like cuddling a child, someone who doesn't know the certainty and precision of life. 

This used to irritate me because I assumed, like the naïve, and I was naïve, that this smile given to the worries of dreaming and saying was an effluvium of an intimate feeling of superiority. It's just a crackle of difference. And if in the past I considered that smile an insult because it implied superiority, today I consider it an unconscious doubt; just as adults often recognize in children a sharpness of mind superior to their own, so they recognize in us, those of us who dream and say things, something different that they suspect is strange. I want to believe that the most intelligent among them often glimpse our superiority and then smile broadly to hide the fact that they see it.

But our superiority is not what so many dreamers have thought their own superiority to be. The dreamer is not superior to the doer because the dream is superior to reality. The superiority of the dreamer is that dreaming is much more practical than living, and that the dreamer derives a much greater and more varied pleasure from life than the man of action. In better and more direct terms, the dreamer is the man of action.
Since life is essentially a state of mind, and whatever we do or think is valid to us to the extent that we think it's valid, it's up to us to make the best of it. The dreamer is an issuer of bills, and the bills he issues run through the city of his mind just as the bills of reality do. What do I care if the paper money of my soul is never convertible into gold, if there is never any gold in the actual alchemy of life? 

The flood comes after all of us, but it's only after all of us. Better and happier are those who, recognizing the fiction of everything, make the novel before it is made for them, and, like Machiavelli, put on their court robes to write well in secret".
(Fernando Pessoa, The book of disquiet, 1982)

E1.6.B.2.wE1.6.D.1.2 "EXERCISE SYSTEMS"
Joy can be derived from creating an "exercise system". This process allows an individual to enhance their skills through repeated tasks, acting as immune device and coping mechanism for life's lack of inherent meaning. In essence, it's through these exercise systems that one shapes their life.
”ANYTHING YOU POLISH WITH ATTENTION WILL BECOME A MIRROR. Anything to which you give yourself fully, vest all your strength and risk all your vulnerability, will return you to your life annealed, magnified, both unselved and more deeply yourself. It can be a garden, or a desert, or a hare. It can be, perhaps most readily, a place. “Place and a mind may interpenetrate till the nature of both is altered,” the Scottish mountaineer and poet Nan Shepherd wrote in her stunning love letter to a mountain long before neuroscience found the seat of personhood in the hippocampus — the brain’s compass for navigating space. Places can become part of us, can imprint themselves on the soul like people we have loved. Because every place is part of a larger landscape, a cell in the body of the world, to fall in love with any one place — to contact its beckoning beauty, its vulnerability, its variousness — is to come to love the world itself more deeply.”
(Maria Popova, The Marginalian)

E1.6.D.1.3. THERE IS NO HIERARCHY BETWEEN THE CONTEMPLATIVE AND THE PRACTICAL LIFE
Aristotle's text presents a tension between the fulfilling practical life and the satisfying contemplative life. This tension remains unresolved in his work. A simplistic interpretation might suggest a hierarchy between the two. However, Aristotle appears to suggest that both types of life are entirely legitimate.

E1.6.D.1.3_REF1_CUR HOW DID THIS WARRIOR KING, considered the most powerful of the Khmer kings, reconcile his practical life as a monarch with his devotion to Buddhism? Ten centuries earlier, the Roman Emperor and Philosopher Marcus Aurelius testified in his writings to the difficulty of reconciling these two aspects of his life and it seems that he carried out his duty as a monarch often resigned and reluctantly.


E1.6.D.1.3_REF2_PH "A MULTI-MILLENNIAL EXPERIENCE seems to show that there is an intermediate level at which people like to situate themselves, because there they find more moral and intellectual comfort, there they feel better or less bad than elsewhere, without resorting to any considerations other than hedonistic ones: this is the level of scientific knowledge, intellectual activity and artistic creation."

E1.6.D.1.3_REF2.1_PH LÉVI-STRAUSS REFLECTS ON THE CONTRADICTION BETWEEN THE LIMITED CAPACITY OF HUMAN THINKING AND THE VASTNESS  OF THE UNIVERSE, and defends the importance of continuing to live, work and think, even without absolute certainty.
"Didier Eribon: "To end a series of books like Mythologies with the disillusioning realization that 'nothing' remains of man's undertakings is almost to proclaim a philosophical creed. They have already pretended to see in this 'nothing' the expression of their profound philosophy."

Claude Lévi-Strauss: "I never said that. I said that man should live, work, think, keep courage, knowing that he will not always be present on earth, that one day this earth will cease to exist, and that then nothing will remain of all the works of man. It's not exactly the same.
My "profound philosophy," as you call it, stumbles over this contradiction and bows before it. Everything that physicists and biologists teach me makes me fall in love; nothing stimulates my thinking more. At the same time, it seems to me that every problem that is solved, or that we believe to be solved, gives rise to new problems, and so on and so forth, endlessly, so that we become more and more convinced of the inadequate certainty that our ability to think is and will always remain real, that the profound nature of reality escapes any effort to represent it. It was Kant who first taught us this. But Kant, who molded himself to a power of knowledge that was hopelessly diseased by certain antinomies, hoped to find an absolute foundation in the moral life. Hyper-Kantian, if I may say so, I include the moral life in the problem of pure reason: it too has its antinomies, which are impossible to overcome. What's more, if scientific knowledge opens us up to the immensely great and the immensely small from perspectives far more dizzying than Pascal imagines, it shows us our insignificance. Whether humanity disappears or the earth disappears, nothing will change in the march of the cosmos. Hence a final paradox: we are not at all sure that this knowledge, which reveals our insignificance, has any validity. We know that we are nothing, or that we are not much, and knowing this, we don't even know if this knowledge is knowledge. Thinking of the universe as immeasurable through thought forces us to question thought itself. We can't get out of it.
So what about the radical skepticism you seem to be accusing me of? It doesn't exist. Because even if we dedicate ourselves to moving from appearance to appearance, it's not indifferent to know that it makes sense to stop somewhere, and where to stop. Between superficial appearances and the tiring search for a between superficial appearances and the tiring search for a meaning behind the meaning that is never the right one, a multi-millennial experience seems to show that there is an intermediate level at which people like to situate themselves, because there they find more moral and intellectual comfort, there they feel better or less bad than elsewhere, without resorting to any considerations other than hedonistic ones: this is the level of scientific knowledge, intellectual activity and artistic creation. Well, then, let's cling to it, and let's act resolutely as if, in order to believe it for all practical purposes, not without nodding from time to time with a sign of intelligence to a memento mori that encompasses our universe and, with it, ourselves".
(From near and far, Conversation between Didier Eribon and Claude Lévi-Strauss, 1988)

E1.6.D.1.4. THE JOY OF KNOWING
Contemplative activity, or the "joy of knowing," is considered "the perfect happiness of man" (Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics, X, 7). This maxim would resonate equally with Descartes or Nietzsche.

E1.D.1.4.a. CONTENTMENT WITH OURSELVES
For Descartes, the optimal use of our freedom—achieving knowledge through reason—brings self-contentment. This reflects the conviction that we have acted in our best interests, a concept Spinoza refers to as "acquiescence with ourselves." Descartes' moral philosophy emphasizes the cultivation of reason and the discovery of new truths. Provisional morality guides us to safeguard ourselves from the world, while philosophical morality encourages us to take control of our lives. The highest form of morality, informed by knowledge from various sciences, leads to the wisdom of beatitude, which is inner contentment. A generous Cartesian identifies more with himself than with others, seeing the exploration of the infinite within himself as a source of deep joy and personal balance.

E1.6.D.1.4.a.i. I SPEND MY WHOLE LIFE CULTIVATING MY REASON
"I considered reviewing the various occupations that people have in life, in order to choose the best one. But without commenting on others' choices, I concluded that I couldn't do better than to continue with my current path. That is, to devote my entire life to cultivating my reason."

E1.6.D.1.4.a.i_REF1_PH ACCORDING TO RENÉ DESCARTES, THE BEST USE OF LIFE IS TO CULTIVATE YOUR REASON AND SEEK THE TRUTH. He asserts that judging well leads to acting well and acquiring all possible virtues and goods, thus ensuring contentment.
"PART THREE [...]
Finally, to conclude this code of morals, I took it upon myself to review the various occupations that men have in this life, in order to try to choose the best one, and, not wanting to say anything about the occupations of others, I thought I could not do better than to continue in that very one in which I found myself, that is to say, spending my whole life cultivating my reason and advancing, as far as I could, in the knowledge of the truth, following the method I had prescribed to myself. I had met with such extreme contentment since the time I had begun to make use of this method, that I did not believe one could obtain any sweeter or more innocent contentment in this life, and, discovering every day by its means some truths that to me seemed quite important and commonly ignored by other men, the satisfaction I had from them so filled my mind that nothing else was of any consequence to me. In addition, the three preceding maxims were founded solely on the plan I had of continuing to instruct
myself, for since God has given each of us some light to distinguish the true from the false, I would not have believed I ought to rest content for a single moment with the opinions of others, had I not proposed to use my own judgment to examine them when there would be time; and I would not have been able to free myself of scruples in following these opinions, had I not hoped that I would not, on that account, lose any opportunity of finding better ones, in case there were any. And finally, I could not have limited my desires or have been content, had I not followed a path by which, thinking I was assured of acquiring all the knowledge of which I was capable, I thought I was assured by the same means of the knowledge of all the true goods that would ever be in my power. For, given that our will tends not to pursue or flee anything unless our understanding represents it to the will as either good or bad, it suffices to judge well in order to do well, and to judge as best one can, in order also to do one's very best, that is to say, to acquire all the virtues and in general all the other goods that one could acquire, and, when one is certain that this is the case, one could not fail to be contented."
(René Descartes, Discourse on Method, 1637)

E1.6.B.2.wE1.6.D.1.4.a.i. "EXERCISE SYSTEMS"
Joy can be derived from creating an "exercise system". This process allows an individual to enhance their skills through repeated tasks, acting as immune device and coping mechanism for life's lack of inherent meaning. In essence, it's through these exercise systems that one shapes their life.

E1.6.D.1.4.a.ii. ENJOY THE FRUITS OF THE EARTH AND FEEL WONDERFULLY EXISTING IN THE FLESH
"Descartes does not hesitate to conjecture that the first passion experienced by the soul when it insinuated itself into the fetal body was that of joy: « From the first moment that our soul was joined to the body, it is likely that it felt joy, and incontinently afterwards love... » (to Chanut, 1-2-47). [...]
For this is how men live, not for the labors of understanding alone, but to enjoy the fruits of the earth and feel wonderfully existing in the flesh, which is the soul's true earthly residence, and from which it would be vain - Descartes is very close to Montaigne here - to try to escape, as some mystics illusorily attempt to. [...] « The mind must be entirely freed from all kinds of sad thoughts, and even from all kinds of serious meditations on the sciences, and occupied only with imitating those who, looking at the greenness of a wood, the colors of a flower, the flight of a bird, and such things that require no attention, persuade themselves that they are thinking of nothing. Which is not to waste time, but to use it well ».

E1.6.D.1.4.a.ii_REF1_PH DESCARTES ARGUES THAT THE SOUL'S UNION WITH THE BODY IS AN ENRICHMENT, NOT A FALL, OPPOSING THE IDEA OF ORIGINAL SIN. He believes the soul's first passion upon incarnation is joy, emphasizing that humans should enjoy earthly existence and not solely focus on speculative knowledge. Descartes advocates for balancing intellectual pursuits with sensory relaxation and appreciating the present moment.
"What does the soul has to gain by uniting with a body, in what sense is incarnation an enrichment rather than a fall? Indeed, the idea of ​​original sin is radically foreign to Cartesian thought, which is resolutely optimistic, and always betting that all that is, is good, and that we can know it as such, if at least we manage to analyze it by the only means of our clear and distinct ideas. There is therefore no question of saying that the fall of the soul into the body is the effect of a theological punishment whose reasons would be impenetrable to our created understanding. [...] Descartes does not hesitate to imagine that the first passion felt by the soul when it incorporates itself into the fetus is that of joy: « From the first moment that our soul was joined to the body, it is probable that it has felt joy, and immediately afterwards love ... »
For this is how men live, not for the works of the understanding alone, but to enjoy the fruits of the earth and to feel themselves wonderfully existing in their flesh which is the true earthly residence of the soul, and from which it would be vain - Descartes is very close to Montaigne here - to want to escape, as certain mystics illusorously attempt. This is why we should only devote littArt: Kate Sweeney, Fall?le time to the most speculative knowledge that leads us to believe that we are only pure spirits, and strive to enjoy our incarnation by relaxing, by tasting the pleasure of passing time, such as it is given to us in the perpetuated miracle of the present, that is to say by the divine activity of continuous creation: « I have employed very few hours per day in the thoughts which occupy the imagination, and very few hours per year for those which occupy the understanding alone, and I have given all the rest of my time to the relaxation of the senses and to the rest of the mind » [...]; « We must completely free the mind from all kinds of sad thoughts, and even also from all kinds of serious meditations touching the sciences, and only concern ourselves with imitating those who, looking at the greenness of a wood, the colors of a flower, the flight of a bird, and such things which require no attention, convince themselves that they are thinking of nothing. Which is not wasting time, but using it well »"
(René Descartes, Discourse on the Method, 1637).

E1.6.D.1.4.a.iii. THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN HAPPINESS AND BLISS
We can say that [Decartes] feels delightfully alive, and that this feeling inspires in him a real bliss, which is much more than mere happiness: « There's a distinction to be made between happiness and bliss. The former is dependent on external factors. It's often experienced by those who are considered more fortunate than wise, having encountered good fortune they did not seek. Bliss, on the other hand, seems to be rooted in a profound sense of inner contentment and satisfaction. » […]
Provisional morality guides us in protecting ourselves from the world’s hostility, while philosophical morality instructs us on claiming our personal power. [...]
It's possible to adopt another kind of morality, not that of a novice seeking protection from the world's dangers to complete their life's work. This morality is for the wise individual who possesses the secrets of wisdom and intends to relish in it during their lifetime. The most elevated and perfect form of morality requires a comprehensive understanding of other sciences. It reaches its peak in the wisdom of bliss, which is contentment with one's inner self. This wisdom mainly involves the correct and appropriate use of passions.

E1.6.D.1.4.a.iii_REF1_PH DESCARTES ASSERTS THAT THE SOUL MUST TURN AWAY FROM PURE SPECULATION IN ORDER TO ORIENT ITSELF TO THE WORLD AND THUS ENRICH ITS EXISTENCE THROUGH INTERACTION WITH OTHERS AND THE WORLD AROUND IT. 
"If, according to Descartes, one should not devote too much time to metaphysics, it is because the true destiny of the soul is not to examine itself or to always work toward the edification of a purely speculative science, but rather to turn away from itself, not for the sake of distraction, but through a clear understanding of the path to true fulfillment and enrichment, to turn toward the world. The body encourages the soul to reverse the direction of its gaze, which through metaphysical conversion had been directed solely toward speculative inwardness - the mental space of the cogito - in order to invite it to progress in the world, to find its good there, and to work there for its greatest happiness.  [...]
To walk with confidence in this life, as Descartes wrote, is therefore to set out with a firm and steady step to discover the world, for the enrichment of our soul and the fulfillment of our health. The methodical march in which the cogito gave us our first enlightenment goes far beyond mere speculation and moves towards the curious discovery of the earth. The metaphysical traveler who has chosen to always seek the truth, in science as in life, does not remain within the circle of the purely spiritual, but goes out to meet things and beings. [...] "To give oneself a morality that is not provisional, but that of a sage who knows himself through the accomplishment of metaphysical experience, is, therefore, to formulate rules of conduct that will allow me to live my passion happily, not to flee from it - for there is nothing wrong with the worldly intentionality by which the body diverts the soul from the sole consideration of itself, and inclines it to the world and to itself, without, however, renouncing the right that belongs to me, as to every thinking being capable of thinking for itself, to remain a free examiner of the value that must be given to the object that invites me to go out of myself and to enrich myself through interaction with others. Metaphysical knowledge has taught my mind to truly enjoy the power of my thoughts and the substantial union of my mind with my body, and has thus inspired in me a very considerable satisfaction that not only makes me know that I am, but also makes me enjoy this existence in every moment given to me by a God who is constantly creating. It can be said that in Descartes we have even encountered a "contented" metaphysician, that is, one who enjoys the content of existence, which he now knows to be irreducibly his own: Throughout the Discourse," writes Gouhier, "Descartes speaks of his satisfaction; Cartesianism is the philosophy of a satisfied man, and he is satisfied with his philosophy" (Descartes, Essays, p. 216). Descartes now fully enjoys the spiritual and carnal radiance of which he is the living source. It can be said that he feels himself to be a delightful being, and that this feeling inspires him with a true bliss that is much more than mere happiness: "There is a difference between happiness and bliss, in that happiness depends only on things that are outside us, which is why those who are esteemed happier than wise are those to whom some good has happened that they have not sought for themselves, whereas bliss, it seems to me, consists in perfect contentment of the spirit and inward satisfaction" (to Elizabeth, 4-8-45). [...]
And if we ask where this great joy comes from, this joy that endures and does not pass away, the philosopher will answer that it comes from contemplating the infinity of the will in us, which is in us as the image of God and promises us an increase of existence that will never end, opening for us the path of the method by which we will learn more and more to enjoy fully the existence that is given to us and to affirm ever more powerfully the radiance of being that we know how to spread in us from the first point of evidence that set us on the path: The punctual and first clarity of the cogito. [Provisional morality teaches us how to protect ourselves from the hostility of the world; philosophical morality teaches us how to take possession of the Kingdom. [...] A different morality is now possible, not that of the beginner who wants above all to protect himself from the dangers of the world in order to carry out his life's work, but that of the sage who holds the secret of wisdom and is determined to enjoy it as much as possible in this life. If the soul responds to the invitation of the body, which directs it to others and to the world, it can go out fearlessly to meet others and, among all the wonders that await it in this world, to meet the wonder of all wonders, my fellow human being, who also thinks as I do and is worthy of that infinity that shines in his soul as in mine, of the same respect and esteem as those whose thoughts I recognize in me as worthy, which is an effect of the creative activity that is in God. It is then no longer a question of provisional morality, but of "the highest and most perfect morality, which presupposes a complete knowledge of the other sciences, [and which] is the last degree of wisdom" (566), represented, in the letter that serves as a preface to the Groundwork of Philosophy, by the highest branch of the tree of philosophy, whose roots are metaphysics (the source of the development of mathematical knowledge), the trunk of which is physics, and the other two branches, lower than morality, are mechanics (which teaches me to become like the master and owner of the earth) and medicine (which teaches me to increase in myself the fire of health and to push back as far as possible the threat of death). And just as we know that it is the highest branch that bears the most fruit, we understand that Descartes' entire philosophy culminates in this wisdom of happiness, which is contentment with one's inner self, a wisdom that consists mainly in the good and right use of the passions. For Descartes, it is not a question of establishing rules that would muzzle and restrain passionate outbursts, but, on the contrary, of accepting them as an experience of joy and growth of the existence that is within us. [...]"
(Jacques Darriulat)

Read the full essay in French: https://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Descartes/DescartesDiscours/Disc.Introd.html

E1.6.D.1.4.a.iii_REF2_PH DESCARTES SEES THE PASSIONS AS AN OPENING TO THE WORLD AND TO OTHERS, AS AN EXPANSION OF THE SOUL THAT LEADS TO HAPPINESS. He asserts that all passions are inherently good, and that wisdom consists in mastering them in order to derive pleasure from them. A life without passions is, in his view, an empty and impoverished life, for passions enable one to fully enjoy the sweetness of human life. The best minds are often those with the most intense passions.
"Because of its union with the body, the soul is indeed exposed to the shock of passion. [For Descartes, far from being a disturbance that blinds the soul, the passions are, on the contrary, an opening to the world and to others that enlarges it and makes it more generous. In this sense, all passions are good in themselves, insofar as they contribute to this expansion of the soul, which is the source of all happiness, whereas the passion that exaggerates the soul, makes it forget its right to think for itself and retain its free judgment, diverts it from the radiant center that gives it joy, and leaves it unhappy, at the mercy of chance and fate. [...]
And now that we know all the passions," Descartes concludes in his treatise, "we have much less reason to fear them than we had before; for we see that they are all good by nature, and that we have nothing to avoid but their abuse or excess" (Art. 211, p. 793-94). And in a letter to the Marquis of Newcastle (March or April 1648), Descartes confessed: "The philosophy I cultivate is not so barbarous or so wild as to reject the use of the passions; on the contrary, in them alone I find all the sweetness and happiness of this life" (1298-99). [...]
And since passion determines the direction of our coming into the world-without this violence we might never have left the purely intellectual realm of metaphysical certainties-it is only through passion that we are given the opportunity to enjoy our purely human nature, that is, the union of our soul with our body, which is the true place where, as human beings, we must exercise the freedom of our will. A life without passions, Descartes teaches, is an empty and poor life, spent on earth without tasting its fruits: "The people who can move the passions the most are capable of tasting the greatest sweetness in this life [...] Wisdom is mainly useful for the sake of the soul.Wisdom is especially useful in this respect, because it teaches one to master them and to manage them with such skill that the evils they cause are quite bearable, and even that one derives pleasure from them all" (art. 212, the last of the Passions of the Soul); so much so that "it is usually the best minds whose passions are more violent and have a stronger effect on their bodies" (to Elizabeth, June 45, p. 1189)".
(Jacques Darriulat)

Read the full essay in French: https://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Descartes/DescartesDiscours/Disc.Introd.html

E1.6.D.1.4.a.iv. THE GENEROUS CARTESIAN DEFINES HIMSELF MORE IN RELATION TO HIMSELF THAN IN RELATION TO OTHERS:
A generous soul is lively and radiant, quietly driven by the profound joy of experiencing the infinite's unfolding within, originating from God the Creator. This strength provides balance to the soul, allowing it to remain composed in all situations. Therefore, a generous person is someone who understands the reason for self-esteem, the infinity of freedom, long before recognizing the need to esteem others.

E1.6.D.1.4.a.iv_REF1_PH ACCORDING TO DESCARTES, GENEROSITY BEGINS WITH ONESELF BEFORE EXTENDING TO OTHERS, AND ONLY THOSE WHO VALUE THEMSELVES CAN TRULY VALUE OTHERS. 
"However, among all the passions that produce each other according to our progress in the land of tenderness, that is, according to the depth of our passionate experience, following a scenario in which the first encounter marks the point of origin and whose path we will trace, there is one, more fundamental than the others, that gives passionate life the weight that restores it to its center, the gravity of a joy that cannot be dismissed by accidental circumstances. This passion is called generosity. It is, in fact, a strange passion, because it cannot be said to be truly passive, the consequence of a shock suffered after the encounter with an external body, with an incarnate soul that is not our own, but, on the contrary, the passion that the soul feels when it feels the activity that is proper to it and that is within it. In this sense, we can say that generosity is the affection of the soul impassioned by the effect of its own activity. Indeed, it is only by considering the infinity of the will that is in it, an infinity that is potentially in the creature and only actively in God, a potential infinity that opens before the soul a path of progress that nothing can legitimately limit, It is in the contemplation of the determination to want to be more and more (which is the essence of the will) that the soul experiences the fire of generosity within itself: "I believe, therefore, that true generosity, which makes a man value himself in the highest degree that he can legitimately value himself, consists, on the one hand, in his knowing that nothing really belongs to him but this free disposition of his will [...] and, on the other hand, in his feeling within himself a firm and constant determination to use it well, that is, never to lack the will to undertake and carry out all those things that he will judge to be best; that is, to follow virtue perfectly" (art. 153). [Virtue, which here must be understood in the sense of virtus, is the strength of the soul that is determined never to allow itself to be diverted from the path that leads to the permanent expansion of its being. Thus the generous Cartesian defines himself in relation to himself and not in relation to others: generous is the radiant and living soul, secretly animated by the grave and profound joy of feeling within itself the unfolding of the infinite, which has its source in God the Creator. Such strength gives the soul its balance and allows it to stand firm in all circumstances. Therefore, the generous person is the one who knows why he has the right to appreciate himself, namely the infinity of freedom, long before he knows why he must appreciate others. Because the foundation of virtue, which is generosity, begins, according to Descartes, with oneself and not with others, unlike those who ignore themselves and always want to intervene in strategies that they assume should make others happy, almost always making those they publicly claim to want to help unhappy. Descartes' moral philosophy is based entirely on this inversion, which holds that the best way to make others happy is to be happy oneself, and that only those who know the legitimate reason why they have the right to value themselves first, which is none other than the infinity of freedom that endlessly renews the dynamism of the will, know how to value others: "I can see only one thing in us that would justify us in being proud of ourselves, and that is the exercise of our free will and the power we have over our own actions" (Art. 152). [Always open to others, but never recognizing their right to interfere with his complete freedom, the generous man, driven by his strength to do great things, is welcoming and courteous to those who happen to cross his path: "Those who are generous in this way are naturally inclined to do great things, and yet to undertake nothing of which they do not feel capable; and since they value nothing more than doing good to others and despise their own interests [but here we are speaking only of material, not spiritual interests], in this respect they are always perfectly courteous, kind, and obliging to everyone" (Art. 156). This radiant man, whose gait is sure and whose gaze is clear, gives the soul the balance that allows it always to remain itself, while opening itself unreservedly to the passion of others. [...] Because I appreciate in myself what is truly valuable, I am able to appreciate others and to recognize in them, as in myself, the radiance of freedom. A strange inversion, peculiar to Cartesian morality: only he who first values himself knows why he must also value others, and he who despises himself cannot help despising others".
(Jacques Darriulat)

Read the full essay in French: https://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Descartes/DescartesDiscours/Disc.Introd.html

E1.6.D.1.4.b. THE SIMPLE, QUIET LIFE OF THE THINKER
According to Friedrich Nietzsche, the lifestyle of a thinker is the simplest and most serene. A thinker can find joy with ease and lead a tranquil, vibrant life. They require society only occasionally and find comfort in solitude and in the company of the dead. They can substitute their friends with the greatest individuals who have ever lived.
Those who are free-spirited and dedicated to knowledge are often satisfied with a modest job or fortune that merely meets their basic needs. They structure their lives in a way that ensures a significant change in public fortune does not simultaneously devastate their lives.

E1.6.D.1.4.b_REF1_PH "CAUTION OF FREE SPIRITS. — Free-spirited people, living for knowledge alone, will soon find they have achieved their external goal in life, their ultimate position vis à vis society and the state, and gladly be satisfied, for example, with a minor position or a fortune that just meets their needs; for they will
set themselves up to live in such a way that a great change in economic conditions, even a revolution in political structures, will not overturn their life with it. They expend as little energy as possible on all these things, so that they can plunge with all their assembled energy, as if taking a deep breath, into the element of knowledge. They can then hope to dive deep, and also get a look at the bottom.
Such a spirit will be happy to take only the corner of an experience; he does not love things in the whole breadth and prolixity of their folds; for he does not want to get wrapped up in them.
He, too, knows the week-days of bondage, dependence, and service. But from time to time he must get a Sunday of freedom, or else he will not endure life.
It is probable that even his love of men will be cautious and somewhat shortwinded, for he wants to engage himself with the world of inclination and blindness only as far as is necessary for the sake of knowledge. He must trust that the genius of justice will say something on behalf of its disciple and protégé, should accusatory voices call him poor in love.
In his way of living and thinking, there is a refined heroism; he scorns to offer himself to mass worship, as his cruder brother does, and is used to going quietly through the world and out of the world. Whatever labyrinths he may wander through, among whatever rocks his river may at times have forced its tortured course—once he gets to the light, he goes his way brightly, lightly, and almost soundlessly, and lets the sunshine play down to his depths."
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human, Aphorism 291, 1878)

E1.6.D.1.4.b_REF2_PH "LIVE CHEAPLY. The cheapest and most carefree way of life is that of the thinker: for, at any crucial moment, he is the one who most needs the things that others despise and give up. - He enjoys the rest easily and does not know the costly access to pleasure; his work is not hard, but, in a way, meridional; his days and nights are not spoiled by remorse; he moves, eats, drinks and sleeps in the measure that suits his mind, so that it becomes more and more quiet, strong and clear; he rejoices in his body and has no reason to fear it; he does not need society, if not from time to time and then embraces his solitude with all the more tenderness; the dead compensate him for the living and he even finds a way to replace his friends, evoking among the dead the best who have ever lived. - Let us ask ourselves for once if it is not the contradicting desires and habits which make people´s lives expensive, and consequently painful and often unbearable. - In another sense, however, the thinker's life is the most expensive, - nothing is too good for him; and to be deprived of the best would be unbearable deprivation for the thinker."
(Friedrich Nietzsche, The Dawn of day, aphorism 566, 1881)

E1.6.D.1.5. INTELLIGENT DETACHMENT
For Lucretius, scientific knowledge is a mode of psychological grace. It is a way of learning to let go.

E1.6.D.1.5_REF1_PH "The aim of Lucretius was not independent scientific knowledge, but the reduction of emotional anxiety. Lucretius saw man as torn between the pleasure of sex and the pain of emotional loss, tormented by superstitions inspired by religion, haunted by the fear of bodily decay and death. He recommended scientific knowledge, which teaches intelligent detachment, equanimity. Scientific knowledge is, for Lucretius, a mode of psychological gracefulness. It is a way of learning to let go."
(Susan Sontag, The anthropologist as hero, 1963)

E1.6.D.1.5_REF2_LIT LEARN WHY THE WORLD WAGS AND WHAT WAGS IT "The best thing for being sad… is to learn something. That is the only thing that never fails. You may grow old and trembling in your anatomies, you may lie awake at night listening to the disorder of your veins, you may miss your only love, you may see the world about you devastated by evil lunatics, or know your honor trampled in the sewers of baser minds. There is only one thing for it then — to learn. Learn why the world wags and what wags it. That is the only thing which the mind can never exhaust, never alienate, never be tortured by, never fear or distrust, and never dream of regretting."
(T.H. White, The Once and Future King, 1958)

E1.6.D.1.5_REF3_LIT WHAT IT’S LIKE TO BE A FALCON: THE PEREGRINE AS A PORTAL TO A WAY OF SEEING AND A STATE OF BEING
"Standing in the fields near the north orchard, I shut my eyes and tried to crystallise my will into the light-drenched prism of the hawk’s mind. Warm and firm-footed in long grass smelling of the sun, I sank into the skin and blood and bones of the hawk. The ground became a branch to my feet, the sun on my eyelids was heavy and warm. Like the hawk, I heard and hated the sound of man, that faceless horror of the stony places. I stifled in the same filthy sack of fear. I shared the same hunter’s longing for the wild home none can know, alone with the sight and smell of the quarry, under the indifferent sky. I felt the pull of the north, the mystery and fascination of the migrating gulls. I shared the same strange yearning to be gone. I sank down and slept into the feather-light sleep of the hawk."
(J.A. Baker, The Peregrine, 1967)

E1.6.D.1.5_REF3.1_LIT J.A. BAKER’S WORK COMBINES METICULOUS OBSERVATION, POETIC LANGUAGE, AND A DEEP REVERENCE FOR NATURE, SERVING AS A TIMELESS ODE TO THE BEAUTY AND WONDER OF THE NATURAL WORLD. In the mid-1950s, he dedicated a decade to observing the peregrine falcon.
"In the mid-1950s, a near-sighted English office worker set out to do for the sky what Rachel Carson had done for the sea thirty years earlier — invite our human imagination, grounded yet boundless, into the world of another creature dwelling in another sphere. J.A. Baker (August 6, 1926–December 26, 1987) spent a decade following earth’s fastest flying bird on bicycle and on foot, possessed by its “restless brilliance.” When he unloosed The Peregrine (public library) into the atmosphere of culture in 1967 — an atmosphere shaped by the new ecological conscience awakened by Carson’s Silent Spring five years earlier — it was a clarion call and a consecration, entirely original, yet emanating Thoreau’s meticulous observation, Whitman’s ecstatic language, and Carson’s soulful reverence for the realities of nature in all their brutal beauty. An epoch later, it remains an ode to wonder, a field guide to observation as devotional practice, a passionate and poetic reminder that by attending closely and tenderly to any one thing, we recover our natural reverence for everything, our love of the world in all its strangeness and splendor."
(Maria Popova, The Marginalian)

E1.6.D.1.5_REF3.2_LIT "YOU CANNOT KNOW WHAT FREEDOM MEANS TILL YOU HAVE SEEN A PEREGRINE LOOSED INTO THE WARM SPRING SKY TO ROAM AT WILL THROUGH ALL THE FAR PROVINCES OF LIGHT. [...]

The eyes of a falcon peregrine weigh approximately one ounce each; they are larger and heavier than human eyes. If our eyes were in the same proportion to our bodies as the peregrine’s are to his, a twelve-stone man would have eyes three inches across, weighing four pounds. The whole retina of a hawk’s eye records a resolution of distant objects that is twice as acute as that of the human retina. Where the lateral and binocular visions focus, there are deep-pitted foveal areas; their numerous cells record a resolution eight times as great as ours. This means that a hawk, endlessly scanning the landscape with small abrupt turns of his head, will pick up any point of movement; by focusing upon it he can immediately make it flare up into larger, clearer view.

The peregrine’s view of the land is like the yachtsman’s view of the shore as he sails into the long estuaries. A wake of water recedes behind him, the wake of the pierced horizon glides back on either side. Like the seafarer, the peregrine lives in a pouring-away world of no attachment, a world of wakes and tilting, of sinking planes of land and water. We who are anchored and earthbound cannot envisage this freedom of the eye. The peregrine sees and remembers patterns we do not know exist: the neat squares of orchard and woodland, the endlessly varying quadrilateral shapes of fields. He finds his way across the land by a succession of remembered symmetries. [...]

Slowly he drifted above the orchard skyline and circled down wind, curving upward and round in long steep glides. He passed from the cold white sky of the south, up to the warm blue zenith, ascending the wind-bent thermal with wonderful ease and skill. His long-winged, blunt-headed shape contracted, dwindled, and darkened to the flinty point of a diamond as he circled high and far over; hanging and drifting above; indolent, watchful, supreme. Looking down, the hawk saw the big orchard beneath him shrink into dark twiggy lines and green strips; saw the dark woods closing together and reaching out across the hills; saw the green and white fields turning to brown; saw the silver line of the brook, and the coiled river slowly uncoiling; saw the whole valley flattening and widening; saw the horizon staining with distant towns; saw the estuary lifting up its blue and silver mouth, tongued with green islands. And beyond, beyond all, he saw the straight-ruled shine of the sea floating like a rim of mercury on the surface of the brown and white land. The sea, rising as he rose, lifted its blazing storm of light, and thundered to freedom to the land-locked hawk… I watched him with longing, as though he were reflecting down to me his brilliant unregarded vision of the land beyond the hill… He sank forward into the wind, and passed slowly down across the sun. I had to let him go. When I looked back, through green and violet nebulae of whirling light, I could just see a tiny speck of dusk falling to earth from the sun, flashing and turning and falling through an immense silence that crashed open in a tumult of shrilling, wing-beating birds."
(J.A. Baker, The Peregrine, 1967)

E1.6.D.1.6. THE EYE OF THE HEART: A PATH TO LIBERATION
The power of “the Eye of the Heart,” which produces insight, is vastly superior to the power of thought, which produces opinions. […]
This is the process of gaining adaequatio, of developing the instrument capable of seeing and thus understanding the truth that does not merely inform the mind but liberates the soul.

E1.6.D.1.6.a. ADEQUATIO
"The understanding of the knower must be adequate to the thing to be known [...] For every one of us only those facts and phenomena “exist” for which we posses adaequatio, and as we are not entitled to assume that we are necessarily adequate to everything, at all times, and in whatever condition we may find ourselves, so we are not entitled to insist that something inaccessible to us has no existence at all and is nothing but a phantom of other people’s imaginations. [...] People say: “Let the facts speak for themselves”; they forget that the speech of facts is real only if it is heard and understood. It is thought to be an easy matter to distinguish between fact and theory, between perception and interpretation. In truth, it is extremely difficult."

E1.6.D.1.6.a_REF1_PH OUR BODILY SENSES PROVIDE BASIC DATA ABOUT INANIMATE MATTER, BUT INTELLECTUAL SENSES ARE NECESSARY TO RECOGNIZE HIGHER-ORDER CONCEPTS LIKE FORM, PATTERN, AND MEANING. These intellectual senses are active qualities of the mind, essential for deeper understanding.
 "What enables man to know anything at all about the world around him? … Nothing can be known without there being an appropriate “instrument” in the makeup of the knower. This is the Great Truth of “adaequatio” (adequateness), which defines knowledge as adaequatio rei et intellectus — the understanding of the knower must be adequate to the thing to be known. [...]
Our five bodily senses make us adequate to the lowest Level of Being — inanimate matter. But they can supply nothing more than masses of sense data, to “make sense” of which we require abilities or capacities of a different order. We may call them “intellectual senses.” Without them we should be unable to recognize form, pattern, regularity, harmony, rhythm, and meaning, not to mention life, consciousness, and self-awareness. While the bodily senses may be described as relatively passive, mere receivers of whatever happens to come along and to a large extent controlled by the mind, the intellectual senses are mind-in-action, and their keenness and reach are qualities of the mind itself."
(E.F. Schumacher, A Guide for the Perplexed, 1977)

E1.6.D.1.6.a_REF2_PH DISTINGUISHING BETWEEN FACT AND THEORY IS CHALLENGING "For every one of us only those facts and phenomena “exist” for which we posses adaequatio, and as we are not entitled to assume that we are necessarily adequate to everything, at all times, and in whatever condition we may find ourselves, so we are not entitled to insist that something inaccessible to us has no existence at all and is nothing but a phantom of other people’s imaginations. [...]
People say: “Let the facts speak for themselves”; they forget that the speech of facts is real only if it is heard and understood. It is thought to be an easy matter to distinguish between fact and theory, between perception and interpretation. In truth, it is extremely difficult. [...]
When the level of the knower is not adequate to the level (or grade of significance) of the object of knowledge, the result is not factual error but something much more serious: an inadequate and impoverished view of reality. [...]
The level of significance to which an observer or investigator tries to attune himself is chosen, not by his intelligence, but by his faith. The facts themselves which he observes do not carry labels indicating the appropriate level at which they ought to be considered. Nor does the choice of an inadequate level lead the intelligence into factual error or logical contradiction. All levels of significance up to the adequate level — i.e., up to the level of meaning … — are equally factual, equally logical, equally objective, but not equally real.

It is by an act of faith that I choose the level of my investigation; hence the saying “Credo ut intelligam” — I have faith as to be able to understand. If I lack faith, and consequently choose an inadequate level of significance for my investigation, no degree of “objectivity” will save me from missing the point of the whole operation, and I rob myself of the very possibility of understanding."
(E.F. Schumacher, A Guide for the Perplexed, 1977)

E1.6.D.1.6.b. EVERYTHING CAN BE SEEN DIRECTLY EXCEPT THE EYE THROUGH WHICH WE SEE
"There is nothing more difficult than to become critically aware of the presuppositions of one’s thought. Everything can be seen directly except the eye through which we see. Every thought can be scrutinized directly except the thought by which we scrutinize. A special effort, an effort of self-awareness, is needed: that almost impossible feat of thought recoiling upon itself — almost impossible but not quite. In fact, that is the power that makes man human and also capable of transcending his humanity. [...]
Only through the “heart” can contact be made with the higher grades of significance and Levels of Being. "

E1.6.D.1.6.b_REF1_PH THE OBSERVER'S UNDERSTANDING IS INFLUENCED BY THEIR FUNDAMENTAL ASSUMPTIONS AND SOCIETAL CONTEXT, MAKING SELF-AWARENESS AND CRITICAL EXAMINATION OF THESE PRESUPPOSITIONS CHALLENGING.
 "The observer depends not only on the adequateness of his own higher qualities, perhaps “developed” through learning or training; he depends also on the adequateness of his “faith” or, to put it more conventionally, of his fundamental presuppositions and basic assumptions. In this respect he tends to be very much a child of his time and of the civilization in which he has spent his formative years; for the human mind, generally speaking, does not just think: it thinks with ideas, most of which it simply adopts and takes over from its surrounding society. [...]
There is nothing more difficult than to become critically aware of the presuppositions of one’s thought. Everything can be seen directly except the eye through which we see. Every thought can be scrutinized directly except the thought by which we scrutinize. A special effort, an effort of self-awareness, is needed: that almost impossible feat of thought recoiling upon itself — almost impossible but not quite. In fact, that is the power that makes man human and also capable of transcending his humanity. [...]
Only through the “heart” can contact be made with the higher grades of significance and Levels of Being. [...]
For anyone wedded to the materialistic Scientism of the modern age … higher levels of Reality simply do not exist, because his faith excludes the possibility of their existence. He is like a man who, although in possession of a radio receiver, refuses to use it because he has made up his mind that nothing can be obtained from it but atmospheric noises. [...]
Faith is not in conflict with reason, nor is it a substitute for reason. Faith chooses the grade of significance or Level of Being at which the search for knowledge and understanding is to aim. There is reasonable faith and there is unreasonable faith. To look for meaning and purpose at the level of inanimate matter would be as unreasonable an act of faith as an attempt to “explain” the masterpieces of human genius as nothing but the outcome of economic interests or sexual frustration."
(E.F. Schumacher, A Guide for the Perplexed, 1977)

E1.6.D.1.6.c. SOME HAVE MORE REACH
"As regards the bodily senses, all healthy people possess a very similar endowment, but no one could possibly overlook the fact that there are significant differences in the power and reach of people’s minds"

E1.6.D.1.6.c_REF1_PH "AS REGARDS THE BODILY SENSES, ALL HEALTHY PEOPLE POSSESS A VERY SIMILAR ENDOWMENT, but no one could possibly overlook the fact that there are significant differences in the power and reach of people’s minds… Beethoven’s musical abilities, even in deafness, were incomparably greater than mine, and the difference did not lie in the sense of hearing; it lay in the mind. Some people are incapable of grasping and appreciating a given piece of music, not because they are deaf but because of a lack of adaequatio in the mind. The music is grasped by intellectual powers which some people possess to such a degree that they can grasp, and retain in their memory, an entire symphony on one hearing or one reading of the score; while others are so weakly endowed that they cannot get it at all, no matter how often and how attentively they listen to it. For the former, the symphony is as real as it was for the composer; for the latter, there is no symphony: there is nothing but a succession of more or less agreeable but altogether meaningless noises. The former’s mind is adequate to the symphony; the latter’s mind is inadequate, and thus incapable of recognizing the existence of the symphony."
(E.F. Schumacher, A Guide for the Perplexed, 1977)

E1.6.D.1.6.d. INSIGHT IS VASTLY SUPERIOR TO OPINIONS
"The power of “the Eye of the Heart,” which produces insight, is vastly superior to the power of thought, which produces opinions. […]

This is the process of gaining adaequatio, of developing the instrument capable of seeing and thus understanding the truth that does not merely inform the mind but liberates the soul. […]

Ideas produce insight and understanding, and the world of ideas lies within us. The truth of ideas cannot be seen by the senses but only by that special instrument sometimes referred to as “the Eye of the Heart,” which, in a mysterious way, has the power of recognizing truth when confronted with it."
(E.F. Schumacher, A Guide for the Perplexed, 1977)

E1.6.D.1.6.d_REF1_PH EMERSON ARGUES THAT OUR SENSE OF BEING IS INTRINSICALLY CONNECTED TO THE UNIVERSE, SHARING THE SAME SOURCE AS EVERYTHING IN EXISTENCE.
"The magnetism which all original action exerts is explained when we inquire the reason of self-trust. Who is the Trustee? What is the aboriginal Self, on which a universal reliance may be grounded? What is the nature and power of that science- baffling star, without parallax, without calculable elements, which shoots a ray of beauty even into trivial and impure actions, if the least mark of independence appear? The inquiry leads us to that source, at once the essence of genius, of virtue, and of life, which we call Spontaneity or Instinct. We denote this primary wisdom as Intuition, whilst all later teachings are tuitions. In that deep force, the last fact behind which analysis cannot go, all things find their common origin. For the sense of being which in calm hours rises, we know not how, in the soul, is not diverse from things, from space, from light, from time, from man, but one with them and proceeds obviously from the same source whence their life and being also proceed. We first share the life by which things exist and afterwards see them as appearances in nature and forget that we have shared their cause."
(Ralph Waldo Emerson, Self-reliance, 1841)

E1.6.D.1.7. TO KNOW IS AN ACT OF JOY THAT POTENTIATES THE OTHERS
For Robert Misrahi, knowing is one of the acts of joy that does not exclude the others (founding, loving, dreaming, acting). It is a reflexive action on other activities that enhances the joy in each.

E1.6.D.1.7_REF1_PH KNOWLEDGE PRODUCES A SPECIFIC JOY, INTRINSIC AND NON-UTILITARIAN, INSEPARABLE FROM THE JOY OF LIVING. This joy is possible because knowledge is a founding act that validates what is founded, doubling the foundation by reflecting on oneself and one's actions.
The joy of founding has another dimension: "epistemological" and not just existential. The traveler of real life realizes this every time he engages in reflective thought or refers to theoretical texts. Knowledge that accounts for itself produces a specific joy, not to be confused with the satisfaction of being able to use the results of this knowledge at a later date; while all knowledge has an existential or practical purpose, being merely the means to a higher end, which is joy itself, it nevertheless has an intrinsic dimension of elation. And for some, the joy of understanding is not inferior to the joy of living, it is not even separable from it: the joy of founding is expressed here as the joy of knowing. 
Why is such joy possible, why is it almost universal, despite the complicit efforts of the will to ignorance? It is because knowledge (like reflective choice) is a founding act. But it does not found in the way one creates or establishes; it founds insofar as it affirms what is founded. Founding knowledge is necessarily super-reflexive: it redoubles the second foundation in which the subject considered itself and its action in order to validate it. [...]"
(Robert Misrahi, The acts of joy, 1987)

E1.6.D.2. BEWARE OF HOURS SPENT CUT OFF FROM LIFE
Gaston Bachelard cautions us about hours spent in isolation from life, similar to Alan Watts, who provides insight on identifying unhealthy intellectual activity.

E1.6.D.2.1. THE AFFECTIVE CHARACTER OF DURATION
Gaston Bachelard writes: “What makes the affective character of duration, the joy or the pain of being, is the proportion or the disproportion of hours of our life used as hours of sterile thought or as hours of sympathy. Matter neglects to exist, life neglects to live, the heart neglects to love.”

E1.6.D.2.1_REF1_PH "WHAT MAKES FOR THE AFFECTIVE CHARACTER OF DURATION, THE JOY OR THE PAIN OF BEING, is the proportion or the disproportion of our life used as hours of thought vs. hours of sympathy. Matter neglects to be, life neglects to live, the heart neglects to love. It is by sleeping that we lose Paradise. [...] Then we feel a dull pain when we go in search of lost moments. We remember those rich hours that are marked by the thousand sounds of the Easter bells, those bells of the resurrection whose blows cannot be counted because they all count, because they each have an echo in our awakened soul. And this memory of joy is already remorse when we compare to these hours of total life the intellectually slow hours because they are relatively poor, the dead hours because they are empty - empty of purpose, as Carlyle said from the depths of his sadness - the interminable hostile hours because they give nothing. 
And we dream of a divine hour that would give everything. Not the fully occupied hour, but the repleated one. The hour when all the moments of time would be used by matter, the hour when all the moments realized in matter would be used by life, the hour when all the living moments would be felt, loved, thought. The hour, therefore, when the relativity of consciousness would be erased because consciousness would be the exact measure of total time. 
In the end, objective time is our maximum time; it is the one that contains all the moments."
(Gaston Bachelard, Intuition of the Instant, 1931)

E1.6.D.2.1_REF2_LIT MARION MILNER DESCRIBES TWO WAYS OF PERCEIVING: a narrow focus, which is reason-driven and limits perception, and a wide focus, which involves the whole body and enhances perception, leading to greater happiness.
"As soon as I began to study my perception, to look at my own experience, I found that there were different ways of perceiving and that the different ways provided me with different facts. There was a narrow focus which meant seeing life as if from blinkers and with the centre of awareness in my head; and there was a wide focus which meant knowing with the whole of my body, a way of looking which quite altered my perception of whatever I saw. And I found that the narrow focus way was the way of reason. If one was in the habit of arguing about life it was very difficult not to approach sensation with the same concentrated attention and so shut out its width and depth and height. But it was the wide focus way that made me happy."
(Marion Milner,  A Life of One’s Own, 1934)

E1.6.D.2.2. MAGNIFY YOUR SPIRIT
"Seek out what magnifies your spirit. Patti Smith, in discussing William Blake and her creative influences, talks about writers and artists who magnified her spirit — it’s a beautiful phrase and a beautiful notion. Who are the people, ideas, and books that magnify your spirit? Find them, hold on to them, and visit them often. Use them not only as a remedy once spiritual malaise has already infected your vitality but as a vaccine administered while you are healthy to protect your radiance."
(Maria Popova, The Marginalian°

E1.6.D.2.2_REF1_LIT "HE IS CAREFUL OF WHAT HE READS, FOR THAT IS WHAT HE WILL WRITE. He is careful of what he learns, for that is what he will know."
(Annie Dillard)

E1.6.D.2.3. DON'T PUSH YOUR BRAIN AROUND
Alan Watts suggests that when individuals try to think or concentrate, they act as though they are forcing their brains, which is unnecessary. He believes that self-consciousness is a disorder and is evident in the intense feeling of separation between "I" and one's experiences. The brain can function appropriately when consciousness is performing its intended role: not struggling to escape the present experience, but being effortlessly aware of it.

E1.6.D.2.3_REF1_PH "IV. THE WISDOM OF THE BODY
"[...] When the heart is out of order, we are clearly conscious of its beating; it becomes a distraction, pounding within the breast. It seems most probable that our preoccupation with thinking and planning, together with the sense of mental fatigue, is a sign of some disorder of the brain. The brain should, and in some cases does, calculate and reason with the unconscious ease of the other bodily organs. After all, the brain is not a muscle, and is thus not designed for effort and strain.
But when people try to think or concentrate, they behave as if they were trying to push their brains around. They screw up their faces, knit their brows, and approach mental problems as if they were something like heaving bricks. Yet you do not have to grind and strain to digest food, and still less to see, hear, and receive other neural impressions. [...]
Working rightly, the brain is the highest form of "instinctual wisdom." Thus it should work like the homing instinct of pigeons and the formation of the foetus in the womb-without verbalizing the process or knowing "how" it does it. The self-conscious brain, like the self-conscious heart, is a disorder, and manifests itself in the acute feeling of separation between "I" and my experience. The brain can only assume its proper behavior when consciousness is doing what it is designed for: not writhing and whirling to get out of present experience, but being effortlessly aware of it."
(Alan Watts, The wisdom of insecurity, 1951)

E1.6.D.2.4. DON'T LOSE TOUCH WITH BEAUTY
As Darwin aged, his capacity to appreciate the world's beauty began to diminish, bringing him considerable sadness. In his autobiography, he expressed regret over not dedicating time to enjoy poetry and music. He speculated that doing so might have prevented the weakening of the brain regions responsible for perceiving beauty.
