If there has to be anything ‘behind the utterance of the formula’ it is particular circumstances, which justify me in saying I can go on—when the formula occurs to me.

Try not to think of understanding as a ‘mental process’ at all.—

For that is the expression which confuses you. But ask yourself: in what sort of case, in what kind of circumstances, do we say, “Now I know how to go on,” when, that is, the formula has occurred to me?—

In the sense in which there are processes (including mental processes) j which are characteristic of understanding, understanding is not a j mental process.

(A pain’s growing more and less; the hearing of a tune or a sentence: these are mental processes.)

Thus what I wanted to say was: when he suddenly knew how to go on, when he understood the principle, then possibly he had a special experience—and if he is asked: “What was it? What took \ place when you suddenly grasped the principle?” perhaps he will describe it much as we described it above-but for us it is the circumstances under which he had such an experience that justify him in saying in such a case that he understands, that he knows how to go on.

1. This will become clearer if we interpolate the consideration of another word, namely “reading”. First I need to remark that I am not counting the understanding of what is read as part of ‘reading’ for purposes of this investigation: reading is here the activity of rendering out loud what is written or printed; and also of writing from dictation, writing out something printed, playing from a score, and so on.

The use of this word in the ordinary circumstances of our life is of course extremely familiar to us. But the part the word plays in our life, and therewith the language-game in which we employ it, would be difficult to describe even in rough outline. A person, let us say an Englishman, has received at school or at home one of the kinds of education usual among us, and in the course of it has learned to read his native language. Later he reads books, letters, newspapers, and other things.

Now what takes place when, say, he reads a newspaper?-His eye passes—as we say—along the printed words, he says them out loud—or only to himself; in particular he reads certain words by taking in their printed shapes as wholes; others when his eye has taken in the first syllables; others again he reads syllable by syllable, and an occasional one perhaps letter by letter,—We should also say that he had read a sentence if he spoke neither aloud nor to himself during the reading but was afterwards able to repeat the sentence word for word or nearly so.—He may attend to what he reads, or again—as we

might put it—function as a mere reading-machine: I mean, read aloud and correctly without attending to what he is reading; perhaps with his attention on something quite different (so that he is unable to say what he has been reading if he is asked about it immediately afterwards).

Now compare a beginner with this reader* The beginner reads the words by laboriously spelling them out.—Some however he guesses from the context, or perhaps he already partly knows the passage by heart. Then his teacher says that he is not really reading the words (and in certain cases that he is only pretending to read them).

If we think, of this sort of reading, the reading of a beginner, and ask ourselves what reading consists in, we shall be inclined to say: it is a special conscious activity of mind.

We also say of the pupil: “Of course he alone knows if he is really reading or merely saying the words off by heart”. (We have yet to discuss these propositions: “He alone knows_”.)

But I want to say: we have to admit that—as far as concerns uttering any one of the printed words—the same thing may take place in the consciousness of the pupil who is "pretending’ to read, as in that of the practised reader who is "reading’ it. The word ""to read” is applied differently when we are speaking of the beginner and of the practised reader.-Now we should of course like to say: What goes on in that practised reader and in the beginner when they utter the word can’t be the same. And if there is no difference in what they happen to be conscious of there must be one in the unconscious workings of their minds, or, again, in the brain.—So we should like to say: There are at all events two different mechanisms at work here. And what goes on in them must distinguish reading from not reading. —But these mechanisms are only hypotheses, models designed to explain, to sum up, what you observe.

Notes:

(a) “Understanding a word”: a state. But a mental state?—Depression, excitement, pain, are called mental states. Carry out a grammatical investigation as follows: we say

“He was depressed the whole day”.

“He was in great excitement the whole day”.

“He has been in continuous pain since yesterday”.—

We also say “Since yesterday I have understood this word”. “Continuously”, though?—To be sure, one can speak of an interruption of understanding. But in what cases? Compare: “When did your pains get less?” and “When did you stop understanding that word?”

(b) Suppose it were asked: “ When do you know how to play chess? All the time? or just while you are making a move? And the whole of chess during each move?—How queer that knowing how to play chess should take such a short time, and a game so much longer!”
(Ludwig Wittgenstein, Philosophical Investigations, 1953)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.ii_REF5_PH LUDWIG WITTGENSTEIN  ARGUES THAT SINCE ANY RULE IS OPEN TO INFINITE INTERPRETATIONS, UNDERSTANDING IS NOT A MYSTERIOUS, PRIVATE MENTAL PROCESS THAT PREDETERMINES ALL ITS APPLICATIONS, BUT IS INSTEAD A PUBLIC PRACTICE GROUNDED IN THE SHARED CUSTOMS AND TRAINING OF A COMMUNITY.

“186. “What you are saying, then, comes to this: a new insight— intuition—is needed at every step to carry out the order ‘+n’ correctly.”—To carry it out correctly! How is it decided what is the right step to take at any particular stage?—“The right step is the one that accords with the order—as it was meant”—So when you gave the order +2 you meant that he was to write 1002 after 1000—and did you also mean that he should write 1868 after 1866, and 100036 after 100034, and so on—an infinite number of such propositions?— “No: what I meant was, that he should write the next but one number after every number that he wrote; and from this all those propositions follow in turn.”—But that is just what is in question: what, at any stage, does follow from that sentence. Or, again, what, at any stage we are to call “being in accord” with that sentence (and with the meaning you then put into the sentence—whatever that may have consisted in). It would almost be more correct to say, not that an intuition was needed at every stage, but that a new decision was needed at every stage.

1. “But I already knew, at the time when I gave the order, that he ought to write 1002 after 1000.”—Certainly; and you can also say you meant it then; only you should not let yourself be misled by the grammar of the words “know” and “mean”. For you don’t want to say that you thought of the step from 1000 to 1002 at that time— and even if you did think of this step, still you did not think of other ones. When you said “I already knew at the time.” that meant something like: “If I had then been asked what number should be written after 1000, I should have replied ‘1002’.” And that I don’t doubt. This assumption is rather of the same kind as: “If he had fallen into the water then, I should have jumped in after him”.—Now, what was wrong with your idea?
2. Here I should first of all like to say: your idea was that that act of meaning the order had in its own way already traversed all those steps: that when you meant it your mind as it were flew ahead and took all the steps before you physically arrived at this or that one.

Thus you were inclined to use such expressions as: “The steps are really already taken, even before I take them in writing or orally or in thought.” And it seemed as if they were in some unique way predetermined, anticipated—as only the act of meaning can anticipate reality.

1. “But are the steps then not determined by the algebraic formula?”—The question contains a mistake.

We use the expression: “The steps are determined by the formula.”.

How is it used?—We may perhaps refer to the fact that people are brought by their education (training) so to use the formula y = x2, that they all work out the same? value for y when they substitute the same number for x. Or we may say: “These people are so trained that they all take the same step at the same point when they receive the order ‘add 3’ ”. We might express this by saying: for these people the order “add 3” completely determines every step from one number to the next. (In contrast with other people who do not know what they are to do on receiving this order, or who react to it with perfect certainty, but each one in a different way.)

On the other hand we can contrast different kinds of formula, and the different kinds of use (different kinds of training) appropriate to them. Then we call formulae of a particular kind (with the appropriate methods of use) “formulae which determine a numbery for a given value of x”, and formulae of another kind, ones which “do not determine the number y for a given value of x”. (y = x2 would be of the first kind, y ≠ x2 of the second.) The proposition “The formula .... determines a number y” will then be a statement about the form of the formula—and now we must distinguish such a proposition as “The formula which I have written down determines j”, or “Here is a formula which determines y”, from one of the following kind: “The formula y = x2 determines the number y for a given value of x”. The question “Is the formula written down there one that determines y?” will then mean the same as “Is what is there a formula of this kind or that?”—but it is not clear off-hand what we are to make of the question “Is y = x2 a formula which determines y for a given value of x?” One might address this question to a pupil in order to test whether he understands the use of the word “to determine”; or it might be a mathematical problem to prove in a particular system that x has only one square.

1. It may now be said: “The way the formula is meant determines which steps are to be taken”. What is the criterion for the way the formula is meant? It is, for example, the kind of way we always use it, the way we are taught to use it.

We say, for instance, to someone who uses a sign unknown to us: “If by x!2 you mean x2, then you get this value for y if you mean 2x, that one.”—Now ask yourself: how does one mean the one thing or the other by “x!2”?

That will be how meaning it can determine the steps in advance.

1. “It is as if we could grasp , the whole use of the word in a flash.” Like what e.g.?—Can’t the use—in a certain sense—be grasped in a flash? And in what sense can it not?—The point is, that it is as if we could ‘grasp it in a flash’ in yet another and much more direct sense than that.—But have you a model for this? No. It is just that this expression suggests itself to us. As the result of the crossing of different pictures.
2. You have no model of this superlative fact, but you are seduced into using a super-expression. (It might be called a philosophical superlative.)
3. The machine as symbolizing its action: the action of a machine—I might say at first—seems to be there in it from the start. What does that mean?—If we know the machine, everything else, that is its movement, seems to be already completely determined.

We talk as if these parts could only move in this way, as if they could not do anything else. How is this—do we forget the possibility of their bending, breaking off, melting, and so on? Yes; in many cases we don’t think of that at all. We use a machine, or the drawing of a machine, to symbolize a particular action of the machine. For instance, we give someone such a drawing and assume that he will derive the movement of the parts from it. (Just as we can give someone a number by telling him that it is the twenty-fifth in the series i, 4, 9, 16, ... .)

“The machine’s action seems to be in it from the start” means: we are inclined to compare the future movements of the machine in their definiteness to objects which are already lying in a drawer

and which we then take out.-But we do not say this kind of thing

when we are concerned with predicting the actual behaviour of a machine. Then we do not in general forget the possibility of a distortion of the parts and so on.-We do talk like that, however, when

we are wondering at the way we can use a machine to symbolize a given way of moving—since it can also move in quite different ways.

We might say that a machine, or the picture of it, is the first of a series of pictures which we have learnt to derive from this one.

But when we reflect that the machine could also have moved differently it may look as if the way it moves must be contained in the machine-as-symbol far more determinately than in the actual machine. As if it were not enough for the movements in question to be empiric¬ ally determined in advance, but they had to be really—in a mysterious sense—already present. And it is quite true: the movement of the machine-as-symbol is predetermined in a different sense from that in which the movement of any given actual machine is predetermined.

1. When does one have the thought: the possible movements of a machine are already there in it in some mysterious way?—Well, when one is doing philosophy. And what leads us into thinking that? The kind of way in which we talk about machines. We say, for example, that a machine has (possesses) such-and-such possibilities of movement; we speak of the ideally rigid machine which can only move in

such-and-such a way.-What is this possibility of movement? It is

not the movement, but it does not seem to be the mere physical conditions for moving either—as, that there is play between socket and pin, the pin not fitting too tight in the socket. For while this is the empirical condition for movement, one could also imagine it to be otherwise. The possibility of a movement is, rather, supposed to be like a shadow of the movement itself. But do you know of such a shadow? And by a shadow I do not mean some picture of the movement—for such a picture would not have to be a picture of just this movement. But the possibility of this movement must be the possibility of just this movement. (See how high the seas of language run here!)

The waves subside as soon as we ask ourselves: how do we use the phrase “possibility of movement” when we are talking about a given machine?-But then where did our queer ideas come from?

Well, I shew you the possibility of a movement, say by means of a picture of the movement: ‘so possibility is something which is like reality’. We say: “It isn’t moving yet, but it already has the possibility of moving”-‘so possibility is something very near reality’. Though we may doubt whether such-and-such physical conditions make this movement possible, we never discuss whether this is the possibility of this or of that movement: ‘so the possibility of the movement stands in a unique relation to the movement itself; closer than that of a picture to its subject’; for it can be doubted whether a picture is the picture of this thing or that. We say “Experience will shew whether this gives the pin this possibility of movement”, but we do not say “Experience will shew whether this is the possibility of this movement”: ‘so it is not an empirical fact that this possibility is the possibility of precisely this movement’.

We mind about the kind of expressions we use concerning these things; we do not understand them, however, but misinterpret them. When we do philosophy we are like savages, primitive people, who hear the expressions of civilized men, put a false interpretation on them, and then draw the queerest conclusions from it.

1. “But I don’t mean that what I do now (in grasping a sense) determines the future use causally and as a matter of experience, but that in a queer way, the use itself is in some sense present.”—But of course it is, ‘in some sense’! Really the only thing wrong with what you say is the expression “in a queer way”. The rest is all right; and the sentence only seems queer when one imagines a different language-game for it from the one in which we actually use it. (Someone once told me that as a child he had been surprised that a tailor could ‘sew a dress’—he thought this meant that a dress was produced by sewing alone, by sewing one thread on to another.)
2. In our failure to understand the use of a word we take it as the expression of a queer process. (As we think of time as a queer medium, of the mind as a queer kind of being.)
3. “It’s as if we could grasp the whole use of a word in a flash.”— And that is just what we say we do. That is to say: we sometimes describe what we do in these words. But there is nothing astonishing, nothing queer, about what happens. It becomes queer when we are led to think that the future development must in some way already be present in the act of grasping the use and yet isn’t present.—For we say that there isn’t any doubt that we understand the word, and on the other hand its meaning lies in its use. There is no doubt that I now. want to play chess, but chess is the game it is in virtue of all its rules (and so on). Don’t I know, then, which game I want to play until I have played it? or are all the rules contained in my act of intending? Is it experience that tells me that this sort of game is the usual consequence of such an act of intending? so is it impossible for me to be certain what I am intending to do? And if that is nonsense—what kind of super-strong connexion exists between the act of intending

and the thing intended?-Where is the connexion effected between

the sense of the expression “Let’s play a game of chess” and all the rules of the game?—Well, in the list of rules of the game, in the teaching of it, in the day-to-day practice of playing.

1. “But how can a rule shew me what I have to do at this point? Whatever I do is, on some interpretation, in accord with the rule.”— That is not what we ought to say, but rather: any interpretation still hangs in the air along with what it interprets, and cannot give it any support. Interpretations by themselves do not determine meaning.

“Then can whatever I do be brought into accord with the rule?”— Let me ask this: what has the expression of a rule—say a sign-post— got to do with my actions? What sort of connexion is there here?— Well, perhaps this one: I have been trained to react to this sign in a particular way, and now I do so react to it.

But that is only to give a causal connexion; to tell how it has come about that we now go by the sign-post; not what this going-by-the- sign really consists in. On the contrary; I have further indicated that a person goes by a sign-post only in so far as there exists a regular use of sign-posts, a custom.

1. Is what we call “obeying a rule” something that it would be possible for only one man to do, and to do only once in his life?— This is of course a note on the grammar of the expression “to obey a rule”.

It is not possible that there should have been only one occasion on which someone obeyed a rule. It is not possible that there should have been only one occasion on which a report was made, an order given or understood; and so on.—To obey a rule, to make a report, to give an order, to play a game of chess, are custom (uses, institutions).

To understand a sentence means to understand a language. To understand a language means to be master of a technique.

200.It is, of course, imaginable that two people belonging to a tribe unacquainted with games should sit at a chess-board and go through the moves of a game of chess; and even with all the appropriate mental accompaniments. And if we were to see it we should say they were playing chess. But now imagine a game of chess translated according to certain rules into a series of actions which we do not ordinarily associate with a game—say into yells and stamping of feet. And now suppose those two people to yell and stamp instead of playing the form of chess that we are used to; and this in such a way that their procedure is translatable by suitable rules into a game of chess. Should we still be inclined to say they were playing a game? What right would one have to say so?

201.This was our paradox: no course of action could be deter¬ mined by a rule, because every course of action can be made out to accord with the rule. The answer was: if everything can be made out to accord with the rule, then it can also be made out to conflict with it. And so there would be neither accord nor conflict here.

It can be seen that there is a misunderstanding here from the mere fact that in the course of our argument we give one interpretation after another; as if each one contented us at least for a moment, until we thought of yet another standing behind it. What this shews is that there is a way of grasping a rule which is not an interpretation, but which is exhibited in what we call “obeying the rule” and “going against it” in actual cases.

Hence there is an inclination to say: every action according to the rule is an interpretation. But we ought to restrict the term “interpretation” to the substitution of one expression of the rule for another.

202.And hence also ‘obeying a rule* is a practice. And to think one is obeying a rule is not to obey a rule. Hence it is not possible to obey a rule ‘privately’: otherwise thinking one was obeying a rule would be the same thing as obeying it.

203.Language is a labyrinth of paths. You approach from one side and know your way about; you approach the same place from another side and no longer know your way about.”
(Ludwig Wittgenstein, Philosophical Investigations, 1953)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.iii. CONNIVANCE VS. KNOWLEDGE
François Jullien refers to "connivance" as another form of relation to the world, one that knowledge has often overlooked but has been unable to eradicate. He describes knowledge as speculative know-how that has disassociated itself from adapting to the world. By considering itself an end, it has disconnected from life.

On the other hand, connivance is a form of understanding that develops over time and stays integrated within a certain environment. It's a type of knowledge that remains beyond any possible demonstration and above any explicitness, and it doesn't distinguish theory from practice.

E1.6.D.3.7.a.iii.1. KNOWLEDGE IS BASED ON OBJECTIFICATION AND ABSTRACTION, WHILE CONNIVANCE IS BASED ON A TACIT UNDERSTANDING OF THE WORLD
"Knowledge is rooted in objectification and abstraction, while connivance stems from  a tacit understanding of the world. Connivance allows us to perceive the world in a more intimate way, uncovering a spiritual dimension and establishing a connection between us and the world. It's a shift from reason, not denying conscious reason, but harmonizing with the resonance and fluctuations of nature.
“In essence, this transformation occurs when a country becomes a landscape. What I grasp in it is no longer alien to me, but communicates, 'speaks to me', 'touches me'. It is not a self-projecting subject that casts itself into the landscape, making it the confidant of its emotion (the common stance of cheap romanticism), but rather the landscape, by standing out, pulls me into its field of tension, resonating with its variation. As Stendhal remarked, the landscape is like a bow "that played on my soul". From beneath the mind asserting itself autonomously (the island subject knowing and wanting), it revives my more original affiliation to the world, a world that has risen from its indifference and into which my vitality plugs in.”
"Knowledge is based on objectification and abstraction, while connivance is based on a tacit understanding with the world. Connivance allows us to perceive the world more intimately, discovering a spiritual dimension and creating a link between ourselves and the world. It's a return upstream from reason, not a rejection of knowing reason, but a resonance with the variation of nature. 
"In fact, this transmutation takes place: when a country becomes a landscape, what I apprehend in it is no longer foreign to me, but makes a sign, "speaks to me", "touches me". That it is not a subject-self that projects itself into the landscape, making it the confidant of its emotion (the banal posture of bad romanticism), but that it is the landscape that, by singling itself out, brings me into its field of tension, in resonance with its variation (the landscape is like a bow "that played on my soul", said Stendhal), brings back - from beneath the mind asserting itself autonomously (the island subject knowing and wanting) my more original belonging to the world: a world that has emerged from its indifference and onto which my vitality plugs in. "

E1.6.D.3.7.a.iii.1_REF1_PH "CONNIVANCE (VS. KNOWLEDGE) 1. In the face of knowledge and its hegemony over European culture, how can we name its other, which it has overshadowed and not taken into account? I will choose to call this other relationship to the world that knowledge has tended to bury, but has not been able to abolish, and whose "coherence" I must recall in the face of this established domination of knowledge. For the fact that knowledge, in its development into speculative knowledge, that is, knowledge for knowledge's sake, has detached itself, through science, from the need to adapt itself to the world from which it emerged, and has taken itself as an end in itself, has detached itself from the vital, now gives us this strictly correlative task: that of returning to that relationship which has been buried by reason and which, operating therefore in the shadows, nevertheless keeps us in a tacit understanding with things - but an "understanding" which we do not reflect upon. This "understanding" therefore remains implicit, beneath the relational work that reason itself performs, and which we can only illuminate by making reason retrace its steps, go back in its history, explore what it has strayed from: not what it heroically fought against (obscurantism versus enlightenment), but what it had to leave aside in its legitimate and powerful struggle to assert itself - and which now lies dormant, thus escaping our understanding. If, as we know, the opposite of knowledge is ignorance, its opposite is the collusion that is established with it on an equal footing, while at the same time turning their backs on each other. Connivere, as the Latin says: to get along "by winking".
For knowledge isolates a "nature" and turns it into an "object," methodically organizes its course, develops tools of abstraction and constructs mediations, renders spaces indifferent and projects an equal and planned time; it develops an argued discourse that promotes the conditions of science as well as politics. But is that all there is to knowledge? Doesn't life presuppose a mode of intelligence or, better, of "understanding" that, woven over the course of days, and even without us thinking about it, without us thinking about thinking about it, is held in adherence, instead of being distanced and edified in thought? For is knowledge not only the illuminated side, which is only made possible by its insidious reverse, to which it refers, but also by forgetting itself? Knowledge that is shadowy, that remains integrated in an environment, that does not abstract itself from a landscape, that does not extract itself from a conditioning, that does not separate theory from practice, that does not detach an "I" from the world: it remains below any possible exposure, above any explicitness, and above all, it does not question. 
Complicity is that knowledge that has not broken its attachment. [...]"
(François Jullien, Chinese thought vs philosophy, 2015)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.iii.1_REF2_PH FRANÇOIS JULLIEN EXPLORES THE NOTION OF LANDSCAPE AS A RELATIONSHIP OF CONNIVANCE WITH THE WORLD, WHERE OBJECTIVE OBSERVATION IS TRANSFORMED INTO A TACTILE UNDERSTANDING. Criticizing European Romanticism for its tendency to project subjectivity onto landscape, he proposes an approach in which landscape becomes a partner that awakens an affective and spiritual dimension. 
"There is 'landscape' when my cognitive capacity switches and is reversed into collusion; when the relationship of objectification, and above all of observation, that I maintain with the world is transformed into tacit understanding and communication: from knowing that I was vis-à-vis the 'country' (the knowledge of geography), I become collusive again in a landscape. Not, strictly speaking, that I "personify" elements of the landscape, or that I "project" myself into them, or that I lend my subjectivity to things, or that I animate the inanimate, as has been said and rehashed, as if to compensate for the rationalism of knowledge, the ordinary romanticism, the chatty romanticism, in Europe - all operations in which the subject still wants to be the master and which are only devices. But this transformation does take place: when the land becomes landscape, what I perceive in it is no longer alien to me, but beckons to me, "speaks to me," "touches me," as we say colloquially (but can we ever escape this colloquialism?). That is to say, as the Chinese literati painters so beautifully expressed it, that the perceptive becomes affective at the same time, or that I perceive then from within as from outside of myself; through this, that a dimension of infinity or of spirit then emanates and is released from the physicality of things, with which my mind "understands" (softly, as the Chinese strategically put it). The land becomes a landscape - do we know, or rather "do we really know", but the Chinese language of thought has proved particularly gifted in expressing this implicit, when this "corner of the land" establishes itself as a partner, the tension that it promotes integrates me and is shared: there is "landscape", in other words, when a relationship is established (re-established) with this "country" that makes me go back upstream - finds its source on this side of that which is established by knowing reason; when the place suddenly becomes a "link"; and when this "corner of the country" thus "becomes the world": when I connect with it as with that which makes the world. [...]
At the same time, there is no fusional shift, no compensatory slide into the ecstatic or the mystical, as so much bad lyricism in Europe has advocated, so I do not indulge in any irrationalism (this is an upstream of reason and not its polemical denial), where the famous "communion" or symbiosis with nature, which comes as reparation for knowing reason, at least leaves ambiguity hanging in the air. [...Let it not be a subject self that projects itself into the landscape, making it the confidant of its emotion (which I have just stigmatized as the banal attitude of bad romanticism), but let it be the landscape that, by setting itself apart, draws me into its field of tension, in resonance with its variation (the landscape is like a bow "that has played on my soul," said Stendhal), brings back - from beneath the mind that asserts itself as autonomous (the insular subject that knows and wants) - my more original belonging to the world: a world that has emerged from its indifference and to which my vitality is bound. [...]

"To tell the truth, we live in the alternation of these two registers, or rather of these two regimes [...] I press more on one key or the other of my inner keyboard; I constantly move the cursor between one pole and the other. Because we live by moving discreetly from one to the other, and in this agility, by advancing (asserting) the knowing, or by retreating, conniving, regressing from our autarkic self. Now, the ideal for the knowing subject is modeling. But how can we go one step further and name the other side of this, the side that has remained in the shadows, which means that the capacity is acquired and grows, but silently, without even paying attention to this continuous process, until we finally see the result?"
(François Jullien, Chinese thought vs philosophy, 2015)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.iii.2. CONNIVANCE, THE LOST PARADISE OF OUR CHILDHOOD?
Is connivance merely a vestige, maybe secretly nostalgic, but ultimately rooted in the past, the lost paradise of our childhood and humanity? Or does it continue to create a hidden but active web of understanding within us and among us, silently, beyond the realm of knowledge and ready to resurface?

E1.6.D.3.7.a.iii.2_REF1_PH FRANÇOIS JULLIEN EXPLORES THE IDEA OF CONNIVANCE BETWEEN INDIVIDUALS, SUGGESTING THAT IT CAN BE A SOURCE OF ACTIVE AND SUBTERRANEAN UNDERSTANDING. He illustrates how two lovers can experience this connivance by talking about "nothing", which strengthens their intimacy, where talking and remaining silent are equivalent, creating a space for intimate communication beyond formal knowledge.
"But is this connivance nothing more than a vestige for which we may secretly feel nostalgic, but which we have definitively closed off in the lost paradise of our childhood and of humanity? Or does it not always weave, silently, beneath the surface of knowledge and ready to resurface, an active, underlying foundation of understanding within us and between us? Don't two lovers experience this re-emergence between them? Or isn't it this re-emergence that makes them live as lovers? Isn't it this buried resource that they rediscover and nurture together, withdrawing from the commerce of knowledge and its busyness? To the point that they can talk to each other all day long without saying anything, in any case nothing that is worthy of interest, that can be made into an object, that can be remembered as a statement. Saying "nothing" that does nothing but express their complicity: "Have you seen this?" "Did you know that ... Beneath the banality of the exchange, as they breathe, they revive an agreement between them: the whole day is this blink of an eye-"loving chatter," as Rousseau put it. The fact that one can speak without saying anything can be understood in these two opposite senses: either it is in vain, speech is empty; or it is "full", if I may say so: they do not need to have "something" to say, as Aristotle wanted, since they speak only to communicate what is intimate between them. In this system of acquiescence, one can be silent as well as speaking, and the two are equivalent: to be silent is not muteness, not even reserve, it does not embarrass or burden one; and to speak, even to say nothing, is not talkativeness. [...]"
(François Jullien, Chinese thought vs philosophy, 2015)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.iv. HOW DO WE BECOME MORE LUCID?
Lucidity is a combined result: it is influenced by our experiences and our response to them. Not everyone who has undergone negative experiences becomes lucid; it also requires the individual's active engagement, allowing these experiences to be part of their reflection. Lucidity is formed from everything I've experienced, which has gradually dissolved and undone the obstructions clouding my consciousness. These obstructions often represent the mind's interpositions that unknowingly veil reality.

E1.6.D.3.7.a.iv_REF1_PH "CLARITY IS NOT INTELLIGENCE, WHOSE OWN QUALITY IS UNDERSTANDING. While intelligence, like language, is a faculty, even the most general, at least partially innate, which focuses on an object both spontaneously and in the moment, clarity, on the other hand, is not given to us, is not even the subject of study and training: it can only be achieved through a process and in a result-oriented manner - can this result even be communicated from one person to another? Lucidity is also not knowledge, which is more rooted in acquisition. While knowledge extends across fields and disciplines, lucidity is a global capacity that cannot be broken down or taught. If we also compare it with the terms that are given as synonyms for it, it seems that penetration and perspicacity (clairvoyance) presuppose that the mind has encountered a resistance - an opacity - and overcome it. Both refer prospectively to a situation whose difficulty is to be resolved. Their use requires a point of application, the former relying more on depth and the latter on clarity. But clarity, on the other hand, is the result of a process of becoming: one becomes clear through experience; it is achieved procedurally and through liberation: light comes of its own accord, by immanence, from all that one has lived and experienced. Insight and perspicacity denote an operative capacity of the mind; lucidity, a level attained by consciousness. While the former signifies the overcoming of an embarrassment that presents itself to the mind, the latter signifies the emergence from a state of obscurity in which one has allowed oneself to be deceived. Also, by signifying the emergence from the confusion in which one remained in one's past life, lucidity actually names the subject's ability to access the second life.
Strictly speaking, lucidity is not achieved by method or willpower. Can I even wish to become lucid? To tell the truth, I would prefer the opposite: to remain in a state of naive vagueness-a primitive confusion-that responds more directly to my desires; not to force myself to see reality stripped of its illusions or "as it is." While we would like to be more intelligent or have more knowledge, and even have a more perceptive or penetrating mind, wouldn't I, on the contrary, fear more clarity? Would I not rather want to protect myself from it? In any case, in order to understand what lucidity is, as well as the experience of the second type from which it proceeds, I must eliminate the opposition between the passive and the active that European language imposes; consequently, I must leave the register of intentionality and of free and conscious choice. For clarity, for its part, is co-creative: I was led to it by my experiences, and I myself contributed to it by taking them into account. Not all those who have had negative experiences have become lucid; it also requires the cooperation of the subject, who must accept to let them enter the field of his or her reflection. Clarity comes to me from all that I have experienced and that has been gradually dissolved and undone - eroded and fragmented, cracked and ruptured, by external coercion at the same time as personally assumed rectification - what clouded my consciousness as representations of the mind intervening without my knowledge and obscuring reality from me - "reality" then emerging from its nebulous ambiguity to signify precisely what remains after this withdrawal."
(François Jullien, A second life, 2017)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.iv_REF1.1_PH "CLARITY IS NOT DISCOVERY, BUT DISCOVERY. Like the refined outline of a watermark, lucidity is born of a stripping away that allows what is no longer embellished - neither coated nor fogged nor stuck - to emerge from beneath all that the mind has allowed to offend it. A "clear winter," as Mallarmé called it. [...] Indeed, from what is thus disinhibited, from all the 'languor' experienced, a 'gay knowledge' can result, as Nietzsche called it gaya scienza when describing illness (in his preface to The Gay Science). "Joyful," that is, alert, because of the joy of having finally taken the initiative, and of having done so by breaking through to the core of our connection with the vital, and thereby suddenly shattering all dogmatism at its foundations. Joyful because we have perceived life more at its roots, in its original risk-taking, because we have extracted more radicality from it, and because we are returning to the offensive, with the impetus of challenge, no longer afraid to dare and no longer wasting a minute of the little time that remains. [...] Plato said in one sentence how the clarity comes from this accumulated and decanted experience, from which we see the possibility of a second life: 
"Is it not inevitable, Theaetetus, that for the majority of those who heard such speeches at that age, a sufficient number of years will pass, advancing age, a closer examination of things, the trials that force them into clear contact with reality, will they not cause them to change the opinions they had then, to find small what seemed great to them, difficult what seemed easy, so that the illusions that the words conveyed will vanish before living reality? (Translation of A. Dies, Sophist, 234 d-e).
Plato clearly emphasizes the two common conditions: what time brings "as an addition," in other words, what is necessary to "advance" in life; but also that if we thus come "as close as possible" to the reality of things, toîs te oûsi prospiptontas egguthen [...], it is because we are forced to do so by the trials we have gone through. It also sheds light on its specific nature: that we then "touch" the "existing" in "full clarity" (ephaptesthai [...]: one puts one's "sure" finger), that is, at the same time one finds oneself placed in the light (enargôs [...]: this clarity is global) and one finds oneself directly in "contact" with things - such is indeed the effect of the contact that constitutes the experience. Finally, it illuminates the consequence: the reversal of the "opinions" formed in the mind, or rather suffered in this first stage of life. These representations, to which one previously adhered, were really "phantasms" based on discourse, and are now found to be "ruined".
(François Jullien, A second life, 2017)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.v. PLATON ASSERTS THAT IT IS IMPOSSIBLE FOR ANYONE TO DEVELOP COMPREHENSION WITHOUT BEING GUIDED BY A MASTER. Comprehension cannot be formulated as we do with sciences. The truth about these subjects can only be reached through personal experience. It's not beneficial for men to argue about these subjects, as only an elite can discover the truth for themselves with a few pointers. "It is only when names, definitions, perceptions of sight and impressions of the senses have been painstakingly rubbed against each other, when we have discussed in benevolent debates where envy dictates neither questions nor answers, that, on the object studied, the light of wisdom and intelligence shines with all the intensity that human forces can bear.”

E1.6.D.3.7.a.v_REF1_PH PLATO ASSERTS THAT IT IS IMPOSSIBLE TO FULLY UNDERSTAND CERTAIN COMPLEX ISSUES THROUGH READING ALONE. TRUTH EMERGES FROM THE MIND AFTER LONG REFLECTION AND INTERACTION WITH THE PROBLEMS. He argues that only those with a natural affinity for virtue and wisdom can achieve this understanding, and that writings on complex subjects are likely to be misinterpreted by others or lead to erroneous conclusions. Plato concludes that the writings should not be taken too seriously because the true thoughts of the author are often not expressed.
"In any case, this is what I can say about all those who have written or will write and who claim to be knowledgeable about what is the subject of my concern, having been taught about it by me or by others, or having discovered it personally: it is impossible, in my opinion, that they have understood anything at all about it. As far as I am concerned, there is no work on such subjects, and there certainly will never be. In fact, there is no way to put it into formulas, as is done with other sciences, but it is when one has dealt with these problems for a long time, when one has lived with them, that the truth suddenly springs forth in the soul, like light springs forth from a spark, and then grows of its own accord. No doubt I know that if I had to explain them in writing or orally, I would be the best person to do it; but I also know that if the explanation were faulty, I would suffer more than anyone else. If I had believed that they could be written down and sufficiently expressed for the people, what finer achievement could I have accomplished in my life than to demonstrate a doctrine so beneficial to mankind and to bring to light for all the true nature of things? Now, I do not think that arguing about it, as they say, is good for people, except for an elite who only need a few hints to discover the truth for themselves. As for the others, they would either be filled with unjust contempt, which is unbecoming, or with a vain and foolish sense of self-importance because of the grandeur of the teachings they have received. Moreover, I intend to elaborate on this subject: perhaps some of the points I am dealing with will become clearer once I have explained myself. There is, in fact, a serious reason why we should not try to write anything on such subjects, a reason which I have often stated before, but which I feel I must repeat again.

[...] In a word, those who have no affinity with the object will not obtain the vision either by their ease of mind or by their memory, because they will not first take root in a foreign nature. - Thus, whether it be those who have no inclination for what is just and beautiful and are not in harmony with these virtues, however well they may otherwise be gifted to learn and retain, or those who, having a kindred soul, are averse to science and lacking in memory, none of them will ever learn the whole truth about virtue and vice that it is possible to know. In fact, it is necessary to learn both at the same time, the false as well as the true of the whole essence, at the cost of much work and time, as I said at the beginning. Only when names, definitions, perceptions of sight and impressions of the senses have been painstakingly rubbed together, when they have been discussed in benevolent discussions where envy dictates neither questions nor answers, does the light of wisdom and intelligence shine on the object studied with all the intensity that human powers can bear. Therefore, every serious man will take care not to deal with serious questions in writing, thus exposing his thoughts to the envy and lack of intelligence of the crowd. From this we must draw the simple conclusion that when we see a written composition, whether by a legislator on laws, or by anyone else on any subject, we can be sure that the author has not taken the matter seriously, if he himself is serious, and that his thoughts are confined to the most valuable part of the writer".
(Plato, Letter VII, Text established by Joseph Souilhé, 1926)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.v_REF2_PH PLATO AFFIRMS THAT IT IS IMPOSSIBLE TO FULLY UNDERSTAND CERTAIN COMPLEX SUBJECTS THROUGH READING ALONE. TRUTH EMERGES FROM THE MIND AFTER LONG REFLECTION AND INTERACTION WITH THE PROBLEMS. He argues that only those with a natural affinity for virtue and wisdom can achieve this understanding, and that writings on complex subjects risk being misinterpreted or leading to erroneous conclusions by others. Plato concludes that writings should not be taken too seriously, as the true thoughts of the author often remain unexpressed.
"He who has no affinity with the object will develop a vision neither by his ease of mind, nor by his memory, - for in a foreign nature it will not take root. - Also, about those who have no propensity for the just and the beautiful and do not harmonize with these virtues, - as good as they may be otherwise at learning and memorizing, - or about those who, possessing the kinship of soul [with the virtues], are resistant to science and deprived of memory - none of them will ever learn about virtue and vice all the truth that it is possible to know about them. For indeed, it is necessary to learn both at the same time, the false as well as the true of the whole essence, putting in a lot of work and time [...] It is only after we have painfully rubbed against each other, names, definitions, perceptions of sight and impressions of the senses, after we have discussed them in benevolent discussions where envy does not dictate neither the questions nor the answers, that the light of wisdom and intelligence shines upon the object of study with all the intensity that human forces can withstand. That is why any serious man will be careful not to write down his thoughts on serious questions and thus abandon them to the envy and the ignorance of the crowd."
(Plato, Letter VII)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.vi. "UNDERSTAND HOW THE LEAST THING IS LINKED TO THE WHOLE; nothing exists in isolation. Therefore, the correct path often leads us beyond ourselves. In doing so, your thoughts will align with the universe, their true domain, and harmonize with the life of your body which is also interconnected with everything. Look into the distance.'

E1.6.D.3.7.a.vi_REF1_PH "THOUGHT MUST TRAVEL AND CONTEMPLATE, IF YOU WANT THE BODY TO FEEL GOOD. Science will lead us there, provided it is neither ambitious, nor talkative, nor impatient; provided that it distracts us from books and takes our gaze away, to the horizon. It must enable perception and travel. The true relations that you discover in an object lead you to another and to a thousand others, and this whirlwind of the river of thought carries your to the winds, to the clouds, and to the planets. True knowledge never comes down to something you have just before your eyes; for to know is to understand how the smallest thing relates to the whole; nothing has its reason in itself, and so the right direction is to move away from yourself; this is no less healthy for the mind than for the eyes. Thus your thought will rest in this universe which is its domain, and it will accord with the life of your body which is also linked to all things. When the Christian said: « Heaven is my domain », he could not put it better. Look far away."
(Alain, On happiness, May 15, 1911)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.vi_REF1.1_PH "TO THE MELANCHOLIC I HAVE ONLY ONE THING TO SAY: 'LOOK INTO THE DISTANCE. The melancholic is almost always a man who reads too much. The human eye is not made for this distance; it rests in wide open spaces. When you look at the stars or at the horizon of the sea, the eye is completely relaxed; when the eye is relaxed, the head is free, the gait is more secure; everything gradually loosens and softens, even down to the bowels. But don't try to make yourself more flexible with your will, because if you use your will on yourself, you'll make everything go wrong and end up strangling yourself; don't think about yourself, look into the distance.
It is very true that melancholy is a disease, and the doctor can sometimes guess the cause and prescribe the remedy, but this remedy brings the attention back to the body, and the worry about following a diet destroys its effect, which is why the doctor, if he is wise, refers you to the philosopher. But when you run to the philosopher, what do you find? A man who reads too much, who thinks myopically, and who is sadder than you.
The state should hold wisdom as a school of medicine. And how? By true science, which is the contemplation of things, and by poetry as great as the world. For the mechanics of our eyes, resting on the wide horizons, teach us a great truth. Thought must free the body and return it to the universe, which is our true home. There is a deep kinship between our human destiny and the functions of our body. As soon as the animals get no more from the world around them, they lie down and sleep; man thinks, and if it is an animal thought, woe to him. There he is, doubling his sufferings and his needs; there he is, working himself up with fear and hope; as a result of which his body never ceases to tense, to move, to launch, to hold back, according to the games of the imagination; always suspecting, always spying on things and people around him. And when he goes to free himself, he finds himself in books, in a universe that is still closed. Too close to his eyes. Too close to his passions. The mind becomes a prison and the body suffers, because to say that the mind narrows and to say that the body works against itself is to say the same thing. The ambitious man rehearses his speeches a thousand times, and the lover rehearses his prayers a thousand times. The mind must travel and contemplate if the body is to be well.
Wherever science may lead us, provided it is not ambitious, talkative, impatient; provided it takes our attention away from books and directs our gaze to the distant horizon. So it must be about perception and travel. One object, through the true relationships you discover there, leads you to another and to a thousand others, and this whirlwind of the river carries your thoughts to the winds, to the clouds, and to the planets. True knowledge never returns to some small thing close to the eyes; for to know is to understand how the smallest thing is connected with the whole; no thing has its reason in itself, and so the right movement takes us away from ourselves; this is no less healthy for the mind than for the eyes. There your thoughts will rest in this universe, which is their domain, and will be in harmony with the life of your body, which is also connected with all things. When the Christian said, "The sky is my home," he was not wrong. Look into the distance."
(Alain, On Happiness, May 15, 1911)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.vi_REF1.2_PO “WHEN I'M SAD, I GET UP AND GO AROUND THE WORLD”.
(Tomaž Šalamun)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.vi_REF2_PH "OUTLOOK

The open day is bright with images for people,
when the green shows itself from a distance,
even before the evening light begins to twilight,
and shimmers gently soften the sound of the day.
Often the inner world of the world seems clouded, closed,
man's mind full of doubts, sullen,
the splendid nature brightens his days
and the dark question of doubt is far away."
(Friedrich Hölderin, 1671)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.vii. THE MACHINES CAN ONLY EVER APPROACH [THE EXPERIENCE OF BEING] SECONDHAND. BUT SECONDHAND IS PRECISELY WHAT BEING HERE ISN’T. THE WORK OF BEING HERE—OF LIVING, SENSING, CHOOSING—STILL AWAITS US. AND THERE IS PLENTY OF IT. Being human is not to have answers. It is to have questions—and to live with them. The machines can’t do that for us. Not now, not ever.
“For the past seventy years or so, the university humanities have largely lost sight of this core truth. Seduced by the rising prestige of the sciences—on campus and in the culture—humanists reshaped their work to mimic scientific inquiry. We have produced abundant knowledge about texts and artifacts, but in doing so mostly abandoned the deeper questions of being which give such work its meaning.

Now everything must change. That kind of knowledge production has, in effect, been automated. As a result, the “scientistic” humanities—the production of fact-based knowledge about humanistic things—are rapidly being absorbed by the very sciences that created the A.I. systems now doing the work. We’ll go to them for the “answers.”

But to be human is not to have answers. It is to have questions—and to live with them. The machines can’t do that for us. Not now, not ever.

And so, at last, we can return—seriously, earnestly—to the reinvention of the humanities, and of humanistic education itself. We can return to what was always the heart of the matter—the lived experience of existence. Being itself.

All that surfaces anew, because we are left alone with that. It alone cannot be taken from us. […]

Historians have long extolled the “power of the archive.” Little did we know that the engineers would come along and *plug it in*. And it turns out that a huge amount of what we seek from a human person can be simulated through this Frankensteinian reanimation of our collective dead letters. What a discovery! We have a new whole of ourselves with which to converse now. Let’s take our time; there is plenty to learn.

But we’ll need vigilance, and a fighting courage, too, as we again take up this unending experience of coming into ourselves as free beings responsible for world-making. Because it is, of course, possible to turn the crank that instrumentalizes people, to brutalize them, to squeeze their humanity into a sickly green trickle called money and leave only a ruinous residue. The new machines are already pretty good at that. The algorithms that drive these systems are the same algorithms that drive the attention economy, remember? They will only get better.

What it is like to be us, in our full humanity—this isn’t out there in the interwebs. It isn’t stored in any archive, and the neural networks cannot be inward with what it feels like to be you, right now, looking at these words, looking away from these words to think about your life and our lives, turning from all this to your day and to what you will do in it, with others or alone. That can only be lived.

This remains to us. The machines can only ever approach it secondhand. But secondhand is precisely what being here isn’t. The work of being here—of living, sensing, choosing—still awaits us. And there is plenty of it.”
(D. Graham Burnett, Will the Humanities Survive Artificial Intelligence?, published in The Newyorker, April 26, 2025)

Read it in The Newyorker: https://www.newyorker.com/culture/the-weekend-essay/will-the-humanities-survive-artificial-intelligence

E1.6.D.3.7.a.viii. NIETZSCHE ALSO DENOUNCE A LACK OF UNDERSTANDING:
"The main cause of imperfection in social life lies in ignorance about what is detrimental in the setup of life, the division of time, the choice of relationships, in both work and leisure, command and obedience, the sensations from nature and art, as well as eating, sleeping, and thinking. It's the ignorance in the most trivial and everyday matters."

E1.6.D.3.7.a.viii_REF1_PH "THE IMPERFECTION OF THE EARTH AND ITS MAIN CAUSE. - 
[...] Not to know what is harmful to us in the order of existence, the division of the day, the weather, and the choice of relationships, in business and leisure, in command and obedience, in the sensations of nature and art, in eating, sleeping, and thinking, to be ignorant in the most trivial and everyday matters - this is what makes the earth a "meadow of shame" for so many people. Let it not be said that here, as everywhere, it is a question of the lack of reason in men: on the contrary, there is reason enough and more than enough, but it is misdirected and artificially diverted from these petty and immediate things. Priests, teachers, and the sublime tyranny of idealists of all sorts, both coarse and refined, persuade the child that it is a question of something quite different: the salvation of the soul, the service of the State, the progress of science, or even consideration and property as a means of rendering service to the whole of humanity, while the needs of the individual, his great and small necessities in the twenty-four hours of the day, are, it is said, something contemptible or indifferent. Socrates already warned against this arrogant neglect of man in favor of man, and he liked to remind us, by quoting Homer, of the limits of the true object of all care and reflection: "It is, he said, and it is only 'what happens to me for good or ill.'. [...]"
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human III, Aphorism 6,  1878)

E1.6.D.3.7.a.ix. “MATURATION (VS MODELLING) [...]
I find it remarkable that the ancient Chinese art of war, as I have mentioned, does not consider modelling and application, or the means to an end, but rather conditions and consequences, and consequently processes and maturation. As we know, a good general is not one who draws up a plan in advance, but one who, based on an 'assessment' of the encountered conditions (the ancient meaning of 'ji'), detects the advantageous factors and realises this potential. Even this 'bringing' is too active and directive. It is better to say that he promotes the development of these favourable conditions by exploiting what constitutes a favourable 'terrain' [...]; or, as Laozi says, he helps what comes of its own accord. The art of strategy is therefore to ensure that the desired result proceeds from the situational development involved. There is no "goal" to aim for strictly speaking, but rather a "profit" to be "reaped" (harvested, written li: the ear of corn and the sickle to cut it). This is why, as long as these conditions are not ripe, he does not intervene or 'act'; when he acts, he no longer really has to 'act' — the famous theme of 'non-action', wu wei, which is not at all a renunciation or passivity: success then comes to him like a ripe fruit ready to fall. This requires not so much patience — a quality that is still too moral and psychological, and therefore linked to the subject — as the ability to allow the situation to produce its own effects from the 'seed' that has been sown, and to accompany its development as it takes place independently. I wait until the situation has reached maturity before intervening, and even then, only sparingly. [...]
cording to Mencius (II, A, 2), a farmer must refrain from 'pulling on the shoots' to make them grow; in other words, he must not seek the desired effect directly. Otherwise, he would be forcing the process already underway and the plant would soon wither. By importing and imposing his project, he would produce the opposite effect. But should he then do the opposite and remain passively at the edge of the field, simply watching it grow? I wait for it to grow... What should he 'do'? Every farmer knows that, from one day to the next and at intervals, they must hoe and weed at the foot of the shoot to encourage growth. Neither pull on the shoots (activism) nor simply watch them grow (passivity); neither force the process nor ignore it. There is nothing brilliant or heroic about this ('there is nothing to praise in the great general...'), but it is modest and discreet, and it alone is effective. Growth can be initiated, stimulated and assisted, but it happens by itself in its course.
This is true of all maturation, including that of the inner self, which, as Mencius tells us, follows its own course, just like the flow of water. Water only advances gradually — let us understand this seemingly simple phrase, which says so much: 'Until it has filled the cavity in its path, water does not go any further' (VII, A, 24). Now, 'penetrating' and 'understanding' are also processes of maturation. They involve accumulation and gradual progression, producing self-unfolding beyond our control. It is impossible to skip steps, leap over milestones or force understanding. Conversely, each time water fills the cavity it encounters, it overflows of its own accord in order to move forward. It continues to progress unperturbed, carried by its own momentum. The same is true of the mind: the light we have gained through our efforts spreads gradually, spreading and 'communicating' from one thought to another and from one reason to the next without our knowledge, spontaneously, through branching connections. This continues until, one day, we finally reap the fruit of our labour, like a hard-won victory, and suddenly realise that we have 'an idea', 'found' it.
(François Jullien, Chinese Thought vs. Philosophy, 2015)

E1.6.D.3.7.b. UNDERSTANDING THAT COMES WITH FULL AWARENESS IS THE FEELING THAT THE WORLD IS AN ORGANIC UNITY
"If a problem can be solved at all, to understand it and to know what to do about it are the same thing. On the other hand, doing something about a problem which you do not understand is like trying to clear away darkness by thrusting it aside with your hands. Light, here, means awareness - to be aware of life, of experience as it is at this moment, without any judgments or ideas about it. In other words, you have to see and feel what you are experiencing as it is, and not as it is named."
The transformation of one's understanding that comes with full awareness is the feeling that the world is an organic unity, rather than feeling separate from everything else. People can reason that the universe is a unity without feeling it to be so, and they can continue to feel separate and lonely even if they have the knowledge of the unity of the universe in theory. 

E1.6.D.3.7.b.i. IS THERE AN EXPERIENCER OR THINKER SEPARATE FROM THE EXPERIENCE?
Approach experiences, sensations, feelings, and thoughts with a fresh perspective, without prejudice, and observe what is happening in the present moment: question whether there is an experiencer or thinker separate from the experience? When we are aware of our thoughts, feelings, sensations, and desires, we cannot find an experiencer or thinker separate from the experience and the feeling that there is one is an illusion.

E1.6.D.3.7.b.i_REF1_PH "UNDERSTANDING COMES THROUGH AWARENESS. Can we, then, approach our experience - our sensations, feelings, and thoughts-quite simply, as if we had never known them before, and, without prejudice, look at what is going on? You may ask, "Which experiences, which sensations and feelings, shall we look at?" I will answer, "Which ones can you look at?" The answer is that you must look at the ones you have now.

That is surely rather obvious. But very obvious things are often overlooked. If a feeling is not present, you are not aware of it. There is no experience but present experience. What you know, what you are actually aware of, is just what is happening at this moment, and no more. [...]

While you are watching this present experience, are you aware of someone watching it? Can you find, in addition to experience itself, an experiencer? Can you, at the same time, read this sentence and think about yourself reading it? You will find that, to think about yourself reading it, you must for a brief second stop reading. The first experience is reading. The second experience is the thought, "I am reading." Can you find any thinker, who is thinking the thought, "I am reading?" In other words, when present experience is the thought, "I am reading," can you think about yourself thinking this thought?

Once again, you must stop thinking just, "I am reading." You pass to a third experience, which is the thought, "I am thinking that I am reading." Do not let the rapidity with which these thoughts can change deceive you into the feeling that you think them all at once. [...]

To be aware, then, is to be aware of thoughts, feelings, sensations, desires, and all other forms of experience. Never at any time are you aware of anything which is not experience, not a thought or feeling, but instead an experiencer, thinker, or feeler. If this is so, what makes us think that any such thing exists? [...]"
(Alan Watts, The wisdom of insecurity, 1951)

E1.6.D.3.7.b.ii. THE IDEA OF PROTECTING AN "I" IS AN ILLUSION
The notion of a separate thinker or "I" that is distinct from experience is an illusion created by memory and the rapidity of thought changes; so is our imagining memory as a direct knowledge of the past rather than a present experience. Trying to separate oneself from experience is impossible, and  there is no "I" apart from some present experience. Understanding this is to realize that life is entirely momentary, that there is no permanence nor security, and that the idea of protecting an "I" is an illusion.

E1.6.D.3.7.b.ii_REF1_PH "THE NOTION OF A SEPARATE THINKER, OF AN "I" DISTINCT FROM THE EXPERIENCE, COMES FROM MEMORY AND FROM THE RAPIDITY WITH WHICH THOUGHT CHANGES. It is like whirling a burning stick to give the illusion of a continuous circle of fire. If you imagine that memory is a direct knowledge of the past rather than a present experience, you get the illusion of knowing the past and the present at the same time. This suggests that there is something in you distinct from both the past and the present experiences. You reason, "I know this present experience, and it is different from that past experience. If I can compare the two, and notice that experience has changed, I must be something constant and apart."

But, as a matter of fact, you cannot compare this present experience with a past experience. You can only compare it with a memory of the past, which is a part of the present experience. When you see clearly that memory is a form of present experience, it will be obvious that trying to separate yourself from this experience is as impossible as trying to make your teeth bite themselves. There is simply experience. There is not something or someone experiencing experience! You do not feel feelings, think thoughts, or sense sensations any more than you hear hearing, see sight, or smell smelling. “I feel fine" means that a fine feeling is present. It does not mean that there is one thing called an "I" and another separate thing called a feeling, so that when you bring them together this "I" feels the fine feeling. There are no feelings but present feelings, and whatever feeling is present is "I." No one ever found an "I" apart from some present experience, or some experience apart from an "I" - which is only to say that the two are the same thing.

As a mere philosophical argument this is a waste of time. We are not trying to have an "intellectual discussion." We are being aware of the fact that any separate "I" who thinks thoughts and experiences experience is an illusion. To understand this is to realize that life is entirely momentary, that there is neither permanence nor security, and that there is no "I" which can be protected. [...]"
(Alan Watts, The wisdom of insecurity, 1951)

E1.6.D.3.7.b.iii. YOU MAY "KNOW" THE WORLD AND YOURSELF ARE A UNITY BUT DO NOT FEEL IT TO BE TRUE
The transformation of one's understanding that comes with full awareness is the feeling that the world is an organic unity, rather than feeling separate from everything else. Without awareness, people may "know" this as a matter of information but do not feel it to be true. Most people feel separate from their surroundings because they are split within themselves. Being aware of the unreality of this division, the universe does not seem foreign anymore.
People are not only what they know, but also what they don't know. The sensation of a house across the street or a star in outer space is no less "I" than an itch on the sole of their foot or an idea in their brain. The world is an inextricably interwoven process called the real world and, whether conscious or unconscious of it, it is all "I" in the sense that the sun, the air, and human society are just as vital to a person as their brain or their lungs.

E1.6.D.3.7.b.iii_REF1_PH "VII.THE TRANSFORMATION OF LIFE
[...] Something must therefore be said about the healed vision of life which comes with full awareness, for it involves a deep transformation of our view of the world. As well as words can describe it, this transformation consists in knowing and feeling that the world is an organic unity.

In the ordinary way, we "know" this as a matter of information but do not feel it to be true. Certainly most people feel separate from Everything that surrounds them. On the one hand there is myself, and on the other the rest of the universe. I am not rooted in the earth like a tree. I rattle around independently. I seem to be the center of everything, and yet cut off and alone. I can feel what is going on inside my own body, but I can only guess what is going on in others. My conscious mind must have its roots and origins in the most unfathomable depths of being, yet it feels as if it lived all by itself in this tight little skull.

Nevertheless, the physical reality is that my body exists only in relation to this universe, and in fact I am as attached to it and dependent on it as a leaf on a tree. I feel cut off only because I am split within myself, because I try to be divided from my own feelings and sensations. What I feel and sense therefore seems foreign to me. And on being aware of the unreality of this division, the universe does not seem foreign any more.

For I am what I know; what I know is I. The sensation of a house across the street or of a star in outer space is no less I than an itch on the sole of my foot or an idea in my brain. In another sense, I am also what I do not know. I am not aware of my own brain as a brain. In just the same way, I am not aware of the house across the street as a thing apart from my sensation of it. I know my brain as thoughts and feelings, and I know the house as sensations. In the same way and sense that I do not know my own brain, or the house as a thing-in-itself, I do not know the private thoughts in your brain.

But my brain, which is also I, your brain and the thoughts within it, as well as the house across the street, are all forms of an inextricably interwoven process called the real world. Conscious or unconscious of it as I may be, it is all I in the sense that the sun, the air, and human society are just as vital to me as my brain or my lungs. If, then, this brain is my brain-unaware of it as I am - the sun is my sun, the air my air, and society my society. [...]"
(Alan Watts, The wisdom of insecurity, 1951)

E1.6.D.3.7.b.iv. YOU HAVE TO FEEL IT!
People can reason that the universe is a unity without feeling it to be so, and they can continue to feel separate and lonely even if they have the knowledge of the unity of the universe in theory. They will only cease to feel isolated when they  develop the understanding that they are not separate from the world.

E1.6.D.3.7.b.iv_REF1_PH "V. ON BEING AWARE
The Question "WHAT SHALL WE DO ABOUT IT?" is only asked by those who do not understand the problem. If a problem can be solved at all, to understand it and to know what to do about it are the same thing. On the other hand, doing something about a problem which you do not understand is like trying to clear away darkness by thrusting it aside with your hands. When light is brought, the darkness vanishes at once. [...]

Light, here, means awareness-to be aware of life, of experience as it is at this moment, without any judgments or ideas about it. In other words, you have to see and feel what you are experiencing as it is, and not as it is named. This very simple "opening of the eyes" brings about the most extraordinary transformation of understanding and living, and shows that many of our most baffling problems are pure illusion. This may sound like an oversimplification because most people imagine themselves to be fully enough aware of the present already, but we shall see that this is far from true.¹ "

Note 1: The word "awareness" is used in the sense given to it by J. Krishnamurti, whose writings discuss this theme with extraordinary perception.

(Alan Watts, The wisdom of insecurity, 1951)

E1.6.D.3.7.b.iv_REF2_PH TRUE UNITY IS FELT WHEN ONE RECOGNIZES THEIR INSEPARABILITY FROM THEIR SENSATIONS AND EXPERIENCES, RATHER THAN MERELY REASONING IT. Genuine understanding transcends verbal expression and philosophical declarations, embodying a direct, unmediated experience of being part of the whole.
"Now there have been many theories about the unity of the universe. But they have not delivered human beings from the isolation of egotism, from conflict, and from the fear of life, because there is a world of difference between an inference and a feeling. You can reason that the universe is a unity without feeling it to be so. You can establish the theory that your body is a movement in an unbroken process which includes all suns and stars, and yet continue to feel separate and lonely. For the feeling will not correspond to the theory until you have also discovered the unity of inner experience. Despite all theories, you will feel that you are isolated from life so long as you are divided within.

But you will cease to feel isolated when you recognize, for example, that you do not have a sensation of the sky: you are that sensation. [...]

Plucking chrysanthemums along the East fence; 
Gazing in silence at the southern hills; 
The birds flying home in pairs
Through the soft mountain air of dusk 
In these things there is a deep meaning, 
But when we are about to express it, 
We suddenly forget the words.

The meaning is not the contemplative, twilight, and, perhaps, superficially idyllic atmosphere beloved of Chinese poets. This is already expressed, and the poet does not gild the lily. He will not, like so many Western poets, turn philosopher and say that he is "one with" the flowers, the fence, the hills, and the birds. This, too, is gilding the lily, or, in his own Oriental idiom, "putting legs on a snake." For when you really understand that you are what you see and know, you do not run around the countryside thinking, "I am all this." There is simply "all this."
(Alan Watts, The wisdom of insecurity, 1951)

E1.6.D.3.7.b.iv_REF2.1_PH ALAN WATTS ARGUES THAT THE PERCEPTION OF BEING SEPARATE FROM THE WORLD INFLUENCES THOUGHT AND ACTION, LEADING PHILOSOPHERS TO OVERLOOK THAT THEIR STATEMENTS ABOUT THE UNIVERSE ALSO APPLY TO THEMSELVES. He asserts that true understanding of reality requires being part of it rather than attempting to stand outside and describing it, which leads to the inherent failure of philosophical and theological systems.
"The feeling that we stand face-to-face with the world, cut off and set apart, has the greatest influence on thought and action. Philosophers, for example, often fail to recognize that their remarks about the universe apply also to themselves and their remarks. If the universe is meaningless, so is the statement that it is so. If this world is a vicious trap, so is its accuser, and the pot is calling the kettle black.

In the strictest sense, we cannot actually think about life and reality at all, because this would have to include thinking about thinking, thinking about thinking about thinking, and so ad infinitum. One can only attempt a rational, descriptive philosophy of the universe on the assumption that one is totally separate from it. But if you and your thoughts are part of this universe, you cannot stand outside them to describe them. This is why all philosophical and theological systems must ultimately fall apart. To "know" reality you cannot stand outside it and define it; you must enter into it, be it, and feel it.

Speculative philosophy, as we know it in the West, is almost entirely a symptom of the divided mind, of man trying to stand outside himself and his experience in order to verbalize and define it. It is a vicious circle, like everything else which the divided mind attempts."
(Alan Watts, The wisdom of insecurity, 1951)

E1.6.D.3.7.b.v_PH ON THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN UNDERSTANDING AND CONSCIOUSNESS: UNDERSTANDING IS TO BE CULTIVATED, CONSCIOUSNESS IS TO BE COMPREHENDED.
“Consciousness emerges out of the entire complex of interactions between an organism and its environment. Consciousness cannot be said to be the exclusive product of either something physical, whether an external sense object or a brain, or something mental, whether a feeling, a perception, or an inclination. Gotama recognizes that consciousness is a seamless whole that is not equivalent to the sum of its parts, much in the same way that a hand as a whole is not reducible to the sum of its fingers, skin, bones, nerves, and muscles. Just as my hand can pick up a glass of water and raise it to my lips (which none of the parts can do on their own), so consciousness has a total, unified awareness of what is happening that none of its constituents (feeling, perception, inclination, etc.) can achieve on their own. To know something (the defining quality of consciousness) differs from merely perceiving something (the quality of perception), because it is a holistic awareness in which all the nama factors of touch, feeling, perception, intention, and attention are integrated. By implication, for Gotama there can be no such thing as “pure” consciousness, an unconditioned or pristine “knowing” that exists independently of the phenomenal world of discrete physical things and mental processes. […]
