If consciousness is produced by eyes and forms, then it is “visual consciousness”; if it is produced by ears and sounds, then it is “auditory consciousness,” and so on. Consciousness is as impermanent, contingent, compounded, and varied as anything else in experience. Open your eyes upon a blue summer sky, and visual consciousness occurs; close your eyes again, and that consciousness is gone. By contrast, we instinctively feel that consciousness lurks as a singular, constant “witness” somewhere in the background, waiting for the opportunity to see, hear, smell, taste, or touch something. As though to dispel this felt-sense of consciousness’s priority and privilege, Gotama emphasizes its derivative and mundane nature. He likens it to different kinds of fires. “Just as a fire is reckoned by what it burns—when it depends on logs, it’s called a ‘log fire,’ when it depends on dung, it’s called a ‘dung fire,’ . . . so consciousness too is reckoned by the particular conditions that give rise to it.

Sariputta, in his dialogue with Maha Kotthita, also presents consciousness as an emergent property, as something that occurs when the necessary conditions for it are in place. He denies that it is something to be refined or developed through dharma practice. Maha Kotthita asks him to explain the relation between consciousness (viññana) and understanding (pañña) or, as it is often translated, wisdom (note that in Pali both terms are rooted in ñña, “to know”). Sariputta says: “Understanding and consciousness, friend—these states are conjoined not disjoined. You cannot separate one from the other in order to describe the difference between them. For what you understand, you are conscious of; and what you are conscious of, you understand. The difference between them is this: understanding is to be cultivated (bhavana), consciousness is to be comprehended (pariñña).”
(Stephen Batchelor, After Buddhism, rethinking the dharma for a secular age, 2016)

E1.6.D.3.7.b.vi_PH ANY DIFFICULTY WE MIGHT HAVE IN SEEING OR AWAKENING TO THE BUDDHA’S TEACHING IS NOT PRIMARILY CONCEPTUAL BUT EXISTENTIAL.
“Since Gotama describes his awakening as a radical shift  in perspective from a “place” to a “ground,” the experience was more than just arriving at the solution to an intellectually challenging problem. It is true that Buddhist orthodoxies often present “conditioned arising” and “nirvana” as subtle and difficult concepts to grasp, yet their definitions in the Pali discourses are not that hard to understand. “Conditioned arising” boils down to the underlying principle of contingency, whereas “nirvana” is simply the ending (for shorter or longer durations) of reactivity. Any difficulty we might have in seeing or awakening to them is not primarily conceptual but existential. […] Conditioned arising and nirvana are not difficult to see and awaken to because they are tricky to grasp with the intellect. They are difficult to see and awaken to because they confront us with a groundless ground that can be overwhelming and terrifying. Conditioned arising entails that life is endlessly fluid, contingent, and unpredictable; nirvana implies that in the absence of our default reactions we find ourselves facing moral dilemmas that require us to risk a response that might make things worse.”
(Stephen Batchelor, After Buddhism, rethinking the dharma for a secular age, 2016)

E1.6.D.3.7.b.vii_PH TO EMBRACE LIFE WITH COMPREHENSION INVOLVES COPING. IT HAS MORE TO DO WITH HOW WE GET ABOUT, DEAL WITH CONFLICTS, REALIZE POSSIBILITIES, AND ENGAGE WITH OTHERS THAN WITH ACQUIRING KNOWLEDGE OF THE NATURE OF THE MIND OR REALITY.
“Suffering (dukkha) is to be comprehended (pariñña).
As the first step in moving from a vision of a twofold ground to the practice of a fourfold task, Gotama encourages comprehension: pariñña—literally, “total knowing.” The prefix pari (total) denotes “around, round about, all round, i.e. completely, altogether.” Such knowing is not concerned with the acquisition of knowledge about anything specific but with a holistic comprehension of a situation at a given moment. The task of knowing requires considering one’s situation from a range of different angles and perspectives.
Here is the classical definition of the dukkha (suffering) to be comprehended:

Birth is dukkha, aging is dukkha, sickness is dukkha, death is
dukkha, encountering what is not dear is dukkha, separation
from what is dear is dukkha, not getting what one wants is
dukkha. In short, these five bundles of clinging are dukkha

[…] The Buddha had no interest in describing what reality is. He is not a metaphysician or an ontologist. He wants people to start paying attention to features of their experience that they habitually overlook or ignore. His reason is entirely pragmatic: by not paying heed to the tragic dimension of life, we become enamored, seduced, and captivated by what is merely agreeable, which leads to cycles of reactive and addictive behavior that keep us trapped, frustrated, and afflicted. Comprehension, by contrast, encompasses the totality of what is happening: it is to embrace a life permeated equally by pain and pleasure, suffering and joy. […]

To embrace life with comprehension involves coping. It has more to do with how we get about, deal with conflicts, realize possibilities, and engage with others than with acquiring knowledge of the nature of the mind or reality. Comprehension requires the opposite of aloofness; it requires being embedded in a culture, a language, a society, not to mention a flesh-and-blood body that inhales and exhales, drinks and eats, pisses and shits.
The kind of knowing entailed in pariñña may therefore be more akin to connaître than to savoir, or to kennen than to wissen, to use French and German comparisons. […]

On one occasion at Savatthi, Gotama posed the rhetorical question “And what, bhikkhus, is comprehension (pariñña)?” to which he replied: “The ending of greed, the ending of hatred, the ending of confusion. That is called ‘comprehension.’” Such comprehension is neither inflected nor determined by the habitual reactivity of being greedy, full of hate, or confused. In positive terms, we might describe it as an understanding that is penhearted, clearheaded, compassionate, and equanimous.
We have already seen that the phrase “the ending of greed, the ending of hatred, the ending of confusion” is the definition of nirvana. If “comprehension” and “nirvana” are synonymous, then the four tasks must overlap. If the first task, to comprehend suffering, is equivalent to the ending of greed, hatred, and confusion, how does it differ from the third task, to behold the ceasing (of greed, hatred, confusion)—that is, the achievement of nirvana? The tasks emphasize different facets of a single experience. At times we might focus on comprehending the world in which we are embedded, and at other times we might focus on being aware that comprehension is devoid of attachment, aversion, and vanity. For this reason, I prefer to think of the tasks as combined into a single fourfold task.”
(Stephen Batchelor, After Buddhism, rethinking the dharma for a secular age, 2016)

E1.6.D.3.7.c. THE FEYNMAN TECHNIQUE HELPS TURN INFORMATION INTO EASILY ACCESSIBLE AND UNDERSTANDABLE KNOWLEDGE. It's based on the principle that information is truly learned when it can be explained and applied in various situations. This technique aids in identifying gaps in understanding and helps organize and simplify information for better retention.

E1.6.D.3.7.c.i. THE FEYNMAN TECHNIQUE HELPS TURN INFORMATION INTO EASILY ACCESSIBLE AND UNDERSTANDABLE KNOWLEDGE. It's based on the principle that information is truly learned when it can be explained and applied in various situations. This technique aids in identifying gaps in understanding and helps organize and simplify information for better retention.
The technique involves four steps: 
1. pretending to teach it to a child, 
2. identifying gaps in your explanation, 
3. organizing and simplifying the information,
4. reviewing the material frequently.

E1.6.D.3.7.c.i_REF1_PH "THE FEYNMAN LEARNING TECHNIQUE
The Feynman Technique is the best way to supercharge your learning. And it works no matter the subject. Devised by Nobel Prize-winning physicist Richard Feynman, it leverages the power of teaching for better learning. [...]

Information is learned when you can explain it and use it in a wide variety of situations. The Feynman Technique gets more mileage from the ideas you encounter instead of rendering anything new into isolated, useless factoids.

When you really learn something, you give yourself a tool to use for the rest of your life. The more you know, the fewer surprises you will encounter because most new things will connect to something you already understand.

Ultimately, the point of learning is to understand the world. But most of us don’t bother to deliberately learn anything.

We memorize what we need to as we move through school, then forget most of it. As we continue through life, we don’t extrapolate from our experiences to broaden the applicability of our knowledge. Consequently, life kicks us in the ass time and again.

To avoid the pain of being bewildered by the unexpected, the Feynman Technique helps you turn information into knowledge that you can access as easily as reaching for a chair. [...]

There are four steps to the Feynman Learning Technique, based on the method Richard Feynman originally used. We have adapted it slightly after reflecting on our own experiences using this process to learn. The steps are as follows [...]:

Step 1: Pretend to teach it to a child

Take out a blank sheet of paper. At the top, write the subject you want to master. Now write out everything you know about the subject as if you were teaching it to a child [...]

It turns out that one of the ways we mask our lack of understanding is by using complicated vocabulary and jargon. The truth is, if you can’t clearly and simply define the words and terms you are using, you don’t really know what you’re talking about. [...]

Some of capturing what you would teach will be easy. These are the places where you have a clear understanding of the subject. But you will find many places where things are much foggier.

Step 2: Identify gaps in your explanation

Areas, where you struggle in Step 1, are the points where you have some gaps in your understanding.

Identifying gaps in your knowledge—where you forget something important, aren’t able to explain it, or simply have trouble thinking of how variables interact—is a critical part of the learning process.

Filling those gaps is when you really make the learning stick.

Now that you know where you have gaps in your understanding go back to the source material. Augment it with other sources. Look up definitions. Keep going until you can explain everything you need to in basic terms.

Only when you can explain your understanding without jargon and in simple terms can you demonstrate understanding. Think about it this way. If you require complicated terminology to explain what you know, you have no flexibility. When someone asks you a question, you can only repeat what you’ve already said.

Simple terms can be rearranged and easily combined with other words to communicate your point. When you can say something in multiple ways using different words, you understand it really well.
Being able to explain something in a simple, accessible way shows you’ve done the work required to learn. Skipping it leads to the illusion of knowledge—an illusion that can be quickly shattered when challenged.

Identifying the boundaries of your understanding is also a way of defining your circle of competence. When you know what you know (and are honest about what you don’t know), you limit the mistakes you’re liable to make and increase your chance of success when applying knowledge.

Step 3. Organize and simplify

Now you have a set of hand-crafted notes containing a simple explanation. Organize them into a narrative that you can tell from beginning to end. Read it out loud. If the explanation sounds confusing at any point, go back to Step 2. Keep iterating until you have a story that you can tell to anyone who will listen.

If you follow this approach over and over, you will end up with a binder full of pages on different subjects. If you take some time twice a year to go through this binder, you will find just how much you retain.

Step 4: Transmit 

This part is optional, but it’s the logical result of everything you’ve just done.

If you really want to be sure of your understanding, run it past someone (ideally someone who knows little of the subject).

The ultimate test of your knowledge is your capacity to convey it to another. [...]
The questions you get and the feedback you receive are invaluable for further developing your understanding.

Hearing what your audience is curious about will likely pique your own curiosity and set you on a path for further learning. After all, it’s only when you begin to learn a few things really well do you appreciate how much there is to know.

[...]

What does it mean to “know?”
Richard Feynman believed that “the world is much more interesting than any one discipline.” He understood the difference between knowing something and knowing the name of something, as well as how, when you truly know something, you can use that knowledge broadly.

When you only know what something is called, you have no real sense of what it is.

You can’t take it apart and play with it or use it to make new connections and generate new insights. When you know something, the labels are unimportant because it’s not necessary to keep it in the box it came in. [...]

Knowing the name of something doesn’t mean you understand it. We talk in fact-deficient, obfuscating generalities to cover up our lack of understanding."
(FS Brain Food)

E1.6.D.3.7.c.ii. PRACTICE VS. CONCEPTS
Richard Feynman, a Nobel Prize-winning physicist, criticized a first-grade science book that attempted to teach kids about energy by showing pictures of a wind-up dog toy and asking "What makes it move?". Feynman argued that this approach is too abstract and does not impart real understanding. He suggested a more useful approach would be to take the toy apart and see how it works, learn about the way it's put together, and the ingenuity of people who made it.

E1.6.D.3.7.c.ii_REF1_PH “STUDY HARD WHAT INTERESTS YOU THE MOST IN THE MOST UNDISCIPLINED, IRREVERENT, AND ORIGINAL MANNER POSSIBLE.”
(Richard Feynman)

E1.6.D.3.7.c.ii_REF2_PH FEYNMAN EMPHASIZED THAT TRUE UNDERSTANDING COMES FROM BREAKING DOWN AND RECOMBINING CONCEPTS, which involves explaining ideas in simple language to reveal gaps in understanding.
"Nobel Prize-winning physicist Richard Feynman describes a dismal first-grade science book that attempts to teach kids about energy by showing a series of pictures about a wind-up dog toy and asking, “What makes it move?”

For Feynman, this was the wrong approach because it was too abstract. You can easily fool yourself into thinking you understand something when you don't. Saying that energy made the dog move was equal to saying ‘movability makes it move.’

Staying at the level of the abstract imparts no real understanding. Kids might subsequently get the question right on a test if they have a decent memory. But they aren’t going to have any real understanding of what energy actually is.

Echoing Albert Einstein, Feynman goes on to describe a more useful approach.

"Perhaps I can make the difference a little clearer this way: if you ask a child what makes the toy dog move, you should think about what an ordinary human being would answer. The answer is that you wound up the spring; it tries to unwind and pushes the gear around.

What a good way to begin a science course! Take apart the toy; see how it works. See the cleverness of the gears; see the ratchets. Learn something about the toy, the way the toy is put together, the ingenuity of people devising the ratchets and other things. That’s good."

Taking things apart allows us to see how they work. It also gives us context for how things fit together. But you need to do more than take things apart to learn. You need to recombine them.

Using the Feynman Technique to explain what we've learned surfaces areas that we don't understand.

When you write things out in simple language, misunderstanding has no place to hide."
(Shane Parrish, FS-Brain Food)

E1.6.D.3.7.c.ii_REF3_PH “Everyone wants the summary. But the summary is what's left after someone else decided what matters. Their priorities aren't yours. Their filters aren't yours. When you operate on summaries, you're thinking with someone else's brain.”
(Shane Parrish, FS-Brain Food, June 29, 2025)

E1.6.D.3.7.d. FOR SPINOZA, TO UNDERSTAND IS TO EXPERIENCE THE ETERNAL ESSENCE OF THINGS

E1.6.D.3.7.d_REF1_PH "PROPOSITION 30
Intellect, in function (actu) finite, or in function infinite, must comprehend the attributes of God and the modifications of God, and nothing else.

PROOF
A true idea must agree with its object [...] ; in other words (obviously), that which is contained in the intellect in representation must necessarily be granted in nature. But in nature [...] there is no substance save God, nor any modifications save those [...] which are in God, and [...] cannot without God either be or be conceived. Therefore the intellect, in function finite, or in function infinite, must comprehend the attributes of God and the modifications of God, and nothing else.

PROPOSITION 31
The intellect in function, whether finite or infinite, as will, desire, love, &c., should be referred to passive nature and not to active nature.

PROOF
By the intellect we do not (obviously) mean absolute thought, but only a certain mode of thinking, differing from other modes, such as love, desire, &c., and therefore [...] requiring to be conceived through absolute thought. It must [...], through some attribute of God which expresses the eternal and infinite essence of thought, be so conceived, that without such attribute it could neither be nor be conceived [...]. It must therefore be referred to nature passive rather than to nature active, as must also the other modes of thinking.
NOTE
I do not here, by speaking of intellect in function, admit that there is such a thing as intellect in potentiality : but, wishing to avoid all confusion, I desire to speak only of what is most clearly perceived by us, namely, of the very act of understanding, than which nothing is more clearly perceived. For we cannot perceive anything without adding to our knowledge of the act of understanding."
(Baruch Spinoza, Ethics, Part One)

Read Spinoza´s Ethics online: http://app.ethica-spinoza.net/en/text

E1.6.D.3.7.d_REF1.1_PH "PROPOSITION 40 [...]

NOTE 2
From all that has been said above it is clear, that we, in many cases, perceive and form our general notions : 1. From particular things represented to our intellect fragmentarily, confusedly, and without order through our senses [...] ; I have settled to call such perceptions by the name of knowledge from the mere suggestions of experience. 2. From symbols, e.g., from the fact of having read or heard certain words we remember things and form certain ideas concerning them, similar to those through which we imagine things [...]. I shall call both these ways of regarding things knowledge of the first kind, opinion, or imagination. 3. From the fact that we have notions common to all men, and adequate ideas of the properties of things [...] ; this I call reason and knowledge of the second kind. Besides these two kinds of knowledge, there is, as I will hereafter show, a third kind of knowledge, which we will call intuition. This kind of knowledge proceeds from an adequate idea of the absolute essence of certain attributes of God to the adequate knowledge of the essence of things."
(Baruch Spinoza, Ethics, Part Two)

Read Spinoza´s Ethics online: http://app.ethica-spinoza.net/en/text

E1.6.D.3.7.d_REF1.2_PH "PROPOSITION 43
He, who has a true idea, simultaneously knows that he has a true idea, and cannot doubt of the truth of the thing perceived. [...]

NOTE
[...] No one, who has a true idea, is ignorant that a true idea involves the highest certainty. For to have a true idea is only another expression for knowing a thing perfectly, or as well as possible. No one, indeed, can doubt of this, unless he thinks that an idea is something lifeless, like a picture on a panel, and not a mode of thinking - namely, the very act of understanding. And who, I ask, can know that he understands anything, unless he do first understand it? In other words, who can know that he is sure of a thing, unless he be first sure of that thing? Further, what can there be more clear, and more certain, than a true idea as a standard of truth? Even as light displays both itself and darkness, so is truth a standard both of itself and of falsity.  [...]"
(Baruch Spinoza, Ethics, Part Two)

Read Spinoza´s Ethics online: http://app.ethica-spinoza.net/en/text

E1.6.D.3.7.d_REF1.3_PH "PROPOSITION 44
It is not in the nature of reason to regard things as contingent, but as necessary.[...]

COROLLARY 2
It is in the nature of reason to perceive things under a certain form of eternity (sub quâdam æternitatis specie).

PROOF
It is in the nature of reason to regard things, not as contingent, but as necessary [...]. Reason perceives this necessity of things [...] truly, - that is [...], as it is in itself. But [...] this necessity of things is the very necessity of the eternal nature of God ; therefore, it is in the nature of reason to regard things under this form of eternity. We may add that the bases of reason are the notions [...], which answer to things common to all, and which [...] do not answer to the essence of any particular thing : which must therefore be conceived without any relation to time, under a certain form of eternity."
(Baruch Spinoza, Ethics, Part Two)

Read Spinoza´s Ethics online: http://app.ethica-spinoza.net/en/text

E1.6.D.3.7.d_REF1.4_PH "PROPOSITION 47
The human mind has an adequate knowledge of the eternal and infinite essence of God.

PROOF
The human mind has ideas [...], from which [...] it perceives itself and its own body [...] and external bodies [...] as actually existing ; therefore [...] it has an adequate knowledge of the eternal and infinite essence of God.

NOTE
Hence we see, that the infinite essence and the eternity of God are known to all. Now as all things are in God, and are conceived through God, we can from this knowledge infer many things, which we may adequately know, and we may form that third kind of knowledge of which we spoke in the Note 2, Prop. 40, and of the excellence and use of which we shall have occasion to speak in Part V. Men have not so clear a knowledge of God as they have of general notions, because they are unable to imagine God as they do bodies, and also because they have associated the name God with images of things that they are in the habit of seeing, as indeed they can hardly avoid doing, being, as they are, men, and continually affected by external bodies. Many errors, in truth, can be traced to this head, namely, that we do not apply names to things rightly.  [...]
(Baruch Spinoza, Ethics, Part Two)

Read Spinoza´s Ethics online: http://app.ethica-spinoza.net/en/text

E1.6.D.3.7.d_REF2_PH FOR SPINOZA, THE QUESTION IS NOT HOW TO DREAM OF ANOTHER LIFE BUT HOW TO UNDERSTAND OUR CURRENT LIFE SO THAT WE CAN RECOGNIZE OURSELVES IN IT, ACCEPT IT, AND ENJOY IT
"Unlike morality, which dictates duties to free will, ethics merely demonstrates how passions engender one another. It is unnecessary to dictate duties to wills that act out of necessity when they are free and suffer the forces that subjugate them when they are oppressed. The question that arises is no longer the one formulated by Kant: 'What should we do?' Rather, it is the question that urges us to understand the necessity that compels us to act. Salvation no longer depends on an imagined way of life, but rather on our more or less clear and profound awareness of the motives of our actions. This is why Spinoza's Ethics has sometimes been interpreted as quietism. This is true in that this wisdom does not claim to change the world. However, it is false in that it does not lead to the renunciation of the will. Rather, it leads to the joyful affirmation of the will's essence insofar as it obeys only its own intrinsic necessity. For Spinoza, the question is not how to dream of another life but how to understand our current life so that we can recognize ourselves in it, accept it, and enjoy it. The goal is not to change life but to recognize and adhere to the perfection of the present life: "By reality and perfection, I mean the same thing" (Definition 6 of Ethics II). Therefore, it is a matter of reconciling ourselves with reality. This is why human passions should not be dealt with in tones of moral condemnation, but rather with the necessity of a geometric demonstration. "It may seem surprising that I should undertake to treat the vices and infirmities of men in the manner of geometers and wish to demonstrate by rigorous reasoning what they constantly proclaim to be contrary to reason, vain, absurd, and worthy of horror. I will therefore consider human actions and appetites as if they were lines, surfaces, and solids" (Preface to Ethics III). However, this method of pure rationality leads only to a political ethic, as defined in Book IV. This ethic is based on the calculation of mutual interests and the reciprocal neutralization of enmities. The ethics of simple reason is therefore the purely negative ethics of political wisdom. At this point, it does not allow us to achieve a positive ethics that teaches us to recognize the actual content of bliss rather than simply removing the forces that stand in the way of this recognition. Perhaps this is the limit of the 'method of geometers', which recognises 'reason' only through the productive force of adequate ideas that are rigorously linked together in an understanding which obeys the inner necessity expressed within it and does not allow itself to be distracted by passionate distractions that would deprive it of its essence. However, according to Spinoza, this demonstrative and deductive reason, which presides over the writing of the Ethics itself and links propositions, demonstrations, scholia and corollaries, is not the highest faculty of knowledge granted to us. In fact, there is another kind of knowledge superior to this, through which we can access the real content of wisdom or supreme bliss, beyond the calculation of geometric reasoning based on well-understood interests. [...]

To transition from the alienating servitude of mutual passions to the sovereign affirmation of one's full nature, reason alone is insufficient. Another, higher, supra-rational knowledge is necessary. This is because reason links together adequate ideas, generated solely by the productive or generative logic of the understanding, and posits them as objective truths. However, to understand the inner power that gives us existence and drives us to act — the source of our most authentic life — we cannot know this truth as an objective truth. That is to say, we cannot posit it as an object in the external world and determine it by exposing it to our observations, which is the nature of rational knowledge. Instead, we must experience it internally as an intrinsic truth inseparable from our nature, which is intuitively understood rather than objectively determined. However, such intuitive knowledge goes beyond the limits of reasoning and requires a higher faculty capable of experiencing the vital impulse that lifts us into existence and determines our direction. It is no longer a matter of constructing a demonstrative discourse, but of feeling and experiencing the burst of life deep within ourselves.[...]"
(Jacques Darriulat, Spinoza and the Fullness of This World, 6- “We feel and experience that we are eternal,” 2017)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Spinoza/Introduction/Introd6.html

E1.6.D.3.7.d_REF2.1_PH THE THIRD KIND OF KNOWLEDGE, WHICH IS STRANGELY AKIN TO THE UNITIVE FRUITION OF THE SOUL THAT LOVINGLY UNITES ITSELF WITH THE GOD WHO CREATED IT AND LIFTED IT INTO EXISTENCE.
"There is, therefore, an almost symbiotic intimacy between the soul and the body to which it is united; between the individual substance of soul and body, and the greater whole of nature; and finally, between nature and God.' Through this 'close union', everything that exists is also intimately linked to the infinite life in God. We 'know' this not through demonstrative reason, but through an immediate feeling — the feeling of life springing from the depths of our being — which is an immediate intuition and not a chain of deduction. This knowledge is also 'regeneration' of ourselves (a notion that lies at the very foundation of the Reformed religion): When we perceive such effects, we can truly say that we are born again, because our first birth took place when we were united with the body. Through this union, such effects and movements of the animal spirits were produced. However, this second birth will take place when we perceive entirely different effects of love in ourselves, thanks to the knowledge of this immaterial object. These effects differ from the first as much as the corporeal differs from the incorporeal and the spirit from the flesh. This can be called regeneration all the more because only from this love and union can eternal and unalterable stability follow' (last words of chapter 22 of the Short Treatise II). In chapter 25 of the second part of the Short Treatise, Spinoza returns to the superiority of intuitive knowledge over objective rationality. Through this knowledge, we can experience the intimate fusion of life in God or nature and that which burns deep within ourselves. This connection gives rise to the highest bliss in those who are aware of it. : 'We finally realise that knowledge through reasoning is not the most important thing for us, but merely a means by which we achieve our desired goal. It is like a good spirit who brings us a true message from the Supreme Good, inspiring us to seek it and unite with it ourselves. Spinoza compares the third kind of knowledge, or intuitive knowledge, to the angel bearing the Good News, or eu-aggelos in Latin, which is the means by which we are inspired to seek the Supreme Good for ourselves. This union is our highest salvation and happiness.' This refers to the entire programme of work to be accomplished, which will be fully realised in the Ethics. [...]
From the scholium of V 23 (GF, p. 325) onwards, the entire sequence until the end (prop. 42) is a long, almost hymn-like development of the bliss that intuitive knowledge of the life of God within us brings. Proposition 25 states that 'the supreme effort (conatus) of the soul and its supreme virtue (virtus) is to know things through the third kind of knowledge' (GF, p. 325). We understand that this is no longer a matter of knowledge, but of bliss; the intimate proximity of God's life to our own, like a centre of eternity radiating throughout our entire being — at least while we remain alive and persevere in existence. 
And so the atheist Spinoza situates himself in the tradition of mystical experience. This is the third kind of knowledge, which is strangely akin to the unitive fruition of the soul that lovingly unites itself with the God who created it and lifted it into existence. However, it is not only the soul that enjoys this union, as is the case with mystics. Ecstasy is rapture; the body is abandoned like a lifeless, useless burden. Instead, it is experienced by both the mind (mens is the creator of concepts and the producer of rationality) and the body (which is raised to a blessed life of glory and health). Earthly bliss does not raise us to the third heaven, as Paul of Tarsus was raptured (2 Cor. 12), but keeps us firmly grounded. It affirms and cultivates our incarnate individuality and our own free will — the nature that expresses itself in us in a unique way. This is why Spinozist bliss must be distinguished from the spiritual deification accomplished by holy souls who have emptied themselves in order to welcome the divinity, at the cost of their own annihilation, as taught by Eckhart and prevalent in northern countries. The countries of the beguinages. Even so, Spinoza's words sometimes seem to echo this wisdom. The bliss of the fifth book of Ethics is one of self-affirmation, not renunciation; an exultation at being alive on Earth, not a detachment that renounces the self and the world, surrendering to rapture in the afterlife."
(Jacques Darriulat, Spinoza and the Fullness of This World, 6- “We feel and experience that we are eternal,” 2017)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Spinoza/Introduction/Introd6.html

E1.6.D.3.7.d_REF2.2_PH THE EXPERIENCE OF ETERNITY, WHICH IS ACCESSIBLE IN THIS LIFE AND NOT THE PRIVILEGE OF THE BLESSED IN THE HEREAFTER, RESULTS FROM THE THIRD KIND OF KNOWLEDGE.
"Reading is another example of the mind's synthetic grasp: the global method has taught us that the mind views the whole before the part. We do not learn to read by adding one letter to another; rather, we grasp the word as an indivisible whole that is immediately and intuitively recognisable to the mind. When we begin to read Ethics, do we not start by reading proposition by proposition? Perhaps we understand the meaning of this or that passage, but we are unable to grasp the guiding thought or the vision of the mind that inspires the text at a glance. And yet, through repeated reading of this text, we eventually perceive it as a poem that expresses and modulates a single joy of life in various ways throughout. Similarly, in the first lesson of music theory, the apprentice laboriously spells out the notes, whereas the virtuoso pianist reads them at a glance, translating them impetuously on the keyboard. Only the latter truly hears the music; the former merely counts what he does not understand. This final example perfectly illustrates Spinoza's point because the authentic pianist, a true automaton both physically and spiritually, adheres to the movement of the music and the necessity of its development. Through the divine necessity expressed in his performance, he experiences the most exhilarating freedom."

Therefore, it is true to say that, perhaps more than deduction, intuition is a mode of knowledge that can be cultivated and deepened. Even if we understand how the mind can translate temporal succession into the totality of eternity — which involves gathering the successive into the simultaneous — the reason why this third kind of knowledge allows us to access bliss itself is still unclear. Through this bliss, we feel and experience that we are eternal: 'sentimus experimurque nos aeternos esse' (V, scholium 23). This experience of eternity, which is accessible in this life and not the privilege of the blessed in the hereafter, results from the third kind of knowledge. It is therefore invoked in the scholium of prop. 23, which prepares for and announces the final movement of Book V, which is devoted to the third kind of knowledge (props. 25–32). 25: 'The supreme effort of the mind and its sovereign virtue is to understand things through the third kind of knowledge'), engendering 'intellectual love of God' (prop. 33 to 37; prop. 33: 'The intellectual love of God, which arises from the third kind of knowledge, is eternal'), leading to bliss (prop. 38 to the end, i.e. Prop. 42; Prop. 38: 'The more things the mind understands through the second and third kinds of knowledge, the less it suffers from evil feelings and the less it fears death'). 
The enigmatic theme of immediate and intuitive knowledge, hinted at since his earliest writings, thus finds its crowning glory and speculative fulfilment in the final book of the Ethics. All of this meditation was aimed solely at this ultimate goal. By 'bliss', Spinoza — who borrows the term from theologians, who use it to refer to the perfect enjoyment of the blessed in paradise, immersed in the vision of God — does not mean a passion due to an external cause. Rather, he means the fulfilment (gaudium) of our own nature, understood from the source that gives us life. Unlike joy (laetitia), which we experience when we encounter an external cause that increases our ability to act, bliss (beatitudo) has its source within ourselves. It is, in a sense, from ourselves — insofar as we experience our own existence — that we draw the strength to attain bliss. While Spinoza writes that this supreme enjoyment comes from our union with God, it must be noted that this God is an immanent cause, not a transitive or transcendent one — internal, not external (I, 18: 'God is the immanent but not transitive cause of all things'). Therefore, to understand how bliss can lead us to experience eternity, we must grasp the nature of our union with God and the intuition that lies at the root of this profound knowledge."
(Jacques Darriulat, Spinoza and the Fullness of This World, 6- “We feel and experience that we are eternal,” 2017)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Spinoza/Introduction/Introd6.html

E1.6.D.3.7.d_REF2.3_PH EXISTENCE, INSOFAR AS IT IS ENDOWED WITH CONSCIOUSNESS, IS ABLE TO EXPERIENCE THE ACTIVITY OF ITS OWN ESSENCE WITHIN ITSELF: THE DYNAMISM OF A DIVINE POWER, ABSOLUTE AND CREATIVE, THE GIVER OF EXISTENCE; THE ACTIVITY OF THE LIVING GOD, EXPRESSING AND RADIATING HIMSELF IN ALL THINGS ENDOWED WITH THE VIRTUE THAT MAINTAINS THEM IN BEING.
"Descartes' physics is static; Spinoza's ontology is dynamic.' This power, or rather this effort (conatus), is the essence of all existence — as the word itself suggests, indicating movement beyond oneself (ex-sistence), projecting oneself forward according to the continuity of one's trajectory or the fulfilment of one's destiny. This makes it universal, as the act that initiates existence and carries it forward is present in all things and beings, whether living or inert, conscious or unconscious. It is also a concrete universal since it can only be apprehended subjectively, by intuition and not deduction. Existence, insofar as it is endowed with consciousness, is able to experience the activity of its own essence within itself: the dynamism of a divine power, absolute and creative, the giver of existence; the activity of the living God, expressing and radiating Himself in all things endowed with the virtue that maintains them in being. The conatus achieves the paradox of being a singular universal since it is a notion that is both common to all and infinitely unique and singular in the intimacy of each individual. [...]

Therefore, let us focus our attention not on existence itself, but on its essence, striving to understand it better through intuitive, non-deductive knowledge — the third kind of knowledge — which, according to the order of reason, develops in the fifth and final book of the Ethics. We intuit this life of God within us as a perpetual source and a constant inclination to live and to live ever more fully. This movement of ecstasy drives us to act and to project ourselves forward through the power of continuous self-creation. 'Desire is the very essence of man insofar as he is determined by some affection to do something' (III, definition of affections, I). However, not all desires are essential; many are provoked by the imagination and find their cause in externality rather than in the essence of the desiring subject. Rather, it is the desire to desire that is essential: the desire to project one's existence with a view to its intensification and the increase in power that carries it beyond itself and determines it to persevere in its momentum. Existence is thus the movement of Being affected by itself — that is, by its essence — by the energy that carries and lifts it. 
Therefore, it is the same thing to grasp something intuitively as existing and alive, and to understand that this power—the act of divinity—is given to us. We are merely tenants, not owners; we receive it from God, who is its perpetual source. This power can have neither beginning nor end: it is a pure vital impulse that is at the origin of this miracle—that there is something rather than nothing—and this institution of Being is determined by its very essence to persevere throughout the ages, eternally present and alive. Therefore, if we are born and die, it is not due to the essential act that sustains and carries us into existence, but due to the myriad passions that cause us to suffer from encounters and the shocks we experience because we are subject to external causes. After all, we are part of nature: temporary creatures in a swarm of countless creatures that are all equally alive (albeit to varying degrees) and constantly transforming and regenerating themselves in the continuous metamorphoses of nature as a whole — God's play with himself."
(Jacques Darriulat, Spinoza and the Fullness of This World, 6- “We feel and experience that we are eternal,” 2017)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Spinoza/Introduction/Introd6.html

E1.6.D.3.7.d_REF2.4_PH WE FEEL AND EXPERIENCE THAT WE ARE ETERNAL [...] WE FEEL THAT OUR SPIRIT, INSOFAR AS IT ENCOMPASSES THE ESSENCE OF THE BODY IN THE CONTEXT OF ETERNITY, IS ETERNAL AND CANNOT BE DEFINED BY TIME.
"In this immense drama — the expanding sphere in which energy unfolds in the life of matter, like invention in the life of the mind — we have been assigned a role from which we cannot escape. However, we have the choice to either live it fully or deny it, passing into the light of Being without ever probing the infinite power that gives us life, existence and the ability to persevere in the bliss of pure existence. The lesson of ethics does not teach us how to choose our destiny — a vain pretension of the imagination which dreams with its eyes open and knows nothing of the life that is its essence — but rather how to live life to the fullest and embrace the knowledge that we are one expression among the infinite possibilities, all of which are destined for necessary realisation, of the living God who animates all things to varying degrees and has granted them the incomprehensible gift of existence. For what we have shown so far applies equally to humans and other individuals, all of whom are animated, albeit to varying degrees' (Scholion II, 13). Life is not valuable because of the choices we are able to make due to an imaginary freedom that we attribute to ourselves only because we are ignorant of the true causes that determine us and the necessity that guides us. Life is valuable because it offers us the experience, albeit limited in time and space, of participating in God's life, of feeling and experiencing ourselves as eternal beings: 
'We feel and experience that we are eternal [...] We feel that our spirit, insofar as it encompasses the essence of the body in the context of eternity, is eternal and cannot be defined by time. In other words, it cannot be explained by duration' (V, scholion 23). However, eternity is not immortality. The former takes Being into a dimension beyond time — a definition that is, admittedly, merely negative and not affirmative — whereas the latter extends the time granted to this singular existence indefinitely. In fact, the ancient Greeks knew only the latter form of bliss. They considered nothing to be more divine than being immortal and even made this attribute the name of divinity. They referred to their gods as the Immortals. However, the Jewish God knows himself as infinite, not indefinite. It is not enough for him to live indefinitely in time; he must live in the glory of eternity, which triumphs over the dissent and diaspora that form the intimate fabric of duration. He must re-establish himself in perfect harmony with himself, in the bliss of being and knowing himself fully and perpetually enjoying this fullness.
(Jacques Darriulat, Spinoza and the Fullness of This World, 6- “We feel and experience that we are eternal,” 2017)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Spinoza/Introduction/Introd6.html

E1.6.D.3.7.d_REF2.5_PH THIS EXTRAORDINARY WORK PARADOXICALLY TEACHES US THAT WE LIVE IN PARADISE AND ENJOY ETERNITY NOW.
"All men are born and die. But, as long as they are granted the gift of being and persevering in its glory, it is up to them to reconcile themselves with themselves and enjoy the flame that burns within them. They must quench their thirst at the source of life that radiates within them and grants them the infinite power of existence. To enjoy this eternity, it is not necessary to prolong our existence indefinitely. On the contrary, this bliss is given to us in the present moment, provided that we become aware of the life-giving power within us, and according to the depth that meditation gives to our intuitive knowledge of this infinite life of which we are a singular expression. Infinity is an intensive quantity, not an extensive one, and there is no limit to the intensity with which we can participate in the divine activity that carries us into existence and continually perseveres in this momentum through the exercise of attention and the penetration of consciousness. As we have seen, it is indeed the nature of intuitive knowledge to extend, through practice, the scope of its investigation and the radius of its horizon of evidence. This knowledge is accompanied by the highest enjoyment, as it enables us to experience the act of divine causation inwardly. This does not affect us passionately through the effect of an external cause, but rather fills us inwardly through the intuition of our very essence. Only creatures enjoy the infinite power of Being since they receive it from the immanent God who gives them life and not from God Himself, who gives existence through the necessity of His infinite power. Therefore, He cannot experience this power as a gift that fills Him since it is His very nature: 
'We conclude that a created thing can be said to enjoy existence because existence is not its essence. However, God cannot be said to enjoy existence because existence is God himself, as is his essence. Therefore, created things enjoy duration, but God does not enjoy it in any way” (Metaphysical Thoughts, II, 1; GF I, 359). This suggests that the creature's status is perhaps more enviable than God's, as he eternally produces the generation of bodies in matter and the succession of ideas in minds out of his own necessity, without being able to enjoy or even be aware of it. Spinoza's God is not a personal God who addresses His creatures by name — Abraham, Isaac or Jacob — He is the eternal activity of universal necessity, always in the process of giving birth, creating, generating and producing. The only wisdom is that which makes us aware of this and enables us to enjoy this force radiating through us while we exist in nature. Spinoza's God begets and produces; he neither loves nor knows — or rather, he loves only himself, insofar as he is the cause of himself and thus affects himself by giving himself existence. However, it is crucial for our happiness that we know him through intuitive, non-deductive knowledge that is subjective rather than objective. This knowledge must spring from an entirely intellectual love that originates in the depths of our being and not from the imprint or image that clouds our reason through an alienating encounter. This "intellectual love of God", which gives us the bliss of feeling and experiencing ourselves as eternal, is not passionate love of the imagination. It is an "intellectual" love born of intuitive knowledge of the mind's essence and the essence of the body of which it is the idea. It is knowledge of the conatus that sustains all existence in its effort to persevere. 
Unlike Tristan and Isolde, who are passionately in love with the fascinating and captivating images they have of each other, thus condemning themselves to lonely fantasies that make the dream of embracing each other seem unrealistic, God's intellectual love is like that of parents for their children. Parents rejoice in seeing their children flourish and in seeing life assert itself in them and triumph over the obstacles that oppress it. It is also like the love of a gardener for a bud bursting forth or a flower blooming. It rejoices in the universal germination that animates nature. It is a benevolent and non-jealous love that recognises the freedom of each individual to be themselves and to become what they are. It can also be the love of lovers, although Spinoza is rather discreet on this point. When the sweetness of love is sharpened by the intimacy of the embrace and both succeed, through the attention of intuitive intelligence, in coming as close as possible to the secret that beats in the other and in oneself, each knowing the other through the source of life that similarly pours forth in the other, they feel and experience themselves as eternal, like Abraham and Sarah. Mad love alienates us from the outside world and pursues an impossible fusion with another person who will always remain distinct from us. Intellectual love of God drinks from the fountain that springs from the depths of our being, enjoying the universal life that animates all things to varying degrees. The bliss arising from intellectual love of the God who gives us life and the power to exist — whose density, richness and depth we will never exhaust — is a kind of deification and regeneration of the creature; an authentic fusion or transfiguration of singular existence into the infinite activity of divine causation. 
Such bliss makes the wise man like the blessed in paradise, transported to a state that theologians call 'glory', the infinite enjoyment of the soul enraptured in contemplating the face of divinity (a privilege denied even to Moses in this life). The body is also redeemed from the humiliation caused by the original sin and restored to its original splendour as the image of God. It is restored to eternity by the resurrection of the flesh; without this, the soul alone would be like a mutilated part of the body, united to it by a divine bond. We clearly understand what our salvation, bliss or freedom consists of: a constant and eternal love of God or God's love for humanity. This love, or bliss, is called Glory in the sacred books, and not without reason. Whether this love relates to God or our intelligence, it is rightly called contentment of the spirit (acquiescentia animi), which is truly not distinct from glory. Insofar as this love relates to God, it is a joy accompanied by the idea of oneself, and the same applies when this love relates to the intellect. Moreover, since the essence of our intelligence consists solely in knowledge, of which God is the principle and foundation, this explains how and in what way our intelligence follows from the divine nature according to its essence and existence, and depends continually on God' (Scholium to V, Prop. 36).

According to Spinoza, life has no purpose other than itself and the intuitive knowledge of the infinite power that sustains it. Life has no meaning or purpose because it is its own meaning and purpose. Therefore, there is no need for any reward in the afterlife, as existence itself and the bliss it inspires in an attentive mind is the greatest gift of all. Living and being aware of the infinity of this gift is enough. It is also vain to hope for salvation as a reward for this supreme and inestimable gift: 'Bliss is not the reward of virtue, but virtue itself' (Prop. 42, the final proposition of Ethics). This extraordinary work paradoxically teaches us that we live in paradise and enjoy eternity now. We need only this world to find the eternity and perfection that people have only been able to imagine in another world. As it is written on the first page of Book I of Ethics: 'By eternity, I mean existence itself insofar as it is conceived as necessarily following from the mere definition of an eternal thing' (I, Def. 8). In other words, it is known through its essence, the effect of the infinite power of divine causation. This idea is reaffirmed on the first page of Book II: 'By reality and perfection, I mean the same thing.' This world is perfect. All is accomplished."
(Jacques Darriulat, Spinoza and the Fullness of This World, 6- “We feel and experience that we are eternal,” 2017)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Spinoza/Introduction/Introd6.html

E1.6.D.3.7.e. PHILOSOPHY IS ONLY ONE OF THE INCOMPLETE PRACTICES ON THE PATH TO UNDERSTANDING

E1.6.D.3.7.e_REF1_PH "PHILOSOPHY 
A theoretical (but not scientific) practice whose sole purpose is reason as a means and whose goal is wisdom. It is about thinking better in order to live better. 
Philosophy is not a science, nor even knowledge (it is not just another piece of knowledge, it is a reflection on existing knowledge), and therefore, as Kant said, one cannot learn philosophy: one can only learn to philosophize. [Philosophizing is thinking about your life and living your thoughts. Of course, this doesn't mean that you should be satisfied with introspection or egocentrism! Thinking about your life means thinking about it as it is: here and now, of course, but also in society, in history, in the world. And to live one's thoughts is to act, as much as one can, as much as one must, for otherwise one could only suffer or dream. Thus, philosophy is an activity in thought, which, at least in principle, leads to a more active, happier, clearer, freer - wiser life. It should be noted, however, that while happiness is one of the traditional goals of philosophy, it cannot be its standard. As a theoretical (discursive, rational, conceptual) practice, philosophy should submit only to what appears to be true. It is up to us to make happiness out of it, if we can. But truth is what counts. It is better to have true sadness than false joy".
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionnary, 2013)

E1.6.D.3.7.e_REF2_PH PHILOSOPHY IS A DEMYSTIFICATION ENTERPRISE THAT CREATES FREE MEN.
"When someone asks "what's the use of philosophy?" the reply must be aggressive, since the question tries to be ironic and caustic. Philosophy does not serve the State or the Church, who have other concerns. It serves no established power. The use of philosophy is to sadden. A philosophy that saddens no one, that annoys no one, is not a philosophy. It is useful for harming stupidity, for turning stupidity into something shameful. Its only use is the exposure of all forms of baseness of thought. Is there any discipline apart from philosophy that sets out to criticise all mystifications, whatever their source and aim, to expose all the fictions without which reactive forces would not prevail? Exposing as a mystification the mixture of baseness and stupidity that creates the astonishing complicity of both victims and perpetrators. Finally, turning thought into something aggressive, active and affirmative. Creating free men, that is to say men who do not confuse the aims of culture with the benefit of the State, morality or religion. Fighting the ressentiment and bad conscience which have replaced thought for us. Conquering the negative and its false glamour. Who has an interest in all this but philosophy? Philosophy is at its most positive as critique, as an enterprise of demystification. And we should not be too hasty in proclaiming philosophy's failure in this respect. Great as they are, stupidity and baseness would be still greater if there did not remain some philosophy which always prevents them from going as far as they would wish, which forbids them — if only by yea-saying — from being as stupid and base as they would wish. They are forbidden certain excesses, but only by philosophy. [...]
Thinking means the activity of thought; but thought has its own ways of being inactive which can occupy it and all its forces entirely. The fictions through which reactive forces triumph form the most base element in thought, the way in which it remains inactive and busies itself with not thinking. [...]
Thinking, like activity, is always a second power of thought, not the natural exercise of a faculty, but an extraordinary event in thought itself, for thought itself. Thinking is the n-th power of thought. It is still necessary for it to become "light", "affirmative", "dancing". But it will never attain this power if forces do not do violence to it. Violence must be done to it as thought, a power, the force of thinking, must throw it into a becoming-active. A constraint a training of this kind is what Nietzsche calls "Culture". Culture, according to Nietzsche, is essentially training and selection (UM III "Schopenhauer Educator" 6). It expresses the violence of the forces which seize thought in order to make it something affirmative and active. - We will only understand the concept of culture if we grasp all the ways in which it is opposed to method. Method always presupposes the good will of the thinker, "a premeditated decision". Culture, on the contrary, is a violence undergone by thought, a process of formation of thought through the action of selective forces, a training which brings the whole unconscious of the thinker into play. The Greeks did not speak of method but of paideia; they knew that thought does not think on the basis of a good will, but by virtue of the forces that are exercised on it in order to constrain it to think. Even Plato still distinguished what forces us to think and what leaves thought inactive; and in the myth of the cave he subordinated the paideia to the violence undergone by a prisoner, either in order to leave the cave or in order to return to it."
(Gilles Deleuze, Nietzsche and Philosophy, 1962)

E1.6.D.3.7.e_REF3_PH GROWING SMALL, I DISAPPEAR IN THE IMMENSE, MORE AND MORE FREE.
“CONVERSATION WITH JEANNE
Let us not talk philosophy, drop it, Jeanne.
So many words, so much paper, who can stand it.
I told you the truth about my distancing myself.
I've stopped worrying about my misshapen life.
It was no better and no worse than the usual human tragedies.

For over thirty years we have been waging our dispute
As we do now, on the island under the skies of the tropics.
We flee a downpour, in an instant the bright sun again,
And I grow dumb, dazzled by the emerald essence of the leaves.

We submerge in foam at the line of the surf,
We swim far, to where the horizon is a tangle of banana bush,
With little windmills of palms.
And I am under accusation: That I am not up to my oeuvre,
That I do not demand enough from myself,
As I could have learned from Karl Jaspers,
That my scorn for the opinions of this age grows slack.

I roll on a wave and look at white clouds.

You are right, Jeanne, I don't know how to care about the salvation of my soul.
Some are called, others manage as well as they can.
I accept it, what has befallen me is just.
I don't pretend to the dignity of a wise old age.
Untranslatable into words, I chose my home in what is now,
In things of this world, which exist and, for that reason, delight us:
Nakedness of women on the beach, coppery cones of their breasts,
Hibiscus, alamanda, a red lily, devouring
With my eyes, lips, tongue, the guava juice, the juice of la prune de Cythère,
Rum with ice and syrup, lianas-orchids
In a rain forest, where trees stand on the stilts of their roots.

Death, you say, mine and yours, closer and closer,
We suffered and this poor earth was not enough.
The purple-black earth of vegetable gardens
Will be here, either looked at or not.
The sea, as today, will breathe from its depths.
Growing small, I disappear in the immense, more and more free.”
(Czeslaw Milosz)

E1.6.E. SOCIAL COMMITMENT
Fighting courageously for a society that embraces our vision of progress, preserves the environment and makes equal opportunity a reality.

E1.6.E.1 WHERE DOES THE BALANCE LIE BETWEEN CARING FOR ONESELF AND CARING FOR OTHERS AND THE WORLD?

E1.6.E.1.1. TRUE HAPPINESS AND SELF-LOVE CANNOT EXIST WITHOUT JUSTICE AND LOVE FOR OTHERS
Self-interest should not be seen as a form of selfishness but rather a means to an end, the end being the general well-being of all. True happiness and self-love cannot exist without justice and love for others. We are all interconnected, thus by quieting the voices of civilization, one can hear the interconnectedness of life. "And yet we are habitually deafened to it by a kind of desensitization, a form of paralysis that comes from the sheer mismatch between the scale of the problems the world hurls at us and our individual locus of agency. The antidote to this paralysis is small action for “however meaningless and vain, however dead life appears, the man of faith, of energy, of warmth… steps in and does something"
"The joy of the courageous one is that of a pure and inspired heart, which is articulate to other hearts and says yes to becoming, rather than withdrawing from the world." "The courageous one, by his action alone, will set right what was upside down."

E1.6.E.1.1.a. “INTEREST
[...] It's a clear misunderstanding to perceive interest as an excuse for egoism. Instead, it is an attempt to overcome or sublimate it. It is in everyone's interest to pursue the common good. Who can achieve happiness alone? Who can love without considering oneself? This is where self-love develops into a love for justice or love for one's neighbor. As the Dalai Lama once stated, "Be selfish: Love one another!"”
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.E.1.1.b. SEEMINGLY SMALL ACTS COLLECTIVELY SHAPE HUMANITY'S COURSE
Elie Wiesel critiques the desensitization to global issues in modern society, attributing it to the vast scale of these problems and media sensationalism. Wiesel understands the emotional toll of continuous exposure to crises and promotes selective, impactful actions instead of paralysis. He agrees with Hannah Arendt's belief that small actions can lead to limitless effects, and he encourages individuals to pick a cause. He emphasizes that seemingly small actions, when taken together, can dramatically shape the course of humanity.

E1.6.E.1.1.b_REF1_LIT ELIE WIESEL DISCUSSES THE EMOTIONAL EXHAUSTION CAUSED BY CONSTANT EXPOSURE TO GLOBAL CRISES AND THE PARALYSIS IT INDUCES. He suggests that the antidote to this paralysis is small, meaningful actions, echoing Hannah Arendt's belief in the boundless impact of even the smallest deeds.
"In Conversations with Elie Wiesel (public library), the Biblical question Cain poses to God after killing Abel — “Am I my brother’s keeper?” — becomes a lens on what makes for brotherhood in the broadest humanistic sense. Wiesel reflects:

'We are all our brothers’ keepers… Either we see in each other brothers, or we live in a world of strangers… There are no strangers in a world that becomes smaller and smaller. Today I know right away when something happens, whatever happens, anywhere in the world. So there is no excuse for us not to be involved in these problems. A century ago, by the time the news of a war reached another place, the war was over. Now people die and the pictures of their dying are offered to you and to me while we are having dinner. Since I know, how can I not transform that knowledge into responsibility? So the key word is “responsibility.” That means I must keep my brother.'

Whenever we quiet the voices of so-called civilization — the voices of selfing and hard-edged individualism — that sense of the interconnectedness of life and of lives becomes audible. And yet we are habitually deafened to it by a kind of desensitization — the kind the poet May Sarton so poignantly captured as she contemplated how to live with tenderness in a harsh world. Much of it, Wiesel observes, is a form of paralysis that comes from the sheer mismatch between the scale of the problems the world hurls at us and our individual locus of agency — a particular pathology of the information age, further exploited by the news media and their crisis-mongering. Wiesel considers the consequence:

'We are careless. Somehow life has been cheapened in our own eyes. The sanctity of life, the sacred dimension of every minute of human existence, is gone. The main problem is that there are so many situations that demand our attention. There are so many tragedies that need our involvement. Where do you begin?' [...]

He traces the emotional attrition that happens when we are bombarded with news of crises and traumatic events — at first deeply moved and invested in allaying the suffering we see, we grow exhausted by trauma-sighting and help-canvassing, just as news of the latest calamity or injustice is piling atop the previous one:

'You couldn’t take it. There is a need to remember, and it may last only a day or a week at a time. We cannot remember all the time. That would be impossible; we would be numb. If I were to remember all the time, I wouldn’t be able to function. A person who is sensitive, always responding, always listening, always ready to receive someone else’s pain… how can one live?'

The antidote to this paralysis, Wiesel argues, is small action — a testament to Hannah Arendt’s conviction that “the smallest act in the most limited circumstances bears the seed of… boundlessness, because one deed, and sometimes one word, suffices to change every constellation.” A century and a half after Van Gogh insisted that “however meaningless and vain, however dead life appears, the man of faith, of energy, of warmth… steps in and does something,” Wiesel insists on choosing from among the innumerable causes soliciting your attention and aid just one in which to get involved — an act seemingly small that, on the cumulative scale of humanity, moves the world."
(Maria Popova's review of Conversations with Elie Wiesel in The Marginalian)

E1.6.E.1.1.b_REF1.1_CUR "I CAN’T ENJOY MYSELF. IF THERE IS ONE GUY DYING OF HUNGER IT KILLS IT FOR ME."
"Life is either terrible or miserable, you should be happy your life is only miserable."
(Woody Allen, Annie Hall, 1977)

Watch a selection of best scenes on YouTube: https://youtu.be/lyKrCAgki-Y?feature=shared

E1.6.E.1.1.b_REF1.2_PH OUR PERSONAL STRUGGLES AREN'T ISOLATED INCIDENTS BUT RATHER SHARED EXPERIENCES REFLECTING THE CHALLENGING AND TRANSFORMATIVE GROWTH OF HUMANITY AS A WHOLE.
“We ourselves are events in history. Things do not merely happen to us, they happen through us. […]

All of us, if we are really alive, are disturbed now in our emotions. We are faced by emotional problems that we do not know how to solve. They distract our minds, fill us with misgiving, and sometimes threaten to wreck our lives. That is the kind of experience to which we are committed. If anyone thinks they are peculiar to the difficulties of his own situation, let him… talk a little about them to other people. He will discover that he is not a solitary unfortunate. We shall make no headway with these questions unless we begin to see them, and keep on seeing them, not as our private difficulties but as the growing pains of a new world of human experience. Our individual tensions are simply the new thing growing through us into the life of mankind. When we see them steadily in this universal setting, then and then only will our private difficulties become really significant. We shall recognize them as the travail of a new birth for humanity, as the beginning of a new knowledge of ourselves and of God."
(John Macmurray, Reason and Emotion, 1935)

E1.6.E.1.1.b_REF2_PH TAKE ON CONCRETE, MODEST BUT VERY REAL ACTIONS
"Today, someone asked me about the pace of the world, what I thought about it and what we could do to improve it. More than ever, the Diamond Sutra once again imposed itself on me: the pace of the world is not the pace of the world, that's why I call it the pace of the world. No, the pace of the world is not the pace of the world. It does not boil down to my concepts, to my prejudices, to my ideas, to my ideology. It is much denser, more complex, richer, grander than what I perceive of it. I can never grasp it, I can never influence it. It goes on, which is why I call it the pace of the world.
There is no question however of retrieving from the world, of abdicating, of resigning but rather one should make his contribution, trying to improve it little by little, on one´s small scale, improving what can be improved. The Diamond Sutra, once again, invites me to be enchanted at what is, although it sometimes troubles me. While inviting me to take on concrete, modest but very real actions."
(Alexandre Jollien, La Pharmacopée n ° 27)

E1.6.E.1.1.b_REF3_PH “GOOD AND EVIL BOTH INCREASE AT COMPOUND INTEREST. That is why the little decisions you and I make every day are of such infinite importance. The smallest good act today is the capture of a strategic point from which, a few months later, you may be able to go on to victories you never dreamed of. An apparently trivial indulgence in lust or anger today is the loss of a ridge or railway line or bridgehead from which the enemy may launch an attack otherwise impossible.”
(C.S. Lewis)

E1.6.E.1.1.c. THE ONLY PROBLEM REALLY WORTH THINKING ABOUT IS HOW TO BE FAIR IN OUR LIFE
He would have always replied that the only problem truly worth contemplating was how to be fair in our life. However, when a claim is raised for an extended period without any progress, one's mind tends to drift off, much like an arm holding something aloft for hours tends to numb. Our thoughts, similar to soldiers parading in the summer heat, cannot remain perpetually upright. If they're made to wait too long, they lose consciousness and collapse. […]
He waited behind his persona,insofar as that term can be defined as the part of a man that allows itself to be shaped by the outside world and life's course. Meanwhile, his quiet despair, held back, rose a little more each day.

E1.6.E.1.1.c_REF1_LIT ULRICH IS OBSESSED WITH THE SEARCH FOR A JUST LIFE. DESPITE HIS EFFORTS IN MATHEMATICS AND SCIENCE, HE IS IN AN EXISTENTIAL CRISIS, LOATHING HIMSELF FOR HIS INACTION. He waits for a change that does not come, feeling trapped by his thoughts and inertia.
"The earth too, but Ulrich in particular, pays homage to the Utopian idea of Essayism. [...]
At any point during his work on mathematical and mathematico-logical problems or while he was concerned with natural science, if he had been asked what aim he had in mind, he would have answered that there was only one question really worth thinking about, and that was the question of right living. But when one has been making a demand for a
long time without any result, the brain goes to sleep, just as the arm does when it has been holding something up for too long. Our thoughts can no more remain standing for an indefinite period than soldiers can on parade in summer; if they have to wait too long, they simply fall down in a faint.

As Ulrich had completed the draft of his view of life when he was twenty-six, now, when he was thirty-two, it no longer seemed quite genuine. He had not elaborated his ideas any further, and apart from a vague, tense feeling, such as one has when waiting for something with one’s eyes shut, there was not much sign of personal emotion in him, now that the days of the first tremulous revelations had passed. [...]

It might be mentioned in his defence that at a certain age life runs away incredibly fast. But the day when one must begin to live out one’s last will, before one leaves the residue of it behind, lies far ahead and cannot be shifted. That had become menacingly clear to him since almost six months had passed without anything’s changing. While he let himself be moved to and fro in the minor and foolish activity that he had taken on, talking, liking to talk too much, living with the desperate persistence of a fisherman putting down his nets in an empty river, and yet doing nothing that was in accord with the person he nevertheless significantly was, and doing it deliberately, he waited. He waited hidden behind his person, in so far as this word refers to that part of a man formed by the world and the course of life; and his quiet desperation,
dammed up behind it, rose higher every day. He was in the worst state of emergency in his life and despised himself for his omissions. Are great ordeals the privilege of great personalities? He would have liked to believe that. But it is not true, for even the simplest neurotic personalities have their crises. So all he was actually left with in this great perturbation was that residue of imperturbability possessed by all heroes and criminals—it is not courage, it is not will-power, it is not confidence, but simply a tough capacity to hold on—something that is as difficult to drive out as the life out of a cat even when it is utterly mangled by the dogs.

To anyone who wants to visualise how such a man lives when he is alone, the most that can be said is that by night the lighted window-panes gaze into his room, and the thoughts, after they have been used, sit about like the clients in the waiting-room of a lawyer with whom they are not satisfied."
(Robert Musil, The Man Without Qualities, Volume 1, 1930)

E1.6.E.1.1.c_REF2_PH "HOW CAN HUMAN LIVES BE WEIGHED ONE AGAINST EACH OTHER? IS THIS EVEN POSSIBLE? John Broome who has a background in economics as well as in philosophy, discusses how governments should allocate scarce resources or make life and death decisions.
Purely utilitarist concepts such as QUALIs have to be balanced by considerations of fairness that introduce an equality of chance. Ex: If you can treat one of 2 patients only, it is not because one is blind and will produce less QUALIs for the rest of his life that the other patients who isn't blind should be favored with the treatment. Fairness of chance seems right in this example, choosing who will be cured by lottery." 
(John Broome on Weighing Lives)

Listen to the episode: http://philosophybites.com/2008/06/john-broome-on.html

E1.6.E.1.1.c_REF2.1_PH "HOW SHOULD SCARCE HEALTHCARE RESOURCES BE DISTRIBUTED?
The black report commissioned by the NHS 30 years after its creation in the late 70s showed that the well off still were healthier than the poor even when they had equal access to health services. Although this advocated for putting more resources in social services and less in healthcare this did not happen because of lobbying and interests of several groups.
The NHS uses cost effectiveness to allocate its budget: The goal is to maximize the QUALYs = Quality Adjusted Live Years for the population as a whole, which is a purely utilitarian approach. Utilitarism has been heavily criticized as a philosophical theory: Is it right to base the definition of NHS resources on it?
(Jonathan Wolff on Political Bioethics)

Listen to the episode: http://philosophybites.com/2012/06/jonathan-wolff-on-political-bioethics-originally-on-bioethics-bites.html

E1.6.E.1.1.d. THE HAPPY MUST SEE THE UNHAPPY AS THEIR MISFORTUNE
Victor Hugo suggests a profession of faith that harmonizes our actions and words, our ethos and logos. This faith involves love for self and others, viewing the rectification of public wrongs as the basis of our understanding. He advocates for the development of the courage or awareness of the non-reciprocity between fairness and law. The fortunate must see the misfortune of others as their own; social egoism is the start of a downfall. If we wish to thrive, we must intertwine our hearts and embrace our collective humanity. "The joy of the courageous is the joy of a pure and inspired heart, which connects with other hearts and affirms life, rather than retreating from the world."
"Above all, it is the irreversibility of time that guides us." The courageous, through their actions alone, can correct what is unbalanced.

E1.6.E.1.1.d_REF1_PH "COURAGE OR THE AWARENESS OF THE NON-RECIPROCITY BETWEEN RIGHT AND LAW [...] 
This could also be another name for the moral conscience that Hugo likes to invoke so often; the one that, feverishly, never falters. Democracy, or the history of the "permanent and perpetual confrontation of the law that makes men with the law that makes men". Democracy, or the regime that makes this confrontation the conduct of its policy. And Hugo's literary destiny is played out in the same movement: "Do not lose heart, oh pity! As for me, I will not tire, and what I have written in all my books, what I have testified by all my actions, [...] before the National Assembly of France as well as the stoned window of the Place des Barricades in Brussels, I will testify, I will write and I will say again and again: we must love one another, love one another, love one another! The fortunate must bear the misfortune of the unfortunate; social selfishness is the beginning of the grave; if we want to live, let us unite our hearts and be one great human race. Let us advance, let us retreat. [...] Let us help, protect, support, admit public wrongdoing and make amends. [...] The duty of the strong is the right of the weak". There is no romantic lyricism or naïve optimism in Hugo's declaration. On the contrary, it is a confession of faith. First of all, Hugo affirms the appropriateness - the moral imperative - of what he said and did, of his ethos and his logos. He wrote what he actually did and confirmed it by telling everyone. He did not divide the territories of courage, did not behave one way in one place and another in another. He always made this doing and saying the substance of his literature. Finally, he appeals to love. Not in the sense of passion for a woman, but in the sense of agape, that which is the ferment of the community of men. In short, the other name for concern for oneself and others, for the common good. "Let us want to live, let us unite our hearts", or the call to common courage. Finally, in the last act of the profession of faith, let us make the redemption of the public wrong, the democratic wrong itself, the ground of our understanding. Or let us make confrontation, the confrontation of rights and law, the political path of the human community; and thus the law will not be a sham, but on the contrary, the very consolation of the weak. Victor Hugo knows how much we are in danger when we lack courage. [...]
It is the other side of the political and moral work, a kind of embodied superego, not severe and therefore not fantastical. It is the stylization of a moral conscience. [...] Within democracy, literature seals a privileged pact with politics: a pact of conscience. It will be the guardian of public strategy. [...] And it is in the knowledge of this subterranean, sometimes forgotten, sometimes secret pact between literature and politics that the courageous construct their actions, find the strength they lack, and build hope for all. For public concern is undoubtedly the profane experience of hope. Certainly a concrete aspect, already a glimmer, but already in fact a providence. Public concern is the very proof that hope is not just an inappropriate passion, to be abandoned like fear. Hope is something like the other side of a moral paradigm of reason. Hugo preaches the efficacy of hope, its ethical and not strictly utopian character. Its voluntary nature, because hope is a sure sign of a will in motion, and therefore it is ethical. Because there is no reason to hope. The facts are never favorable. Everything conspires against hope. [...]"
(Cynthia Fleury, The end of courage, 2010)
