E1.6.E.1.5_REF6_LIT ADRIENNE MAREE BROWN ON PLEASURE ACTIVISM: I WANT TO MAKE JUSTICE AND LIBERATION THE MOST PLEASURABLE EXPERIENCES THAT WE CAN HAVE

"Krista Tippett:Oh, one thing I do — I don’t want to end without getting to is 'Pleasure Activism'. I think it’s been so fascinating — it’s been fascinating for me because I have this cumulative conversation. And at some point in the last couple of years, over and over and over again people kept making this very clear, very insistent connection between justice and joy. [laughter] And you — and you talk about 'pleasure activism.' And so describe that.

Adrienne Maree Brown: I want to make justice and liberation the most pleasurable experiences that we can have, as a species. I want to make it feel like: Ahh. When I make the best choice, it feels good, and I know it, from my bones up and out. And I know that that means a deep reclamation, especially for those of us who’ve experienced oppression or trauma; that what gets taken from us, often, is the sense that we deserve pleasure, that we know how to feel it, that we’re allowed to feel it. So one of the things I say is, pleasure is not a frivolous thing. Actually, it’s a measure of freedom; that when you are free, one of the ways you know that is that you can feel pleasure. You can feel the present moment.

For most people, it’s really just being able to feel the erotic aliveness of a moment and the erotic yes, I am alive. I’m here right now, and this is how I want this moment to go. This is good. And I have to shout out the queen mother of our lineage for pleasure activism is Audre Lorde. She wrote an essay in 1978, called “The Uses of the Erotic as Power.” And I highly recommend listening to it. You can actually listen to her read it. […]

Krista Tippett: And you point out, when you write about pleasure activism, that so much of how we try to organize and mobilize and even inspire, orient — and yes, in social justice work, but in journalism, as well — is about planting facts and guilt and shame, even though — I mean, if we go back to that list of — if we go back to Octavia Butler and the way you’ve talked about what is successful life, right — that does not create change. But you’re talking about, how do we create practices and communities that everybody sees — that are magnetic, that you want to run towards?

Adrienne Maree Brown: Toni Cade Bambara said we have to make the “revolution irresistible.” And I think about that — she’s quoted, and there’s a whole essay from Alexis Pauline Gumbs in the book about her work. […]

And we want to make it feel good, smell good, taste good; we want to make sure that everyone feels like they could belong in it; we want to make everyone feel like their needs could be met in there. And we know that we can’t get there through punishment.

And there’s a pleasure — and I promise it. I always love to promise it to people, because I’m just like, I know everyone can experience this. There’s a pleasure from being present, truly present, where you’re like, I’m with the people I want to be with. I’m doing what I want to do. It only comes from being really present. And capitalism has us socialized to think we constantly have to be looking elsewhere for it, so we’re running around, not satisfied, not satisfiable, no sense of what that would be like.

And in our justice movements, that’s not a good look, because if we have no idea what it feels like to be satisfied, we won’t know when we win. [laughs] So I’m always like, we need to be satisfiable. We need to know what that feels like. And one of the fastest ways to know what that feels like is to be satisfied in the body. Like, you know what an orgasm feels like, most of us do, and even if we can’t feel an orgasm, we can feel pleasure. We can feel the beauty — […]

Krista Tippett: Yeah, I mean, you’re really I keep using this language of, how we really internalize in our bodies that this is what it’s about, that what you do, what you practice in your everyday, is a pattern that is part of that larger pattern that you want to see.

Adrienne Maree Brown: That’s right. And there’s so much awakening, so I always tell people that you’re always practicing things. So it’s not like you go from not practicing to practicing; but it’s, Are you practicing things on purpose? Are you practicing things you would want to practice, or are you practicing what someone else has told you [laughs] is the right way to do stuff? And once you start practicing on purpose, then you can actually practice liberation and justice and freedom and — then I think you begin to have this contentment that comes from practice. Like I know that I won’t see total liberation in my lifetime, but I also feel very satisfied with how I’m practicing liberation every single day and in every relationship.

Krista Tippett: And moving towards life.

Adrienne Maree Brown: And moving towards life, yeah. [laughs] Life moves towards life, you know. That’s the trick.”

(Adrienne Maree Brown in conversation with Krista Tippett, On Radical Imagination and Moving Towards Life, On Being, June 23, 2022)

Listen to the podcast or read the full transcript: https://onbeing.org/programs/adrienne-maree-brown-on-radical-imagination-and-moving-towards-life/

E1.6.E.1.5_REF7_LIT “HUMILITY DOES NOT MEAN SELF-ABNEGATION, LASSITUDE, DETACHMENT; it’s more a calm recognition that you must trust in that which does not make sense, that which is unreasonable, illogical, silly, ridiculous, crazy by the measure of most of our culture. You must trust that you being the best possible you matters somehow… That doing your chosen work with creativity and diligence will shiver people far beyond your ken. That being an attentive and generous friend and citizen will prevent a thread or two of the social fabric from unraveling. […]
This is what I know: that the small is huge, that the tiny is vast, that pain is part and parcel of the gift of joy, and that this is love, and then there is everything else. You either walk toward love or away from it with every breath you draw. Humility is the road to love. Humility, maybe, is love.”
(Brian Doyle, One Long River of Song: Notes on Wonder, 2019)

E1.6.D.3.7.wE1.6.E.1.5. THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN KNOWING AND UNDERSTANDING
The key is to understand, meaning to apply knowledge to transform one's life, rather than merely accumulating it.

E1.6.E.1.6. HOW CAN I CONTRIBUTE TO THE JOY OF OTHERS IF I'M NOT HAPPY MYSELF? 

E1.6.E.1.6.a. HOW CAN I CONTRIBUTE TO THE JOY OF OTHERS IF I HAVE LOST MINE?
It's important not to overextend oneself or lose sight of personal happiness when aiding the less fortunate. One must continuously nurture their own joy in order to effectively share it. After all, how can one contribute to the joy of others if they have lost their own?

E1.6.E.1.6.a_REF1_PH "« AND BECOME BRIGHT AGAIN » 
We, openhanded and rich in spirit, standing by the road like open wells with no intention to fend off anyone who feels like drawing from us--we unfortunately
do not know bow to defend ourselves where we want to: we have no way of preventing people from darkening us: the time in which we live throws into us what is most timebound; its dirty birds drop their filth into us; boys their gewgaws; and exhausted wanderers who come to us to rest, their little and large miseries. But we shall do what we have always done: whatever one casts into us, we take down into our depth -for we are deep, we do not forget-and become bright again."
(Friedrich Nietzsche, The Gay Science, 1882)

E1.6.E.1.6.b. LET'S BE SUPPORTIVE AND NOT JUST COMPASSIONATE
We should approach others with kindness and an inclination to encourage mutual joy, despite our differences. This is because we share a basic set of common values that form a society. However, the attitude in our commitment is crucial: we should aim to be supportive, not just compassionate!

E1.6.E.1.6.b.i. PASSING ON THE PRIVILEGE OF HAVING FOUND A PATH
"The authentic watermark running through the background of a life's work is an arrival at generosity and, as a mark of that generosity, delight in the hopes of the young: and the giving away to them, not only of rewards that may have been earned but the reward in the secret itself, the core artistry that made the journey a journey. Perhaps the greatest legacy we can leave from our work is the passing on of a sense of sheer privilege, of having found a road, a way to follow, and then having been allowed to walk it, often with others, with all its difficulties and minor triumphs."

E1.6.E.1.6.b.i_REF_PH "AMBITION

[...] Ambition is natural to the first steps of youth, who must experience its essential falsity to know the larger reality that stands behind it, but held on to too long, and especially in eldership, it always comes to lack surprise, turns the last years of the ambitious into a second childhood, and makes the once successful into an object of pity.

The authentic watermark running through the background of a life's work is an arrival at generosity and, as a mark of that generosity, delight in the hopes of the young: and the giving away to them, not only of rewards that may have been earned but the reward in the secret itself, the core artistry that made the journey a journey.

Perhaps the greatest legacy we can leave from our work is not to instil ambition in others, though this may be the first way we describe its arrival in our life, but the passing on of a sense of sheer privilege, of having found a road, a way to follow, and then having been allowed to walk it, often with others, with all its difficulties and minor triumphs; the underlying primary gift of having been both a witness to and a full participant in the conversation."
(David Whyte, Consolations, The Solace, Nourishment and Underlying Meaning of Everyday Words, 2015)

E1.6.E.1.6.b.iii. HOPE AND TRUST MAKE UP FOR A WONDERFUL ELIXIR

E1.6.E.1.6.b.iii_REF1_PH "THERE IS A KNOWLEDGE THAT DIMINISHES LIFE, A KNOWLEDGE THAT IS SAD, COMMONLY CALLED PITY, and which is one of the scourges of humanity. You have to see how a sensitive woman talks to a skinny man who is supposed to have tuberculosis. [So how can we do it? Here's how. We can't be sad; we have to be hopeful; you only give people the hope that you have. We have to rely on nature, see the future in a positive light, and believe that life will triumph. It's easier than you think because it's natural. All living things believe that life will triumph, otherwise they would die right away. This vital energy will soon make you forget that poor man, so we should give him this vital energy. You really shouldn't feel too sorry for him. Not to be hard and insensitive. But to show joyful friendship. No one likes to be pitied; and when a patient sees that he is not extinguishing the joy of a good person, he is uplifted and comforted. Trust is a wonderful elixir.
(Alain, On Happiness, October 5, 1909)

E1.6.E.1.6.b.iv. "THE SYMPATHETIC. SYMPATHETIC NATURES, ALWAYS HELPFUL IN A MISFORTUNE, ARE RARELY THE SAME ONES WHO SHARE OUR JOY: when others are happy, they
have nothing to do, become superfluous, do not feel in possession of their superiority, and therefore easily show dissatisfaction."
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human, Aphorism 321, 1878)

"Pity is the opposite of the uplifting emotions that increase the energy of vital feeling: it is depressing. To sympathize is to lose strength. Compassion increases and amplifies the loss of strength that suffering alone already inflicts on life.
As for suffering, compassion makes it contagious. In some cases, it means that "the amount of life and vital energy lost is absurdly disproportionate to the importance of its cause."
(Nietzsche, The Antichrist, Aphorism 321)

E1.6.E.1.6.b.v. WE CAN NEVER DETERMINE IF THE "I" IN A CERTAIN INDIVIDUAL IS COMPLETELY EXTINGUISHED OR MERELY INERT.
”For those whose I is dead, nothing can be done, absolutely nothing. But we never know whether, in a given human being, the I is completely dead or merely inanimate. If it's not quite dead, love can revive it as if by a jolt. But it must be love that is completely pure, devoid of even a hint of condescension, as the slightest trace of disdain can precipitates it towards death.”

E1.6.E.1.6.b.v_REF1_PH "THE SELF
We possess nothing in the world—a mere chance can strip us of everything—except the power to say ‘I’. That is what we have to give to God—in other words, to destroy. There is absolutely no other free act which it is given us to accomplish—only the destruction of the ‘I’.

[...] Nothing in the world can rob us of the power to say ‘I’. Nothing except extreme affliction. Nothing is worse than extreme affliction which destroys the ‘I’ from outside, because after that we can no longer destroy it ourselves.

[...] In affliction the vital instinct survives all the attachments which have been torn away, and blindly fastens itself to everything which can provide it with support, like a plant fastens its tendrils. Gratitude (except in a base form) and justice are not conceivable in this state. Slavery. There is no longer the extra amount of energy required to support free-will by which man takes the measure of things. Affliction, from this point of view, is hideous as life in its nakedness always is, like an amputated stump, like the swarming of insects. Life without form. Survival is then the only attachment. That is where extreme affliction begins—when all other attachments are replaced by that of survival. Attachment appears then in its nakedness without any other object but itself—Hell.

It is by this mechanism that to those in affliction life appears as the one thing desirable, at the very time when their life is in no way preferable to death."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.6.E.1.6.b.v_REF1.1_CUR "IN JEZREEL VALLEY IN THE DARK, REAL MOLD SPORES GROW AND MULTIPLY in puzzle-shaped agricultural "plots" filled with coffee dregs and sugar. The Jezreel Valley - one of the emblems of the agricultural settlements founded by Zionist pioneers - has acquired the status of a mythological landscape in Israeli collective memory. In this work, Weinstein created an "agricultural laboratory" - an experiment in "mold agriculture" that grows on the black coffee fields of the Jezreel Valley, with spores nourished by the local Venetian micro-organisms in the air. Like the steel wool rusting gradually on the ground floor, this work too will be transformed in the course of the exhibition, as the coffee mixture thickens in the trays and the spore colonies growing upon it change their color and form. In an ironic inversion of agricultural processes, actions related to order, cultivation, and maintenance are replaced by inaction and neglect. Paradoxically, the mold symbolic of desolation and dissolution is the very element that infuses this iconic image of the valley with a sense of life and temporal duration."
(Curatorship, Istrael's Pavillion, Venice Biennale, 2017)

E1.6.E.1.6.b.v_REF1.2_PH "THE SELF
[...]  When we do a service to beings thus uprooted and we receive in exchange discourtesy, ingratitude, betrayal, we are merely enduring a small share of their affliction. It is our duty to expose 28 gravity and grace ourselves to it in a limited measure just as it is our duty to expose ourselves to affliction. When it comes we should endure
it as we endure affliction, without referring it back to particular people, for it cannot be referred back to anything. There is something impersonal in quasi-infernal affliction as there is in perfection.

For those whose ‘I’ is dead we can do nothing, absolutely nothing. We never know, however, whether in a particular person the ‘I’ is quite dead or only inanimate. If it is not quite dead, love can reanimate it as though by an injection, but it must be love which is utterly pure without the slightest trace of condescension, for the least shade of contempt drives towards death.

When the ‘I’ is wounded from outside it starts by revolting in the most extreme and bitter manner like an animal at bay. But as soon as the ‘I’ is half dead, it wants to be finished off and allows itself to sink into unconsciousness. If it is then awakened by a touch of love, there is sharp pain which results in anger and sometimes hatred for whoever has provoked this pain. Hence the apparently inexplicable vindictiveness of the fallen towards their benefactors.

It can also happen that the love of the benefactor is not pure. Then, in the ‘I’, awakened by love but immediately wounded afresh by contempt, there surges up the bitterest of hatreds, a hatred which is legitimate."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.6.E.1.6.b.v_REF2_PH "HOPE
In the political debate, at some point, aren’t your options either anger/revolution or resignation - when it becomes obvious that the good will not win out? Even anger is probably sustained by some kind of hope, so the only alternative to hope might be resignation. But if resignation is something that you do, you have to hope somehow that you can stop hoping in order to truly resign - creating a paradox - so isn't hope tied to agency? Every time you do something it is because you hope that what you do will succeed, otherwise you would not bother. Hope is impossible to eradicate so long as you are an agent. 
That is what Schopenhauer concludes:  you cannot escape willing yourself for it has to be drawn out of you either:
1. by suffering to a point when the will to live is killed in you or;
2. by the realization that your own perspective is limited and that you can't expand it -  in which case, you realize your joy is someone else's suffering, so that the will ceases to will."

Listen to the program: https://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/m00017vl

E1.6.E.1.6.b.v_REF2.1_CUR "THE GERMAN ARTIST ANSELM KIEFER created his grand painting series titled Questi scritti, quando verranno bruciati, daranno finalmente un po' di luce (These writings, when burned, will finally cast a little light) between 2020 and 2021, specifically for the Sala dello Scrutinio in the Palazzo Ducale, or Doge's Palace. The invitation to Kiefer by the Fondazione Musei Civici dates back to 2019, and the decision to host his work in the Palazzo Ducale was aimed at showing the ability of this site-a symbol of the Serenissima Republic-to still serve as a dynamic cultural center and not just a memory. It was a great challenge because it involved adding, even if temporarily, a new series of paintings to these rooms after almost three hundred years, installing them on top of the older ones. Historically, this was something that happened in response to changing tastes or needs of the state. It was an even greater challenge for Kiefer to work alongside important painters from the past-Tintoretto, Palma il Giovane, Andrea Vicentino-who had been called upon by the Senate of the Republic to repaint the glory of Venice, on land and at sea, on the walls of the Sala dello Scrutinio after the devastating fire of 1577-

The result of Kiefer's impressive undertaking is before our eyes, beginning with the title, a quotation from the philosopher Andrea Emo; both he and the artist remind us that these paintings-as in the inevitable design of life-emerge from negation, from the cancellation of others on which they are superimposed. In a certain sense they are the outcome of the fire that destroyed the room's earlier decoration in 1577, and the new paintings, too, are destined to die when they are removed from the Palazzo Ducale. The tragic and irresolvable unity of opposites. Venice is at the center of this striking contemporary installation, not as an object to be celebrated, but rather as a grand metaphor for cultural transits and passages between East and West, as a pretext for a narrative that brings back to the surface the stratification of millennial myths, of solitudes and concerns to which the artist gives form through a new epic with accents as serious as the obscurity of our time."
(Curatorship: Gabriella Belli and Janne Sirén, 2022)

E1.6.E.1.6.b.vi. FORMS OF CHARITY
"The highest form of charity consists in assisting in the subsistence of a person before he becomes impoverished, by offering a substantial gift in a worthy manner, or by granting a suitable loan, or by helping him to find employment or that he may establish himself in business in such a way that it is not necessary for him to become dependent on others."

E1.6.E.1.6.b.vi_REF1_PH ACCORDING TO MAIMONIDES’S EIGHT-LEVELS RANKING OF CHARITY, to which level would correspond dropping coins on the beggers head through the slot?
"1. The highest form of charity is to help sustain a person before he becomes impoverished, by offering a substantial gift in a dignified manner, or by extending a suitable loan, or by helping him find employment or establish himself in business so as to make it unnecessary for him to become dependent on others.
2. A lesser level of charity than this is to give to the poor without knowing to whom one gives, and without the recipient knowing from whom he received.
3. A lesser level of charity than this is when one knows to whom one gives, but the recipient does not know his benefactor.
4. A lesser level of charity than this is when one does not know to whom one gives, but the poor person does know his benefactor.
5. A lesser level than this is when one gives to the poor person directly into his hand, but gives before being asked.
6. A lesser level than this is when one gives to the poor person after being asked.
7. A lesser level than this is when one gives inadequately, but gives gladly and with a smile.
8. A lesser level than this is when one gives unwillingly."
(Barbara Bloom, As it were… So to speak)

E1.6.E.1.6.b.vi_REF1.1_CUR "A SLEEPING BAG IN A PLEXIGLASS VITRINE; appearing almost shrine-like, yet with a slot for coins, the sculpture becomes akin to a donation box where the coins fall in the place where the head would have lain, opening up the reading to wider issues around homelessness, charity and the gaze. It was made by Nelson in memory of a close friend Erlend Williamson. He died while climbing in 1996 and it is his sleeping bag from childhood that occupies the plexiglass vitrine. For Nelson, the death of his friend was a pivotal moment and the work takes on the quality of a relic, reminding us of the powerful material traces of those who came before us.)"
(Curatorship: Art Basel)

E1.6.E.1.6.b.vii. "PSYCHOANALYSTS ARE WELL AWARE THAT GOOD INTENTIONS DON'T ALWAYS LEAD TO BENEFICIAL OUTCOMES FOR PEOPLE. IN FACT, QUITE OFTEN, THE RESULT IS THE OPPOSITE."

E1.6.E.1.6.b.vii_REF1_TH "THE PSYCHOANALYST'S WEAKNESS, HIS TENDENCY, HE KNOWS QUITE WELL THAT HE MUST BE CAREFUL OF GIVING IN TO IT, and in daily practice, of course, he keeps himself in check. On the other hand, the psychoanalyst taken collectively, if there is a crowd of psychoanalysts, a whole bunch of them, want people to know that they are there for the good of all. 
But they are very careful not to have the weakness of rushing too quickly to the good of the individual, to the good of the person with whom they are dealing, because they know very well that you don't get good for people by wanting it, and that in most cases it is even the opposite. Fortunately, this is an idea that they take for granted because of their experience. 
They are far from being real propagandists of psychoanalysis on the outside, whereas it would be healthy if more people knew that you don't do good to your neighbor by wanting too much good for him. It could be useful".
(Jacques Lacan, My Teaching, Place, Origin and End of My Teaching, 1967)

E1.6.E.1.6.b.vii_REF1.1_CUR "JOANNA PIOTROWSKA'S PSYCHOLOGICALLY CHARGED PHOTOGRAPHS PROBE HUMAN BEHAVIOUR AND THE DYNAMICS OF FAMILIAL RELATIONS, exploring intimacy, violence, control, and self-protection. The artist reveals moments of care as well as hierarchies of power, anxieties, and imposed conventions that play out in the domestic sphere. Piotrowska invites her subjects, for instance, to construct makeshift shelters in their own homes out of readily available domestic objects; an activity that, while reminiscent of the innocent children's activity of fort-building, also exposes the home as a space loaded with complex emotions and embodied memory. In one photograph a couple crouches, barely covered by their construction, while in another (both Untitled; 2017) a young woman lies on the floor surrounded by pillows and blankets - typically suppliers of comfort - with her arms raised in surrender and with a fixed gaze. The black-and-white photographs in Piotrowska's Self-Defense series (2014-2015) depict young women, most often in their bedrooms, performing gestures and actions from self-defense manuals, implying the possibility of rebellion against a patriarchal culture. Another series depicts family members in ambiguously intimate poses. Through these unsettling images, Piotrowska challenges conventional narratives of the family and the home and their irreconciled tension with the reality of the wider socio-political realm. LC"
(Curatorship: Venice Biennale)

E1.6.E.1.6.b.viii. SELF-RIGHTOUS CHARITY
"As action without content is agitation in a vacuum, so charity which loves no one with direct and primary love, and which becomes intoxicated with itself by reflection, or devours itself by autophagy, this charity is vain self-righteousness."

E1.6.E.1.6.b.viii_REF1_PH "A CHARITY « MODE » DEPRIVED OF A TARGET FOR ITS LOVE WOULD BE AS HOLLOW AS A THOUGHT WITHOUT THOUGHTFUL IDEAS, as vain and verbal as a representation of nothing at all. A charity mode deprived of its neighbor is like love that, for want of fuel or food, would love itself; who would love to love; would be illusory love of love; love squared or literary complacency. And as an action without content is an agitation in the void, like charity which has no direct and primary love, and which intoxicates itself by reflection, or devours itself by autophagy, this charity is a vain [...] hypocrisy"
(Vladimir Jankélévitch, The Vertues and Love, 1970)

E1.6.E.1.6.b.viii_REF2_PH MAXIMS OF RENÉ GIRARD ON TODAY’S DO-GOODERS:
"1. The current process of spiritual demagoguery and rhetorical overkill has transformed the concern for victims into a totalitarian command and a permanent inquisition.
2. Democracy is one vast middle-class court where the courtiers are everywhere and the king is nowhere.
3. We shouldn’t go so far as to allow good manners to prevent us from thinking.
4. There can be rivalries of renunciation rather than acquisition, of deprivation rather than of enjoyment.
5. On political correctness: It’s the religion of the victim detached from any form of transcendence.
6. In general, history is written by the victors. We’re the only society that wants history to be written by the victims, and we don’t see the unprecedented nature of the reversal.
7. It’s not enough to put people on the same social level because they’ll all find new ways of excluding one another.
8. Marxism wants of course to save victims, but it thinks that the process that makes victims is fundamentally economic.
9. It’s now no longer possible to persecute except in the name of victims."
(René Girard, All desire is a desire of being)

E1.6.E.1.6.c. ONE IS NOT ASKED TO SACRIFICE HIMSELF BUT TO PLACE HIS INTERESTS OUTSIDE HIMSELF
"A happy life is, to a great extent, the equivalent of a good life. Professional moralists have made too much of self-abnegation, and have thus put the emphasis where it should not be put. Conscious self-denial leaves a man absorbed in himself and preserves for him the memory of what he has sacrificed; that is why it often misses its immediate purpose and almost always its final goal. A man is not asked to sacrifice himself but to place his interests outside himself, which will spontaneously and naturally lead to the same acts that a person absorbed in the pursuit of his own virtue could accomplish through conscious sacrifice."
True happiness is something that is internal and comes from within us; its source is not common happiness: intimate and proper happiness is not contrary to virtue, but rather is itself virtuous.

E1.6.E.1.6.c_REF1_PH "THE HAPPY LIFE IS TO AN EXTRAORDINARY EXTENT THE SAME AS THE GOOD LIFE. Professional moralists have made too much of self-denial, and in so doing have
put the emphasis in the wrong place. Conscious self-denial leaves a man self-absorbed and vividly aware of what he has sacrificed; in consequence it fails often of its immediate object and almost always of its ultimate purpose. What is needed is not self-denial, but that kind of direction of interest outward which will lead spontaneously and naturally to the same acts that a person absorbed in the pursuit of his own virtue could only perform by means of conscious self-denial. I have written in this book as a hedonist, that is to say, as one who regards happiness as the good, but the acts to be recommended from the point of view of the hedonist are on the whole the same as those to be recommended by the sane moralist. The moralist, however, is too apt, though this is not, of course, universally true, to stress the act rather than the
state of mind. The effects of an act upon the agent will be widely different, according to his state of mind at the moment. If you see a child drowning and save it as the result of a direct impulse to bring help, you will emerge none the worse morally. If, on the other hand, you say to yourself, ‘It is the part of virtue to succour the helpless, and I wish to be a virtuous man, therefore I must save this child’, you will be an even worse man afterwards than you were before. What applies in this extreme case applies in many other instances that are less obvious."
(Bertrand Russell, The Conquest of Happiness, 1962)

E1.6.E.1.6.c_REF2_PH "WE HAVE SAID MANY THINGS ABOUT THE COUÉ METHOD. It is true that in the realm of spiritual life there is no magic wand, no instruction manual. But our good Emile Coué was right when he said: "The more good you do for others, the more good you do for yourself. The altruist finds without seeking what the egoist seeks without finding. What better invitation than to follow Coué's advice, at least in this respect? [...] The path to joy requires taking great care of oneself while paying little attention to oneself... [...]" 
(Alexandre Jollien, La Pharmacopée n°58)

E1.6.E.1.6.c_REF3_PH ALAIN DIFFERENTIATES BETWEEN TWO TYPES OF HAPPINESS: ONE THAT DEPENDS ON EXTERNAL CIRCUMSTANCES, SUCH AS INHERITANCE OR GLORY, AND ONE THAT DEPENDS ON OUR OWN ABILITIES, which is deeper and more lasting.
"There is a kind of happiness that does not belong to us any more than a coat. This is the happiness of inheritance or of winning the lottery; it is also the happiness of fame, because it depends on encounters. But the happiness that depends on our own powers is, on the contrary, incorporated; we are even more dyed with it than wool is dyed purple. [The sages of old sought happiness; not their neighbor's happiness, but their own. Today's wise men agree that personal happiness is not a noble thing to seek; some practice to say that virtue despises happiness, and this is not difficult to say; others teach that the common happiness is the true source of personal happiness, which is undoubtedly the most vacuous opinion of all, for there is no more futile occupation than to pour happiness into the people around you like into pierced wineskins; I have observed that those who are bored with themselves cannot be amused; and on the contrary, it is to those who do not beg that one can give something, for example, music to those who have become musicians. In short, there is no point in sowing in the sand; and I think I have understood, on reflection, the famous parable of the sower who judges those who lack everything to be incapable of receiving. Those who are powerful and happy by themselves will be happy and powerful by others. Yes, the fortunate will trade and barter well, but they must still have happiness within them to give it. And the resolute man must look once and for all in that direction, which distracts him from a certain kind of love that is of no use.
In my opinion, therefore, this intimate and proper happiness is not contrary to virtue, but rather is virtue itself, as that beautiful word of virtue warns us, which means power. For the most happy in the fullest sense is clearly the one who is best able to throw off other happiness, as one throws off a garment."
(Alain, On Happiness, 1922)

E1.6.E.1.6.c_REF4_PH "THEN AGAIN, DO NOT TELL ME, AS A GOOD MAN DID TO-DAY, OF MY OBLIGATION TO PUT ALL POOR MEN IN GOOD SITUATIONS. Are they my poor? I tell thee, thou foolish philanthropist, that I grudge the dollar, the dime, the cent I give to such men as do not belong to me and to whom I do not belong. There is a class of persons to whom by all spiritual affinity I am bought and sold; for them I will go to prison if need be; but your miscellaneous popular charities; the education at college of fools; the building of meeting-houses to the vain end to which many now stand; alms to sots, and the thousand-fold Relief Societies; though I confess with shame I sometimes succumb and give the dollar, it is a wicked dollar, which by and by I shall have the manhood to withhold.

Virtues are, in the popular estimate, rather the exception than the rule. There is the man and his virtues. Men do what is called a good action, as some piece of courage or charity, much as they would pay a fine in expiation of daily non-appearance on parade. Their works are done as an apology or extenuation of their living in the world-as invalids and the insane pay a high board. Their virtues are penances. I do not wish to expiate, but to live. My life is for itself and not for a spectacle. I much prefer that it should be of a lower strain, so it be genuine and equal, than that it should be glittering and unsteady. I wish it to be sound and sweet, and not to need diet and bleeding. I ask primary evidence that you are a man, and refuse this appeal from the man to his actions. I know that for myself it makes no difference whether I do or forbear those actions which are reckoned excellent. I cannot consent to pay for a privilege where I have intrinsic right."
(Ralph Waldo Emerson, Self-reliance, 1841)

E1.6.E.1.6.c_REF5_PH IT IS WHEN YOU DON'T NEED THE WORLD ANYMORE, THAT YOU CAN MAKE A REAL CONTRIBUTION TOWARD BRINGING ABOUT A BETTER ONE.
“Your primary task is not to seek salvation through creating a better world, but to awaken out of identification with form. You are then no longer bound to this world, this level of reality.  You can feel your roots in the Unmanifested and so are free of attachment to the manifested world.  You can still enjoy the passing pleasures of this world, but there is no fear of loss anymore, so you don't need to cling to them.  Although you can enjoy sensory pleasures, the craving for sensory experience is gone, as is the constant search for fulfillment through psychological gratification, through feeding the ego.  You are in touch with something infinitely greater than any pleasure, greater than any manifested thing.
In a way, you then don't need the world anymore.  You don't even need it to be different from the way it is.
It is only at this point that you begin to make a real contribution toward bringing about a better world, toward creating a different order of reality.  It is only at this point that you are able to feel true compassion and to help others at the level of cause.  Only those who have transcended the world can bring about a better world.
You may remember that we talked about the dual nature of true compassion, which is awareness of a common bond of shared mortality and immortality.  At this deep level, compassion becomes healing in the widest sense.  In that state, your healing influence is primarily based not on doing but on being.  Everybody you come in contact with will be touched by your presence and affected by the peace that you emanate, whether they are conscious of it or not.  When you are fully present and people around you manifest unconscious behavior, you won't feel the need to react to it, so you don't give it any reality.  Your peace is so vast and deep that anything that is not peace disappears into it as if it had never existed. […]
If you feel called upon to alleviate suffering in the world, that is a very noble thing to do, but remember not to focus exclusively on the outer; otherwise, you will encounter frustration and despair.  Without a profound change in human consciousness, the world's suffering is a bottomless pit.  So don't let your compassion become one-sided.  Empathy with someone else's pain or lack and a desire to help need to be balanced with a deeper realization of the eternal nature of all life and the ultimate illusion of all pain.  Then let your peace flow into whatever you do and you will be working on the levels of effect and cause simultaneously.”
(Eckhart Tolle, The Power Of Now, 1997)

E1.7.B.2.5.c. A SINCERE MIND IS INTERESTED IN PEOPLE AND PROBLEMS AND ACTS ACCORDING TO THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE MOMENT, NOT ACCORDING TO RULES. Its aim is liberty, not security. Morality based on rules and regulations cannot lead to freedom.

E1.6.E.2. WHAT IS AN ACTIVITY COMPATIBLE WITH THE COMMON GOOD?

E1.6.E.2.1. WHAT CONSTITUTES AN ACTIVITY COMPATIBLE WITH THE COMMON GOOD?
Some people have more talent for social, generous or philanthropic action; others will contribute to the common good by other more indirect means. What matters is that the pursuit of one's own happiness is compatible with the good of society as a whole, and that it engages us in activities that are objectively valuable. This means that they provide services to others, that they develop knowledge, that they promote excellence in a field, or that they are creative or aesthetic.

E1.6.E.2.1.a. DO YOU HAVE A GENUINE CALLING FOR CHARITABLE WORK ?
Henry David Thoreau posits that authentic charity should originate from genuine concern and compassion for others, not from duty or obligation. Reflecting on his experiences with philanthropic endeavors, he indicates that they don't coincide with his personal values or principles. He ultimately encourages others to follow their passions and vocations, rather than succumbing to pressure to participate in charitable work.

E1.6.E.2.1.a_REF1_PH "I WOULD NOT SUBTRACT ANYTHING FROM THE PRAISE THAT IS DUE TO PHILANTHROPY, but merely demand justice for all who by their lives and works are a blessing to mankind. I do not value chiefly a man’s uprightness and benevolence, which are, as it were, his stem and leaves. Those plants of whose greenness withered we make herb tea for the sick serve but a humble use, and are most employed by quacks. I want the flower and fruit of a man; that some fragrance be wafted over from him to me, and some ripeness flavor our intercourse. His goodness must not be a partial and transitory act, but a constant superfluity, which costs him nothing and of which he is unconscious. This is a charity that hides a multitude of sins. The philanthropist too often surrounds mankind with the remembrance of his own cast-off griefs as an atmosphere, and calls it sympathy. We should impart our courage, afid not our despair, our health and ease, and not our disease, and take care that this does not spread by contagion. [...]
I believe that what so saddens the reformer is not his sympathy with his fellows in distress, but, though he be the holiest son of God, is his private ail. Let this be righted, let the spring come to him, the morning rise over his couch, and he will forsake his generous companions without apology. [...]
Do not stay to be an overseer of the poor, but endeavor to become one of the worthies of the world."
(Henry David Thoreau, Walden, 1854)

E1.6.E.2.1.a_REF1.1_PH "BUT ALL THIS IS VERY SELFISH, I HAVE HEARD SOME OF MY TOWNSMEN SAY. I confess that I have hitherto indulged very little in philanthropic enterprises. I have made some sacrifices to a sense of duty, and among others have sacrificed this pleasure also. There are those who have used all their arts to persuade me to undertake the support of some poor family in the town; and if I had nothing to do—for the devil finds employment for the idle — I might try my hand at some such pastime as that. However, when I have thought to indulge myself in this respect, and lay their heaven under an obligation by maintaining certain poor persons in all respects as comfortably as I maintain myself, and have even ventured so far as to make them the offer, they have one and all unhesitatingly preferred to remain poor. While my townsmen and women are devoted in so many ways to the good of their fellows, I trust that one at least may be spared to other and less humane pursuits. You must have a genius for charity as well as for anything else. As for Doing-good, that is one of the professions which are full. Moreover, I have tried it fairly, and, strange as it may seem, am satisfied that it does not agree with my constitution. Probably I should not consciously and deliberately forsake my particular calling to do the good which society demands of me, to save the universe from annihilation; and I believe that a like but infinitely greater steadfastness elsewhere is all that now preserves it. But I would not stand between any man and his genius; and to him who does this work, which I decline, with his whole heart and soul and life, I would say, Persevere, even if the world call it doing evil, as it is most likely they will."
(Henry David Thoreau, Walden, 1854)

E1.6.E.2.1.b. WHAT IS OBJECTIVELY VALUABLE?
Susan Wolf contends that a meaningful life isn't necessarily a happy one or a life committed to others. Instead, it's a life separate from fulfillment or morality, engaged in activities with objective value. These activities can relate to personal relationships or a craft, like cooking for the family or making a Halloween costume. To discern what holds objective value, communal intuition can be useful. Ask whether the activity pursues knowledge or excellence, is creative or aesthetic, or offers a service to others. Striving for excellence in any field, such as athletics, can also be meaningful, serving as an example for others to practice virtues like discipline and teamwork.

E1.6.E.2.1.b_REF1_PH "WHAT MAKES A LIFE MEANINGFUL? IS THE CHOICE OF WHERE WE FIND MEANING COMPLETELY ARBITRARY? These questions are different from the question 'What is the meaning of life?'
The meaning of life is a metaphysical question - wether my life is meaningful is a different question. Meaning is one dimension of a good life, together with happiness/fulfillment and morality. Susan Wolf advocates that a meaningful life is not necessarily a happy one or one dedicated to others. Meaning is separate from fulfillment or morality: it requires one to have engagements that are objectively valuable. Even if sisyphus was happy, W. argues stone pushing does not constitute an objectively valuable good even if it is subjectively valuable to Sisyphus. So what is an objectively valuable? Activities that we engage in out of our personnel relationship with others - such as cooking meals for the family or making a halloween costume - or out of relationship with a craft - such as putting in long hours of piano practice to attain excellence.
For W. Sartre's existencialism collapses in the end if what you choose to spend your freedom on has no objective value. Finding your passion and going after it is not enough. 
What does make something objectively valuable then? We can use our communal intuition about what is valuable and what isn't. We can also ask if it is in the pursuit of knowledge or excellence? Is it creative or aesthetic? Does it provide a service to others?
The pursuit of excellence in any field (ex: athletic) is meaningful in that it poses the exercise of virtues as an example for others (discipline, team work, etc.) A recluse could do something that then goes to other people and becomes meaningful. Even if a beautiful work is destroyed and nobody ever has seen it except its creator, why is it we feel the life of this artist was still meaningful?
Like happiness that you attain better not trying too hard to reach it, meaning is better achieved not becoming obsessive about it."
(Susan Wolf on Meaning In Life in Philosophy Bites)

Listen to the episode: http://philosophybites.com/2010/07/susan-wolf-on-meaning-in-life.html

E1.6.E.2.1.c. WHAT IS A VIABLE SOLUTION TO THE TASK OF STAYING ALIVE ?
"There are many ways in which man can find a solution to the task of staying alive and of remaining sane. Some are better than others and some are worse. By « better»  is meant a way conducive to greater strength, clarity, joy, independence; and by « worse » the very opposite. But more important than finding the better solution is finding some solution that is viable."

E1.6.E.2.1.c_REF1_PH "MAN IS BORN AS A FREAK OF NATURE, BEING WITHIN NATURE AND YET TRANSCENDING IT. He has to find principles of action and decision making which replace the principles of instinct. He has to have a frame of orientation that permits him to organize a consistent picture of the world as a condition for consistent actions. He has to fight not only against the dangers of dying, starving, and being hurt, but also against another danger that is specifically human: that of becoming insane. In other words, he has to protect himself not only against the danger of losing his life but also against the danger of losing his mind. The human being, born under the conditions described here, would indeed go mad if he did not find a frame of reference which permitted him to feel at home in the world in some form and to escape the experience of utter helplessness, disorientation, and uprootedness. There are many ways in which man can find a solution to the task of staying alive and of remaining sane. Some are better than others and some are worse. By « better»  is meant a way conducive to greater strength, clarity, joy, independence; and by « worse » the very opposite. But more important than finding the better solution is finding some solution that is viable."
(Erich Fromm, The Revolution of Hope: Toward a Humanized Technology, 1968)

E1.6.E.2.1.c_REF2_CUR NOT TO BE TAKEN AT FACE VALUE
"Because altruists are the least sexy 
people on earth, unable 
to say “I want” without embarrassment,

we need to take from them everything 
	they give,
then ask for more,

this is how to excite them, and because 
it’s exciting 
to see them the least bit excited 

once again we’ll be doing something 
for ourselves,
who have no problem taking pleasure,

always desirous and so pleased to be 
pleased, we who above all 
can be trusted to keep the balance."
(Stephen Dunn, Manifesto of the Selfish)

E1.6.E.2.1.d. HOW TO GIVE ?
"TO RICH GIVERS
What you give me I cheerfully accept,
A little sustenance, a hut and garden, a little money, as I rendezvous with my poems,
A traveler’s lodging and breakfast as journey through the States,—why
should I be ashamed to own such gifts? why to advertise for them?
For I myself am not one who bestows nothing upon man and woman,
For I bestow upon any man or woman the entrance to all the gifts of
the universe."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.6.E.2.1.e. “CHARITY SHOULD MAKE
THE GIVER SING
Charity should make the giver sing,
Happy in the undertow of sharing.
A gift should not be given out of duty,
Remiss in both its pleasure and its beauty,
Intent on rectitude, bereft of caring.
The burdens of the world may be worth bearing,
Yet gifts should still a certain lightness bring.”
(Nicholas Gordon, Philosophical poems, 2002)

E1.6.E.2.2. HOW CAN WE MOTIVATE OURSELVES TO PROTECT THE CLIMATE? 
Given the urgency of the climate situation, we must take action to preserve the environment. To inspire ourselves to move away from materialism, we can question whether its primary appeal is simply to boost our self-esteem.

E1.6.E.2.2_REF1_PH KATE SOPER ARGUES THAT LIVING A VIRTUOUS LIFE IN FAVOR OF THE ENVIRONMENT CAN LEAD TO GREATER HAPPINESS THAN THE PREVAILING CONSUMERIST LIFESTYLE. She introduces the concept of "alternative hedonism," suggesting that non-consumerist ways of living can offer more enjoyment, such as more time together, less stress, and better health. 
"An unexpected twist to Aristotle's view of happiness deriving from virtue is to see a virtuous life as one in favour of the environment; and it might actually be a happier one than that of the prevailing consumerist way of life.
Kate Soper believes that we need to rethink how we live in the light of impending environmental catastrophe. But the unexpected element in her thought is that alternative ways of living can be more enjoyable than consumerism. This leads to her notion of alternative hedonism. All sorts of pleasures are missing from the consumerist way of life: more time to be together, more enjoyment of time and space delivered from the reliance on fast transportation methods - and it brings byproducts such as stress, overwork, ill-health, pollution, consumerisation of childhood, obesity. The phenomenon of work-alcoholism is a pretty new one: should we organize differently the work-leisure divide, people who today can't think of how they would occupy their lives without work might change their position. Capitalism is providing for the goods we desire at a cost that could be reduced in an alternative system - while increasing quality: Would we need Tinder if we had time for building relationships? Would we cram in gyms if it was enjoyable to exercise outside in our cities? Would we need escapes such as luxury holidays to regain our quality time? Would we enjoy more time to cook ourselves? 
One challenge of a post consumerist society would be how to provide for the need of human beings to satisfy « l'amour propre » in its definition by Rousseau. Consumerism is very efficient at letting us excel our neighbours in a quantified manner. It might explain part of its success!"
(Kate Soper on Alternative Hedonism, Philosophy Bites)

E1.6.E.2.2_REF1.1_CUR SUCH STRANGELY REFINED AESTHETIC AND SUCH CREATIVITY! It's hard to formulate a critique of  consumerism with more panache and humor! I absolutely need the huge backpack to do my shopping!

Watch the 2'50" video on Vimeo: https://vimeo.com/332745222

E1.6.E.2.2_REF1.2_PH "SELF-ESTEEM 
Self-love as seen through the eyes of the other: the desire to be loved, approved, and admired, and the horror of being hated or despised. La Rochefoucauld saw it as the mainspring of our passions and the driving force of all. Rousseau, more generously and correctly, distinguished it from self-love: "Self-love is a natural emotion which leads every animal to care for its own preservation, and which, directed in man by reason and modified by pity, produces humanity and virtue. Self-love is only a relative, artificial sentiment, born in society, which leads each individual to value himself above all others, which inspires in men all the evils they do to each other, and which is the true source of honor" (Discourse on the Origin of Inequality, note XV). The transition from the first to the second is all too clear. We live primarily for ourselves, but only with and through others. How could we not want to be loved in return? Self-esteem is this love of love centered on oneself and mediated by others. It is loving others only for oneself, and oneself only through others. A double error, or double trap, which explains why self-love, as Alain said, is an unhappy love. But these are small wounds compared to the great dramas of existence. True unhappiness sometimes heals from them. Perhaps so does true happiness."
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.E.2.2_REF1.3_PH "EUDAIMONISM 
Any ethics that makes happiness (eudaimonia) the highest good. Since Socrates, this has been the case in almost all ancient schools, which agreed that everyone wants to be happy and that this is also the goal of philosophy. This did not prevent philosophers from resolutely disagreeing with each other - not about this goal, which they shared, but about its content or its conditions. What constitutes happiness? [These different forms of eudaimonism are more opposed than complementary. They seek the same thing - happiness - but they do not find the same happiness. Eudaimonism is a commonplace of Greek wisdom. But this place is an arena where philosophers confronted each other. Moderns prefer to talk about something else. Not that they have necessarily renounced happiness. But because they have renounced the sovereign good (see this word)".
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.E.2.2_REF1.4_PH "SOVEREIGN GOOD 
In every action, in every choice," wrote Aristotle, "the good is the end, for it is in view of this end that we accomplish everything else" (Nicomachean Ethics, I, 5). But most of the ends we pursue have no value in themselves: they are merely means to other ends. For example, work is an end (and therefore a good) for many of us only through the money it allows us to earn, just as money is a good only through the comfort or luxury it allows us to enjoy... But if every end were only a means to another end, which is only a means to another end, and so on ad infinitum, our desire would by definition be insatiable. It will be said that this is indeed the case, and yet it is what the Greeks refused to accept. Aristotle again: "If, then, there is an end of our activities which we desire for its own sake and the others only because of it, and if we do not endlessly choose one thing in view of another (for then we should go on like this ad infinitum, so that desire would be futile and vain), it is clear that this end can only be the good, the highest good" (ibid., I, 1). This is valid as a definition, at least in a formal sense: the highest good would be the final end, as Aristotle says, that is, the end that is not the means to any end, and of which all other ends are only the means. It would be the ultimate goal of all our actions. 
What is it? What is above all good, what do we desire for its own sake, and in view of what do we desire everything else? "Happiness, because we always choose it for its own sake and never in view of something else" (ibid., 1.5; see also X, 6). Epicurus would answer: "Pleasure", because happiness is worthwhile only insofar as it is pleasurable, while pleasure without happiness is still worthwhile. The Stoics would answer: "virtue," since it is sufficient to make us happy and is better than happiness, which, moreover, no one can claim to do without. Between these three ethics, centered on three sovereign goods, we will avoid widening the gap too much. Eudaimonism is the common lot of Greek wisdom: for Epicurus or Zeno, the idea that happiness might not be pleasant or virtuous was purely academic, something they never seriously considered. Happiness for all is the goal: it is activity in accordance with virtue (Aristotle), pleasure in the repose of the soul (Epicurus), or virtue in action (Zeno).
This is what proves Kant right, some two thousand years later, when he proves them wrong. The word "sovereign" is ambiguous, he notes in the "Dialectic of Practical Reason": it can mean "supreme" or "perfect". But even if virtue were, as Kant claims, "the supreme condition of everything that can seem desirable to us," it would only be "the complete and perfect good" if it were accompanied by happiness. Indeed, if the sovereign good is the absolutely desirable, everyone will agree that it cannot exist without either happiness or virtue: what we desire is the conjunction or, as Kant says, the "exact proportion" of the two. This is what the Epicureans (for whom happiness is virtue) and the Stoics (for whom virtue is happiness) sought. They were both wrong. The union of happiness and virtue, Kant explains, is synthetic, not analytical: they are two "completely different" concepts, the union of which is never guaranteed and almost never given on this earth (C. R. Praxis, I, II, ch. 2). We must therefore renounce the sovereign good or hope for it only in another life, after death. This is where modernity begins to separate us from happiness."
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.E.2.3. WHY WORK TOWARDS REDUCING INEQUALITY?
Considering the existing inequalities and lack of social mobility, actions promoting fairness should lead to greater social justice. Striving for greater equality of opportunity should be a goal, irrespective of political views. This viewpoint aligns with Kant's assertion that such a position is reached through exercising reason. John Rawls helps us define the minimum acceptable comfort level that our society should guarantee for all. However, we must acknowledge that happiness inequality is decreasing, even as material inequalities continue to rise in economically and socially liberal societies. So, does inequality matter?

E1.6.E.2.3.a. SINCE CHANCE IS UNPREDICTABLE, EQUALITY OF OPPORTUNITY SHOULD NOT BE LEFT TO CHANCE - IT'S A QUESTION OF JUSTICE
We shouldn't rely on chance to deliver justice. Instead, it's about shielding the future, as much as possible, from the injustices of the past and even the present.

E1.6.E.2.3.a_REF1_CUR "IN A SEQUENCE OF PHOTOGRAPHS, AGUILAR HOLDS UP FIVE CARDBOARD SIGNS, staging herself in the position of someone asking passersby for help. Three of the cards show dictionarylike definitions of each term in the work's title, written in the artist's hand and separated by signs with plus and equal signs. Together they form the equation Access + Opportunity = Success. In the photos in which the artist is visible, the compositions are cropped such that the top half of her head is obscured- echoing the exclusions art institutions have historically committed by denying access and recognition to artists, like Aguilar, who identify as queer, Latinx, or disabled."
(Curatorship, MomA, NYC)

E1.6.E.2.3.a_REF2_PH "THAT LUCK IS UNFAIR IS PART OF ITS DEFINITION. Equality of opportunity is only an ideal that presupposes something other than luck. 
It is a mistake to believe that we can possess luck (if it is luck that possesses us) or that we deserve it (if everything deserves it). There is no need to be grateful. Luck is just a chance that happens, and happens by chance. 
But without it, there is no happiness. Fate is always stronger. Only luck sometimes gives us the feeling that the opposite is true. So do modest happiness or serene misfortune. Neither is deserved. [...]

FORTUNE
Chance or wealth. As Alain points out, the convergence of these two meanings is revealing. "It reduces the origin of wealth to pure chance, which it is in part because work does not enrich one without some stroke of luck. So, to ask whether fortune is fair is to ask whether the lottery is fair." Fortunately, there are taxes and social security."
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.E.2.3.a_REF3_PH "EQUAL OPPORTUNITIES Chance, wherever it manifests itself, is always unequal. Take the lottery, health, beauty... If everyone won the jackpot, the lottery would no longer be a game of chance: it would no longer be chance, but justice. So why talk about equal chances? Because all players have the same chance of winning, with the same stakes: they are equal before the outcome. The calculation of probabilities predicts it. The rules of the game guarantee it. The officials supervise it. This puts us on the right track. Equal chances cannot depend on luck. This means that it depends on us, that it must be desired, organized, verified - instituted. It is not a matter of luck, but of justice. Not nature, but society. Not chance, but politics and laws. It is only an equality of rights to compensate for the de facto inequalities, which are innumerable. This does not condemn it. On the contrary, it makes it indispensable. We cannot rely on chance to put us in the right place. 
How can we be equal in the face of what is by definition unequal? By giving everyone the right, on the same basis as everyone else, to try their luck, to take full advantage of the opportunities they have, to compensate as much as possible for those they lack. [...] 
It is a matter of compensating, especially in school, for the inequalities that nature and society constantly create or perpetuate. Equality of opportunity means the right not to depend solely on luck or misfortune. It is the equal right of everyone to prove themselves, to make the most of their talents, to overcome, at least partially, their weaknesses: the right to succeed as much as one can deserve; the right not to remain a prisoner of one's origins, one's background or one's status. It is equality, but in the present, in the face of the future - the right to be free by giving oneself the means to become so. It is like anticipatory justice: it is about protecting the future as much as possible from the injustices of the past and even of the present. It is never fully achieved. All the more reason to strive to get closer and closer to it."
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.E.2.3.a_REF4_CUR THE POLITICAL DIMENSIONS OF DISABILITY, ILLNESS, AND CARE
“Berenice Olmedo's is known for her sculptures and kinetic objects, in which she often integrates prostheses and orthoses, revealing the la political dimensions of disability, illness, and care. In Nakewé, Olmedo used different perspectives of tumors, visualized in computerized tomographies and processed in a medical software; from which, she transforms clinical materials into abstract forms that no longer directly reference the human body but instead en become autonomous entities. Through the assemblage and reconfiguration of orthopedic components, splints, and other medical devices, the artist challenges stereotypes, proposing alternative ways of being rooted in what is often overlooked, excluded, or considered residual. As Olmedo herself states, in the world she envisions, "there is no stigma of disability, but only variations of existence, variations of movement, variations of slowness and speed.”
(Curatorship: Jumex, Mexico City)

E1.6.E.2.3.b. FOR KANT, REASON IS NOT ONLY A FACULTY OF KNOWLEDGE, BUT ALSO AN AUTHORITY THAT PRESCRIBES A LAW FOR MORAL ACTION.
In Kant's Critique of Practical Reason, he explores the concept of freedom and questions our capacity for doing good. Kant suggests that the moral law might conflict with our desires, but there is a certain nobility in resisting temptation - this, he argues, is freedom. Furthermore, he posits that the moral law establishes freedom. We can't answer the question "What must I do?" without first addressing "What can I know?"

E1.6.E.2.3.b.i. IMPERATIVE
The imperative is a command expressed in the first person. It doesn't oppose freedom, but instead imposes itself upon it. Kant differentiates between two types of imperative: the hypothetical and the categorical. The hypothetical imperative is conditional, tied to a specific end, while the categorical imperative is universal and has no particular goal in sight. According to Kant, morality is universally applicable because it applies to each individual; the only duty is to be free.

E1.6.E.2.3.b.i_REF1_PH "IMPERATIVE
A command, but one expressed in the first person: not the opposite of freedom, but what it imposes on itself. It is not the same to obey a sovereign or a God (command) and to obey only oneself (imperative). To obey a commandment is to submit, and no doubt this is often necessary. To obey an imperative is to govern oneself, and it is always necessary. 
Since Kant, two types of imperatives have been distinguished: the hypothetical imperative and the categorical imperative. 
The first remains subject to a condition, which is usually the end sought. For example: "If you want your friends to be loyal to you, be loyal to them." Or, "If you want to avoid prison, be honest." These are only rules of prudence or skill. It is a matter of choosing means adapted to a certain end, and they are valid only on condition that the end is pursued.
The categorical imperative, on the other hand, is unconditional. It has nothing to do with an end. For example: "Be loyal to your friends." Or, "Don't lie." These are moral imperatives that are absolutely binding: they have nothing to do with success or efficiency, like prudence or skill, but with duty. Thus, Kant explains when it comes to testifying in court: "He who asks himself for what purpose he should tell the truth is already a wretch. 
Hypothetical imperatives remain particular: they are valid only for those who verify the condition, that is, for those who aim at a certain goal (loyal friends, security, success...). The categorical imperative, because it is unconditional and has no goal, is universal: it is valid for every finite, rational being, as Kant says, including those who do not submit to it. It is the universal itself, insofar as reason states it and prescribes it to itself. This is what determines Kant's well-known and demanding formulation: "Act only according to that maxim which makes it possible for you to want and at the same time makes it a universal law" (Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals, II). This means obeying only oneself (autonomy) by freeing oneself from oneself (universality). Morality is only valid for everyone because it is valid for each individual ("alone, as Alain said, universally"), and the only duty is to be free".
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.E.2.3.b.i_REF2_PH ARE WE FREE TO DO GOOD? "Kant's Critique of Practical Reason On the one hand, there is the humiliation of our self-respect by the moral law, which can contradict our desires, but there is a greatness that goes beyond this humiliation in the act of resisting temptation: it is freedom. What connects man to the world is not so much his sensitive dimension, which always connects him to his finiteness, but human dignity - a sense of power.
Kant is an excellent thinker to help us think about what is beyond us and transcends us - which is usually sudden - such as the ideas of metaphysics, the soul, or freedom. For Kant, transcendental means any philosophical inquiry that is not interested in the nature of an object, but in the conditions of possibility of its knowledge. He was the great "destroyer" of Platonic metaphysics, which held that there were essences and ideals that man could understand. Man has no access to the knowledge of that which transcends the limits of experience.
The Critique of Pure Reason is concerned with the question, "What can I know?" or "What are the conditions of possibility of knowledge? The Critique of Practical Reason addresses the question, "What should I do?" or "What are the conditions of possibility of freedom? With Kant, for the first time, reason is no longer defined solely as the ability to know, but also and primarily as an authority that prescribes a law for us to act morally. For Kant, the second question cannot be answered without having answered the first, because in order to do the good, one must know what the good is - and what it is cannot come from transcendental knowledge. Since Plato, good and evil have been a matter of knowledge ("No one is deliberately evil" - Plato). Kant separates theoretical knowledge from morality. The principle that saves us from moral relativism is that of the universal moral law: what comes first is not good and evil, but the moral law that makes it possible to distinguish them by judgment.

It is the moral law that establishes freedom, illustrated by the passage of the man who is free to obey or not to obey the imperative of the moral law imposed on him when he must either bear false witness against his friend or perish. And freedom and the moral law become clear when we contrast this example with that of the man who will renounce lust without remorse if the knowledge of it means death: in this second case there is only a calculation between two desires (to enjoy and to survive) - morality and freedom are not a choice between sensitive motives of greater or lesser intensity, it is a choice between the satisfaction of a desire and the moral law and its categorical imperative. Freedom is the possibility for man to autonomously abstract himself from all sensitive motives in order to conform to the universal law. On the other hand, there is no reason to renounce the satisfaction of a desire that does not conflict with the moral law. Kant explores the notion of autonomy that Rousseau reserved for politics: Freedom is not opposed to obedience, but it is linked to the conformity of our actions to a law which does not come to us from outside (divine laws, etc.), but which is dictated to us by the critical concept of autonomy, which allows us to discern the universal law of reason. A major difference between external law and the law of reason is that the latter has no content - it is not limited to material prescriptions. Its criterion is formal or logical. For example, lying is not a universalizable behavior because if everyone lied, lying would no longer make sense. Universalization is contradictory because the liar relies on the belief in the truth of the person to whom he is lying. The liar exempts himself from a law - one of the criteria is that he must hide from other people - a law that he recognizes. Evil is the act of deceiving others and, to some extent, oneself, as when the liar allows himself one last little lie. Morality is prescriptive, even for Kant, but the norm does not come from God, nor from society (Durkeim), but from two principles: universalization and non-contradiction - these two principles do not seem to depend on culturalism and thus resist moral relativism (even if there are differences in customs)".
(Michaël Foessel in the program Les chemins de la philosophie, broadcast on France Culture on 10/08/2018)

Listen to the podcast in French: https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/critique-de-la-raison-pratique-de-kant-14-sommes-nous-libres-de-faire-le-bien

E1.6.E.2.3.b.ii. AUTONOMY
Autonomy is the control one has over oneself, embodying the freedom that comes from obeying self-prescribed laws. This concept is primarily associated with Kant's philosophy, which defines autonomy as the capacity to adhere solely to one's own legislations, independent of any external or internal influences, and only to the universal aspect within oneself, that is, reason. As such, autonomy becomes the principle of morality because it signifies obeying the part of oneself that is free, or in other words, self-governance. The term is also regarded as an ideal, representing a process of liberation from all unfree elements within us. Autonomy is not a state but an ongoing process of empowerment.

E1.6.E.2.3.b.ii_REF1_PH "AUTONOMY It is to obey the law that we have prescribed for ourselves, as Rousseau said, and that is what it means to be free. 
The word, in its philosophical use, is mainly due to Kant. Autonomy is self-rule (freedom), but through the (nomos) that reason imposes on itself and imposes on us, which is the moral law. The will is autonomous, Kant explains, when it submits only to its own legislation (as practical reason), independently of any sensible or affective determination, thus independently of the body, but also of the self in its contingent particularity, and even of a law - in other words, only to the universal that one carries within oneself, which is oneself (hence the question of freedom), but as a rational and legislative being (hence the question of autonomy). 
However, the two concepts of autonomy and freedom do not completely overlap. Those who do evil certainly act freely, but without autonomy: they freely submit to the part of themselves that is not free (their instincts, passions, weaknesses, interests, fears). On the other hand, what is autonomy? It is freedom for the good: to be autonomous is to obey the part of oneself that is free, "independently of any of the objects of the faculty of desire," as Kant says, and even independently of the "dear self," in other words, of the particular individual that one is. This is why autonomy is the moral principle: egoism is the source of all evil; reason, which has no ego, is the source of all good. The only duty is to be free, and that is what autonomy means: it is to obey the duty to govern oneself.
If one is not a Kantian, the word has a special value as an ideal. It does not indicate a fact, but a horizon, a process, a task. It is a matter of freeing oneself as much as possible from everything in us that is not free. Reason alone makes this possible (Spinoza, Marx, Freud), and this is where the idea of autonomy retains its meaning. A free man", writes Spinoza, "is one who lives according to the sole commandment of reason...". This autonomy is never given: it is to be done and always to be done again. There is no autonomy; there is only a process, always unfinished, of becoming autonomous. One is not born free; one becomes free."
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.E.2.3.b.iii. MORAL VS. ETHICS
”Morality encompasses our duties, i.e., obligations or prohibitions we impose on ourselves, regardless of any expected reward or sanction, or even hope. According to Kant, a moral action does not rely on its anticipated effects, but solely on the rule it follows, without any personal inclination or calculation. Morality does not require religion or any other end goal, making it secular, even among believers. It's the law that the individual sets for himself and follows unconditionally.

Ethics, on the other hand, involves knowledge and choices, forming a thoughtful, prioritized set of our desires. Ethics addresses the question, "How should we live?" It's always specific to an individual or a group. As an art of living, it aims for happiness and culminates in wisdom.

The mistake would be to choose between the two. No one can do without ethics, as morality provides only an incomplete answer to the question "How to live," and is insufficient for achieving happiness or wisdom. Only a wise person could do without morality, as for them, knowledge and love would suffice.”

E1.6.E.2.3.b.iii_REF1_PH "MORAL 
The whole of our duties, that is to say, the obligations or prohibitions which we impose on ourselves, independently of any expected reward or sanction, or even of any hope. [The present is enough for it. It does not need hope. The will is enough. "An action performed out of duty derives its value not from the end which it is intended to attain," Kant emphasizes, "but from the maxim by which it is determined. Its value does not depend on its expected effects, but only on the rule to which it is subject, regardless of any inclination, any selfish calculation, in short, "regardless of any object of the faculty of desire" and "regardless of the ends that can be attained by such an action" (Groundwork..., I). Even if you act for fame, for your happiness, for your salvation, and even if you act in accordance with morality in everything, you are still not acting morally. An action has true moral value, Kant explains, only insofar as it is selfless. [...] "It has no need of religion," Kant insists, "nor of any end or purpose whatsoever: -not even" (Religion within the Limits of Reason Alone. Preface). This is what makes it secular, even among believers, and absolutely obligatory, or at least that is how we feel. Whether God exists or not, what does that change about the duty to protect what we do not need to know about that existence in order to act humanely?
Let's imagine the opposite, an example we find in Kant, that God exists and is known to all. What would happen? "God and eternity, with their awe-inspiring majesty, would be constantly before our eyes. No one would dare to disobey. The fear of hell and the hope of heaven would give unparalleled power to the divine commandments. Self-interested or fearful submission would reign supreme as an absolute moral order: "Transgression of the law would, of course, be avoided; what is commanded would be done." But no morality would survive: "Most lawful acts would be produced by fear, few by hope, and none by duty, so that the moral value of acts, on which alone the value of man and even of the world in the eyes of supreme wisdom rests, would cease to exist" (C. R. Practice, Dialectic, II, 9). Not only is it not necessary to hope in order to do one's duty, but one can only truly do it on the condition, as Kant also says, that "one does not hope for anything in this respect" (Doctrine of Virtue, § 30). [It is the law that the individual prescribes to himself: in this he is free, as Rousseau would say ("obedience to the law which one has prescribed to himself is freedom"), or autonomous, as Kant would say (because the individual is subject only to "his own and yet universal legislation"). I believe, contrary to Kant and Rousseau, that this freedom or autonomy is itself only relative, but this does not change the feeling that we have of a practical absolute (which comes from the will, not from knowledge) and of a requirement that is unconditional for us. I am convinced that all morality is historical. But far from abolishing morality, it is, on the contrary, what makes it exist and subjects us to it: for we are in history and produced by it. Relative autonomy, then, but better than the slavery of inclinations or fears. [...]
If we leave aside a few painful archaisms that are due more to religious or historical inertia than to strictly moral judgments (I'm thinking in particular of the sexual question, the status of women, and so-called religious duties), we must recognize that what is meant by "good person" in France is not very different - and will undoubtedly become less and less so - from what the equivalent expressions can mean in America or India, Norway or South Africa, Japan or the Maghreb countries. [Where does this morality come from? From God? It is not impossible: as Rousseau believed, He may have placed within us the "immortal and heavenly voice" of conscience, which takes precedence, or should take precedence, over all other considerations, even those of our salvation or His own glory... But what if there is no God? Then we must accept that morality is purely human, that it is merely a product of history, that it is the set of standards that humanity has chosen, valued, and maintained over the centuries. Why these? No doubt because they were favorable to the survival and development of the species (this is what I call Darwinian morality), to the interests of society (this is Durkheimian morality), to the demands of reason (this is Kant's morality), and finally to the recommendations of love (this is Jesus' or Spinoza's morality). Imagine a civilization that advocated lying, selfishness, theft, murder, violence, cruelty, and hatred... [...]"
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.E.2.3.b.iii_REF2_PH "ETHICS [...] In short, morality commands, ethics recommends. These oppositions lead to two different definitions, which I will only recall here:
By morality I mean the normative and imperative discourse that results from the opposition of good and evil, considered as absolute or transcendent values. It consists of do's and don'ts; it is the sum total of our duties. Morality answers the question, "What shall I do? It aims to be unified and universal. It tends toward virtue and culminates in holiness (in Kant's sense: in the sense that a holy will is a will that conforms in all respects to the moral law).
