And by ethics I mean a normative but non-imperative discourse (or without imperatives other than hypothetical ones), resulting from the opposition of good and evil, considered as immanent and relative values. It consists of knowledge and choices: it is the thoughtful and hierarchical whole of our desires. Ethics answers the question "How shall we live? It is always specific to an individual or a group. It is a way of life: it usually tends toward happiness and culminates in wisdom. 
The mistake would be to try to choose between the two. No one can do without ethics, since morality is a very incomplete answer to the question "How should we live?", since it is not enough for either happiness or wisdom. And only a sage could do without morality: because knowledge and love would be enough for him. We are far from that; that is why we need morality (see this word)".
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.E.2.3.b.iv. "CATEGORICAL 
Kant's notion of the categorical imperative]: it's the one that commands absolutely, unconditionally and without escape ("Don't lie"). They all boil down to a single one: "Act only in accordance with the maxim which makes it possible for you, at the same time, to will that it should become a universal law" (Foundations..., II). This is the law that the will imposes on itself, or must impose on itself, insofar as it is a legislator (as practical reason). Every man is free to violate it, but is autonomous only by respecting it."
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.E.2.3.b.v. "GOLDEN RULE
It's found in different forms in most civilizations. It seems to be moral, in this case thought of as reciprocity or reversibility. [..]
This golden rule has two different formulations, one negative (the traditional one): "Do not do to others what you do not want done to you"; the other positive, found in the Gospels: "Whatever you want others to do for you, do it yourselves for them; this is the Law and the Prophets" (Mt 7:12; see also Lk 6:31)."
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.6.E.2.3.c. A THEORY OF JUSTICE
John Rawls' "A Theory of Justice" advocates for a fair distribution of income and wealth based on three principles: the liberty principle, the fair opportunity principle, and the difference principle. According to the difference principle, inequalities are justified only if they result in the least advantaged being as well-off as possible. Rawls employs a thought experiment known as the original position, where individuals consider justice from behind a "veil of ignorance" without prejudice, to establish the minimum level of comfort anyone would consent to.

E1.6.E.2.3.c_REF1_PH "RAWLS' A THEORY OF JUSTICE, 1971 IS ONE OF THE MOST IMPORTANT WORKS OF POLITICAL PHILOSOPHY OF THE TWENTIETH CENTURY. In this podcast interview Nigel Warburton interviews Jonathan Wolff about Rawls' main ideas and their limitations.
Raw argues that what matters most is the position of the worst off in 3 principles:
1. The liberty principle, 2. The fair opportunity principle and 3. The difference principle. A fair distribution of income & wealth requires an equal distribution among people but they are cases when inequalities will be beneficial to everyone (ex: meritocracy encouraging the best to give their best and rewarding performance with higher salaries. However, Raw argues that inequalities are justified only if they make the worst off as well as possible (= the crux of the difference principle).
His argument to defend the difference principle is based on the thought experiment of the original position: when people disagree about justice it is mainly because they are biased by their own interests. Ex: If the rich & the poor could think about taxation oblivious of their own social status, they would come to an agreement. Behind the veil of ignorance - imagining themselves stripped of everything that biases their judgment in their favor - people will create, thinking out of their self-interest, the principles of a fair society.

An opposition would be that I might prefer to gamble that I might make it to the position of unfair privilege. Raw´s response is to ask if you would be ready to gamble with your liberty or opportunities - in the same vein, it would not be rational to gamble with economic fairness. Someone with a different temperament and who puts less weight on liberty, might still want to introduce a little gambling - associated with safety nets for the very poor - Raw's temperament colors the argument and is arguable.

Another critic to the original position though experiment is that it allows those who define the principles to smuggle in their prejudice. What if instead of well-intended philosophers you had fascists defining the principles? Raw's theory is based on the assumption that ignorance is a basis for impartiality. Raw assumes people intrinsically value the primary good he defines as liberty, opportunity, social basis for self-respect and money. What about a monk arguing that money is in the way? Raw would argue that the monk had the opportunity and liberty to chose this life, so he values liberty and opportunity. He might not value money for himself, but he wants the worst off to be as well as possible. A critic by communitarians is that Raw's theory is biased towards an individualistic liberal position: The monk might value other primary goods such as a kind of community that Raw's list of primary goods does not account for."
(Jonathan Wolff on John Rawls' A Theory of Justice in Philosophy Bites)

Listen to the episode: http://philosophybites.com/2010/02/jonathan-wolff-on-john-rawls-a-theory-of-justice.html

E1.6.E.2.3.c_REF2_CUR "POPE.L STAGED THE GUERRILLA STREET ACTION DOCUMENTED IN THESE PHOTOGRAPHS in the guise of his alter ego, Mr. Poots. Wearing a business suit and holding a potted flower, he crawled military-style along the perimeter of Tompkins Square Park in the East Village until his boots crumbled. Since the 1980s, the park had been a site of ongoing riots involving the homeless population who took shelter there, squatters, activists, and police. By forcing onlookers to direct their gazes downward, Pope L aimed to make historically disenfranchised bodies and displaced communities visible. "My take on homelessness in New York was that we'd gotten tco used to seeing these people on the streets I wanted people to have this retarde"
(Curatorship, MomA, NYC)

E1.6.E.2.3.c_REF3_SOC "ARE AMERICAN PROGRESSIVES MAKING THEMSELVES SAD?
Conservatives seem more excited about change

Surely few developments could be less surprising than the recent news that America has slipped down the global happiness rankings. Gallup reported in mid-March that America had dropped out of the top 20 for the first time since it started taking its survey in 2012, falling in a year from 15th place to 23rd. (Yes, annoyingly, Finland came out on top, for the seventh straight year.)

Their economy and technology may be the envy of the world, but Americans are becoming a dyspeptic bunch, anxious about the future and uneasy about foundational institutions, from the armed forces to the press to organised religion. Yet all are not equally sad. Numerous studies and surveys—Americans are obsessed with this subject—show that some groups tend to lag behind others in the pursuit of happiness: bankers are said to be sadder than lumberjacks, the unmarried sadder than the married, teenage girls sadder than teenage boys.

One distinction that holds true today has persisted for decades: liberals are sadder than conservatives. This is a global symptom of political difference, but it is particularly strong in America. Of whatever age group or whichever sex, liberals are also far more likely than conservatives to report having been diagnosed with a mental illness.

In the new Gallup survey self-reported happiness fell for every age group, but most precipitously for those 30 and younger. Older Americans ranked tenth globally in happiness, whereas younger Americans ranked 62nd. That is a change from a decade ago, when the two groups reported similar levels of happiness. The trend is consistent with data from the Centres for Disease Control and Prevention, which surveys 17,000 high-school students every two years. Rates of mental-health problems have increased with every survey since 2011, and last year the cdc reported the highest rates of sadness found in a decade, particularly among girls.

In a study in 2021 called “The Politics of Depression”, a group of scholars zeroed in on the possible link between political ideology and unhappiness among teenagers. They found an alarming rise in depression among young people starting in 2012, and, like the cdc, a particular increase among girls. But ideological difference mattered more than gender difference. Liberal boys reported higher rates of depression than conservative boys or girls, and liberal girls reported the highest rates of all.

Disentangling correlation from cause to explain the happiness gap between conservatives and liberals has long vexed social psychologists and political commentators. So, no doubt, has the task of disentangling one’s own politics from one’s hypotheses. The authors of the study connected the rise of depression with the spread of social media. They also argued that conservative ideology may help protect mental health, for reasons that did not flatter conservatives: “This group presumably benefits from the American cultural myth of an equal playing field in which exceptional social positions are thought to be earned through hard work and talent rather than inherited through codified privilege.” Liberal adolescents, they wrote, may feel alienated in contrast to conservative peers “whose hegemonic views were flourishing”.

A possible flaw in this theory is that, in the first four years that young liberals’ mental health declined, Barack Obama was president and conservative views were not so successfully hegemonic. Even before 2012, when teenagers reported relatively stable mental health, young liberals, like older liberals, reported higher rates of depression. Sceptics of the authors’ hypothesis have noted that being conservative could confer psychological benefits for less cynical reasons. Conservatives tend to be healthier, more patriotic and more religious, and to report finding higher levels of meaning in their lives. These characteristics correlate with happiness.


It is possible that liberalism does not just correlate with sadness but may exacerbate it. Musa al-Gharbi, a sociologist at Stony Brook University, has noted that educated, affluent white liberals have come to endorse the idea that America is systemically racist, leading them to view other racial and ethnic groups more warmly than their own. “This tension—being part of a group that one hates—creates strong dissociative pressures on many white liberals,” he wrote in the journal American Affairs. Another hypothesis, advanced by Jonathan Haidt, a social psychologist, and Greg Lukianoff, a lawyer, is that liberals are performing a reverse cognitive behavioural therapy on themselves: promoting not resilience and optimism about incrementally improving the world but catastrophic rumination about problems such as climate change and fearfulness of disagreement even on university campuses. Such habits of mind can deepen depression."
(The Economist, April 4th 2024)

Listen to the article: https://www.economist.com/united-states/2024/04/04/are-american-progressives-making-themselves-sad

E1.6.E.2.3.d. UNEQUAL, YET HAPPY
Researchers have found that, although the US national average for happiness has remained consistent, inequality in happiness has significantly decreased in recent decades. This is true both in terms of the dispersion of answers and disparities among demographic groups. Studies also indicate a relationship between economic growth and reductions in happiness inequality, even when income inequality is simultaneously rising.
This could be because national income growth allows for the increased provision of public goods. Alternatively, it may be due to society's growing cultural diversity, which enables people to achieve similar levels of happiness despite differences in income, preferences, and consumption.

E1.6.E.2.3.d_REF1_SOC THE GENERAL SOCIAL SURVEY  IN THE US REVEALS THAT WHILE THE NATIONAL AVERAGE OF SELF-REPORTED LIFE SATISFACTION HAS REMAINED CONSTANT, HAPPINESS INEQUALITY HAS DECREASED SIGNIFICANTLY. Disparities in happiness between demographic groups, such as black-white and gender gaps, have narrowed. Other studies corroborate these findings, showing a correlation between economic growth and reductions in happiness inequality, even when income inequality rises. This trend is evident in rich countries with continuous GDP growth, where happiness inequality has generally declined.
"The General Social Survey (GSS) in the US is a survey administered to a nationally representative sample of about 1,500 respondents each year since 1972, and is an important source of information on long-run trends of self-reported life satisfaction in the country.

Using this source, Stevenson and Wolfers (2008) show that while the national average has remained broadly constant, inequality in happiness has fallen substantially in the US in recent decades.

The authors further note that this is true both when we think about inequality in terms of the dispersion of answers, and also when we think about inequality in terms of gaps between demographic groups. They note that two-thirds of the black-white happiness gap has been eroded (although today white Americans remain happier on average, even after controlling for differences in education and income), and the gender happiness gap has disappeared entirely (women used to be slightly happier than men, but they are becoming less happy, and today there is no statistical difference once we control for other characteristics).

The results from Stevenson and Wolfers are consistent with other studies looking at changes of happiness inequality (or life satisfaction inequality) over time. In particular, researchers have noted that there is a correlation between economic growth and reductions in happiness inequality—even when income inequality is increasing at the same time. The visualization below, from Clark, Fleche and Senik (2015) shows this. It plots the evolution of happiness inequality within a selection of rich countries that experienced uninterrupted GDP growth.

In this chart, happiness inequality is measured by the dispersion—specifically the standard deviation—of answers in the World Value Survey. As we can see, there is a broad negative trend. In their paper the authors show that the trend is positive in countries with falling GDP."
(Our world in data)

Read the full article:
https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/evolution-of-happiness-inequality-within-countries-during-periods-of-uninterrupted-economic-growth?time=1981..2007

E1.6.E.2.3.d_REF2_SOC "WHY COULD IT BE THAT HAPPINESS INEQUALITY FALLS WITH RISING INCOME INEQUALITY?

Clark, Fleche, and Senik argue that part of the reason is that the growth of national income allows for the greater provision of public goods, which in turn tighten the distribution of subjective well-being. This can still be consistent with growing income inequality, since public goods such as better health affect incomes and well-being differently.

Another possibility is that economic growth in rich countries has translated into a more diverse society in terms of cultural expressions (e.g. through the emergence of alternative lifestyles), which has allowed people to converge in happiness even if they diverge in incomes, tastes and consumption. Steven Quartz and Annette Asp explain this hypothesis in a New York Times article, discussing evidence from experimental psychology."
(Steven Quartz and Anette Asp, Unequal, yet happy, The New York Times, April 11, 2015)

Read the full article: https://www.nytimes.com/2015/04/12/opinion/sunday/unequal-yet-happy.html?smid=nytcore-ipad-share&smprod=nytcore-ipad&_r=0

E1.6.E.2.3.e. INEQUALITY IS DETRIMENTAL WHEN IT COMPROMISES HUMAN RELATIONSHIPS AND ERODES DIGNITY
Inequality becomes harmful when it results in both political and economic domination. For instance, if the threat of poverty restricts job mobility, inequality can damage human relationships and compromise the dignity of individuals forced to accept menial jobs. It also undermines societal values of friendship and a shared vision for a common societal goal. As Rousseau aptly put it, "In a just society, no man should be rich enough to buy another and no man poor enough to want to sell himself."

E1.6.E.2.3.e_REF1_PH "IN A JUST SOCIETY NO MAN SHOULD BE RICH ENOUGH TO BUY ANOTHER AND NO MAN POOR ENOUGH TO WANT TO SELL HIMSELF
"Does inequality really matter? Or is it better to strive for improving the lot of the worst off, even if there is still a huge disparity between what different individuals have? Is there ground to be preoccupied how we are doing in comparison with others at all? Joke: The first man asks to the second man: « How are your children? »; the second man responds: « Compared to what? ». Shouldn't we be concerned about how we are doing in absolute terms and not in relation to others? 
Inequality is wrong if it results in domination, both political and economic. Or if you can't change jobs because the risk of poverty makes it too risky; this corrodes human relations because some people have no choice but to become sub-servants , servile and robbed of their self-esteem and dignity. Values of friendship and of a shared purpose in society also become eroded. Paraphrasing Rousseau on the subject: In a just society no man should be rich enough to buy another and no man poor enough to want to sell himself. 
Inequality also matters because it determines the strength of peoples' claim on resources. Suppose 2 persons are disabled, one very severely, the other moderately. If it takes the same amount of resources to make the severely disabled moderately disabled than to make the moderately disabled, not disabled at all, the shared perception of fairness is that resources should be allocated to making the severely disabled moderately handicapped."
(Alex Voorhoeve on Inequality in Philosophy Bites)

E1.6.E.2.3.e_REF2_PH “WHETHER PROPERTY CAN BE SQUARED WITH JUSTICE.—When the injustice of property is strongly felt (and the hand of the great clock is once more at this place), we formulate two methods of relieving this injustice: either an equal distribution, or an abolition of private possession and a return to State ownership. The latter method is especially dear to the hearts of our Socialists, who are angry with that primitive Jew for saying, “Thou shalt not steal.” In their view the eighth commandment should rather run, “Thou shalt not possess.”—The former method was frequently tried in antiquity, always indeed on a small scale, and yet with poor success. From this failure we too may learn. “Equal plots of land” is easily enough said, but how much bitterness is aroused by the necessary division and separation, by the loss of time-honoured possessions, how much piety is wounded and sacrificed! We uproot the foundation of morality when we uproot boundary-stones. Again, how much fresh bitterness among the new owners, how much envy and looking askance! For there have never been two really equal plots of land, and if there were, man's envy of his neighbour would prevent him from believing in their equality. And how long would this equality, unhealthy and poisoned at the very roots, endure? In a few generations, by inheritance, here one plot would come to five owners, there five plots to one. Even supposing that men acquiesced in such abuses through the enactment of stern laws of inheritance, the same equal plots would indeed exist, but there would also be needy malcontents, owning nothing but dislike of their kinsmen and neighbours, and longing for a general upheaval.—If, however, by the second method we try to restore ownership to the community and make the individual but a temporary tenant, we interfere with agriculture. For man is opposed to all that is only a transitory possession, unblessed with his own care and sacrifice. With such property he behaves in freebooter fashion, as robber or as worthless spendthrift. When Plato declares that self-seeking would be removed with the abolition of property, we may answer him that, if self-seeking be taken away, man will no longer possess the four cardinal virtues either; as we must say that the most deadly plague could not injure mankind so terribly as if vanity were one day to disappear. Without vanity and self-seeking what are human virtues? By this I am far from meaning that these virtues are but varied names and masks for these two qualities. Plato's Utopian refrain, which is still sung by Socialists, rests upon a deficient knowledge of men. He lacked the historical science of moral emotions, the insight into the origin of the good and useful characteristics of the human soul. He believed, like all antiquity, in good and evil as in black and white—that is to say, in a radical difference between good and bad men and good and bad qualities.—In order that property may henceforth inspire more confidence and become more moral, we should keep open all the paths of work for small fortunes, but should prevent the effortless and sudden acquisition of wealth. Accordingly, we should take all the branches of transport and trade which favour the accumulation of large fortunes—especially, therefore, the money market—out of the hands of private persons or private companies, and look upon those who own too much, just as upon those who own nothing, as types fraught with danger to the community.”
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human III, Aphorism 285, 1878)

E1.6.E.2.3.f. "IN DIRE MISFORTUNE, THE SURPLUS ENERGY THAT SUPPORTS FREE WILL, ALLOWING MAN TO DISTANCE HIMSELF FROM THE WORLD, NO LONGER EXISTS. 
The vital instinct survives severed ties and blindly clings to anything that can support it, much like a plant clings with its tendrils. In this state, recognition and justice are inconceivable. It's akin to slavery."

E1.6.E.1.6.b.v_REF1_PH.wE1.6.E.2.3.f.

E1.6.E.2.3.g. WHEN YOU MAKE HAPPINESS A RIGHT YOU CHANGE THE NOTION OF HAPPINESS ITSELF INTO AN ACTIVE PURSUIT
In a secular culture happiness is not a promise you will experiment in an afterlife. If life presents contingencies such as illness or injustice, making happiness a right dictates we have a right and a duty to intervene against social injustice.

E1.6.E.2.3.g_REF1_PH "SUSAN NEIMAN DRAWS ON ENLIGHTENMENT THINKING TO ILLUMINATE THE VALUES WE SHOULD LIVE BY IN THE 21ST CENTURY.
People think there is widespread disagreement about moral principles but when you get them to talk about individual cases, it turns out there is a great deal more moral agreement than one thinks. 
One of the divides that remain is the clash between secular values and fundamentalist religions. Fundamentalism being on the rise is in great proportion attributable to the rampant consumism prevalent in liberal societies: people want their lives to mean more than that and want to be guided by certain ideals about the way the world should be. To defend the values of the enlightment is to defend the modern world with its possibilities for self-criticism and transformation. Susan Neiman identified 4 robust values from the enlightment that can guide us still today:
1. The right to happiness. In a secular culture happiness is not a promise you will experiment in an afterlife. If life presents contingencies such as illness or injustice, making happiness a right dictates we have a right and a duty to intervene against social injustice: when you make happiness a right you change the notion of happiness itself into an active pursuit.
2. The value of reason as opposed to superstition and blind authority.
3. Reverence detached from organized and established religion. Even if the enlightment did not get rid of the figure of a creator, the awe and gratefulness encompassed by the value of reverence can be directed to a world which we did not make - uniting secular and religious people alike. If you feel reverence for the world, you have to feel it towards other human beings as well.
4.  Hope, which is very different from optimism. That the enlightment believed in necessary universal progress is a contre-sens. See Kant and Voltaire in Candide. The perfectionability of man seen by Rousseau to be the root of regression as well as of progress. Pessimism is an attitude that may look brave, but if you only think things are going downhill you stop acting. Hope makes us responsible to make the world a better place."
(Susan Neiman on Morality in the 21st Century, Philosophy Bite)

Listen to the episode: http://philosophybites.com/2010/03/susan-neiman-on-morality-in-the-21st-century.html

E1.6.E.2.3.h. BEWARE OF POLITICIANS WHO PROMISE HAPPINESS FOR ALL WHILE THEIR ACTIONS FOCUS ONLY ON IMPROVING THEIR OWN LOT.
“I would rather be the unwilling subject of an absolute monarch than the willing slave of my own ignorance, of which advantage is taken by those who spend their time in endeavoring to prove to me that I am free and in singing the glories of my condition, to hoodwink my reason and to blind my perception… That system of government by which it is possible for a class of people to practice upon my credulity, and, under false pretenses, first entice me to acquiesce in laws by which immense corporations and monopolies are established, and then to induce me to submit to their extortions because they exist according to law, pursuing none but lawful means, is an infernal despotism, compared to which the Russian Czar is a thousand times to be preferred.”
(Victoria Woodhull, Manifesto published a few months before running for president of the USA, 1872)

E1.6.E.2.3.h_REF1_PH “THE REVOLUTION-SPIRIT AND THE POSSESSION-SPIRIT.—The only remedy against Socialism that still lies in your power is to avoid provoking Socialism—in other words, to live in moderation and contentment, to prevent as far as possible all lavish display, and to aid the State as far as possible in its taxing of all superfluities and luxuries. You do not like this remedy? Then, you rich bourgeois who call yourselves “Liberals,” confess that it is your own inclination that you find so terrible and menacing in Socialists, but allow to prevail in yourselves as unavoidable, as if with you it were something different. As you are constituted, if you had not your fortune and the cares of maintaining it, this bent of yours would make Socialists of you. Possession alone differentiates you from them. If you wish to conquer the assailants of your prosperity, you must first conquer yourselves.—And if that prosperity only meant well-being, it would not be so external and provocative of envy; it would be more generous, more benevolent, more compensatory, more helpful. But the spurious, histrionic element in your pleasures, which lie more in the feeling of contrast (because others have them not, and feel envious) than in feelings of realised and heightened power—your houses, dresses, carriages, shops, the demands of your palates and your tables, your noisy operatic and musical enthusiasm; lastly your women, formed and fashioned but of base metal, gilded but without the ring of gold, chosen by you for show and considering themselves meant for show—these are the things that spread the poison of that national disease, which seizes the masses ever more and more as a Socialistic heart-itch, but has its origin and breeding-place in you. Who shall now arrest this epidemic?”
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human II, Aphorism 304, 1878)

E1.6.E.2.3.i. “DANGER IN WEALTH.—Only a man of intellect should hold property: otherwise property is dangerous to the community. For the owner, not knowing how to make use of the leisure which his possessions might secure to him, will continue to strive after more property. This strife will be his occupation, his strategy in the war with ennui. So in the end real wealth is produced from the moderate property that would be enough for an intellectual man. Such wealth, then, is the glittering outcrop of intellectual dependence and poverty, but it looks quite different from what its humble origin might lead one to expect, because it can mask itself with culture and art—it can, in fact, purchase the mask. Hence it excites envy in the poor and uncultured—who at bottom always envy culture and see no mask in the mask—and gradually paves the way for a social revolution. For a gilded coarseness and a histrionic blowing of trumpets in the pretended enjoyment of culture inspires that class with the thought, “It is only a matter of money,” whereas it is indeed to some extent a matter of money, but far more of intellect.”
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human II, Aphorism 310, 1878)

E1.6.E.2.4. WE ARE IN THIS TOGETHER
The emphasis one places on action versus contemplation can vary based on temperament. However, regardless of our personal tendencies, it is ethically challenging to dismiss our responsibilities to others under the pretense of pursuing individual happiness.

E1.6.E.2.4_REF1_LIT OLIVIA LAING ARGUES THAT IN LATE CAPITALISM, DIFFICULT EMOTIONS ARE SEEN AS A PROBLEM TO BE FIXED RATHER THAN A RESPONSE TO STRUCTURAL INJUSTICE or the nature of being human. She suggests that the cure for loneliness is not necessarily meeting someone, but rather learning to befriend oneself and understanding the impact of larger societal forces on individual experiences. She also emphasizes the importance of kindness, solidarity and staying open in navigating loneliness and the world. Loneliness is both personal and political and it is important to remember that the search for individual happiness does not excuse obligations to others.

E1.6.E.2.4_REF1.1_LIT "THERE IS A GENTRIFICATION THAT IS HAPPENING TO CITIES, AND THERE IS A GENTRIFICATION THAT IS HAPPENING TO THE EMOTIONS TOO, with a similarly homogenising, whitening, deadening effect. Amidst the glossiness of late capitalism, we are fed the notion that all difficult feelings — depression, anxiety, loneliness, rage — are simply a consequence of unsettled chemistry, a problem to be fixed, rather than a response to structural injustice or, on the other hand, to the native texture of embodiment, of doing time, as David Wojnarowicz memorably put it, in a rented body, with all the attendant grief and frustration that entails.

I don’t believe the cure for loneliness is meeting someone, not necessarily. I think it’s about two things: learning how to befriend yourself and understanding that many of the things that seem to afflict us as individuals are in fact a result of larger forces of stigma and exclusion, which can and should be resisted.

Loneliness is personal, and it is also political. Loneliness is collective; it is a city. As to how to inhabit it, there are no rules and nor is there any need to feel shame, only to remember that the pursuit of individual happiness does not trump or excuse our obligations to each another. We are in this together, this accumulation of scars, this world of objects, this physical and temporary heaven that so often takes on the countenance of hell. What matters is kindness; what matters is solidarity. What matters is staying alert, staying open, because if we know anything from what has gone before us, it is that the time for feeling will not last."
(Olivia Laing, The Lonely City: Adventures in the Art of Being Alone)

E1.6.E.2.4_REF2_PH "THE ANCESTRAL OPPOSITION BETWEEN CONFUCIUS AND LAO-TZU. Confucianism and Taoism: two great archetypes, two postulates, an eternal paradigm to be studied: the mythical paradigms; Plato/Aristotle, Voltaire/Rousseau, Dostoevsky/Tolstoy. Let us remember:

Confucius	                   Lao-Tzeu
Preaching → 			   Retreat
Legislation → 			   Nature
Social Man → 			   Individual
Harmony → 			   Rest 
Suitability → 			   Let go
« Do for nothing » →    	   « do nothing »"
(Roland Barthes, The Neutral, Course at the Collège de France, 1977-1978)

E1.6.E.2.4_REF3_PH "THE GREAT BEAST (5)

[...] To contemplate the social is as good a way of detachment as to retire from the world. That is why I have not been wrong to rub shoulders with politics for so long.

Note: 

(5) On the origin of this myth, cf. Plato, Republic, book VI. - To worship the 'great beast' is to think and act in accordance with the prejudices and reflexes of the crowd, to the detriment of any personal search for truth and goodness.”
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.6.E.2.5. HAPPINESS WITH AUTHENTICITY AND RESPONSIBILITY
Viktor Frankl argues that happiness cannot be pursued directly. Instead, it spontaneously arises as a byproduct of living authentically and with integrity. He perceives life as a duty and a significant obligation. Frankl suggests that the question of life's meaning is often misdirected: it is life that poses the questions and directs them at us. He insists that living equates to being questioned, and our existence is entirely about responding to and being responsible for life.

E1.6.E.2.5_REF1_PH FRANKL EMPHASIZES THAT LIFE POSES QUESTIONS TO US, AND OUR RESPONSIBILITY IS TO RESPOND MEANINGFULLY TO THESE QUESTIONS IN THE PRESENT MOMENT. The meaning of life is not a general question but a specific, situational one that varies for each individual. Thus, life always offers opportunities for meaningful existence, and we must continually answer life's questions with responsibility and awareness.
"In consonance with Camus’s view of happiness as a moral obligation — an outcome to be attained not through direct pursuit but as a byproduct of living with authenticity and integrity — Frankl reflects on Tagore’s poetic point:

"So, life is somehow duty, a single, huge obligation. And there is certainly joy in life too, but it cannot be pursued, cannot be “willed into being” as joy; rather, it must arise spontaneously, and in fact, it does arise spontaneously, just as an outcome may arise: Happiness should not, must not, and can never be a goal, but only an outcome; the outcome of the fulfillment of that which in Tagore’s poem is called duty… All human striving for happiness, in this sense, is doomed to failure as luck can only fall into one’s lap but can never be hunted down. [...]

At this point it would be helpful [to perform] a conceptual turn through 180 degrees, after which the question can no longer be “What can I expect from life?” but can now only be “What does life expect of me?” What task in life is waiting for me?
Now we also understand how, in the final analysis, the question of the meaning of life is not asked in the right way, if asked in the way it is generally asked: it is not we who are permitted to ask about the meaning of life — it is life that asks the questions, directs questions at us… We are the ones who must answer, must give answers to the constant, hourly question of life, to the essential “life questions.” Living itself means nothing other than being questioned; our whole act of being is nothing more than responding to — of being responsible toward — life. With this mental standpoint nothing can scare us anymore, no future, no apparent lack of a future. Because now the present is everything as it holds the eternally new question of life for us. [...]
The question life asks us, and in answering which we can realize the meaning of the present moment, does not only change from hour to hour but also changes from person to person: the question is entirely different in each moment for every individual.
We can, therefore, see how the question as to the meaning of life is posed too simply, unless it is posed with complete specificity, in the concreteness of the here and now. To ask about “the meaning of life” in this way seems just as naive to us as the question of a reporter interviewing a world chess champion and asking, “And now, Master, please tell me: which chess move do you think is the best?” Is there a move, a particular move, that could be good, or even the best, beyond a very specific, concrete game situation, a specific configuration of the pieces? [...]

One way or another, there can only be one alternative at a time to give meaning to life, meaning to the moment — so at any time we only need to make one decision about how we must answer, but, each time, a very specific question is being asked of us by life. From all this follows that life always offers us a possibility for the fulfillment of meaning, therefore there is always the option that it has a meaning. One could also say that our human existence can be made meaningful “to the very last breath”; as long as we have breath, as long as we are still conscious, we are each responsible for answering life’s questions."
(Maria Popova, Brain Pickings, Review on Viktor Frankl's  Yes to Life: In Spite of Everything, 1946)

E1.6.E.2.5_REF2_LIT "COMPETENCE IS HOW GOOD YOU ARE WHEN THERE IS SOMETHING TO GAIN. CHARACTER IS HOW GOOD YOU ARE WHEN THERE IS NOTHING TO GAIN. People will reward you for competence. But people will only love you for your character.”
(Mark Manson)

E1.6.E.2.6. THERE ARE MANY ACTIVITIES WE DO BECAUSE WE FEEL PART OF A HUMAN ENTERPRISE THAT WILL SURVIVE US

E1.6.E.2.6.a. CONSIDER THIS THOUGHT EXPERIMENT:
Imagine a forthcoming apocalypse that will wipe out humanity in a short time. Many activities we prioritize today may seem pointless in this scenario. This thought experiment encourages us to consider the impact of our actions on future generations. This argument holds power as it connects to the meaning of our present life, not just our moral responsibility to the future. While future generations are indeed dependent on us, this idea flips the narrative: our current life's significance heavily relies on future generations!

E1.6.E.2.6.a_REF1_PH "WHAT GIVES VALUE TO WHAT WE DO? IF ALL SENTIENT LIFE WERE TO END A FEW MINUTES AFTER MY DEATH, HOW WOULD THAT AFFECT THE MEANING OF WHAT I'M DOING NOW? Many of our activities would then seem pointless (Scientific research, Schooling, etc.). This though experiment demonstrates that there are many activities we do because we feel we are part of a human enterprise that will survive us. If the apocalypse was not to be sudden, but deferred permitting that all the people currently alive die a natural death (supposing all humans become infertile and no assisted reproduction is possible for example), the impact of the though experiment is the same - with a little less sadness due only to the fact that people we love will live their natural life.
Even activities which pleasure is wholly grounded in the present, like listening to music, have their value somewhat diminished in the scenario because our receptivity to art and beauty is dependent on our general state of mind.
It is interesting that the fact that humankind is actually doomed for extinction in a distant future, like all species, does not affect us in the same way the short-span scenario does. It might be because we hope for some solution of preservation that will be invented by our descendants (colonization of outer space for example). In addition to the world surviving for some finite length of time after we're gone, so will our legacy last for a finite time, too. Whether we're figuring out how to beat cancer, or designing a better bridge, what we do in this life will last for some time after we're gone, but not an infinite time. We might think of our legacy as having a "half life" of, say, 300 (or 10 or 1000) years. The half life of citations of scientific papers is actually measured and cataloged, so it's fair to use the weighted average of these half lives as a decent measure of a scientist's legacy. The point is: if I judge the half-life of my legacy to be longer than my expectation of the end of the world, then I would say, as Singer did, that schoolwork is pointless, and not otherwise.
It might also be because of a cognitive limitation we have of representing to ourselves the time span of millions of years - which seems to us equivalent to eternity. Another cognitive bias might be what Derek Parfit's called our "bias towards the near" (in time), which is also documented by Daniel Kanheman's WYSIATI (What You See Is All There Is). It also might be because we conform ourselves more easily to a scenario where Humans had a chance to live their natural cycle vs. a disruptive or accidental end. There might also be rational pressure to standardize our reaction to a distant dooms day perspective: one is to loose confidence in the value of our activities now. Another way to rationalize would be not to loose confidence in the value of our activities even in the short term case: we should apply to that case the indifference we display to the long-term natural disparition.
The thought experiment helps us weight the importance our actions on favoring or disfavouring future generations (depleting the environment for example) should have in the way we conduct our lives. The argument is especially powerful because it appeals to the meaning of our own life right now and does not rest on acceptance of a moral responsibility towards future generations. Of course future generations are dependant on us but we just reversed the argument: we are dependant on future generation for a large share of the meaning of our life today!"
(Samuel Scheffler on the Afterlife in Philosophy Bites)

Listen to the episode: http://philosophybites.com/2013/07/samuel-scheffler-on-the-afterlife.html

E1.6.E.2.6.a_REF2_PH TAKING PERSONAL RISKS TO SAVE THE COLLECTIVE ARE“COURAGE” AND “PRUDENCE”SINCE YOU ARE LOWERING RISKS FOR THE COLLECTIVE.
"WHO IS “YOU”?
Let us return to the notion of “tribe.” One of the defects modern education and thinking introduces is the illusion that each one of
us is a single unit. In fact, I’ve sampled ninety people in seminars and asked them: “what’s the worst thing that can happen to you?” Eighty-eight people answered “my death.”
This can only be the worst-case situation for a psychopath. For after that, I asked those who deemed that their worst-case outcome was their own death: “Is your death plus that of your children, nephews, cousins, cat, dogs, parakeet, and hamster (if you have any of the above) worse than just your death?” Invariably, yes. “Is your death plus your children, nephews, cousins (…) plus all of humanity worse than just your death?” Yes, of course. Then how can your death be the worst possible outcome?
Unless you are perfectly narcissistic and psychopathic—even then—your worst-case scenario is never limited to the loss of only your life.
Thus, we see the point that individual ruin is not as big a deal as collective ruin. And of course ecocide, the irreversible destruction of our environment, is the big one to worry about.
To use the ergodic framework: my death at Russian roulette is not ergodic for me but it is ergodic for the system. The precautionary principle, as I formulated with a few colleagues, is precisely about the highest layer.
About every time I discuss the precautionary principle, some overeducated pundit suggests that “we take risks by crossing the street,” so why worry so much about the system? This sophistry usually causes a bit of anger on my part. Aside from the fact that the risk of being killed as a pedestrian is less than one in 47,000 years, the point is that my death is never the worst-case scenario unless it correlates to that of others."
(Nassim Nicholas Taleb, Skin in the game, 2017)

E1.6.E.2.6.b. FALLING IN LOVE WITH THE TREMENDOUSNESS OF THE FUTURE
"When you realize you are mortal you also realize the tremendousness of the future. You fall in love with a Time you will never perceive.”
(Etel Adnan)

E1.6.E.2.6.b_REF1_PO "DEAD STARS

Out here, there’s a bowing even the trees are doing.
            Winter’s icy hand at the back of all of us.
Black bark, slick yellow leaves, a kind of stillness that feels
so mute it’s almost in another year.

I am a hearth of spiders these days: a nest of trying.

We point out the stars that make Orion as we take out
      the trash, the rolling containers a song of suburban thunder.

It’s almost romantic as we adjust the waxy blue
      recycling bin until you say, Man, we should really learn
some new constellations.

And it’s true. We keep forgetting about Antlia, Centaurus,
      Draco, Lacerta, Hydra, Lyra, Lynx.

But mostly we’re forgetting we’re dead stars too, my mouth is full
      of dust and I wish to reclaim the rising —

to lean in the spotlight of streetlight with you, toward
      what’s larger within us, toward how we were born.

Look, we are not unspectacular things.
      We’ve come this far, survived this much. What

would happen if we decided to survive more? To love harder?

What if we stood up with our synapses and flesh and said, No.
      No, to the rising tides.

Stood for the many mute mouths of the sea, of the land?

What would happen if we used our bodies to bargain

for the safety of others, for earth,
      if we declared a clean night, if we stopped being terrified,

if we launched our demands into the sky, made ourselves so big
people could point to us with the arrows they make in their minds,

rolling their trash bins out, after all of this is over?"
(Ada Limón)

E1.6.E.2.6.b_REF2_CUR “WHEN YOU REALIZE YOU ARE MORTAL YOU ALSO REALIZE THE TREMENDOUSNESS OF THE FUTURE, YOU FALL IN LOVE WITH A TIME YOU WILL NEVER PERCEIVE.”
(Etel Adnan, Journey to Mount Tamalpais)

"The five limestone vats of Olivestone had been used to decant olive oil at the Palazzo Durini since the 18th century. Beuys inserted blocks from the same quarry into their cavities, leaving a gap into which oil is regularly poured. In decades to come the oil will be absorbed further and further into the stone, gradu ally turning it back into the fertile primeval slime from which it was created 50 million years ago."
(Curatorship: Kunsthaus Zürich)

E1.6.E.2.6.b_REF3_PO "LORSQUE L'ANGE FAIT SIGNE 
Nous savons que ce qui est né de nous 
      ne cessera plus d'advenir 
En avant de nous, à notre insu 
      soudain nous dépasse, nous sauve"
(François Cheng, Le livre du vide médian)

E1.6.E.2.6.b_REF4_PO "OLD AGE SUPERBLY RISING! O WELCOME, INEFFABLE GRACE OF DYING DAYS!

Every condition promulges not only itself, it promulges what grows after and out of itself,
And the dark hush promulges as much as any. [...] 

There is no stoppage and never can be stoppage,
If I, you, and the worlds, and all beneath or upon their surfaces, were this moment reduced back to a pallid float, it would not avail the long run,
We should surely bring up again where we now stand,
And surely go as much farther, and then farther and farther."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.6.E.2.6.c. WE ARE NOT ASKED TO SACRIFICE OURSELVES!
However, can we be content with a job that contributes to a better future but leaves us unfulfilled? It would take great generosity or resignation to be satisfied with such a situation.

E1.6.E.2.6.c_REF1_CUR THIS OCEAN DWELLING SPIRIT — called the Sea Monk [Umibōzu] because of his smooth monk-like round head — is said to capsize the ship of anyone who dares speak to it. The specific tale illustrated in Kuniyoshi's woodblock tells of a sailor Kawanaya Tokuzo who, despite it being considered unlucky in the world of seafaring, decides to go to sea on the last day of the year. A terrible storm breaks out, and the giant figure of the Sea Monk appears. Against the roar of the waves the apparition asks, « Name the most horrible thing you know! » Tokuzo yells in reply, « My profession is the most horrible thing I know! » The answer apparently satisfies the monster as he then disappears along with the storm.
"An honest man is not accountable for the vice or foolishness of his trade; and yet must not refuse to practice it. It is the custom of his country, and there is profit in it: One must live in the world, and take advantage of it, as one finds it." (Montaigne, Essays, III, X)

E1.6.E.2.7. ARE WE FORGING NEW VALUES FOR OUR TIME?

E1.6.E.2.7.a. ARE WE FORGING NEW VALUES FOR OUR TIME?
Are we living in a society where scientists and philosophers are creating new values for our era? Nihilism, for Nietzsche, occurs when we cease to believe in the established values of a society. Philosophers often respond with joy to this disappearance, embodying an active nihilism that will generate new values more conducive to life's flourishing. We are likely still in a period of passive nihilism...

E1.6.E.2.7.a_REF1_PH NIETZSCHE CRITICIZED PLATONIC AND CHRISTIAN VALUES, SEEING THEM AS FALSE AND INIMICAL TO LIFE. He distinguished between passive nihilism, which results from despair at the loss of these values, and active nihilism, which calls for the creation of new, more fulfilling values. Is today's society still in a period of nihilism, without having forged new values?

"God is Dead
We must understand the paradoxical nature of this sentence, which tells us the impossible: the immortal being is dead. To understand the meaning of this aphorism, we must return to the Nietzschean context, we must take this sentence out of its strictly theological environment: it is not a phrase that has a primarily and above all religious meaning. What Nietzsche means by the word God is not only the divinity that is the object of belief... [...] God is an image... Nietzsche almost always expresses himself in an imaginary way, he plays with it brilliantly, "God" means something that has a philosophical depth. Why choose this word? God represents the sacred, what we worship, what is most precious to us when we are believers, what constitutes the unquestionable, objective norm of existence, and it is precisely this that Nietzsche borrows from the concept of God in order to transfer it to the whole of human life... By God he will mean those things that are sacred, the fundamental, capital beliefs that regulate human life as it is organized in the West. Nietzsche tells us that the sacred, the inviolable (what he will technically call "values" when addressing philosophers), these values are in crisis, even in the process of losing their status as values, norms, references..."
(Patrick Wotling)

When the madman in paragraph 125 of The Gay Science proclaims the death of God in the marketplace and says that he has come too soon in the face of the incomprehension of his contemporaries, what he means is that the conditions of life, the values, the beliefs, and the truths to which a civilization is attached, change slowly and with great inertia.
Between Heraclitus and Nietzsche, philosophers have never questioned the notion of truth more (the Platonic era - Nietzsche will say in Beyond Good and Evil that Christianity is Platonism for the people). Perhaps only Dostoevsky, a little before N. in The Brothers Karamasov, with his "If God is dead, everything is permitted!" to justify the murder of the father. For N., this passive, nihilistic reading, which one draws out of despair as a consequence of the death of God, is that of those who have not understood - those with whom the madman is confronted in The Gay Science. For Nietzsche, our standards were choices, probably poorly chosen values - he is not saying that we can live without standards, but that we must choose better ones. There is no life without a value system. Passive nihilism is what unfortunately happened at the beginning of the 20th century.

For Nietzsche, nihilism is when one ceases to believe in values. The reaction of philosophers to the observation of this disappearance is one of joy, that of an active nihilism that will create new values more favorable to the fulfillment of life. N. will say that it is not his personal genius that allows him to make this diagnosis, but the very history of the evolution of values that allows him to arrive at the right moment. The Platonic values that lead one to think that the good is that which is not sensitive, that which is not alive, are crumbling in N.'s time.
Nietzsche is not a relativist because he does not say that all values are equal or that everyone has his own: a culture has chosen values that become conditions of life respected by its members. Unfortunately, values are often imposed by the law of the strongest and are not the best. So much so that there is no theoretical standard of comparison of values, but there is a hierarchy of values: some are more favorable to life. Example: the Greek miracle at the time of the pre-Socratic tragedy, which valued life. See also Human, all too human, preface of 1886, 6 &amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp; 7. The fact that fanaticism is still proliferating today is a confirmation, 150 years after N., of the despair proclaimed by those who experience the collapse of values in the form of passive nihilism.

Are we in a society where scientists and philosophers are forging new values for our time? We are probably still in the age of nihilism. See also Human, All Too Human 25."
(Patrick Wotling in the program Les Chemins de la Philosophie, broadcast on France Culture on 09/10/2019)

Listen to the podcast in French: https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/quatre-malentendus-nietzscheens-24-dieu-est-mort

E1.6.E.2.7.a_REF2_PH "KNOWLEDGE BRINGS A SPECIFIC JOY, INDEPENDENT OF ITS PRACTICAL USE. This joy is possible because knowledge is a founding act that validates what is founded, and it is intrinsically linked to the joy of understanding.
The joy of founding has another dimension: "epistemological" and not just existential. The traveler of real life realizes this every time he engages in reflective thought or refers to theoretical texts. Knowledge that accounts for itself produces a specific joy, not to be confused with the satisfaction of being able to use the results of this knowledge at a later date; while all knowledge has an existential or practical purpose, being merely the means to a higher end, which is joy itself, it nevertheless has an intrinsic dimension of elation. And for some, the joy of understanding is not inferior to the joy of living, it is not even separable from it: the joy of founding is expressed here as the joy of knowing. 
Why is such joy possible, why is it almost universal, despite the complicit efforts of the will to ignorance? It is because knowledge (like reflective choice) is a founding act. But it does not found in the way one creates or establishes; it founds insofar as it affirms what is founded. Founding knowledge is necessarily super-reflexive: it redoubles the second foundation in which the subject considered itself and its action in order to validate it. [...]"
(Robert Misrahi, The Acts of Joy, 1987)

E1.6.E.2.7.b. MEN OF THE OLD WORLD KNEW BETTER HOW TO REJOICE
The men of the old world knew better how to rejoice. They managed to be less sad. As for humanity's suffering, the Ancients attempted to either forget or direct their emotions towards the pleasant side. They used palliatives, while we address the root causes of evil and prefer to take a prophylactic approach.

E1.6.E.2.7.b_REF1_PH "THE ANTIQUE WORLD AND PLEASURE.—The man of the antique world understood better how to rejoice, we understand better how to grieve less. They
continually found new motives for feeling happy, for celebrating festivals, being inventive with all their wealth of shrewdness and reflection. We, on the other hand, concentrate our intellect rather on the solving of problems which have in view painlessness and the removal of sources of discomfort. With regard to suffering existence, the ancients sought to forget or in some way to convert the sensation into a pleasant one, thus trying to supply palliatives. We attack the causes of suffering, and on the whole prefer to use prophylactics.—Perhaps we are only building upon a foundation whereon a later age will once more set up the temple of joy."
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human II, Aphorism 187, 1878)

E1.6.E.2.7.c. SECESSION IS NECESSARY TO CREATE NEW VALUES. 
Wittgenstein considers that culture is born from the isolation of cultured people from other habits considered as "culture", that produce little innovation. The constant progression in distancing oneself from conventions is necessary to preserve the purity of a modernizing project.  which is why the rhythm of permanent secession characterizes the art of the twentieth century. This is why a rhythm of continuous secession characterizes 20th-century art. The concept of secession is significant for life's organizational forms, even in ancient manifestations. Finding the right form is a design mission that incorporates a moral and logical practice. Philosophy itself implies such a mission and can become mainstream because a school of thought, by its nature, is a secession phenomenon.

E1.6.E.2.7.c_REF1_PH SECESSION IS A NECESSARY BREAK WITH THE ESTABLISHED CONVENTIONS  IN ORDER TO PRESERVE THE PURITY OF THE MODERNIZING PROJECT BY CREATING NEW MODELS OF LIFE AND ORGANIZATION. Philosophy and culture are born from the individual's ability to transform his life.
“Culture is an observance.”

Twilight of life forms, Disciplinarity

Staggering without domination [...]

Culture arises out of secession [...] 

Wittgenstein's use of the concept of "culture" leaves no doubt: culture, in the ambitious sense of the word, arises in his eyes only through the isolation of truly cultivated people from the other so-called "culture," that confused aggregate of good or bad habits which, when added up, produces little more than ordinary "filth.
From this point, it is easier to explain why Wittgenstein is one of the few authors of modernity - perhaps the only one of high standing in the period between Nietzsche and Foucault, apart from Heidegger - in whom one could observe the retrograde metamorphosis of philosophy from a school subject to a committed discipline. [...] 

Only constant progress in distancing oneself from the misery of conventions can preserve the purity of the modernizing project - hence the rhythm of permanent secession that characterizes twentieth-century art and continues until there is nothing left to secede from. [...]

As Allan Janik and Stephen Toulmin have shown, secessionist motives in the broadest sense were inherent to Viennese Modernism. For its protagonists, the founding gesture of culture was to break out of the system of conventions in which the aristocratic bourgeois public of the imperial capital indulged. [...]

Form and Lifestyle 

I would not need to recall these contextual connections if the figure of the Secession, independent of its Viennese history, did not have great significance for everything that will be said in the following pages about the forms of organization of the practice of life, even in its ancient and very ancient manifestations. The act of secession as such already expresses the imperative without which there could never have been any "order," reform, or "revolution: You must change your life! Here it is assumed that life contains something for whose transformation the individual has - or can acquire - competence. In 1937, Wittgenstein observed: "That life is problematic means that your life does not correspond to the form of life. You must therefore change your life, and when it corresponds to such a form, the problem will disappear". [...] Finding the right form is a design task that involves a moral and logical practice. It is only because philosophy itself implies such a mission from the beginning that it can "set an example" - the school as such is already a phenomenon of secession, with Plato, the founder of the Academy, almost in the same way as with the Viennese moderns. Where there is secession, the reformers have reached the end of their patience with the given facts. They no longer want to see either the situations to which they are accustomed or their reproductions. The time has come to turn to models. The model does not reproduce life, it precedes it. One can almost speak of the birth of philosophy out of the spirit of secession - it is not entirely by chance that Plato opened his Athenian educational institution, founded in 387 B.C., in a decentralized place - and which operated continuously until its destruction by Sulla in 86 B.C.: it was a little more than a mile west of the city center, but very well located, next to a rather large area dedicated to sports, the gymnásion, which was obviously soon integrated into the educational curriculum.
To found a school is to reject the kitsch of fate, whether it goes back to the end of Athens or the end of the Habsburg Empire. It requires that questions of destiny be transformed into missions of discipline. [...] 

Let us be frank about the aim of these reflections: by showing that in Wittgenstein's concept of culture there is expressed a strongly secessionist understanding of the "work" on personal faults and those of collective sensibility, we remove all possibilities of recovering it in favor of the egalitarian and relativist ideology that goes hand in hand with the various variants of Anglo-Saxon analytic philosophy. In reality, Wittgenstein's "work" could embody the harshest manifestation of ethical elitism known in the 20th century, with the possible exception of Simone Weil, who is considered the only reformist elitist of equal stature. His secessionist and elitist approach descends to such depths that the author might even have preferred to withdraw from himself and his "filth" if that had been possible. Once Wittgenstein's inexorable elitism has been exposed-an elitism, moreover, that is as radical as it is apolitical and ahistorical-it will not only contaminate the understanding of his most successful theorem, the theory of "language games": Wittgenstein's role as a teacher also appears in a completely different light. [...]"
(Peter Sloterdijk, You must change your life, 2009)

E1.6.E.2.7.c_REF1.1_PH SECESSION IS A NECESSARY BREAK WITH ESTABLISHED CONVENTIONS TO PRESERVE THE PURITY OF THE MODERNIZING PROJECT BY CREATING NEW MODELS OF LIFE AND ORGANIZATION. Philosophy and culture arise from the individual's ability to transform their life.
"Only the constant progression in distancing oneself from the misery of convention can preserve the modernising projec's purity  - hence the rhythm of permanent secession that characterises twentieth-century art and remains in motion until there is nothing left with which to secede. [...] Secessionist motifs, in the broadest sense of the term, were inherent in Viennese modernity. For its protagonists, the founding gesture of culture consisted in breaking out of the system of conventions in which the aristocratic-bourgeois public of the empire's capital was indulging. [...]
The secession's gesture expresses the imperative without which there could never have been « a new order », « reform », « revolution »: You must change your life! Here it is assumed that  the individual has a competence for transforming his life - or can acquire it. In 1937, Wittgenstein noted: « If life is problematic, this means that your life does not fit the form of living. You must then change your life, and if it fits such a form, what is problematic will disappear. » [...]
Finding the right form is a design task that requires moral and logical practice. It is only because philosophy itself implies such a mission from the outset that it can « be passed on as the teachings of a school » - Plato's Academy was already a phenomenon of secession, almost in the same way as in the Viennese moderns. Where secession broods, the reformers have lost patience with the status quo. They want to depart from the situations they are used to and from their reproductions. The time has come to turn to a new model. The model does not reproduce life, it precedes it. One can almost speak of the birth of philosophy from the spirit of secession."
(Peter Sloterdijk, You must change your life, 2009)

E1.6.F. FORGETTING, FORGIVENESS, KINDNESS, AND GRATITUDE
Forgetting and forgiving often lead to happiness, even though they're largely involuntary. Can we not nurture these feelings by beginning with kindness and benevolence, and even gratitude if we're able to?

E1.6.F.1. FORGETTING

E1.6.F.1.1. HE WHO CANNOT FORGET WILL NEVER BE HAPPY
Happiness requires the ability to forget or to perceive things outside of their historical context. To be happy, one needs to free themselves from the constraints of the past by acknowledging their own freedom. A distinct, active, and delegated force should support consciousness, restoring its freshness, fluidity, and lightness at every moment. This force is the power of forgetting. A common mistake in psychology is to view forgetting as a negative trait, overlooking its active and positive nature. Nietzsche describes the power of forgetting as a "dampening apparatus", a "plastic, regenerative, and curative force."

E1.6.F.1.1.a. THE BENEFITS AND DRAWBACKS OF HISTORY FOR LIFE
According to Nietzsche, humans grapple with the weight of the past, which can feel like an invisible burden impeding progress. He underscores the importance of forgetting for happiness and critiques an overemphasis on historical perspective, noting that all action involves some degree of forgetting. High levels of historical consciousness can result in the weakening and even collapse of the individual, society, or civilization.

"Parmiggiani's work follows in the footsteps of Arte Povera, the Italian movement of the 1960s whose creative methods opposed the productivist logic of the consumer society. By creating a controlled fire, the artist in this case allowed a thin film of soot to form on the shelves of his well stocked bookcase. Once the furnishings were removed, an imprint with vaporous contours emerged in negative. "All that remained were the shadows of things, almost the ectoplasm of vanished forms, like the shadows of human bodies vaporised on the walls of Hiroshima."
(Curatorship: Musée de l’Orangerie, Paris)

E1.6.F.1.1.a_REF1_PH FRIEDRICH NIETZSCHE CONTRASTS THE ANIMAL'S ABILITY TO LIVE ENTIRELY IN THE PRESENT WITH MAN'S BURDEN OF THE PAST, WHICH HINDERS HIS HAPPINESS AND PROGRESS. He argues that true happiness requires the ability to forget and live in the moment, free from historical perspective.
"The animal, [...] lives in a non-historical way: it resolves itself entirely in the present like a figure that divides itself without leaving any remainder, it does knows how to simulate, it hides nothing and appears every second as it is, therefore it can only be sincere. Man, on the other hand, bows against the ever more crushing burden of the past, which throws him to the ground or lays him on his side, which hinders his walk like an obscure and invisible burden. This burden, he may occasionally affect to deny it and, in the trade with his fellow men, denies it only too willingly in order to arouse their envy. But he is moved, as if by the memory of a lost paradise, when he sees the herd at pasture or, closer and more familiar, the child who does not yet have a past to deny and who plays, blind and fulfilled, between the barriers of the past and the future. However, his game will have to be disturbed, and he will only too soon be teared away from his unconsciousness. He will then learn to understand the expression « it used to be », a formulation which abons Man to fights, suffering and disgust, and reminds him that his existence is basically nothing else than an eternal imperfection. [...]
There is always one thing which makes happiness becomes happiness: the faculty to forget or, in more elaborate terms, the faculty to feel things, as long as happiness lasts, outside any historical perspective. He who does not know how to settle on the threshold of the moment, forgetting victory, standing on a single point, without fear and without vertigo, will never know what happiness is, and even worse: will never do anything to make others happy.
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Untimely Considerations II, 1874)

E1.6.F.1.1.a_REF2_PH NIETZSCHE ARGUES THAT HAPPINESS AND EFFECTIVE ACTION REQUIRE THE ABILITY TO FORGET. He contrasts the vitality of those with limited historical knowledge, who live with immediacy and vigor, against those who are consumed by constant reflection, without the capacity for oblivion.
"There is always one thing by which happiness becomes happiness : the faculty of forgetting or, in more scholarly terms, the faculty of feeling things, for as long as happiness lasts, isolated from any historical perspective. Whoever does not know how to settle on the threshold of the moment, forgetting victory, standing on a single point, without fear and without vertigo, will never know what happiness is, worse still : he will never do anything that makes others happy. [...] All action requires oblivion, just as all organic life requires not only light, but also darkness. A man who would like to feel things in an absolutely and exclusively historical way would resemble someone who has been forced to deprive himself of sleep or an animal which should live only by constantly ruminating on the same food. It is therefore possible to live, and even to live happily, almost without any memory, as the animal shows; but it is absolutely impossible to live without forgetting. Or, to explain myself even more simply on my subject : there is a degree of insomnia, of rumination, of historical meaning, beyond which the living being is shaken and finally destroyed, whether it is an individual, a people or a civilization. [...]

Everyone has already made the following observation on this subject: an individual's historical knowledge and sensitivity may be extremely limited, his horizon as bounded as that of an inhabitant of an Alpine valley, he may be unfair in all his judgments and believe himself to hold the exclusivity of every experience he makes - he enjoys, despite all his errors and injustices, a health, an irresistible vigor in which all eyes take pleasure; while by his side, the infinitely more just and learned man is consumed and withered, for his lines of horizin are constantly shifting, for he cannot free himself from the much more tenuous web of his justice and truth, to rediscover the elemental immediacy of will and desire. ""
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Untimely meditations II, 1874)

E1.6.F.1.1.a_REF3_PH CHARACTER INVOLVES THE COURAGE TO FORGE NEW PATHS, INSPIRE OTHERS AND THE ABILITY TO TRANSCEND PAST ADVERSITIES WITHOUT BEING HAUNTED BY THEM. 
"The difference between talents and character is adroitness to keep the old and trodden round, and power and courage to make a new road to new and better goals. Character makes an overpowering present; a cheerful, determined hour, which fortifies all the company, by making them see that much is possible and excellent that was not thought of. Character dulls the impression of particular events. When we see the conqueror, we do not think much of any one battle or success… The great man is not convulsible or tormentable; events pass over him without much impression. People say sometimes, ‘See what I have overcome; see how cheerful I am; see how completely I have triumphed over these black events.’ Not if they still remind me of the black event. True conquest is the causing the calamity to fade and disappear, as an early cloud of insignificant result in a history so large and advancing. [...]

The one thing which we seek with insatiable desire is to forget ourselves, to be surprised out of our propriety, to lose our sempiternal memory, and to do something without knowing how or why; in short, to draw a new circle. Nothing great was ever achieved without enthusiasm. The way of life is wonderful: it is by abandonment. The great moments of history are the facilities of performance through the strength of ideas… They ask the aid of wild passions… to ape in some manner these flames and generosities of the heart."
(Ralph Waldo Emerson, Circles, 1841 - Essays and Lectures)

E1.6.F.1.1.a_REF4_PH AN EXAMPLE OF SUFFERING DUE TO THE INABILITY TO FORGET “NOT BUYING TOO DEAR.—The things that we buy too dear we generally turn to bad use, because we have no love for them but only a painful recollection. Thus they involve a twofold drawback.”
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human II, Aphorism 395, 1878)

E1.6.F.1.1.a_REF5_PO “EVERYTHING THAT EXISTS IN THE WORLD
HAS A HISTORY
Except the frogs croaking in the depths 
Of my memory.

Every place in the world has somewhere
To be
Except the pond from where this croaking 
Is coming.

Within me a false moon rises
Over rushes,
And the pond appears, lit more and less 
By the moon.

In what life, where and how, was I
What I remember
Thanks to the frogs croaking in the wake 
Of what I forget?

Nothing—just silence dozing among rushes. 
At the end
Of my huge old soul the frogs croak 
Without me.”
(Fernando Pessoa, 13 August 1933)

E1.6.F.1.1.b_REF1_PH "THE ANTIQUE WORLD AND PLEASURE.—The man of the antique world understood better how to rejoice, we understand better how to grieve less. They continually found new motives for feeling happy, for celebrating festivals, being inventive with all their wealth of shrewdness and reflection. We, on the other hand, concentrate our intellect rather on the solving of problems which have in view painlessness and the removal of sources of discomfort. With regard to suffering existence, the ancients sought to forget or in some way to convert the sensation into a pleasant one, thus trying to supply palliatives. We attack the causes of suffering, and on the whole prefer to use prophylactics.—Perhaps we are only building upon a foundation whereon a later age will once more set up the temple of joy."
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human II, Aphorism 187, 1878)

E1.6.F.1.1.c. FREEING OURSELVES FROM PAST DETERMINISMS BY TAKING THE MEASURE OF OUR FREEDOM

E1.6.F.1.1.c_REF1_PH "SUFFERING FROM CULTURE’S PAST. — Whoever has clearly understood the problem of culture suffers from a feeling similar to that of a man who has
inherited riches that were acquired through illegal means, or a prince who rules because of his forefathers’ atrocities. He thinks of his origin with sadness, and is often ashamed, often irritable. The whole sum of the strength, will to life, and joy that he expends on his estate is often balanced by a deep weariness: he cannot forget his origin. He regards the future with melancholy: he knows in advance that his descendants will suffer from the past as he does."
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human, Aphorism 249, 1878)
